The compound joins two distinct elements, each with significant West-Semitic background. // **Element 1: *El* (אֵל).** The shared West-Semitic high-god name. Attested across the West-Semitic family: **Ugaritic *ʾIl*** — the chief of the divine assembly, the *bull-El* (*ṯr ʾil*), the *father of years* (*ʾab šnm*), creator and high-god of the Canaanite pantheon (Cross, *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 13-75; Smith, *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001, pp. 41-66); **Phoenician *ʾl*** (the Karatepe inscription, Eshmunazar, the Tabnit inscriptions); **Akkadian *ilu*** as the generic god-word and as a personal divine name; **Aramaic *ʾl*** at Sefire and elsewhere. In the Hebrew Bible, *El* functions both as a generic god-word (often paralleled with *Elohim*) and as the divine-name component in theophoric compounds: *El Shaddai* (Gen 17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 48:3, Exod 6:3), *El Olam* (Gen 21:33), *El Bethel* (Gen 31:13, 35:7), *El Elohei Yisra'el* (Gen 33:20). The patriarchal narratives' systematic use of *El*-compounds has been read since **Albrecht Alt's *The God of the Fathers* (1929)** and **Frank Moore Cross's *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* (1973)** as preserving an earlier stratum of patriarchal religion in which the patriarchs worshipped *El* at named sanctuary-sites — *El Shaddai* at one sanctuary, *El Bethel* at another, *El Elyon* at Jerusalem-Shalem — and the *YHWH*-name harmonization came later (with the editor's appositional construction at v 22 being the explicit harmonizing move). The reading is mainstream modern critical scholarship. // **Element 2: *Elyon* (עֶלְיוֹן).** The epithet *highest / uppermost / most-high*, derived from the root *ʿ-l-h* (*go up*). *Elyon* is itself attested as an **independent divine name** in West-Semitic: **Philo of Byblos** (preserved in Eusebius, *Praeparatio Evangelica* 1.10), citing the (purportedly) Phoenician priest **Sanchuniathon**, names **Elioun** (Ἐλιοῦν), called *Hypsistos* (Most High), as a distinct deity — the father of *Ouranos* and *Ge* (Sky and Earth) in the Phoenician theogony. The attestation is late (Philo writes ca. 1st-2nd century CE, citing material he claims is far older), but it preserves a memory of *Elyon* as an independent divine name in the West-Semitic pantheon. **Within the Hebrew Bible**, *Elyon* occurs alone (not compounded with *El*) at several anchor points: **Num 24:16** — Balaam's third oracle, *u-yodea da'at Elyon* — *and one who knows the knowledge of Elyon*; **Deut 32:8** — the load-bearing divine-council verse (see below); **Ps 9:3, 7:18, 21:8, 46:5, 47:3, 50:14, 57:3, 73:11, 77:11, 78:17, 78:35, 78:56, 82:6, 83:19, 91:1, 91:9, 92:2, 97:9, 107:11, Isa 14:14, Lam 3:35, 3:38** — the Psalter uses *Elyon* widely as YHWH's epithet, often in royal-psalm and praise contexts. // **Deuteronomy 32:8-9 is the most lens-relevant locus** for the *Elyon* divine-council material. The verse: *b-hanchel Elyon goyim b-haphrido bnei adam, yatzev g'vulot amim l-mispar bnei [Elohim / Yisra'el]* — *when Elyon allotted the nations as their inheritance, when he separated the sons of man, he established the borders of the peoples according to the number of the [sons of Elohim / sons of Israel]*. **Textual variant: MT reads *bnei Yisra'el* (sons of Israel); the DSS reading at 4QDeut^j and 4QDeut^q reads *bnei Elohim* (sons of Elohim) — divine sons / divine beings; the LXX reads *aggelōn theou* (angels of God), itself a translation of an underlying *bnei Elohim* / *bnei El*.** The DSS / LXX reading is the modern scholarly consensus as the original reading: **Skehan** (*A Fragment of the 'Song of Moses' (Deut. 32) from Qumran*, BASOR 1959), **Tov** (*Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible*, 3rd ed. 2012), **Tigay** (*JPS Deuteronomy*, 1996, Excursus 31 on Deut 32:8), and the **NRSV** (which translates from the DSS / LXX reading: 'according to the number of the gods') and **NJPS** all accept the DSS reading. The reading recovers a divine-council picture: *Elyon* is the high-god who, at the time of the post-Babel scattering of the nations, allots the peoples to the *bnei Elohim* — the divine sons, the divine council — with each people receiving a divine patron. **Verse 9** continues: *ki chelek YHWH amo, Ya'akov chevel nachalato* — *for YHWH's portion is his people, Jacob is the lot of his inheritance*. **The two verses together place Elyon and YHWH in a hierarchical-distinct relation in one stratum of the corpus**: *Elyon* is the allocating high-god; *YHWH* is the divine patron who receives Jacob/Israel as his allotment. The reading is the canonical critical reading of Deut 32:8-9. // **The Gen 14 editor's harmonization at v 22.** Abram swears by *YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz* — explicitly identifying YHWH *with* the El Elyon of Melchizedek's blessing at vv 18-20. The appositional construction is the harmonizing-syncretism move: the Genesis editor accepts the Melchizedek-tradition's El Elyon as the name of the high-god, but identifies that high-god with YHWH. The move is precisely parallel to the Documentary-Hypothesis reading of Exod 6:3 — *I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them* — where the editor harmonizes the patriarchal *El Shaddai*-religion with the Mosaic YHWH-name. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, pp. 50-60), **Smith** (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001, pp. 135-148), **Mullen** (*The Divine Council in Canaanite and Early Hebrew Literature*, 1980) treat the harmonization-pattern in detail. // **The lens-relevant material — the West-Semitic-pantheon framework, the Deut 32:8 divine-council reading, the El-and-YHWH harmonization** — is **mainstream modern critical scholarship**, not a Wheel-of-Heaven-distinctive claim. The WoH lens reads through this scholarly framework but does not need to extend it; the framework already supplies the layered-divine-council picture the lens is interested in. The Editor's posture on this entry is therefore *measured*: the entry reports what the mainstream scholarship reports, with the Deut 32:8 DSS-versus-MT textual variant flagged accurately and the West-Semitic *El* and *Elyon* attestations cited with the standard scholarly sources. The WoH lens-specific reading lives elsewhere (in the wiki, in the chapter-essay layer of the corpus, in the methodology page) — not in this glossary entry. // **The WoH translation renders 'El Elyon'** as a bare transliteration of the divine-name compound (rather than the conventional 'God Most High'), per the WoH convention of preserving Hebrew divine-name compounds in translation. The convention preserves the West-Semitic-pantheon character of the name and refuses the silent-singularization that the English 'God Most High' performs. // The claim_type is *direct* on the name itself (the compound *El Elyon* is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:18, 14:19, 14:20, 14:22; the West-Semitic *El* and *Elyon* attestations are direct documentary evidence; the Deut 32:8 DSS *bnei Elohim* reading is direct textual data). The West-Semitic-pantheon framework reading is *scholarly synthesis* (Cross 1973, Smith 2001) but the synthesis is mainstream modern critical scholarship — direct in the sense that the field treats it as the consensus reconstruction, not as a fringe inference.

terms.literal: acquirer / creator of heaven and earth
terms.standardEnglish: possessor of heaven and earth / maker of heaven and earth
terms.wohChoice: creator of heaven and earth
terms.rationale:
**The epithet *koneh shamayim va-aretz* at Gen 14:19 and 14:22 is an inherited West-Semitic high-god title** — attached in the Hebrew Bible to *El Elyon* — and one of the most directly attested cross-Semitic philological links in the canon.

The Hebrew verb-root *q-n-h* has a lexical duality: in dominant Biblical Hebrew usage it means *acquire / possess / buy* (Gen 4:1, 25:10, Exod 21:2, and the noun *miqneh*); in an older, more poetic register it means *create / produce / beget*. The create-sense is preserved at **Deut 32:6** *halo hu avikha qanekha* ('is he not your father who created you'), at **Ps 139:13** *ki atah qanita khilyotai* ('for you created my inward parts' — a womb-formation context), and at **Prov 8:22** *YHWH qanani reshit darko* (the personified Wisdom's origin — and the verse over which the entire 4th-century Arian-Athanasian Christological dispute turned).

The **cross-Semitic parallels are decisive for the *create*-sense in divine-epithet contexts**. The Ugaritic high-god **El** bears the epithet ***qny ʾrṣ*** — *creator of the earth*. The Phoenician **Karatepe inscription** (KAI 26, 8th c. BCE) contains the divine-name compound ***ʾl qn ʾrṣ*** — *El, creator of the earth* — the closest comparative cognate to the Hebrew Gen 14 formula (identical form, same divine-name pairing). The Hittite-Hurrian **Elkunirsa myth** preserves the same divine epithet as a personal name (*Elkunirsa* = ʾEl-qōnē-ʾaraṣ). The 2nd-millennium West-Semitic *qny ʾrṣ* epithet is well-attested as a stable, transmissible high-god title attached to *El*, and in every attested cross-Semitic instance the meaning is unambiguously *create*, not *possess*.

Westermann (1985, p. 204), Sarna (1989, p. 109), Wenham (1987, p. 317), Hamilton (1990, p. 410), Cross (1973, pp. 15-18, 50-52), and Smith (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001) all treat the epithet as inherited West-Semitic. The modern English translation tradition splits on which Hebrew sense — *possess* or *create* — best preserves the inherited meaning: ASV / KJV / RSV read 'possessor'; NRSV / NJPS / NIV / Alter / Speiser read 'maker / creator'.

The WoH translation reads **'creator of heaven and earth'**, departing from ASV in favor of the modern critical consensus. The choice rests on the cross-Semitic epithet evidence: in the specific *El qn ʾrṣ* high-god-epithet context that Gen 14:19, 22 inherits, the West-Semitic comparators preserve only the *create* sense. The internal Hebrew create-sense attestations (Deut 32:6, Ps 139:13, Prov 8:22) reinforce that the verb's create-sense is alive in early Hebrew divine-context usage. The dominant Biblical Hebrew *possess*-sense of *q-n-h* is preserved in the glossary apparatus for the lexical layer; the wohChoice 'creator' preserves the inherited West-Semitic epithet meaning in the translation.

terms.literal: tithe / tenth-part
terms.standardEnglish: tithe
terms.wohChoice: tithe
terms.rationale: **Gen 14:20 is the first occurrence of *ma'aser* (tithe) in the Hebrew Bible.** *Va-yiten lo ma'aser mi-kol* — *and he gave him a tithe of all*. The verse establishes the institution of the tithe at a pre-Sinai, pre-Mosaic, pre-Aaronic locus: Abram gives a tenth of the spoils to a non-Israelite priest of El Elyon. The first-occurrence locus is structurally and theologically load-bearing. // **The grammatical ambiguity of *va-yiten lo*.** The Hebrew is ambiguous about the direction of the giving: *va-yiten lo* — *and he gave to him* — does not specify who gave to whom. Reading 1: **Abram gave the tithe to Melchizedek** (the consensus reading, supported by the parallel structure of the previous verses: Melchizedek blesses Abram in vv 19-20a; Abram responds with a tithe in v 20b — the standard ancient Near Eastern priest-king/recipient pattern); the reading is what **Hebrews 7:2** explicitly assumes (*and to him Abraham apportioned a tenth of everything*) and what the patristic, rabbinic, and modern critical traditions take. Reading 2: **Melchizedek gave the tithe to Abram** (a minority reading; would imply Melchizedek shared a tenth of the priestly stores with Abram; the reading is grammatically possible but contextually strained). The WoH translation follows the consensus Reading 1: 'and he [Abram] gave him [Melchizedek] a tithe of all'. // **The pre-Sinai, pre-Aaronic institution.** Gen 14:20 establishes the tithe as a Hebrew Bible institution before the Sinai legislation. The Aaronic / Levitical tithe-legislation is not given until **Lev 27:30-33** (the holiness-code tithe-law: *every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the tree, is YHWH's*) and **Num 18:21-32** (the Levitical tithe: the Levites receive *kol-ma'aser b-Yisra'el* — *every tithe in Israel* — as their inheritance in lieu of land). The Deuteronomic tithe-laws (**Deut 12:6, 11, 17**; **Deut 14:22-29** — the *ma'aser sheni*, the second tithe; **Deut 26:12** — the triennial tithe for the Levite, sojourner, orphan, and widow) further develop the institution. The full corpus of biblical tithe-law builds out from Sinai; Gen 14:20 stands as the *aetiological precedent*: the patriarch's tithe to the El-Elyon-priest pre-figures and warrants the later institution. // **Gen 28:22 — Jacob's tithe at Bethel** is the second pre-Sinai tithe-instance and the structural parallel to Gen 14:20. After the Bethel theophany, Jacob vows: *v-kol asher titen-li aser a-asrenu lakh* — *and of all that you give me, I will surely give the tenth to you*. The two pre-Sinai tithe-instances — Abram at Gen 14:20 (to the Melchizedek-Elyon priest) and Jacob at Gen 28:22 (to YHWH at Bethel) — together establish the patriarchal-stratum tithe institution. The rabbinic tradition (Tg. Yer. on Gen 14:20; Tg. PsJ on Gen 28:22) and the modern critical commentaries (Sarna, *JPS Genesis*, 1989, pp. 109, 200; Wenham, *WBC Genesis*, 1987, pp. 317-318) treat the two verses as a deliberate set. // **Cross-cultural tithe-parallels.** The ancient Near Eastern tithe institution is well-attested: **Mesopotamian *ešrû / ešretu*** (tithe) — Sumerian and Akkadian temple-economy tithes from the third millennium onward; **Ugaritic *mʿšr*** — the cognate noun, attested in the Ugaritic economic and administrative texts; **Egyptian temple-tithes** — the New Kingdom temple economy depended heavily on tithe-like state revenues. **Phoenician** evidence (the Carthaginian tariff inscriptions, KAI 69 and the Marseilles Tariff) attests detailed sacrificial-tithe regulations cognate to the Levitical material. The Genesis tithe-institution is intelligible in the broader West-Semitic / ANE temple-economy context; the patriarchal narratives' use of the institution is consistent with the 2nd-millennium West-Semitic background. // **DOWNSTREAM-RECEPTION (flagged, not preempted): Hebrews 7:1-10.** The pre-Aaronic locus of Gen 14:20 becomes the load-bearing fact of an entire Christological priesthood-argument. **Heb 7:2-10** — *to him Abraham apportioned a tenth of everything... see how great this man was to whom even the patriarch Abraham gave a tithe of the spoils!... and one might even say that Levi himself, who receives tithes, paid tithes through Abraham, for he was still in the loins of his ancestor when Melchizedek met him* — uses the Genesis tithe-instance to argue the superiority of the Melchizedek priesthood over the Aaronic / Levitical order. The reading is post-biblical Christian exegesis; the structural fact it leans on — that Abram tithed to a non-Aaronic priest before the Aaronic priesthood existed — is direct. // **Later prophetic tithe-material.** **Mal 3:8-10** — *will a man rob Elohim? Yet you rob me. But you say, How have we robbed you? In tithes and offerings... bring the whole tithe into the storehouse* — the post-exilic prophetic indictment of withheld tithes. The Malachi material extends the tithe-institution into the Second Temple liturgical-economic frame; it is the canonical Hebrew Bible's last major tithe-passage. // **The WoH translation renders 'a tithe of all'**, matching ASV / NRSV / NJPS consensus. The first-occurrence status, the pre-Sinai / pre-Aaronic locus, the Gen 28:22 Jacob-tithe cross-reference, the ANE tithe-context, and the Hebrews 7 downstream reception are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:20. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the first-occurrence status is verifiable by lexical search of the Hebrew Bible; (b) the pre-Sinai locus is grammatically explicit (the verse is at Gen 14, before Exod 19's Sinai event); (c) the Lev 27 / Num 18 / Deut 12-14 / Deut 26 cross-references for the developed tithe-legislation are grammatically explicit; (d) the Gen 28:22 Jacob-tithe parallel is grammatically explicit; (e) the Hebrews 7:1-10 downstream reception is flagged as post-biblical without entering the translation.

terms.literal: YHWH, El Elyon
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD, God Most High
terms.wohChoice: YHWH, El Elyon
terms.rationale: **Gen 14:22 is the chapter's keystone theological move: Abram's oath identifies YHWH appositionally with El Elyon.** *Harimoti yadi el-YHWH El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz* — *I have lifted my hand to YHWH, El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth*. The construction places **YHWH** (the Abrahamic divine name) in apposition with **El Elyon** (the Melchizedek-tradition divine name from vv 18-20). The appositional reading is the natural Hebrew syntax — no waw conjunctive between the names, no construct relation — and yields: *YHWH [who is] El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth*. The Genesis editor's narrative move is to harmonize the two divine-name traditions by identifying them. // **The appositional construction is the consensus reading.** **LXX** renders *kyrion ton theon ton hypsiston* — *the Lord, the Most High God* — preserving the apposition. **Samaritan Pentateuch** preserves the appositional reading. **Targum Onkelos** reads *Memra de-YHWH El Elyon* (the Memra of YHWH El Elyon), preserving the appositional identification. **Vulgate** reads *ad Dominum Deum excelsum* — same apposition. The reading is uncontested across the early versions. // **The textual variant.** A small minority of textual witnesses preserves a different reading: a fragment of the **Samaritan tradition** and some **Peshitta witnesses** omit *YHWH* and read only *El Elyon koneh shamayim va-aretz*. The variant has been read by **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 208) and others as preserving the *older* form of the verse, with the *YHWH* gloss being a later Yahwistic harmonization. The reasoning: at the pre-redactional level, Abram swears by *El Elyon* (matching Melchizedek's blessing-formula at vv 19-20); a later Yahwistic editor adds *YHWH* in apposition to identify the patriarchal divine name with the canonical Israelite divine name. The reading is plausible source-critical analysis and is consistent with the broader patriarchal-El-religion reconstruction (Alt 1929, Cross 1973). The MT reading with *YHWH* is what the canonical text preserves; the variant is a redactional shadow. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973, p. 51) discusses the variant in the context of the El-YHWH harmonization-pattern. // **The harmonization-pattern across Genesis.** The Gen 14:22 move is one instance of a broader Genesis editorial pattern: identifying the patriarchal-stratum *El*-religion with the *YHWH*-name. **Exod 6:2-3** is the most explicit harmonization-passage: *Va-y'daber Elohim el-Moshe va-yomer elav ani YHWH; va-era el-Avraham el-Yitzchak v-el-Ya'akov b-El Shaddai u-shemi YHWH lo nodati lahem* — *And Elohim spoke to Moses and said to him, I am YHWH. I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them*. The Exod 6:3 verse explicitly states the harmonization: the patriarchal-stratum divine name was *El Shaddai*, the Mosaic-stratum revelation is *YHWH*, and the two are the *same deity under different names*. The Gen 14:22 appositional construction is the Genesis-narrative-stratum version of the same harmonization-move, applied to the *El Elyon* of the Melchizedek tradition. The pattern recurs with *El Olam* (Gen 21:33: *va-yiqra sham b-shem YHWH El Olam* — *and he called there on the name of YHWH, El Olam* — the same appositional construction at the Beer-Sheba sanctuary); *El Bethel* (Gen 31:13, 35:7); *El Elohei Yisra'el* (Gen 33:20). Each patriarchal sanctuary-site preserves an *El*-name with a distinctive epithet; the Genesis editor harmonizes each with YHWH. // **The lens-relevant editorial-syncretism.** The Gen 14:22 move is *direct* in that it is grammatically explicit in the text: Abram swears by *YHWH El Elyon*, with no conjunction between the names, in apposition. The Genesis editor explicitly is identifying YHWH with the El Elyon of the Melchizedek tradition. The fact that this is an editorial move (rather than a pre-textual fact) is the modern critical reading — that the harmonization is a Genesis-editor's harmonization-of-traditions move, working to unify the El-stratum patriarchal religion with the YHWH-stratum Mosaic religion. **Cross** (*Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*, 1973), **Smith** (*The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*, 2001), **Albertz** (*A History of Israelite Religion in the Old Testament Period*, 1994), and **Mark Smith** (*The Early History of God*, 2nd ed. 2002) treat the pattern in detail. // **The WoH translation renders 'YHWH, El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth'** with comma-apposition, matching ASV (which reads 'Jehovah, God Most High'). The comma-apposition preserves the Hebrew construction (no conjunctive between the names) while making the appositional reading unambiguous in English. The harmonization-reading, the textual variant, and the broader Genesis El-YHWH editorial-pattern are flagged in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 14:22. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the appositional construction is grammatically explicit at Gen 14:22; (b) the LXX, Samaritan, Targum, and Vulgate uniform appositional reading is direct documentary evidence; (c) the broader Genesis harmonization-pattern (*El Shaddai* at Exod 6:3, *El Olam* at Gen 21:33, *El Bethel* at Gen 31:13 / 35:7, *El Elohei Yisra'el* at Gen 33:20) is grammatically explicit at each cited verse; (d) the textual variant (omission of *YHWH*) is direct manuscript evidence even though minority. The redactional reading is scholarly synthesis (Westermann, Cross), but the synthesis is the mainstream modern critical consensus on the Genesis editor's harmonization-of-traditions work.

terms.literal: the word-of-YHWH came / was
terms.standardEnglish: the word of the LORD came
terms.wohChoice: the word of YHWH came
terms.rationale: **Gen 15:1 is the inaugural occurrence of the *davar-YHWH* prophetic-word-formula in the Hebrew Bible.** *Va-y'hi devar-YHWH el-Avram ba-machazeh lemor* — *and the word of YHWH came to Abram in a vision, saying*. The formula — *hayah devar-YHWH el-X lemor*, with the prefixed waw-consecutive — becomes the standard introductory formula of the prophetic corpus. By a conservative count, the formula recurs more than 250 times across Samuel, Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the Twelve; in Ezekiel alone the formula introduces nearly every oracle (more than 50 instances). // **The Genesis editor's placement of the formula at 15:1 marks Abram as the first prophetic recipient.** The patriarchal stratum is here annexed to the prophetic stratum by a single formulaic move: Abram is the canonical first *navi* (prophet) — a designation made retroactively explicit at Gen 20:7, where Abimelech is told *ki navi hu* — *for he is a prophet*. Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 220) treats the formula's first occurrence as theologically load-bearing: the introductory formula is no incidental detail but the framing-move that makes Abram a prototype of the later prophets. Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 112) and Wenham (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 326) concur. // **The formula's structural pattern.** The full formula has three slots: (a) *hayah devar-YHWH* (the word-of-YHWH came), (b) *el-X* (to X), (c) *lemor* (saying), followed by direct speech. Variants include the personal-name slot (Abram, Samuel, Nathan, Elijah, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, the Twelve), the dating-formula prefix (most developed in Ezekiel and the dated Jeremiah oracles), and the place-formula (where the prophet was when the word came). The formula's variants are catalogued in Westermann's *Basic Forms of Prophetic Speech* (1967). // **The recurrence-pattern across the prophets.** The formula opens 1 Sam 15:10 (to Samuel about Saul's rejection); 2 Sam 7:4 (to Nathan, the Davidic covenant); 1 Kgs 6:11, 12:22, 13:20, 16:1, 17:2, 17:8, 18:1, 21:17, 21:28 (Elijah-Elisha cycle); 1 Kgs 18:1 (the Mt Carmel narrative); and pervasively across Jeremiah and Ezekiel. Isaiah uses the formula less frequently than the other major prophets; Amos and the other early prophets prefer *koh amar YHWH* (thus says YHWH) as their primary introducer. // **The 15:1 collocation with *ba-machazeh*** — *in a vision* — is unique to this verse (the lexeme *machazeh* has its own entry); the prophetic word and the prophetic vision converge in a single inaugural revelation. // The WoH translation renders 'the word of YHWH came to Abram in a vision', matching ASV's 'the word of Jehovah came unto Abram in a vision'. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula is grammatically explicit at the cited verse; (b) the inaugural-status is verifiable by lexical search of the Hebrew Bible; (c) the prophetic-corpus distribution is well-attested.

terms.literal: vision (from ḥ-z-h, to see)
terms.standardEnglish: vision
terms.wohChoice: vision
terms.rationale: **The lexeme *machazeh* names the specific Hebrew Bible state of prophetic-revelatory seeing.** *Ba-machazeh* at Gen 15:1 — *in a vision* — is the first occurrence; the lexeme recurs at Num 24:4, 16 (Balaam's oracles, *machazeh Shaddai yechezeh* — *the vision of Shaddai he sees*) and Ezek 13:7 (the false-prophet polemic, *halo machazeh-shav chazitem* — *have you not seen a false vision?*). Four occurrences total — a tightly-restricted lexeme. // **Distinct from cognate lexemes.** The Hebrew Bible distinguishes several seeing-revelation terms: *chazon* (vision, from the same root ḥ-z-h; the standard prophetic-book superscription term, cf. Isa 1:1 *chazon Yesha'yahu*); *mar'eh* (vision, from r-ʾ-h, *to see*; the term for the Mosaic theophany at Exod 3:3 and the Daniel night-visions); *chizayon* (vision, by-form of *chazon*; especially Job 4:13, 7:14, 20:8, Joel 2:28); *machazeh* — narrower, the specific revelatory-seeing state. BDB and HALOT separate the four nouns; modern critical scholarship (Westermann *Basic Forms of Prophetic Speech*, 1967; Lindblom *Prophecy in Ancient Israel*, 1962) treats the lexical-semantic distinction as substantive. // **The collocation with *davar-YHWH* at Gen 15:1 is unique.** The other three occurrences of *machazeh* do not pair the vision-lexeme with the prophetic-word-formula; the Gen 15:1 collocation makes the inaugural revelation simultaneously a word and a vision. Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 220) reads the doubled formula as the Genesis editor's most explicit framing-move marking Abram as prototypical prophet. // **The Balaam-Genesis link.** The two Num 24 occurrences are both in oracles delivered by Balaam, a non-Israelite prophet who falls into a trance state at the moment of receiving the *machazeh Shaddai*. The lexeme's distribution therefore links the patriarchal stratum (Gen 15) to the Transjordan-prophet stratum (Num 24) by a shared revelation-vocabulary; the Ezek 13:7 occurrence inverts the term polemically (a false *machazeh*) against false prophets. // The WoH translation renders 'in a vision', matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct*.

terms.literal: I am a shield to you
terms.standardEnglish: I am your shield
terms.wohChoice: I am your shield
terms.rationale: **Gen 15:1 introduces the *magen* divine-epithet — *anokhi magen lakh* — *I am a shield to you* — at the inaugural patriarchal prophetic revelation.** The lexeme *magen* (shield, buckler, protector) is here first deployed as a divine epithet; the form has a wide recurrence across the Hebrew Bible's poetic and prophetic corpora as a standard image of divine protection. // **The recurrence-set.** **Deut 33:29** — *Yisra'el... am nosha b-YHWH magen ezrekha* — *Israel... a people saved by YHWH, the shield of your help*. **2 Sam 22:3** (= **Ps 18:3**) — *Elohei tzuri echeseh-bo, magini v-qeren yish'i* — *the God of my rock, in him will I trust; my shield, and the horn of my salvation*. **2 Sam 22:31** (= **Ps 18:31**) — *magen hu l-khol ha-chosim bo* — *he is a shield to all those who trust in him*. **Ps 3:4** — *v-atah YHWH magen ba'adi* — *but you, YHWH, are a shield about me*. **Ps 28:7** — *YHWH uzi u-magini* — *YHWH is my strength and my shield*. **Ps 33:20** — *ezrenu u-magenenu hu* — *he is our help and our shield*. **Ps 84:12** — *ki shemesh u-magen YHWH Elohim* — *for YHWH Elohim is sun and shield*. **Ps 115:9-11** (three-fold refrain) — *ezram u-maginam hu* — *he is their help and their shield*. **Ps 144:2** — *chasdi u-m'tzudati misgabi u-m'falti li, magini u-vo chasiti* — *my lovingkindness and my fortress, my high tower and my deliverer, my shield, and he in whom I take refuge*. **Prov 30:5** — *magen hu l-chosim bo* — *he is a shield to those who trust in him*. // **The Ps 84:12 *shemesh u-magen* pairing** is particularly distinctive: the shield-epithet is paired with the sun-epithet, making the divine self-introduction cosmographic as well as martial. The combination is unique to Ps 84:12 in the Hebrew Bible; the *magen* alone is the common form. // **Mainstream scholarly treatment.** The epithet is discussed in Eichrodt (*Theology of the Old Testament*, vol I, 1961, pp. 192-194); Childs (*Old Testament Theology*, 1985); and the standard Psalms commentaries (Dahood *Psalms* AB, 1965-1970; Kraus *Psalms 1-59* and *Psalms 60-150*, 1988-1989; Goldingay *Psalms* BCOTWP, 2006-2008). The shield-of-the-fathers register is the conventional Davidic / Psalter image of divine protection; its first occurrence at Gen 15:1 is structurally important because it inaugurates the protective-divine-epithet register that the Davidic Psalter will develop. // **The conceptual nuance.** *Magen* in the Hebrew Bible denotes the small round shield (as opposed to the *tzinnah*, the large body-shield; 1 Kgs 10:16-17 distinguishes the two clearly). The divine epithet is the *magen* — the personal, hand-held protective shield — rather than the *tzinnah*. The personal-protection register matches the patriarchal context: the divine protection of an individual, not (yet) of a national army. // The WoH translation renders 'I am your shield', matching ASV. The claim_type is *direct*.

terms.literal: Lord YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: Lord GOD / Lord LORD
terms.wohChoice: Lord YHWH
terms.rationale: **The compound divine-name *Adonai YHWH* — אֲדֹנָי יֱהוִה — first occurs in the patriarchal narratives at Gen 15:2, recurring within the chapter at v 8.** The two occurrences frame Abram's two interrogative-prayer speeches (*mah titen-li* at v 2; *ba-mah eda* at v 8), marking the address as solemn-petitionary. // **The masoretic pointing is a *qere perpetuum*.** The Tetragrammaton *YHWH* is pointed in this collocation with the vowels of *Elohim* (rather than the usual Adonai-vowels) precisely because *Adonai* already precedes; reading the pointing publicly as *Adonai-Adonai* would be a stylistic repetition, so the masoretes substitute *Elohim*-vowels to signal a different oral substitution. The convention is documented in Gesenius §17c, Joüon-Muraoka §16f, and the standard masoretic apparatus discussions. The masoretes' move is a reading-practice marker, not a textual variant: the consonantal text reads *YHWH*; the oral substitution becomes *Adonai-Elohim*. // **The first patriarchal-stratum occurrence.** Before Gen 15:2, no character in the patriarchal narratives uses the compound; Adam's curses and prayers (Gen 3-4), Noah's blessings (Gen 9), and the call narratives (Gen 12-13) use *YHWH* or *YHWH Elohim* or *Adonai* singly. Abram's deployment of the compound at the covenant-cutting chapter elevates the register. The compound recurs sparsely across the patriarchal narratives — at Deut 3:24 (Moses's plea), 9:26 (Moses at the golden-calf intercession) — and then becomes a fixed Ezekielian formula, occurring more than 200 times in the book of Ezekiel alone. The Ezekielian usage is documented in Zimmerli (*Ezekiel I*, Hermeneia, 1979, pp. 36-40) as the book's distinctive divine-name. // **The compound's effect.** The doubling of divine-names creates an *intensification* register, used at moments of solemn covenant-language, intercession, or formal oracle. Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 113) reads Gen 15:2 as the patriarchal-prayer's elevated register at the chapter's threshold. The Ezekielian usage extends the pattern: the prophet uses *Adonai YHWH* as the formal-oracular introducer (especially in the *koh amar Adonai YHWH* formula). // **Distinct from *YHWH Elohim* of Gen 2-3.** The Eden narrative's compound *YHWH Elohim* (Gen 2:4-3:24, 21 occurrences) is a *different* compound: *YHWH* + *Elohim* in apposition, both as written consonants. The Gen 15:2 *Adonai YHWH* is *Adonai* + *YHWH* in apposition, with the masoretic vowel-substitution masking the oral reading. The two compounds are not collapsible. // **The WoH convention.** The translation renders 'Lord YHWH', preserving the consonantal *YHWH* and using 'Lord' (capitalized; not 'LORD' in small caps) for *Adonai*. The ASV / KJV convention 'Lord GOD' (with 'GOD' in small caps) reflects the masoretic substitution rather than the consonantal text; the WoH choice reads the consonants. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the compound is grammatically explicit in the cited verses; (b) the masoretic *qere perpetuum* convention is documented; (c) the patriarchal first-occurrence status and the Ezekielian fixed-formula distribution are verifiable by lexical search.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (hiphil) + prep + divine name / verb (qal) + 3fs suffix + prep + 3ms suffix + noun (feminine singular)
terms.literal: and he supported-himself-on / put-trust-in YHWH, and he reckoned it to him as righteousness
terms.standardEnglish: and he believed in the LORD, and he counted it to him for righteousness
terms.wohChoice: and he believed in YHWH; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:6 is among the three most-quoted Hebrew Bible verses in Pauline theology and a foundational proof-text of the Reformation.** *V-he'emin ba-YHWH va-yachshebeha lo tzedakah* — *and he supported-himself-on YHWH; and he reckoned it to him as righteousness*. The Hebrew lexical core is direct; the downstream reception is downstream. This entry holds the line: the translation reads the Hebrew; the apparatus carries the reception history.

**Hebrew lexical analysis.** (a) *He'emin* is the hiphil of אמן *aman*, whose qal core is 'to be firm, reliable, support-bearing'; the hiphil derived sense is *to consider firm, to put trust in, to support oneself on*. BDB §539 reads the hiphil as 'cause to stand firm, support oneself on, believe, trust'; HALOT reads similar. The hiphil is followed by the preposition *b-* (in) governing the divine name: *trust in / lean on YHWH*. Trust-in-as-supporting-oneself-on is the core; *believe* (the conventional English) introduces a propositional-content sense that is more developed in Greek *pisteuō* than in Hebrew *aman*. The Hebrew is closer to relational covenant-loyalty trust than to propositional belief-in-X. (b) *Va-yachshebeha lo tzedakah* — *and he reckoned it to him as righteousness*. The verb *chashav* (qal) is the standard Hebrew Bible verb for accounting, calculating, attributing. The 3fs pronominal suffix *-ha* (it, feminine) has an ambiguous antecedent: most likely the *act of trusting* (the verbal action of v 6a is morphologically feminine when nominalized), but possibly *the promise itself*, *Abram's faith*, or *the relational-covenant act*. *Tzedakah* (feminine; *righteousness, justice, right-conduct, right-relation*) names a relational-juridical category rather than a Pauline forensic-imputation category. The verse therefore states: *Abram leaned-on YHWH; and YHWH reckoned that leaning-on as righteousness*.

**The downstream Pauline reception (FLAGGED, not preempted).** **Rom 4:3** — central proof-text of *dikaiosynē ek pisteōs* (justification by faith) — *episteusen de Abraam tō theō, kai elogisthē autō eis dikaiosynēn* (LXX-quotation). **Rom 4:9, 4:22** — Paul iterates the same verse three times in the Rom 4 chapter. **Gal 3:6** — the proof-text in the anti-Judaizing argument: *kathōs Abraam episteusen tō theō, kai elogisthē autō eis dikaiosynēn*. **James 2:23** — the same verse cited for the *opposite* conclusion: *and the Scripture was fulfilled which says, And Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness; and he was called the friend of God* — used to argue that *faith without works is dead*. The Pauline-Jamesian split on the same verse is itself a flag of the reception's complexity. **The Reformation justification-by-faith superstructure** (Luther's *sola fide*; Calvin's *Institutes* III.11; the Westminster Confession of Faith XI) builds out from the Pauline Rom 4 reading.

**The modern critical reception of the Pauline reading.** The Hebrew verse, on its own terms, is not about justification-by-faith in the Pauline-Reformation sense. **Moberly** (*The Old Testament of the Old Testament*, 1992, pp. 195-216) argues at length that the Genesis verse describes a covenant-loyalty trust rather than a forensic-imputation. **Sanders** (*Paul and Palestinian Judaism*, 1977) re-opens the Pauline reading itself, reading first-century Judaism as covenant-loyalty rather than as legalism. **Dunn** (*Romans 1-8*, WBC, 1988) and **N.T. Wright** (*Paul and the Faithfulness of God*, 2013) develop the New Perspective on Paul, reading Rom 4 in covenantal-faithfulness terms rather than in justification-by-faith terms. **Cranfield** (*Romans*, ICC, 1975) and **Westerholm** (*Perspectives Old and New on Paul*, 2004) preserve more traditional readings of the Pauline material. The Pauline scholarship is genuinely divided.

**The WoH editorial discipline.** The Hebrew is direct. The translation reads the Hebrew without preempting the Pauline-Reformation superstructure. The English 'and he believed in YHWH; and he reckoned it to him for righteousness' matches ASV / NRSV / NJPS at every word and does not theologically load the verse. 'Believed in YHWH' carries the *aman*-trust without forensic-imputation language; 'reckoned' (ASV, NRSV) matches the *chashav* accounting register. The Pauline-Reformation reception belongs to downstream wiki, long-form article material, and the New Perspective on Paul scholarship, not to the translated text.

**The reading-history.** The patristic and Reformation readings (Augustine's anti-Pelagian use of the verse; Luther's *Lectures on Galatians*; Calvin's *Institutes* III.11.6, III.11.23) are downstream Christian theology. The rabbinic reception (Tg. Onkelos; Tg. Ps-Jonathan; Genesis Rabbah 44:14; the *Mekhilta* on Exod 14:31) reads the verse in covenant-merit terms. The translation does not adjudicate; it preserves the Hebrew. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the Hebrew lexical core (*he'emin*, *chashav*, *tzedakah*) is grammatically explicit; (b) the four cited NT receptions are direct textual evidence of the downstream reading; (c) the modern critical re-evaluation (Sanders, Dunn, Wright, Moberly, Westerholm) is direct documentary evidence of the scholarly debate. The entry does not commit to a theological reading; it preserves the Hebrew and flags the reception.

terms.literal: covenant between the pieces
terms.standardEnglish: covenant between the pieces
terms.wohChoice: covenant between the pieces
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:9-11, 17-18 together constitute the *brit bein ha-betarim* — the covenant between the pieces — the Hebrew Bible's most detailed description of the animal-bisection covenant-cutting ritual.** The verses describe each phase of the rite: (v 9) the divine instruction to take a three-year-old heifer, a three-year-old she-goat, a three-year-old ram, a turtle-dove, and a young pigeon; (v 10) the bisection of the larger animals (*va-y'vater otam ba-tavekh*, *and he divided them in the midst*) and the laying of each half opposite the other (*va-yiten ish bitro liqrat re'ehu*, *and he laid each half over against the other*), with the birds explicitly not divided; (v 11) the driving away of the birds of prey from the carcasses; (v 17) the passage of the *tanur ashan* (smoking furnace) and *lappid esh* (flaming torch) between the halved pieces; (v 18) the framing-verb *karat YHWH et-Avram brit* (YHWH cut a covenant with Abram).

**Ancient Near Eastern parallels for the ritual are extensively documented.** The most direct parallels: **Mari archives** — **ARM II 37** (Old Babylonian, eighteenth century BCE) and **ARM II 80** describe sacrificial-animal bisection in covenant-ratification contexts; the formula *qatāl ḫayyāri* (killing of an ass-foal) is the Mari ritual idiom for covenant-cutting. **Alalakh tablets** — **AT 1** and **AT 76** (Middle Bronze Age, Late Bronze Age) describe parallel rites. **The Sefire Aramaic treaties** — **KAI 222-224** (eighth century BCE, between Bar-Ga'yah of KTK and Mati'el of Arpad) preserve the curse-formula explicitly: *kma zy yqd 'gl' znh kn yqd Mati'el* — *as this calf is burned, so shall Mati'el be burned*; the parallel formula with *kma zy ysq 'gl' znh* — *as this calf is cut up* — and other variants. **The Mesha stele** preserves further ritual covenant-curse vocabulary in the same West-Semitic register. **The Tell Fakhariyeh inscription** (ninth-century Aramaic) attests covenant-curse vocabulary. **The Esarhaddon vassal-treaties** preserve a developed Neo-Assyrian version of the same ritual-curse pattern, with explicit animal-bisection imagery. Fitzmyer (*The Aramaic Inscriptions of Sefire*, 1995) is the standard edition; Weinfeld 1970 *JAOS* 90 *The Covenant of Grant in the Old Testament and in the Ancient Near East* is the foundational modern critical treatment.

**Within the Hebrew Bible.** **Jer 34:18-20** is the explicit cross-reference: the Judahite princes had ratified a covenant to release Hebrew slaves by walking between the halves of a calf; their violation of the covenant draws an explicit invocation of the same rite-curse — *I will give them up... whose carcasses shall be food for the birds of the heavens and the beasts of the earth*. The Jeremiah passage confirms that the *brit bein ha-betarim* was a known Israelite-context legal practice, attested into the late seventh / early sixth century BCE, not merely a literary trope of the patriarchal narratives. The Genesis 15 description is the canonical Hebrew Bible aetiology for the rite; the Jeremiah passage is the latest Hebrew Bible attestation.

**Modern critical commentary.** **Mendenhall** (*Law and Covenant in Israel and the Ancient Near East*, 1955) treats the rite as one of the earliest comparative-covenant pieces. **Hillers** (*Covenant: The History of a Biblical Idea*, 1969) collects the comparative material. **McCarthy** (*Treaty and Covenant*, 2nd ed. 1978, pp. 91-105) gives the developed comparative case. **Weinfeld** 1970 *JAOS* 90 distinguishes *covenant of grant* from *suzerainty treaty* and reads Gen 15 as the prototypical *covenant of grant* (unconditional divine promise). **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 220-231), **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, pp. 112-117), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 326-335), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 416-446) all treat the chapter at length.

**The unilateral-divine-passage move.** In the standard ANE bilateral covenant, both parties pass between the halved animals, invoking the curse-formula on whichever side breaks the covenant. At Gen 15:17, Abram is in *tardemah* (deep sleep, v 12); only the divine fire — the smoking furnace and flaming torch — passes between the pieces. The covenant is therefore *unilateral*: Yahweh alone takes the self-imprecation. The reading is consensus among the modern critical commentators (Westermann, Sarna, Wenham, von Rad). The theological move converts the bilateral-treaty form into a unilateral-grant form — the patriarch is the *beneficiary*, not the *co-signatory*, of the covenant.

**The *betarim* / *gezarim* lexical pair.** *Bitro* at v 10 (from בֶּתֶר *betar*, piece-of-halved-thing) and *gezarim* at v 17 (from גֶּזֶר *gezer*, cut-piece) are different lexemes for the same physical halved-carcasses. The variation is rhetorical rather than referential; the chapter's stylistic register varies the cut-piece vocabulary. The translation preserves both as 'pieces'. // The WoH translation matches ASV at every word; the lens-relevant comparative material (the ANE parallels, the Jer 34:18-20 cross-reference, the unilateral-divine-passage reading) lives in the per-verse commentary and in this glossary entry. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the ritual is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the ANE parallels (Mari ARM II 37, Sefire KAI 222-224, Alalakh AT 1/76, Esarhaddon vassal-treaties) are direct documentary evidence; (c) the Jer 34:18-20 cross-reference is direct textual evidence within the Hebrew Bible.

terms.literal: deep sleep, divinely-induced trance
terms.standardEnglish: deep sleep
terms.wohChoice: deep sleep
terms.rationale:
**The lexeme *tardemah* recurs across the Hebrew Bible as a vehicle for divine action upon the patient — revelation, anaesthesia, or judgment.** Gen 15:12 — *tardemah naflah al-Avram* (*a deep sleep fell upon Abram*) — is the lexeme's second occurrence and its second pairing with divine covenant-cutting action. The recurrence-chain links the patriarchal stratum, the wisdom stratum, the historical stratum, and the prophetic stratum by a shared revelation-vocabulary.

**The recurrence-set.** **Gen 2:21** — *va-yappel YHWH-Elohim tardemah al-ha-adam va-yishan* — *and YHWH-Elohim caused a deep sleep to fall upon the human, and he slept* — at the *tzela* (side) extraction from the human, the foundational human-formation operation. **Gen 15:12** — *tardemah naflah al-Avram* — at the covenant-cutting theophany; Abram is in *tardemah* throughout the unilateral divine passage between the pieces. **1 Sam 26:12** — *ki tardemat YHWH naflah aleihem* — *for a deep sleep from YHWH had fallen upon them* — at David's incursion into Saul's camp, where Saul and all his men sleep through David's taking of the spear and water-jug. The verse explicitly attributes the sleep to YHWH. **Job 4:13** — *bi-s'ifim me-chezyonot laylah b-n'fol tardemah al-anashim* — *amid disquieting thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls upon men* — in Eliphaz's vision-revelation framing. **Job 33:15** — *ba-chalom chezyon laylah bi-n'fol tardemah al-anashim* — *in a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falls upon men* — Elihu's dream-revelation framing, near-parallel to Job 4:13. **Isa 29:10** — *ki nasakh aleichem YHWH ruach tardemah* — *for YHWH has poured out upon you a spirit of deep sleep* — the *tardemah* of judgment, closing the eyes of the prophets and the seers.

**Across the seven occurrences, the lexeme consistently carries:** (a) divine causation — the *tardemah* is caused by Elohim / YHWH, never by ordinary fatigue; (b) supernatural-anaesthetic quality — it falls upon (*nafal al-*) the patient, suggesting external imposition rather than internal sleep-onset; (c) function as a vehicle — for divine action upon the patient (Gen 2:21 surgery; Gen 15:12 covenant theophany; 1 Sam 26:12 incursion-protection), for revelation (Job 4:13, 33:15), or for judgment (Isa 29:10). The lexeme is distinct from ordinary sleep *shenah* (שֵׁנָה), which has no necessary supernatural quality.

**LXX rendering and reception.** The Greek tradition renders *tardemah* as *ekstasis* (ἔκστασις) — *trance, ecstasy, displacement* — at Gen 2:21 and Gen 15:12. The Greek lexical choice carries the trance-state connotation more explicitly than the English 'deep sleep'. The LXX rendering becomes load-bearing for the New Testament's *ekstasis* vocabulary (Acts 10:10, 11:5, 22:17 of Peter's trance-vision at Joppa and Paul's trance-vision in the Temple).

**Lichtenstein 1968 *JANES* 1** *Dream-Theophany and the E Document* collects the dream-revelation contexts and reads the Job 4:13 and Job 33:15 occurrences in relation to the dream-revelation pattern of the E source. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 226) treats the Gen 15:12 *tardemah* as functionally parallel to the Gen 2:21 *tardemah* — both are divine-induced anaesthetic states for the duration of a foundational divine operation.

**The Gen 15:12 pairing with *eymah chashekhah gedolah*** — *a horror of great darkness* — is unique in the Hebrew Bible; the lexeme *eymah* (terror, dread) is poetic-prophetic (Exod 15:16, 23:27; Josh 2:9; Job 9:34, 13:21, etc.), and the combination with *chashekhah gedolah* is unparalleled. The *tardemah* + *eymah*-darkness combination frames the v 17 theophany: Abram is unconscious throughout the divine fire's passage between the pieces, which makes the covenant-cutting an Yahweh-only unilateral ratification.

**Lens posture.** The recurrence-chain is direct: the Hebrew Bible itself uses one lexeme for divine-induced unconsciousness across seven occurrences and three patriarchal/historical/wisdom strata. What the chain *means* — whether the underlying phenomenon is anaesthesia, dream-induction, surgical sedation, or a generic divine intervention — is for downstream wiki material, not for the translation. The translation matches ASV ('a deep sleep fell upon Abram'); the lens-relevant cross-references live here. // The claim_type is *direct* because the seven occurrences are grammatically explicit and the lexical-semantic pattern is documented in BDB, HALOT, and Lichtenstein 1968.

**Note on the chapter-2 entry.** The pre-existing glossary entry `tardemah` (chapter 2 only, *appliesTo: GEN-2:21*) is retained at its narrower scope; this new entry `tardemah-divine-induced-deep-sleep` is the cross-corpus parallel covering Gen 15:12 and the broader recurrence-chain. The two entries are consistent; the chapter-2 entry is the narrower local-context handling, and this entry is the cross-corpus consolidation.

terms.literal: four hundred years / fourth generation
terms.standardEnglish: four hundred years / the fourth generation
terms.wohChoice: four hundred years / in the fourth generation
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:13's 400 years and Gen 15:16's *dor revi'i* (fourth generation) frame an affliction-prophecy whose chronology is in well-known tension with the canonical Egyptian-sojourn figure.** The two figures at vv 13 and 16 are themselves in tension with each other (400 years vs. 4 generations, requiring generation-of-100-years to reconcile); both are in tension with Exod 12:40-41's 430 years; the NT reception (Acts 7:6; Gal 3:17) cites both numbers in different contexts.

**The Hebrew Bible's witnesses.** **Gen 15:13** — *gera yihyeh zar'akha b-eretz lo lahem va-avadum v-innu otam arba me'ot shanah* — *your seed shall be a sojourner in a land that is not theirs, and they shall serve them, and they shall afflict them four hundred years*. The 400 is here applied specifically to the *affliction-period* (the *va-avadum v-innu* — servitude and affliction). **Gen 15:16** — *v-dor revi'i yashuvu henna* — *and in the fourth generation they shall come hither again*. **Exod 12:40-41** — *u-moshav b'nei Yisra'el asher yashvu b-Mitsrayim shloshim shanah v-arba me'ot shanah... va-y'hi mi-qetz shloshim shanah v-arba me'ot shanah... yatz'u kol-tzivot YHWH me-eretz Mitsrayim* — *the dwelling of the children of Israel who dwelt in Mitsrayim was four hundred and thirty years; and at the end of four hundred and thirty years, all the hosts of YHWH went out from the land of Mitsrayim*. The MT Exodus figure is 430 years specifically *in Mitsrayim*.

**The Samaritan Pentateuch and LXX harmonization.** Both the SP and the LXX read Exod 12:40 as *the dwelling of the children of Israel and their fathers, which they dwelt in the land of Mitsrayim and in the land of Kena'an, was four hundred and thirty years* — splitting the 430 years across the patriarchal sojourn in Canaan (from Abraham's call) and the Egyptian sojourn proper. The Samaritan-LXX harmonization yields approximately 215 years in Canaan + 215 years in Mitsrayim. The reading is preserved across the Christian tradition.

**Mainstream scholarly treatment.** **Sarna** (*JPS Exodus*, 1991, excursus 2 on the chronology of the sojourn, pp. 254-256) collects the four chronological systems: (a) MT Exod 12:40 — 430 years in Mitsrayim; (b) SP/LXX Exod 12:40 — 430 years total (Canaan + Mitsrayim); (c) Gen 15:13 — 400 years of affliction; (d) Gen 15:16 — four generations. Sarna treats the 400 of Gen 15:13 as a *round figure* approximating the 430 of Exod 12:40, and the four generations of v 16 as a *separate chronological system* — perhaps a Levitical-genealogical system (Levi → Kohath → Amram → Moses being four generations) rather than a chronological-years system. **Hoffmeier** (*Israel in Egypt: The Evidence for the Authenticity of the Exodus Tradition*, 1997, pp. 124-126) collates the evidence with the Egyptological chronology. **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 332-333), **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 227-228), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 435-437) all discuss the chronology without committing to a definitive resolution.

**The NT reception is itself bifurcated.** **Acts 7:6** (Stephen's speech) cites 400 years — closer to the Gen 15:13 form — in the context of the Egyptian sojourn proper. **Gal 3:17** (Paul) cites 430 years — *ho meta tetrakosia kai triakonta etē gegonōs nomos* — *the law, which came four hundred and thirty years afterward* — from the Abrahamic promise to the Sinai legislation. The two NT figures are not contradictory if Gen 15:13's 400 is read as the *affliction-period* proper (matching Acts 7:6) and Exod 12:40's 430 as the *full sojourning from Abraham's call* (matching Gal 3:17 + SP/LXX harmonization). Various NT chronologies make use of both figures.

**The *dor revi'i* — fourth generation — at v 16.** The figure adds a third independent witness: four generations between Abram's vision and the conquest. Reconciliation strategies include: (a) generation-of-100-years (matching the patriarchal lifespans — Abraham 175 at Gen 25:7; Isaac 180 at Gen 35:28; Jacob 147 at Gen 47:28); (b) the Levitical-genealogical reading (Levi → Kohath → Amram → Moses, with each generation spanning a long-lived patriarchal life); (c) the conventional-round-number reading. Sarna and Wenham prefer (a) or (b); Westermann treats the four-generations figure as a discrepant tradition.

**Posture.** The entry presents the dispute without taking a definitive position. The four chronological witnesses (Gen 15:13 / Gen 15:16 / Exod 12:40 MT / Exod 12:40 SP-LXX) are independently attested and the harmonization strategies are well-documented. The translation reads each verse literally — *four hundred years* at v 13; *in the fourth generation* at v 16 — and the chronology dispute lives in the glossary apparatus. // The claim_type is *direct* because the four chronological witnesses are grammatically explicit at their cited verses, the SP/LXX harmonization is direct documentary evidence, and the NT receptions at Acts 7:6 and Gal 3:17 are direct textual evidence.

terms.translit: ki lo shalem avon ha-Emori ad henna
terms.partOfSpeech: conjunction + negation + adjective (qal participle) + noun + gentilic + preposition + adverb
terms.literal: for not complete-is the iniquity of the Emori up to here / up to now
terms.standardEnglish: for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full
terms.wohChoice: for the iniquity of the Emori is not yet complete
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:16 articulates one of the Hebrew Bible's most theologically distinctive moral propositions: divine timing of the conquest is conditioned on the moral-fulfillment of the Amorite's iniquity.** *V-dor revi'i yashuvu henna ki lo shalem avon ha-Emori ad henna* — *and in the fourth generation they shall come hither again; for the iniquity of the Emori is not yet complete*. The conquest cannot proceed until a divinely-tracked moral threshold is reached. The construction encodes a *cup-of-iniquity* model: iniquity fills, like a vessel, until it overflows and triggers displacement.

**Lexical analysis.** (a) *Lo shalem* — the negation + qal participle of שׁ-ל-ם *shalem* (to be complete, whole, finished). The participial construction names a *state* (not yet complete) rather than an *event* (has not yet happened); the iniquity is in process of filling but has not yet reached completion. (b) *Avon* (עָוֹן) — *iniquity, guilt, moral debt*; the noun carries the weight-of-accumulated-wrong sense in the Hebrew Bible (cf. *avon* as a heavy weight to be carried at Lev 5:1, 17, 7:18, etc.). The pairing of *avon* + *shalem* is the cup-filling image. (c) *Ad henna* (עַד־הֵנָּה) — *up to here / up to now*; the temporal-locative phrase marks the present moment as the not-yet point of the moral threshold.

**The *avon ha-Emori* formula and the Deuteronomic conquest-justification.** The formula is theologically load-bearing for the Pentateuchal conquest-theology. **Deut 9:4-5** — *al-tomar bi-l'vavekha... b-tzidqati hevi'ani YHWH la-reshet et-ha-aretz ha-zot... ki b-rish'at ha-goyim ha-eleh YHWH morisham mi-panekha* — *do not say in your heart... For my righteousness YHWH has brought me in to possess this land... but for the wickedness of these nations YHWH dispossesses them before you*. The verse explicitly grounds the conquest in the wickedness of the dispossessed peoples, not the merit of Israel. **Lev 18:24-28** — *al-tittam'u b-khol-eleh ki b-khol-eleh nitm'u ha-goyim asher ani m'shaleach mi-pneichem... va-titteme ha-aretz va-efkod avonah aleyha va-taqi ha-aretz et-yoshveyha* — *be not defiled by any of these things; for in all these the nations are defiled which I am casting out before you... and the land was defiled; therefore I visited the iniquity thereof upon it, and the land vomited out its inhabitants*. The land itself is figured as vomiting out its iniquity-defiled inhabitants. **Lev 20:23** reiterates the formula. The Pentateuchal conquest-theology is consistent: the land-grant to Israel is conditional on the moral-displacement of prior inhabitants; Israel is not unilaterally favored but is the inheritor of a vacuum created by accumulated moral failure.

**The cup-of-iniquity image.** The metaphor of iniquity as a vessel filling toward overflow recurs across the Hebrew Bible. **2 Macc 6:14** (deuterocanonical) — *to mē phthasai eis to plērōma tōn hamartiōn* — *not allowing it to reach the fullness of the sins*. **Jer 25:15-29** — the cup-of-wrath imagery. **Rev 14:10, 16:19, 18:6** — the developed NT cup-of-wrath imagery.

**The Pauline downstream reception (FLAGGED).** **1 Thess 2:16** — *kōlyontōn hēmas tois ethnesin lalēsai hina sōthōsin, eis to anaplērōsai autōn tas hamartias pantote* — *hindering us from speaking to the Gentiles that they may be saved; to fill up their sins always*. The Pauline phrase *anaplērōsai autōn tas hamartias* (to fill up their sins) directly invokes the Gen 15:16 *shalem avon* image. The verse has a long and ethically complex reception-history: it has been used in anti-Judaic polemics across the Christian tradition, and modern critical scholarship (Beker, *Paul the Apostle*, 1980; Donfried, *The Theology of the Shorter Pauline Letters*, 1993) has revisited the verse with attention to its polemical use. The Pauline reception is flagged here as downstream; the entry does not preempt or extend the polemical use.

**Mainstream scholarly commentary.** **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 227-228) reads the verse as one of the Hebrew Bible's most ethically distinctive — the divine deferral of the conquest on moral grounds is unusual against the ANE land-grant background. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 116) treats the verse as theologically load-bearing for the Pentateuchal conquest-narrative. **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 333) reads the verse against the Deut 9:4-5 conquest-theology. **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 437-438) collates the cross-references.

**The geographic *Emori*.** The lexeme here refers not narrowly to the Amorite gentilic group of Gen 14:7, 13 (which the chapter treats as one of several pre-Israelite peoples) but more broadly to the *land's prior inhabitants* — *Emori* functioning as a synecdoche for the Canaanite peoples collectively. The synecdochic usage is well-attested (cf. Josh 24:15, 18 *ha-Emori asher attem yoshvim b-artzam* — *the Amorites in whose land you dwell*; Amos 2:9 *anokhi hishmadti et-ha-Emori mi-pneihem* — *I destroyed the Amorite before them*, where *ha-Emori* refers to all Canaanite displacement). // The WoH translation renders 'for the iniquity of the Emori is not yet complete', matching ASV's 'for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full'. The translation 'complete' (matching the *shalem* root sense) preserves the participial-state register slightly better than 'full'; ASV / NRSV / NJPS variation is minimal. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexical pairing *lo shalem avon* is grammatically explicit; (b) the Deut 9:4-5 / Lev 18:24-28 / Lev 20:23 cross-references are direct documentary evidence; (c) the 1 Thess 2:16 reception is flagged but not preempted.

terms.literal: an oven of smoke and a torch of fire / a smoking furnace and a flaming torch
terms.standardEnglish: a smoking furnace and a flaming torch
terms.wohChoice: a smoking furnace and a flaming torch
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:17 is one of the most concretely described theophanies in the Hebrew Bible.** *V-hineh tanur ashan v-lappid esh asher avar bein ha-gezarim ha-eleh* — *and behold, a smoking furnace and a flaming torch that passed between these pieces*. The two images are sensory-specific: a *tanur* emitting *ashan* (smoke) and a *lappid* (torch) of *esh* (fire), moving together through the space between the halved carcasses of the v 10 ritual.

**Lexical analysis.** (a) *Tanur* (תַּנּוּר) — *oven, furnace*. The lexeme is used elsewhere for the cooking-vessel: Lev 2:4 (*ma'afe tanur*, *baked in an oven*); Lev 7:9; Lev 11:35 (the vessel-purity legislation); Lev 26:26; Hos 7:4, 6, 7 (the lust-of-the-king imagery as a heated oven). Metaphorically: Ps 21:10 *t'shitemo k-tanur esh l-et panekha* — *you will make them as a fiery furnace at the time of your wrath*; Mal 3:19 *hineh ha-yom ba bo'er ka-tanur* — *behold the day comes, burning like a furnace*. The cooking-vessel sense is primary; the divine-presence metaphorical sense develops in the wisdom and prophetic strata. (b) *Ashan* (עָשָׁן) — *smoke*. A recurring theophany-marker: Exod 19:18 at Sinai; Isa 6:4 at the Temple seraph-vision; Ps 18:9 (*alah ashan b-apo* — *smoke went up from his nostrils*, divine-anger imagery); Joel 2:30. (c) *Lappid* (לַפִּיד) — *torch, firebrand*. The lexeme covers held flames: Judg 7:16-20 (Gideon's 300 men with torches inside jars); Judg 15:4-5 (Samson's foxes with firebrands); Isa 62:1 (*ki-lappid yiv'ar* — *like a torch that burns*, Zion-salvation imagery); Ezek 1:13 (the *chashmal* vision — *u-d'mut ha-chayot mar'eihem k-gachalei esh bo'arot k-mar'eh ha-lappidim* — *and the likeness of the living creatures: their appearance was as burning coals of fire, as the appearance of torches*); Nah 2:5; Zech 12:6; Dan 10:6. The Ezek 1:13 occurrence is particularly important: the torch-image is preserved in the most developed Hebrew Bible theophany at the head of the Ezekiel chariot-vision. (d) *Esh* (אֵשׁ) — *fire*. The standard theophany-element across the Hebrew Bible. Exod 3:2 (the burning bush); Exod 13:21-22 (the pillar of fire by night); Exod 19:18 (Sinai); Exod 24:17 (*u-mar'eh kvod-YHWH k-esh okhelet b-rosh ha-har* — *and the appearance of the glory of YHWH was as a devouring fire on the top of the mountain*); Deut 4:11, 12, 15, 24, 33, 36; 5:4, 5, 22-26; 9:3, 10, 15; Ps 18:14; etc. The fire-element is the most distributed Hebrew Bible theophany-marker.

**The fire-and-smoke theophany pattern.** **Exod 19:18** at Sinai: *Har Sinai ashan kullo mi-pnei asher yarad alav YHWH ba-esh; va-ya'al ashano k-eshen ha-kivshan* — *Mount Sinai was wholly in smoke because YHWH descended upon it in fire; and its smoke went up like the smoke of a furnace*. The Sinai theophany explicitly invokes a *kivshan* (kiln-furnace) — lexically distinct from *tanur* but the same smoke-from-furnace image. The Genesis 15 *tanur ashan* and the Exodus 19 *eshen ha-kivshan* form a paired anticipation: the patriarchal covenant-fire prefigures the Sinai covenant-fire. **Exod 13:21-22** — *YHWH holekh lifneihem yomam b-amud anan la-nchotam ha-derekh v-laylah b-amud esh l-ha'ir lahem* — *YHWH went before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them on the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light* — pairs the same two elements (smoke/cloud + fire) as the mode of YHWH's presence in the wilderness. **Isa 6:4** at the Temple seraph-vision: *va-yana'u amot ha-sippim mi-qol ha-qore v-ha-bayit yimale ashan* — *and the foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him that called, and the house was filled with smoke*. **Ezek 1** — the *chashmal* and the wheels-within-wheels — operates in the same fire-cloud-glory register at much higher technical density: *v-tokho k-mar'eh ha-chashmal mi-tokh ha-esh* — *and from out of the midst thereof, as it were the appearance of glowing-bronze, out of the midst of the fire* — and the *gachalei esh bo'arot k-mar'eh ha-lappidim* — *coals of fire, as the appearance of torches*.

**The Genesis 15 theophany stands at the head of the canonical Hebrew Bible fire-theophany corpus.** The Gen 15:17 image is the patriarchal-stratum's most concrete divine-presence description; the Sinai theophany (Exod 19, 24) is the Mosaic-stratum's developed form; the Ezekiel chariot-vision (Ezek 1, 10) is the prophetic-stratum's most technical form. The three stages form a developing canonical theophany-vocabulary.

**The unilateral-passage theological move.** The fire-and-smoke divine presence passes *alone* between the halved carcasses; Abram is in *tardemah* (v 12). The covenant-cutting is therefore unilateral — only YHWH takes the self-imprecation. The reading is consensus among the modern critical commentators: **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, pp. 228-229); **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, pp. 332-333); **von Rad** (*Genesis*, OTL, 1972, p. 187); **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 117); **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 1-17*, 1990, pp. 437-440).

**Lens posture (WoH).** The Gen 15:17 theophany is high-lens-leverage for the Wheel of Heaven project — the chapter's keystone concrete-sensory divine appearance, with smoke-and-fire imagery that the project's broader lens-reading will surface in long-form articles and wiki material. The lens stays in the apparatus. The translation matches ASV at every word; the lens-relevant cross-references (Exod 19:18, Exod 13:21, Ezek 1) and the broader fire-theophany corpus are carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary at 15:17. The translation does not editorialize on what the smoke-and-fire is; what the corpus's apparatus surfaces is the lexical-philological mainstream-scholarly synthesis, not a Wheel of Heaven cosmological reading. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the four lexemes (*tanur*, *ashan*, *lappid*, *esh*) are grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the recurrence-patterns (Exod 19:18 Sinai, Exod 13:21 pillar-of-fire, Isa 6:4, Ezek 1) are direct documentary evidence; (c) the modern critical scholarly synthesis (Westermann, Wenham, von Rad, Sarna, Hamilton) is mainstream and uncontested.

terms.literal: to cut a covenant
terms.standardEnglish: to make a covenant
terms.wohChoice: to cut a covenant
terms.rationale:
**Gen 15:18 contains the first explicit *karat brit* — *cut a covenant* — of the Abraham cycle.** *Ba-yom ha-hu karat YHWH et-Avram brit* — *on that day YHWH cut a covenant with Abram*. The Hebrew idiom preserves the literal animal-bisection rite of vv 9-11, 17 in its very verb: covenants in the Hebrew Bible are *cut*, not *made*. The idiom is foundational; the WoH translation preserves the verb-literal *cut* rather than the smoothed-conventional *made*.

**The lexical evidence.** The verb *karat* (כָּרַת) in the qal has the basic sense of *cut down, cut off, cut up* — used for cutting trees (Deut 19:5; 2 Kgs 6:4), cutting flesh (1 Sam 31:9 cutting off Saul's head), cutting off persons (the *karet* legal-penalty formula: Gen 17:14 *v-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ameyha* — *and that soul shall be cut off from his people*). The collocation *karat brit* applies the cutting-verb to *brit* (covenant), preserving the etymological link to the animal-bisection ritual. BDB §3772 reads the idiom as *make a covenant, by cutting in two parts the victim*; HALOT concurs.

**The recurrence-distribution.** The idiom recurs approximately 80 times across the Hebrew Bible — one of the most distributed legal-religious idioms in the corpus. Selected occurrences: **Gen 21:27, 21:32** (Abimelech's covenant with Abraham at Beer-Sheba); **Gen 26:28** (Isaac with Abimelech at Beer-Sheba); **Gen 31:44** (Jacob with Laban at Mizpah); **Exod 23:32** (the commandment not to cut covenant with the Canaanite gods); **Exod 24:8** (the Sinai covenant-cutting); **Exod 34:10, 34:27** (the renewed-covenant after the golden calf); **Deut 4:23** (Moses's warning); **Deut 5:2-3** (the Horeb covenant); **Deut 7:2, 9:9, 28:69, 29:13, 31:16**; **Josh 9:6-7, 9:15-16, 24:25**; **Judg 2:2; 1 Sam 11:1, 18:3, 20:8, 22:8, 23:18; 2 Sam 3:12-13, 3:21, 5:3; 1 Kgs 5:26, 8:9, 8:21, 20:34; 2 Kgs 11:4, 11:17, 17:15, 17:35, 17:38, 23:3; Ezra 10:3; Neh 9:8, 10:1; Job 31:1, 40:28; Ps 50:5, 83:6, 89:4; Isa 28:15, 55:3, 57:8, 61:8; Jer 11:10, 31:31-33, 32:40, 34:8, 34:13, 34:15, 34:18; Ezek 17:13, 34:25, 37:26; Hos 2:20, 10:4; Zech 11:10**; etc. The full distribution covers the patriarchal narratives, the legal corpora, the Deuteronomistic history, the wisdom literature, and the prophetic books. The idiom is one of the most cross-corpus-reachable Hebrew Bible expressions.

**The variant idiom *karat amanah*** (Neh 9:38) and *karat eshet* (Isa 28:15 *karatnu brit et-mavet* — *we have cut a covenant with death*) extend the same verb to other compact-formation contexts.

**The ANE comparative context.** The idiom is uniquely Hebrew; the ANE corpus uses different vocabulary for covenant-ratification (Akkadian *riksu rakāsu* — to bind a binding; Hittite *iškiya- linkiya-* — to bind by oath; Aramaic *gzr ʿdyʾ* — to cut a treaty, the Sefire-treaty idiom — which is the *closest* parallel to the Hebrew *karat brit*). The Aramaic *gzr* (cut) + *ʿdyʾ* (treaty/pact) at Sefire KAI 222-224 is the precise cognate idiom — the same cutting-verb applied to the same legal-religious noun. The Hebrew-Aramaic shared idiom suggests a West-Semitic legal background for the rite-and-idiom together. **Weinfeld 1970 *JAOS* 90** discusses the comparative material; **Hillers** (*Covenant*, 1969) and **McCarthy** (*Treaty and Covenant*, 1978) collate the parallels.

**The verb-literal *cut* vs. the smoothed *make*.** The conventional English Bibles overwhelmingly smooth *karat brit* to *make a covenant* (KJV, ASV, NRSV, NIV). The smoothing loses the etymological link to the animal-bisection ritual. The WoH translation preserves the literal *cut*: *YHWH cut a covenant with Abram*. NJPS reads *made a covenant*; modern critical scholarly translations (Alter, *The Five Books of Moses*, 2004; Friedman, *Commentary on the Torah*, 2001) vary between *made* and *cut*; Alter at Gen 15:18 reads *Yahweh sealed a covenant with Abram*, preserving the formal register without the etymological link.

**The WoH choice.** The translation preserves *cut a covenant* throughout the Hebrew Bible wherever *karat brit* appears. The choice is justified by (a) the etymological-literal preservation of the verb's link to the ritual; (b) the parallel to the Aramaic *gzr ʿdyʾ* West-Semitic legal idiom; (c) the consistency with the Gen 15:9-11, 17 ritual described in the same chapter; (d) the recovery of a lexical dimension that the conventional smoothing erases. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the idiom is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the cross-corpus distribution (80+ occurrences) is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Aramaic-cognate evidence from Sefire is direct documentary evidence.

terms.partOfSpeech: list of ten gentilic-nouns with definite article
terms.literal: the Qeini, the Qenizi, the Qadmoni, the Chitti, the Perizi, the Refa'im, the Emori, the Kena'ani, the Girgashi, the Yevusi
terms.standardEnglish: the Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites, Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaim, Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, Jebusites
terms.wohChoice: the Qeini, the Qenizi, the Qadmoni, the Chitti, the Perizi, the Refa'im, the Emori, the Kena'ani, the Girgashi, the Yevusi
terms.rationale:
**The ten-fold list of vv 19-21 is unique in the Hebrew Bible.** Every other Pentateuchal enumeration of the land's pre-Israelite peoples has six, seven, or fewer. The Gen 15:19-21 list is the *maximal* form: ten peoples, deliberately matched to the *maximal* land-claim of v 18 (from the river of Mitsrayim to the Euphrates).

**The Hebrew Bible's enumeration-variations.** **Six-fold lists**: Exod 3:8, 3:17 (*Kena'ani, Chitti, Emori, Perizi, Chivi, Yevusi*); Exod 13:5 (same six); Exod 23:23 (*Emori, Chitti, Perizi, Kena'ani, Chivi, Yevusi*); Exod 33:2 (same six in varied order); Exod 34:11 (same six); Deut 20:17 (same six). **Seven-fold lists**: Deut 7:1 (adds *Girgashi*: *Chitti, Girgashi, Emori, Kena'ani, Perizi, Chivi, Yevusi*); Josh 3:10 (same seven); Josh 24:11 (same seven). **Variations**: Gen 10:15-18's Table-of-Nations enumeration of Kena'an's descendants overlaps but is structured differently. **Gen 15:19-21's ten-fold list** is unique: it adds the *Qeini, Qenizi, Qadmoni* triad to the standard land-peoples and substitutes *Refa'im* for *Chivi*. The maximization is structural and editorial.

**The Qeini / Qenizi / Qadmoni triad.** The three peoples added uniquely at vv 19-20 appear in no other Pentateuchal land-peoples list. (a) **Qeini** (קֵינִי) — the Kenites. The gentilic recurs at Num 24:21-22 (Balaam's oracle); Judg 1:16 (the Kenites with Moses's affines descending from Jethro); Judg 4:11, 4:17, 5:24 (Heber the Kenite, Jael); 1 Sam 15:6 (the Kenites explicitly spared by Saul in the Amaleq campaign); 1 Sam 27:10, 30:29; 1 Chr 2:55. The Kenites are typically allied or in-married with Israel rather than displaced by it. The name's etymology is debated (Halpern, *The Emergence of Israel in Canaan*, 1983; Knauf, *Midian*, 1988); some readings derive from *qayin* (smith) and link to metalworking, others to a tribal name. (b) **Qenizi** (קְנִזִּי) — the Kenizzites. The gentilic recurs at Num 32:12 (Caleb the Kenizzite); Josh 14:6, 14:14 (Caleb's gentilic). Caleb's lineage is Kenizzite; the Kenizzites are absorbed into Judah. The name is genealogically connected to the Edomite Kenaz (Gen 36:11, 15, 42). (c) **Qadmoni** (קַדְמֹנִי) — *eastern ones*. The lexeme is a hapax in this gentilic sense. The etymology is direct: from *qedem* (east), *qadmoni* names *the eastern people*. The most likely referent is the Trans-Jordanian / Syrian-desert peoples east of the Jordan and the Salt Sea — *b'nei qedem*, *children of the east*, the standard Hebrew Bible term for the eastern desert peoples (cf. Gen 29:1; Judg 6:3, 6:33, 7:12, 8:10; 1 Kgs 5:10; Job 1:3 *gadol mi-kol b'nei qedem*; Isa 11:14; Jer 49:28; Ezek 25:4, 25:10). The Qadmoni would then be the *b'nei qedem* in gentilic-singular form.

**The substitution of *Refa'im* for *Chivi*.** The standard six-fold list has *Chivi* (Hivites) where Gen 15:19-21 has *Refa'im*. The substitution is editorially significant: it links the Gen 15 list to the giant-peoples chain of Gen 14:5 (the existing *refaim-giant-peoples* entry) and to the broader Deut 2-3 giant-peoples corpus. The maximalist character of the Gen 15 list extends to its inclusion of the giant-stature population among the land-peoples to be displaced.

**The geographical scope.** The ten-fold list matches the v 18 maximal land-claim. The reduced six-fold list of Exod 3:8 etc. covers the Wadi-of-Egypt-to-entrance-of-Hamath standard land; the Gen 15 ten-fold list covers the Euphrates-to-Wadi-of-Egypt maximal land. Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, 1985, p. 230), Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 117), and Wenham (*WBC Genesis 1-15*, 1987, p. 334) read the ten-fold list as a deliberate editorial maximization matched to the maximal land-claim of v 18.

**Modern critical reading.** The standard documentary-hypothesis reading attributes the ten-fold list to a late editorial expansion (J or a post-J redactor); the standard six-fold list is read as the earlier core, with later editors maximizing the enumeration. **Westermann** treats the list as a redactional development; **Sarna** treats it as a deliberate stylistic feature; **Wenham** treats it as consistent with the chapter's maximal-claim register. The redactional reading is not contested; the question is whether the maximization is early or late.

**Land-claim geography.** *Min'har Mitsrayim ad ha-nahar ha-gadol n'har P'rat* — *from the river of Mitsrayim unto the great river, the river Perat* — covers the territory from the Wadi el-Arish (or, on the maximal reading, an arm of the Nile) in the southwest to the Euphrates in the northeast. The reduced *me-Naḥal Mitsrayim ad l'vo Chamat* of Num 34:5 and 1 Kgs 8:65 covers a much smaller territory. The Solomonic kingdom briefly approached the Euphrates land-claim (1 Kgs 4:21 *u-Sh'lomo hayah moshel b-khol ha-mamlakhot min ha-nahar* — *Solomon was ruling over all the kingdoms from the River*; 1 Kgs 5:1).

**The WoH translation.** The transliterations preserve the Hebrew gentilic forms (*Qeini, Qenizi, Qadmoni, Chitti, Perizi, Refa'im, Emori, Kena'ani, Girgashi, Yevusi*) rather than the Anglicized forms (Kenites, Kenizzites, etc.), matching the chapter's pattern (the *refaim-giant-peoples* entry handles *Refa'im*). The transliterations are documented in the per-verse commentary. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the ten-fold list is grammatically explicit at vv 19-21; (b) the variations across Exod 3:8, Deut 7:1, Josh 3:10, etc. are direct documentary evidence of the enumeration-variability; (c) the *qedem* / *b'nei qedem* etymological link for *Qadmoni* is grammatically explicit; (d) the documentary-hypothesis redactional reading is scholarly synthesis but mainstream and uncontested.

terms.literal: maidservant, female slave (of a specific legal class)
terms.standardEnglish: handmaid (ASV/KJV); slave-girl (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: handmaid (with ANE substitute-wife legal background carried in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**The *shifchah* of Gen 16:1-3 is a specific legal-social institution, not merely a generic female slave.** The Hebrew lexeme *shifchah* (שִׁפְחָה) denotes a class of female slave whose offspring by the master, at the mistress's initiative and on her body, could legally count as the mistress's children. The narrative of vv 1-3 (Sarai gives Hagar to Abram *l-ishah* — *for a wife*) is the canonical Hebrew Bible enactment of the institution; the parallel narratives at Gen 30:3-13 (Bilhah and Zilpah given to Jacob by Rachel and Leah) follow the same legal-narrative pattern.
