Fuchs *Sexual Politics in the Biblical Narrative* 2000** on the gender-political reading; the **annunciation-type-scene** treatment in **Alter *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 pp. 47-62** (one of the founding chapter-cases of Alter's type-scene methodology, with Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Manoah's wife, and the Shunammite as the canonical annunciation-type-scene set).

**Translation.** The ASV-baseline *had fast closed up every womb* is preserved, with the doubled infinitive-absolute construction visible in the *fast* intensifier. The verb *atzar* (restrain, hold back, close up) is preserved as *closed up* (rather than smoothed to *prevented from bearing* or similar); the doubling is preserved as *fast closed up* (rather than smoothed to *had completely / utterly closed*). The closed-wombs are preserved as *every womb* rather than the smoothed *all the women* — the body-part metonymy is the Hebrew's own and is preserved.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the doubled infinitive-absolute construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the closed-wombs motif and the cross-corpus matriarchal-barrenness pattern are documentary; (c) the *sagar / atzar / paqad / zakhar* verbal cluster for divine womb-action is documented in HALOT and BDB and in the cited scholarly literature; (d) the chapter's structural irony is mainstream-scholarly. Project-specific synthesis (any Wheel of Heaven reading of the matriarchal-barrenness motif beyond the mainstream-scholarly treatment) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: playing, jesting, sporting, mocking (intensive of *tzachaq* — to laugh)
terms.standardEnglish: mocking (ASV/KJV); playing (NJPS, Alter); playing with her son Isaac (LXX/Vulgate/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: playing
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:9 is one of the most editorially-contested verbs in the Abraham cycle.** Sarah sees Yishma'el *metzacheq* — and the consequence is the demand for his expulsion at v 10. The piel participle of *tzachaq* (the same root that has driven the chapter's laughter-pattern: *tzachoq* at v 6, *yitzchaq* at v 6, and the name *Yitzchaq* itself at vv 3, 4, 5, 8) is here morphologically intensive and semantically polysemous. The translation choice carries heavy editorial weight on the Hagar / Yishma'el narrative.

**The four readings, none consensus.** Modern critical scholarship recognizes four distinct readings of *metzacheq*, each with substantial defense in the philological literature and none uniquely consensus.

*(a) Playing / sporting / jesting* — neutral. The piel of *tzachaq* used in its most lexically default sense: *play, jest, sport*. The participle is then a deliberate verb-name pun with *Yitzchaq*: at the feast celebrating the weaning of *he-will-laugh*, the elder brother is *the one laughing / playing*. The name-play is the editorial point. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 146) reads *playing* and explicitly rejects the harsher readings: *the Hebrew has only mit-zaheq, the same root as Isaac's name; what is most likely meant is innocent play*; **Alter** (*The Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 103-104) translates *playing* and reads the participle as continuing the chapter's laughter-name-play with no harsher implication; **NJPS** *playing*; **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 338-339) reads the participle as semantically neutral, with the offense in Sarah's perception rather than in Yishma'el's act. The reading turns on the lexical default of *tzachaq*, the chapter's running name-play, and the absence of any explicit harsher direct object in the MT.

*(b) Playing-with-Isaac* — the LXX expansion. The Septuagint adds *meta Isaak tou huiou autēs* (with Isaac her son) to the verse, giving *Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian playing with Isaac her son*. The LXX expansion is reflected in the Vulgate, the Peshitta, and a number of modern translations (NRSV *playing with her son Isaac*; CEB; some critical editions). The reading is also presupposed in **Galatians 4:29** (Paul's Pauline-allegorical *the one born according to the flesh persecuted the one born according to the Spirit*), suggesting the Pauline-Jewish Hellenistic interpretive tradition had already received the expanded reading. **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, p. 155) defers to the LXX as preserving an earlier Hebrew. The reading makes the offense rivalrous play between the two boys at Isaac's weaning-feast — Yishma'el playing with the heir is itself the threat to the heir's inheritance status. The textual question is whether the LXX expands the Hebrew or preserves an older Hebrew lost in the MT; modern critical scholarship is divided (Wevers's *Notes on the Greek Text of Genesis* 1993 takes the LXX as expansion; some others as preservation).

*(c) Mocking / abusing* — ASV/KJV. The piel of *tzachaq* used in its harsher sense: *mock, deride, sport-at*. The reading is supported by other piel uses of *tzachaq* in Genesis itself: **Gen 19:14** (Lot's sons-in-law take him to be *metzacheq* — *mocking, jesting* — in his warning about Sodom's destruction); **Gen 39:14, 39:17** (Potiphar's wife accuses Joseph of having come *l-tzacheq bi* — *to mock me / to sport with me*, with a sexualized register). The non-Genesis piel occurrences carry the broadest semantic range: **Exod 32:6** (*va-yaqumu l-tzacheq* — *they rose up to play* — at the golden-calf incident, with the rabbinic-tradition reading the play as idolatry and sexual immorality; the Pauline reception at 1 Cor 10:7 *neither be idolaters as some of them were, as it is written: the people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play*); **Judg 16:25** (Samson made to *l-tzacheq* — *to make sport, to perform* — before the Philistines). The piel's harsher senses are well-attested. The ASV/KJV *mocking* preserves the harsher possibility without committing to any one sub-reading. **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 78-79) presents the piel range in detail and notes the editorial pull of the harsher readings without committing.

*(d) Rabbinic-tradition maximal reading.* The rabbinic tradition reads *metzacheq* maximally, importing the harsh senses from the piel's wider distribution. **Genesis Rabbah 53:11** offers three readings: (i) idolatry (the *l-tzacheq* of Exod 32:6 read into Gen 21:9); (ii) sexual immorality (the *l-tzacheq bi* of Gen 39:14, 17 read into Gen 21:9); (iii) bloodshed (a tradition of Yishma'el shooting arrows at Yitzchaq under cover of *play*, drawing on 2 Sam 2:14 *y-saheq* — *let the young men play* — preceding the deadly contest). **Rashi** (following **b. Sotah 26b**) endorses the three-fold maximal reading and harmonizes it with Sarah's demand at v 10 (the offense supplies the motive). The Pauline-Galatians 4:29 *persecuted* (Greek *diōkōn*) appears to reflect a Hellenistic-Jewish version of the maximal reading (likely the third sub-reading, the bloodshed-play variant). The reception-history is heavy: the *Yishma'el-as-threat-to-Yitzchaq* reading furnishes the narrative its psychological-moral motive for the expulsion and is the dominant pre-modern reading in both Jewish and Christian tradition. Modern critical scholarship — Sarna, Alter, Westermann, Hamilton, NJPS — moves away from the maximal reading, preferring (a) on philological grounds and reading the rabbinic tradition as a downstream interpretive expansion that imports the harsher senses to motivate the harsh consequence.

**The translation choice: *playing*.** The WoH translation renders *metzacheq* as *playing*, adopting the modern critical default of Sarna / Alter / NJPS / Westermann. The choice is editorially motivated by (i) the lexical default of *tzachaq*; (ii) the chapter's running name-and-verb laughter-play (the *metzacheq* participle continues the *tzachoq* / *yitzchaq* / *Yitzchaq* sequence of vv 6-8, and the participle is most naturally read as the continuation of the laughter-pattern); (iii) the absence of any explicit harsher direct object in the MT (the LXX *with Isaac her son* is treated as later expansion); (iv) the modern critical scholarly consensus against the rabbinic-tradition maximal reading; (v) the editorial principle of not importing rabbinic-tradition harshness on Yishma'el where the MT does not itself carry it. The harsher readings — *mocking* (ASV/KJV), *playing with Isaac* (LXX/NRSV), and the rabbinic-tradition maximal — are documented in apparatus but not adopted. The mainstream English Bibles are split: NJPS, NRSV (with LXX expansion), NIV-2011 *mocking*; ESV *laughing*; CSB *mocking*; ASV/KJV/RSV *mocking*; Alter *playing*. The split itself attests the irreducibility of the dispute.

**Sarah's perception, not Yishma'el's act.** The verse's syntax — *va-tere Sarah et-ben-Hagar ha-Mitzrit asher yaldah l-Avraham metzacheq* — *and Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Avraham, playing* — locates the act in Sarah's perception and chains the description through Hagar's Egyptian identity and her status as the mother-of-the-rival-son. Modern critical scholarship reads the verse's structure as foregrounding Sarah's perception (Westermann pp. 338-339; Sarna p. 146): what Sarah sees motivates v 10's demand, regardless of what Yishma'el is in fact doing. The verb's semantic ambiguity is then part of the verse's deliberate construction — the reader is left, like Avraham at v 11 (*va-yera ha-davar me'od b-eynei Avraham* — *and the thing was very grievous in Avraham's eyes*), uncertain how to weigh Sarah's reaction.

**Cross-references.** See also *yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern* (the chapter's running laughter-name-play across vv 3, 4, 6, 8, 9); *yishmael-name-etymology* (Yishma'el's name and the *shama*-pattern that frames the cycle); the *brit olam* / Yitzchaq-as-covenant-line distinction documented at *twelve-princes-of-ishmael* and at *meqim-brit*.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 146; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 103-104; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 338-339; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 81-82; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 78-79; Speiser *Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 155-156; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, pp. 322-323. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 53:11; b. Sotah 26b; Rashi ad loc.; Pauline reception at Gal 4:29.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the piel participle *metzacheq* is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the four readings and their philological grounds are documentary-attested in the cited scholarly literature; (c) the LXX expansion is textually documentary; (d) the rabbinic-tradition maximal reading is sourced to specific named texts (Gen Rab 53:11, b. Sotah 26b, Rashi); (e) the modern critical preference for the *playing* default is documented in the named scholars. The dispute itself is direct; the translation choice (the *playing* default) follows the modern critical consensus and is one of the two mainstream positions. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: messenger of Elohim
terms.standardEnglish: angel of God (ASV/KJV); angel of God (NRSV); a messenger of God (less common)
terms.wohChoice: messenger of Elohim
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:17 is the first occurrence of *mal'akh Elohim* — the messenger of Elohim — in the Hebrew Bible, and is editorially distinct from *mal'akh YHWH*.** מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים. The chapter's second Hagar-scene (vv 14-21) uses *Elohim* consistently throughout (vv 12, 17 twice, 19, 20) where the first Hagar-scene of Gen 16 used *YHWH* and *mal'akh YHWH* consistently (Gen 16:7, 9, 10, 11, 13). The divine-name register is consistently different across the two Hagar-scenes, and the *mal'akh Elohim* construction is a distinct lexeme from *mal'akh YHWH*. The translation accordingly renders *mal'akh Elohim* with *Elohim* preserved (not collapsed into the generic *God*) and *mal'akh* preserved as *messenger* (not imported as *angel*), parallel to the [malakh-yhwh-messenger](#malakh-yhwh-messenger) construction but with the divine-name distinction maintained.

**The source-critical reading.** The divine-name shift between Gen 16 and Gen 21:8-21 is the principal source-critical evidence for the Documentary Hypothesis identification of the two Hagar-scenes with different documentary sources. The standard modern critical reconstruction reads **Gen 16** as belonging to the **J-document** (the Yahwist, which uses *YHWH* consistently in the patriarchal narratives) and **Gen 21:8-21** as belonging to the **E-document** (the Elohist, which uses *Elohim* in the patriarchal narratives prior to Exod 3:14-15, where the divine name *YHWH* is first revealed to Moses on the E-document's account). The two Hagar-scenes are then a *doublet*: the same basic narrative (Hagar in the wilderness with the child, divine encounter at a well, promise of Yishma'el's tribal future) attested in two parallel source-versions.

**Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 339-340) is the standard modern-critical-commentary statement: *the Hagar-narratives at Gen 16 and Gen 21:8-21 are clearly parallel; the divine-name distribution (YHWH at Gen 16, Elohim at Gen 21:8-21) and the differences of detail (the wilderness vs. the well; the encounter with the *mal'akh YHWH* who speaks vs. the *mal'akh Elohim* who calls from heaven; the promise-of-Yishma'el's-descendants delivered to Hagar at Gen 16 vs. to Hagar via the messenger at Gen 21) together support the documentary attribution*. **Speiser** (*Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 155-157) reads the two scenes as J and E variants. **Friedman** (*Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987, pp. 246-247 in the source-table) attributes Gen 16 to J and Gen 21:8-21 to E. **Skinner** (*ICC Genesis* 1910, pp. 320-325) gives the earliest English-language full presentation: Gen 16 is J's Hagar; Gen 21:8-21 is E's Hagar; the editor (R) has preserved both versions side-by-side in the canonical text.

**The post-documentary critical scholarship.** More recent critical scholarship is divided on the E-document's existence as a continuous independent source. **Van Seters** (*Abraham in History and Tradition* 1975; *Prologue to History* 1992) collapses J and E into a single Yahwistic stratum and reads the Gen 21:8-21 scene as a later post-J expansion. **Carr** (*Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996) and **Schmid** (*Erzväter und Exodus* 1999) maintain the J/E distinction with revisions. **Friedman** preserves the classical four-source documentary model. The doublet-relationship between Gen 16 and Gen 21:8-21 is recognized across the critical spectrum; only the source-attribution itself is disputed. For the WoH translation, the doublet-recognition is the load-bearing scholarly datum; the source-attribution is documented but not adjudicated.

**The *min ha-shamayim* (from heaven) distinctive.** The Gen 21:17 messenger *calls from heaven* (*va-yiqra mal'akh Elohim el-Hagar min ha-shamayim*) — a remoter mode of encounter than the Gen 16 messenger's direct wilderness-appearance. The Gen 16 messenger is *found* by Hagar at a spring (*va-yimtza'ah mal'akh YHWH al-ein ha-mayim ba-midbar* — *the messenger of YHWH found her by a spring of water in the wilderness*), speaks face-to-face, and is identified as YHWH at v 13. The Gen 21 messenger does not appear physically; he *calls* (*va-yiqra*) from heaven and is heard by Hagar, with the well of v 19 then revealed by Elohim's opening of Hagar's eyes (the [paqach-eynayim-revelatory-perception](#paqach-eynayim-revelatory-perception) entry). The *from-heaven-mediated-encounter* register is editorially significant: the Gen 21 scene's divine-presence is mediated and remote where the Gen 16 scene's was direct and immanent. The pattern is consistent with the E-document's broader tendency to mediate divine-presence through *mal'akh* and through dreams (Gen 20:3 *Elohim came to Avimelekh in a dream*; Gen 28:12 the *mal'akhei Elohim* on the ladder; Gen 31:11 the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in Ya'aqov's dream; Gen 46:2 *Elohim said to Israel in visions of the night*) — the standard E-document immanence-mediation pattern.

**The cross-corpus *mal'akh Elohim* distribution.** The construction *mal'akh Elohim* (and the related *mal'akh ha-Elohim* with the article) is distributionally sparser than *mal'akh YHWH* (which has 50+ occurrences across the HB). The *mal'akh Elohim* occurrences:

*Patriarchal.* **Gen 21:17** (here — the Hagar / Yishma'el well-scene); **Gen 31:11** (the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in Ya'aqov's dream at Padan-Aram, instructing Ya'aqov to return to Canaan — the verse explicitly identifies the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* with the deity who appeared at Bethel: v 13 *anokhi ha-El Beit-El*); both attributed to E in the standard documentary reconstruction.

*Exodus.* **Exod 14:19** (*va-yisa mal'akh ha-Elohim ha-holekh lifnei machaneh Yisrael va-yelekh me-achareihem* — *and the messenger of the Elohim who went before the camp of Israel removed and went behind them* — at the Sea-crossing; the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* with the article); the verse is debated in source-critical terms (some assign to E, some to a redactional layer combining E with J's pillar-of-cloud).

*Judges.* **Judg 13:6, 13:9** (Manoah's wife describes the man-of-Elohim as *k-mar'eh mal'akh ha-Elohim nora me'od* — *like the appearance of the messenger of the Elohim, very awesome* — at the Samson-annunciation; the Manoah scene then shifts to *mal'akh YHWH* at vv 13, 15, 16, 17, 18, 20, 21, with the wife's initial *mal'akh ha-Elohim* designation flagging her perception); the alternation is editorially significant in its own right.

*Samuel-Kings.* **1 Sam 29:9** (Achish to David: *tov atah b-einay k-mal'akh Elohim* — *you are good in my eyes as a messenger of Elohim* — a non-Israelite king's complimentary register); **2 Sam 14:17** (the Tekoite woman to David: *k-mal'akh ha-Elohim ken adoni ha-melekh* — *as a messenger of the Elohim, so is my lord the king*); **2 Sam 14:20** (the woman again: *k-chochmat mal'akh ha-Elohim* — *as the wisdom of the messenger of the Elohim*); **2 Sam 19:28** (Mephibosheth to David: *k-mal'akh ha-Elohim* — again the king-as-messenger-of-Elohim complimentary register).

The distribution falls into two registers: the **theophanic-narrative register** (Gen 21:17, Gen 31:11, Exod 14:19, Judg 13:6, 13:9 — actual messenger-encounters narratively presented) and the **complimentary-king register** (1 Sam 29:9, 2 Sam 14:17, 14:20, 19:28 — the king-as-messenger-of-Elohim as a high-praise idiom in the Davidic court). The theophanic-narrative register's distribution (Gen 21:17; Gen 31:11; Exod 14:19; Judg 13) overlaps closely with the standard E-document attribution; the complimentary-king register is independent of source-critical attribution.

**The translation choice.** *Messenger of Elohim* is editorially load-bearing on three independent grounds: (a) it preserves the lexical etymology of *mal'akh* (the standard ordinary-language word for an envoy or messenger; see the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry for the full lexical treatment); (b) it preserves the divine-name distinction from *mal'akh YHWH* — the source-critical and theophanic-mediation distinctions documented above; (c) it is consistent with the WoH practice of preserving *Elohim* in transliteration rather than collapsing it into the generic *God* (see the [elohim-as-translation](#elohim-as-translation) entry). The conventional *angel of God* (ASV/KJV/NRSV) imports later angelology, collapses *Elohim* into the generic, and obscures the *mal'akh YHWH* / *mal'akh Elohim* distinction. The translation choice is a parallel-but-distinct entry from the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry, not a sub-variant of it.

**Relation to the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry.** The two entries cover parallel but distinct lexemes. The cross-corpus distribution differs (the *mal'akh YHWH* has 50+ occurrences across the HB; the *mal'akh Elohim* has the sparser distribution documented above). The source-critical attribution differs (the *mal'akh YHWH* is distributed across J/E/D/P/redactional layers; the *mal'akh Elohim* theophanic-narrative occurrences are concentrated in the E-document attribution). The mode of encounter differs (the *mal'akh YHWH* often appears physically and is identified as YHWH; the *mal'akh Elohim* tends to call from heaven or appear in dreams and maintains greater theophanic mediation). The messenger-identity question (whether the messenger is YHWH himself, a distinct envoy, or a literary-theological device — see the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry for the three-position landscape) recurs for the *mal'akh Elohim* but with the additional source-critical layer: the documentary attribution to E correlates with the E-document's broader divine-immanence-mediation pattern. The WoH translation is editorially neutral on the messenger-identity question and on the source-critical question; both are documented in apparatus and neither is preempted.

**Note on the prior entry's *appliesTo*.** Prior to this v2.18.0 entry, the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry's *appliesTo* list included GEN-21:17. The inclusion was incorrect — Gen 21:17 uses *mal'akh Elohim*, not *mal'akh YHWH*. The v2.18.0 release corrects this by (i) creating this *malakh-elohim-messenger* entry to govern Gen 21:17 and the cross-corpus *mal'akh Elohim* loci; (ii) removing GEN-21:17 from the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry's *appliesTo*. The correction is semver-major on the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry. The *mal'akh Elohim* cross-corpus occurrences (Gen 31:11; Exod 14:19; Judg 13:6, 13:9; 1 Sam 29:9; 2 Sam 14:17, 14:20, 19:28) are documented in this entry's rationale; the *appliesTo* anchor is Gen 21:17 only, with cross-corpus expansion deferred to those chapters' translations.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 339-342; Speiser *Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 155-157; Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 (the documentary source-table at pp. 246-258); Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, pp. 320-325; Van Seters *Abraham in History and Tradition* 1975 and *Prologue to History* 1992 (the post-documentary critique); Carr *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996; Schmid *Erzväter und Exodus* 1999. For the messenger-figure broadly: Heidt 1949, Hamori 2008, Sommer 2009, MacDonald 2009 (per the *malakh-yhwh-messenger* entry).

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construct *mal'akh Elohim* is grammatically explicit in the MT at Gen 21:17 and at the cross-corpus loci cited; (b) the source-critical reading of the Gen 16 / Gen 21:8-21 doublet is the standard modern-critical treatment, documented in the named commentary literature, with the post-documentary critique also presented; (c) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the translation choice preserves the lexical etymology and the divine-name distinction without committing the project to a particular position on either the messenger-identity question or the source-critical question. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb qal imperfect (waw-consec) + direct object marker + noun + suffix
terms.literal: and-he-opened Elohim *acc.* her-eyes
terms.standardEnglish: and God opened her eyes (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and Elohim opened her eyes
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:19 is the patriarchal corpus's first occurrence of the *paqach et ha-eynayim* (open the eyes) idiom in the revelatory-discovery register.** *Va-yifqach Elohim et-eyneha va-tere be'er mayim* — *and Elohim opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water*. The idiom names a developed Hebrew Bible figure for revelatory perception: a deity-induced shift in the recipient's perceptual capacity that allows them to see what was previously hidden, unrecognized, or unperceived. The Gen 21:19 occurrence is the chapter's keystone divine-intervention verse — the well-of-water that saves Yishma'el's life is revealed not by a new act of creation but by Hagar's now-opened sight.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The lexeme *paqach* (פָּקַח) with *eynayim* (עֵינַיִם) as direct object recurs across the Hebrew Bible in three distinguishable registers.

*(i) Revelatory-perception / hidden-thing-revealed register.* **Gen 21:19** (the present verse; Hagar sees the well); **Num 22:31** (*va-yegal YHWH et-eynei Bilam va-yare et-mal'akh YHWH nitzav ba-derekh* — *and YHWH uncovered Balaam's eyes, and he saw the messenger of YHWH standing in the way* — the *gillah*-the-eyes variant verb, but the same revelatory-perception register; the donkey had seen the *mal'akh YHWH* throughout vv 23-30 while Balaam had not, and v 31's eye-uncovering brings Balaam's perception into alignment with the donkey's); **2 Kgs 6:17** (the canonical extended-treatment occurrence: *va-yitpallel Elisha va-yomar YHWH p'qach-na et-eynav v-yir'eh va-yifqach YHWH et-einei ha-na'ar va-yare v-hineh ha-har male susim v-rekhev esh saviv Elisha* — *Elisha prayed and said, YHWH, open his eyes, that he may see; and YHWH opened the young man's eyes, and he saw, and behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire round about Elisha* — the chariot-of-fire revelation, perhaps the corpus's most concrete instance of the idiom); **2 Kgs 6:20** (the inversion: Elisha asks YHWH to *paqach* the eyes of the Aramean army now within Samaria — *YHWH p'qach et-einei eleh v-yir'u* — *YHWH, open the eyes of these and let them see* — and they discover they are inside the enemy city; the same verb, the same syntax, the same revelatory-perception register, but with reversed strategic outcome).

*(ii) Recognition-of-state register.* **Gen 3:5, 3:7** (the eyes of Adam and Chavvah opened — *v-nifqechu eyneikhem* / *va-tipaqachnah eynei sh'neihem* — after eating from the tree of knowledge; recognition-of-nakedness as the perception-shift; see the [niphqecho-eineihem](#niphqecho-eineihem) glossary entry for the foundational treatment). The recognition-of-state register is conceptually distinct from the revelatory-perception register: the recognition is of one's own changed condition, not of a previously-hidden external reality.

*(iii) Eschatological-restoration register.* **Isa 35:5** (*az tipaqachnah einei ivrim v-oznei chershim tipatachnah* — *then the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped* — the eschatological-restoration prophecy); **Isa 42:7** (the Servant's mission *l-fqoach einayim ivrot* — *to open blind eyes*); **Isa 42:18-20** (the blind-and-deaf people of YHWH); **Ps 146:8** (*YHWH poqeach ivrim* — *YHWH opens the eyes of the blind*). The eschatological-restoration register collapses the physical-medical and the spiritual-perceptive into a single eye-opening figure. The Lukan reception (Luke 4:18, 7:21-22 — the citation-quotation of Isa 35 / 42 in Jesus' programmatic statement) carries the register into the NT.

**The Gen 21:19 register: revelatory-perception, not creation.** The verse's most-contested editorial question is whether the well at v 19 is **(a)** a pre-existing well that Hagar had been unable to see and is now revealed to her by the divine eye-opening (the modern critical default: Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 147; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 343; NJPS; NRSV; Alter 2004, p. 105) or **(b)** a newly-created well brought into existence at the moment of revelation (the rabbinic-tradition reading: Genesis Rabbah 53:14 — *the well was created at twilight on the sixth day of creation, with the other miraculous wells of the wilderness*; Pseudo-Jonathan; Midrash Tanchuma — the well as a created-on-the-spot miracle). The modern critical default reads the verb's morphology as decisive: *paqach* takes *eyneha* (her eyes) as direct object, not *be'er* (a well); the eye-opening is the divine action, the well's perception is the consequence. The reading is reinforced by the cross-corpus pattern: in every occurrence of the idiom across the HB (Num 22:31; 2 Kgs 6:17, 6:20), the deity opens the recipient's eyes to a reality that was already present but unseen — the *mal'akh YHWH* was always standing in Balaam's road; the chariots of fire were always on the mountain around Elisha; the Aramean army was already within Samaria. The pre-existing reality is the consistent pattern; ad hoc creation is not the lexical core of the idiom.

The rabbinic-tradition reading is editorially significant for the reception-history (it is the dominant pre-modern reading in both Jewish and Christian commentary) but is downstream of the lexical core. The WoH translation adopts the modern critical default. The rabbinic-tradition reading is documented in apparatus.

**Hagar as the second recipient of the idiom.** Within Genesis itself, the Gen 21:19 occurrence is the second instance of the *paqach-eynayim*-cluster (after Gen 3:5, 3:7). The recurrence is editorially notable: at Gen 3 the eye-opening was the consequence of disobedience and produced shame; at Gen 21 the eye-opening is the consequence of divine compassion and produces salvation. The two occurrences frame the idiom's range from the foundational-shaming sense to the patriarchal-salvific sense. Hagar — the foreign-Egyptian woman who at Gen 16:13 became the first to name a divine name (*El Ro'i* — *the El who sees*) — is now the first to have her own seeing opened by the deity. The *seeing*-pattern of the two Hagar-scenes is editorially significant: at Gen 16:13 Hagar names the deity who saw her; at Gen 21:19 the same deity opens her seeing. The foreign-woman-as-seer pattern is documented by **Trible** (*Texts of Terror* 1984), **Reis** (*Reading the Lines* 2002), and **Fewell and Gunn** (*Gender, Power, and Promise* 1993).

**The identity of the well.** The text does not name the well at v 19, and modern critical commentary is divided on whether it is to be identified with (i) *Be'er Lachai Ro'i* of Gen 16:14 (Hagar's first-encounter-site well; would frame the two Hagar-scenes as a return-to-the-well pattern); (ii) the well of *Be'er Sheva* (the chapter's other well, named only at v 31 after the Avimelekh covenant; would frame the verse as the implicit etiology for the *Be'er Sheva* name's hydrological component); (iii) a third, unnamed wilderness well (would preserve the well-of-Hagar as a distinct geographical referent). The geographical placement of vv 14-19 in *midbar Be'er Sheva* (the wilderness of Be'er Sheva, v 14) is consistent with (ii) but does not entail it. The WoH translation preserves the textual silence: the well is left unnamed, with the three identification possibilities documented in apparatus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 147; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 343-344; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 84-85; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 83-84; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 104-105; Speiser *Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 156-157; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, pp. 323-324. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 53:14; Pseudo-Jonathan ad loc.; Midrash Tanchuma. For the *foreign-woman-as-seer* reading: Trible 1984; Reis 2002; Fewell and Gunn 1993.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *paqach* with *eynayim* as direct object is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of the idiom is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the three registers (revelatory-perception / recognition-of-state / eschatological-restoration) are documented in the cited scholarly literature; (d) the modern critical preference for the *well-was-always-there* reading is documented in the named scholars and reflects the lexical core of the idiom; (e) the rabbinic-tradition reading is documented and not preempted. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: Elohim (is) with-you
terms.standardEnglish: God is with thee (ASV/KJV); God is with you (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Elohim is with you
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:22 is the first patriarchal occurrence of the *Elohim/YHWH im-X* (Elohim/YHWH is with X) divine-presence formula — the Hebrew Bible's standard idiom for divine accompaniment and consequent success.** *Va-yhi ba-et ha-hi va-yomer Avimelekh u-Pichol sar tzva'o el-Avraham lemor Elohim imkha b-khol asher atah oseh* — *and it came to pass at that time, that Avimelekh and Pichol the captain of his host spoke to Avraham, saying: Elohim is with you in all that you do*. The formula is a developed HB recurring phrase for the deity's accompaniment-as-favor and as the basis for inter-personal trust, treaty, and political alignment. The verse is doubly significant: it is the formula's first patriarchal occurrence, and it is on the lips of a *non-Israelite king* — Avimelekh of Gerar — recognizing the divine accompaniment of the patriarch from the outside. The recognition-from-outside register is itself a developed HB pattern (Pharaoh recognizing the *ruach Elohim* in Yosef at Gen 41:38; Bil'am the foreign-prophet recognizing YHWH's blessing of Israel at Num 22-24; Nebuchadnezzar's edict at Dan 3:28; etc.).

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The formula's cross-corpus reach is one of the most extensive in the HB — by some counts, the most-attested recurring formula in the Hebrew Bible outside the divine names themselves. The distribution falls into a number of structural clusters.

*Patriarchal recognition by non-Israelite kings.* **Gen 21:22** (here — Avimelekh to Avraham); **Gen 26:3, 26:24, 26:28** (the Yitzchaq-Avimelekh doublet: v 3 YHWH self-disclosure *anokhi imkha*; v 24 *anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha al-tira ki-itkha anokhi*; v 28 Avimelekh's recognition formula identical to Gen 21:22: *ra'inu ki-hayah YHWH imkha* — *we have plainly seen that YHWH is with you*); the v 28 wording explicitly mirrors v 22, marking the chapter-21 / chapter-26 doublet (the *wife-sister type-scene*, the *Avimelekh-Pichol*, and the *Elohim-imkha-Be'er-Sheva-covenant* triad all repeat in the Yitzchaq cycle).

*Patriarchal divine self-introduction.* **Gen 26:3** (YHWH to Yitzchaq: *anokhi imkha*); **Gen 28:15** (YHWH to Ya'aqov at Bethel: *hineh anokhi imakh u-shmartikha b-khol asher telekh* — *behold, I am with you and will keep you wherever you go*); **Gen 31:3** (YHWH to Ya'aqov at Padan-Aram: *shuv el-eretz avotekha u-l-moladtekha v-ehyeh imakh* — *return to the land of your fathers, and to your kindred; and I will be with you*); **Gen 31:5** (Ya'aqov to Leah and Rachel: *Elohei avi hayah imadi*); **Gen 35:3** (Ya'aqov en route to Bethel: *El ha-oneh oti b-yom tzarati va-yhi imadi ba-derekh asher halakhti* — *El who answered me in the day of my distress and was with me in the way that I went*); **Gen 39:2, 39:3** (Yosef in Potiphar's house: *va-yhi YHWH et-Yosef* — *and YHWH was with Yosef* — twice; the cleanest extended formula-deployment in Genesis); **Gen 39:21, 39:23** (Yosef in prison: *va-yhi YHWH et-Yosef va-yet elav chesed* — *YHWH was with Yosef and showed him kindness*; v 23 *ba-asher YHWH ito v-asher hu oseh YHWH matzliach* — *because YHWH was with him; and what he did, YHWH made it to prosper* — the verbal echo of v 22's *b-khol asher atah oseh*); **Gen 48:21** (Israel to Yosef on his deathbed: *Elohim imakhem v-heshiv et-khem el-eretz avoteikhem*).

*Wilderness and Moses-Joshua transition.* **Exod 3:12** (the burning bush, foundational Moses-call: *ki-ehyeh imakh v-ze l-kha ha-ot* — *certainly I will be with you; and this shall be the sign to you*); **Exod 18:19** (Yitro to Moses: *lekh ha-laqar-na b-qoli i'atzkha v-yhi Elohim imakh* — *go, hearken to my voice; I will counsel you, and Elohim be with you*); **Num 14:9, 14:43** (the Joshua-Caleb good-report vs the spies' bad-report contrast: *al-tira'u et-am ha-aretz... va-YHWH itanu al tira'um* — *do not fear the people of the land... YHWH is with us, fear them not* — at v 9; the inverted negative at v 43 *ki-ein YHWH b-qirbekhem* — *YHWH is not in your midst*).

*The Joshua-transition cluster.* **Deut 31:6, 31:8** (Moses to Israel and to Joshua at the transition: *YHWH Elohekha hu ha-holekh imakh lo yarpekha v-lo ya'azvekha* — *YHWH your Elohim, he is the one who goes with you; he will not fail you nor forsake you*); **Deut 31:23** (the explicit YHWH-to-Joshua transition: *chazak v-ematz ki atah tavi et-bnei Yisrael el-ha-aretz asher nishbati la-hem v-anokhi ehyeh imakh* — *be strong and of good courage, for you shall bring the children of Israel into the land that I swore to them, and I will be with you*); **Josh 1:5, 1:9, 1:17** (the inaugural Joshua-narrative threefold: v 5 *ka-asher hayiti im-Moshe ehyeh imakh*; v 9 *ki imkha YHWH Elohekha b-khol asher telekh*; v 17 the people's affirmation *raq yihyeh YHWH Elohekha imakh ka-asher hayah im-Moshe*); **Josh 3:7** (the Jordan-crossing: *ha-yom ha-zeh achel gadelkha b-einei kol-Yisrael asher yedu ki ka-asher hayiti im-Moshe ehyeh imakh*); **Josh 6:27** (the post-Jericho summary: *va-yhi YHWH et-Yehoshua va-yhi shoma'o b-khol-ha-aretz*).

*Judges, Samuel, David.* **Judg 1:19, 1:22** (the early-Judges conquest fragments: *va-yhi YHWH et-Yehudah* at v 19; *va-YHWH imam* at v 22); **Judg 2:18** (*ki-yehi YHWH im-ha-shofet*); **Judg 6:12-16** (the Gideon-call: v 12 the *mal'akh YHWH* to Gideon *YHWH imkha gibor he-chayil* — *YHWH is with you, mighty man of valor*; v 16 *YHWH imakh*); **1 Sam 3:19** (Samuel: *va-yhi YHWH imo*); **1 Sam 10:7** (Samuel to Saul: *ki ha-Elohim imakh*); **1 Sam 16:18** (David first identified: *va-YHWH imo*); **1 Sam 17:37** (David to Saul before Goliath: *YHWH asher hitzilani... yatzileini va-YHWH yhi imakh*); **1 Sam 18:12, 18:14, 18:28** (the recurring David-narrative summary: v 12 *va-yira Sha'ul mi-lifnei David ki-hayah YHWH imo u-me-im Sha'ul sar*; v 14 *va-yhi David l-khol drakhav maskil va-YHWH imo*; v 28 *va-yare Sha'ul va-yeda ki YHWH im-David*); **2 Sam 5:10** (the Davidic-rise summary: *va-yhi YHWH Elohei Tzva'ot imo*); **2 Sam 7:3, 7:9** (Nathan's oracle: v 3 Nathan to David *ki YHWH imakh*; v 9 YHWH to David *va-ehyeh imkha b-khol asher halakhta*).

*Solomonic and post-Solomonic.* **1 Kgs 1:37** (Benaiah to Solomon: *ka-asher hayah YHWH im-adoni ha-melekh ken yhi im-Shlomoh*); **1 Kgs 8:57** (Solomon's temple-dedication prayer: *yhi YHWH Elohenu imanu ka-asher hayah im-avotenu*); **1 Kgs 11:38** (YHWH to Yarav'am via Achiyah: *v-hayiti imakh u-vaniti l-kha bayit ne'eman ka-asher baniti l-David*); **2 Kgs 18:7** (Hezekiah: *va-yhi YHWH imo*).

*Prophetic.* **Isa 7:14** (the foundational *Imanu-El* — *Elohim with us* — sign-prophecy to Achaz, with the cross-corpus prophetic-resonance loci at Isa 8:8, 8:10 — *ki imanu El*); **Isa 41:10** (*al tira ki imkha-ani*); **Isa 43:2, 43:5** (the Servant-Israel passages: *ki avor ba-mayim itkha-ani* / *al tira ki itkha-ani*); **Jer 1:8, 1:19** (the inaugural Jeremiah-call: v 8 *al-tira mi-pneihem ki-itkha ani l-hatzilekha*; v 19 *v-nilchamu elekha v-lo yuchlu lakh ki itkha ani*); **Jer 15:20** (*v-natatikha l-am ha-zeh l-chomat nechoshet b'tzurah v-nilchamu elekha v-lo yuchlu lakh ki itkha ani l-hoshia'akha u-l-hatzilekha*); **Jer 20:11** (*va-YHWH oti k-gibor aritz*); **Jer 30:11, 46:28** (the Israel-restoration passages: *ki-itkha ani*); **Ezek 34:30** (the future-restoration: *v-yade'u ki ani YHWH Eloheihem itam*); **Hag 1:13** (*ani itkhem* — YHWH to the returning community); **Hag 2:4** (*hizqu... va-asu ki-ani itkhem*); **Zech 8:23** (the eschatological-Jerusalem promise: *ki shama'nu Elohim imakhem*).

*Chronicles.* **1 Chr 9:20** (early-Chronicles: *YHWH hayah imo*); **1 Chr 11:9** (Davidic-rise parallel: *va-yhi YHWH Tzva'ot imo*); **1 Chr 17:2, 17:8** (Nathan's oracle parallel to 2 Sam 7); **1 Chr 22:11, 22:16, 22:18** (David to Solomon: v 11 *va-yhi YHWH imakh*; v 16 *atah qum va-aseh va-YHWH imakh*; v 18 *halo YHWH Eloheikhem imakhem*); **1 Chr 28:20** (David to Solomon at the temple-charge: *YHWH Elohai Elohekha imakh lo yarpekha v-lo ya'azvekha*); **2 Chr 1:1; 13:12; 15:2, 15:9; 17:3; 20:17; 25:7; 32:7-8; 35:21; 36:23** (the Chronicler's extended distribution).

**The formula's two structural functions.** Across the cross-corpus distribution, the *Elohim/YHWH im-X* formula performs two distinguishable functions: (i) **divine self-disclosure** (YHWH speaking, as in Gen 28:15, Exod 3:12, Josh 1:5, Isa 41:10, Jer 1:8) — the deity assuring the elected figure of accompaniment as the basis for the mission; (ii) **external recognition** (a third party identifying the divine accompaniment, as in Gen 21:22, Gen 26:28, 1 Sam 18:28, Hag 1:13) — the foreign-or-internal witness recognizing the elected figure as divinely-accompanied. The two functions are structurally distinct: in (i) the deity speaks; in (ii) a human speaks. The Gen 21:22 instance is the corpus's foundational external-recognition occurrence: the formula's first deployment in patriarchal narrative is on the lips of a non-Israelite king. The cross-corpus pattern bears this out — external-recognition occurrences are concentrated at moments of inter-political contact (Gen 21:22 Avimelekh; Gen 26:28 Avimelekh; 1 Sam 18:28 Saul; the David-and-the-Philistines material).

**The Avimelekh-recognition context.** Within the Gen 20-21 Avimelekh sequence, the formula at v 22 is the chapter's structural-pivot from the Hagar-expulsion narrative (vv 1-21) to the Avimelekh-treaty narrative (vv 22-34). The pivot is editorially deliberate: the chapter has just demonstrated *Elohim imkha* through the Hagar-and-Yishma'el rescue (vv 17-21, where *Elohim* appears five times), and Avimelekh's recognition at v 22 is the external-confirmation of the divine-accompaniment that the preceding pericope has just narratively established. The structural logic — internal divine action ↔ external recognition — is a pattern across the *im-X* formula's distribution. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 343) treats v 22 as a pivot-verse; **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 147-148) reads the *Elohim imkha* recognition as the basis for Avimelekh's treaty-initiative.

**Modern scholarly literature.** **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 343-345; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 147-148; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 91-92; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 85-86; **Alter** *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 105-106; **H. D. Preuss** *Jahweglaube und Zukunftserwartung* (BWANT 87) 1968 — the classic monograph-treatment of the *im-X* formula across the HB, with the foundational distinction between the divine-self-disclosure and external-recognition functions; **D. Vetter** *Jahwes Mit-Sein* (Calwer Theologische Monographien 1) 1971; **W. C. van Wyk** in *Studies in the Pentateuch* (1989) on the patriarchal recurrence; **H. F. van Rooy** in *Old Testament Essays* (1990) on the Avimelekh-treaty pivot.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construct *Elohim imkha* / *YHWH im-X* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the two structural functions (divine self-disclosure vs. external recognition) are documented in Preuss 1968 and the subsequent monograph-tradition; (d) the Avimelekh-recognition pivot at v 22 is mainstream scholarly. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.strongs: H884 (Be'er Sheva); H7651 (sheva — seven); H7650 (shava — to swear)
terms.literal: well of seven / well of oath (the place-name's double-etymology)
terms.standardEnglish: Beer-sheba (ASV/KJV); Beer-sheba (NRSV/NJPS); Beersheba (NIV)
terms.wohChoice: Be'er Sheva (transliterated, preserving the double-etymology visibly)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:31 is the in-text etiology of Be'er Sheva — and the chapter sustains a layered double-etymology.** *Al-ken qara la-maqom ha-hu Be'er Sheva ki sham nishb'u sh'neihem* — *therefore he called that place Be'er Sheva, because there they both swore an oath*. The place-name is morphologically a construct chain: *be'er* (well) + *sheva*. The second element *sheva* (שָׁבַע — *seven*) and *shava* (שָׁבַע — *to swear an oath*) share the same triliteral root **sh-b-ʿ** and are morphologically identical in their citation form. The chapter deliberately deploys both senses.

**The two layered etymologies.** *(i) The seven-ewe-lambs object-etymology.* At vv 28-30 Avraham sets seven ewe-lambs (*sheva kivsot ha-tzon*) aside from the flock; Avimelekh asks what they are for; Avraham explains: *ki et-sheva kivsot tiqach mi-yadi ba-avur tihyeh-li l-edah ki chafarti et-ha-be'er ha-zot* — *because you shall take these seven ewe-lambs from my hand, that it may be a witness to me that I have dug this well*. The seven ewe-lambs are the legal witness (the *l-edah*) to Avraham's prior digging of the well, and the place-name's *sheva* element encodes this seven-fold witness-arrangement. The well is the *well of the seven (ewe-lambs)*.

*(ii) The swearing-of-oath event-etymology.* At v 31 the etiological clause is *ki sham nishb'u sh'neihem* — *because there they both swore an oath*. The verb *nishb'u* is the niphal of *shava* (to swear), and the place-name's *sheva* element encodes the swearing-of-the-oath event. The well is the *well of the oath*.

*The two etymologies are not alternatives.* The chapter sustains both simultaneously. The seven-ewe-lambs (vv 28-30) are the materials of the oath-swearing (v 31); the oath-swearing (v 31) ratifies the seven-ewe-lambs arrangement (vv 28-30). The place-name compresses both the witness-object and the witness-act into a single phonological cluster. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 149): *the name allows for a double interpretation; the chapter exploits this through the deliberate placement of the seven ewe-lambs immediately before the swearing-clause*. **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 92-95): *the etymology is doubled, with the chapter making both senses visible*. **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 348-349) reads the doubled-etymology as the chapter's literary signature.

**The Gen 26:33 doublet.** The same well receives a parallel etiology in the Yitzchaq cycle at **Gen 26:33**: after Yitzchaq's parallel-but-distinct treaty with Avimelekh, the well receives the name *Shiv'ah* (שִׁבְעָה — likewise from the **sh-b-ʿ** root). *Va-yiqra otah Shiv'ah al-ken shem ha-ir Be'er Sheva ad ha-yom ha-ze* — *he called it Shiv'ah; therefore the name of the city is Be'er Sheva to this day*. The Gen 26:33 etiology activates only the *sheva*-as-oath sense (the swearing-clause at v 31 *va-yashkimu va-yishav'u ish l-achiv* — *they rose early and swore to one another*; the verb is *yishav'u*, the niphal of *shava*, with no parallel to the seven-ewe-lambs object-arrangement of Gen 21). The two etiologies are deliberately partial vs. layered: Gen 26:33 is single-sense (oath only); Gen 21:31 is double-sense (seven + oath). The chapter-21 / chapter-26 doublet is part of the wider *wife-sister + Avimelekh + Be'er Sheva* triad-doublet across the two cycles.

**The patriarchal well-naming pattern.** Be'er Sheva joins the patriarchal cycle's recurring well-naming convention. Cross-corpus pattern: **Gen 16:14** *Be'er Lachai Ro'i* — *well of the Living One who sees me* (the Hagar-encounter site; see [beer-lachai-roi](#beer-lachai-roi)); **Gen 21:31** *Be'er Sheva* — *well of seven / well of the oath* (here); **Gen 26:20-22** *Eseq / Sitnah / Rechovot* (Yitzchaq's three contested wells in the Gerar dispute); **Gen 26:33** *Shiv'ah* (the second Be'er Sheva); **Exod 15:23** *Marah* (bitter water); **Num 21:16-18** *Be'er* (the well of the wilderness song). The pattern names the well after the event, the person, the act, or the property; the name fixes the site in narrative memory. *Be'er Sheva* is unusual within the pattern in that its etymology is *double*: every other patriarchal well-name has a single-sense etymology, and the chapter's deliberate double-etymology is itself a marker of the well's editorial significance.

**The toponym's later cross-corpus significance.** Be'er Sheva becomes one of the canonical southern-boundary markers of the Israelite land: the recurring formula *mi-Dan v-ad Be'er-Sheva* (from Dan to Be'er Sheva) at Judg 20:1, 1 Sam 3:20, 2 Sam 3:10, 17:11, 24:2, 24:15, 1 Kgs 4:25, 1 Chr 21:2, 2 Chr 30:5; the deuteronomic centralization-anchor at 2 Kgs 23:8 (Josiah's removal of the high places *from Geva to Be'er Sheva*); the Amos-1 indictment of the *derekh Be'er Sheva* (*the way of Beer-sheba*) at Amos 5:5 and 8:14; the late-prophetic-and-postexilic settlement-references at Neh 11:27, 11:30. The toponym is the southern bookend of the canonical Israelite-land formula, paired with Dan as the northern bookend. The chapter's etiology is editorially foundational for the entire cross-corpus toponym-distribution.

**The geography.** The historical Be'er Sheva is securely identified with **Tell es-Seba / Tel Be'er Sheva** (8 km east of modern Beer-Sheva), excavated by **Y. Aharoni** in 1969-76 and by **Z. Herzog** in subsequent seasons. The Iron-Age stratification (Stratum VIII-VII the early-Iron-Age occupation; Stratum V the planned-fortified city of the 9th-8th centuries BCE; Stratum II the late-Iron-Age destruction layer attributed to Sennacherib's 701 BCE campaign) is well-documented (Aharoni *Beer-Sheba I* 1973; Herzog *Beer-Sheba II* 1984; Herzog and Singer-Avitz *Beer-Sheba III* 2016). The patriarchal-narrative reference to Be'er Sheva is geographically realistic for the south-Judah Negev-fringe pattern but is not anchored to a specific Iron-Age stratum (the patriarchal narratives' historiographical status is itself a long-standing debate — see the wider discussion in **Dever** *Who Were the Early Israelites and Where Did They Come From?* 2003 and **Finkelstein and Silberman** *The Bible Unearthed* 2001).

**Translation.** *Be'er Sheva* is preserved in transliteration, matching the WoH compound-place-name convention (already applied to *Be'er Lachai Ro'i*, *Eseq / Sitnah / Rechovot*, *Mitsrayim*, *Bet-El*, etc.). The Anglicized *Beer-sheba* (ASV) is the conventional English but loses both the construct-chain structure (the *Be'er* + *Sheva* visibility) and the *sheva* / *shava* double-etymology play that the transliteration preserves. A semantic translation (*Well of Seven* or *Well of the Oath*) would preempt the doubled-etymology in favor of one sense; the transliteration is editorially conservative.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 149; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 348-349; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 92-95; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 88-91; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 106; Speiser *Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 157-158. For the archaeological-historical material: Aharoni *Beer-Sheba I* 1973; Herzog *Beer-Sheba II* 1984; Herzog and Singer-Avitz *Beer-Sheba III* 2016; for the Iron-Age boundary-formula's reception-history: **N. Na'aman** in *Tel Aviv* 1980 on *Dan-to-Beer-Sheva*; **Finkelstein and Silberman** *The Bible Unearthed* 2001 on the patriarchal-narratives' historiographical status; **Dever** 2003 on the wider Iron-Age south-Judah settlement-pattern.

**Cross-corpus *appliesTo*.** The entry is anchored at Gen 21:31 (first occurrence in the chapter; the in-text double-etymology). The cross-corpus loci (Gen 22:19; Gen 26:23, 26:33; Gen 28:10; Gen 46:1, 46:5; Josh 15:28, 19:2; Judg 20:1; 1 Sam 3:20, 8:2; 2 Sam 3:10, 17:11, 24:2, 24:7, 24:15; 1 Kgs 4:25, 19:3; 2 Kgs 12:2, 23:8; 1 Chr 4:28, 21:2; 2 Chr 19:4, 24:1, 30:5; Neh 11:27, 11:30; Amos 5:5, 8:14) are documented in this entry's rationale but not added to *appliesTo* in this chapter; expansion is deferred to those chapters' translations.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the place-name's morphology and the **sh-b-ʿ** root are grammatically explicit; (b) the in-text double-etymology is sustained across vv 28-31; (c) the cross-corpus toponym-distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the Gen 26:33 doublet and the *Dan-to-Be'er-Sheva* boundary-formula are documentary; (e) the archaeological-historical Tell es-Seba identification is documented in the Aharoni / Herzog excavation reports. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: YHWH, El of long-distance-time / YHWH, Everlasting El / YHWH, El of the remotest age
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD, the Everlasting God (ASV/KJV); the LORD, the Everlasting God (NRSV); the LORD, the Everlasting God (NJPS); the Eternal God (NIV)
terms.wohChoice: YHWH, the Everlasting El
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:33 is the first occurrence of *El Olam* in the Hebrew Bible — the Everlasting El — and the fourth instance of the patriarchal El-compound divine-naming pattern in the Avraham cycle.** *Va-yita eshel bi-Be'er Sheva va-yiqra-sham b-shem YHWH El Olam* — *and he planted a tamarisk at Be'er Sheva, and called there on the name of YHWH, El Olam*. The chapter's keystone cultic-site verse names the cult-site (Be'er Sheva), the cultic-marker (the *eshel* — see [eshel-tamarisk-cultic-site](#eshel-tamarisk-cultic-site)), the invocation-formula (*qara b-shem YHWH* — see [qara-b-shem-yhwh](#qara-b-shem-yhwh)), and the divine name (*YHWH El Olam*) in a single compact verse. The verse is the only formal Avraham-cultic-site-establishment in the Genesis cycle that combines all four elements.

**The fourth El-compound in the Avraham cycle.** The verse completes the four-fold patriarchal El-compound divine-naming pattern that the cycle develops at four distinct sanctuary-sites:

*(i) **El Elyon*** at Gen 14:18-22 (Avram and Melchizedek at Salem; see [el-elyon](#el-elyon) and [yhwh-el-elyon-apposition](#yhwh-el-elyon-apposition)). The verbal apposition *YHWH El Elyon* at Avram's oath in v 22 (*harimoti yadi el-YHWH El Elyon qoneh shamayim va-aretz* — *I have lifted up my hand to YHWH, El Elyon, possessor of heaven and earth*).

*(ii) **El Ro'i*** at Gen 16:13 (Hagar at the wilderness spring; see [el-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant](#el-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant)). The first divine-name-naming by a woman in the HB; not paired with a YHWH apposition in the same verse, but the speaker is identified as the *mal'akh YHWH* of vv 7-12 by the introductory clause *va-tiqra shem-YHWH ha-dover eleha*.

*(iii) **El Shaddai*** at Gen 17:1 (the covenant-of-circumcision divine self-introduction to Avraham; see [el-shaddai](#el-shaddai)). YHWH's self-disclosure *ani-El Shaddai* at the covenant-of-circumcision establishment. The P-document programmatic harmonization at Exod 6:3 — *I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchaq, and to Ya'aqov as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them* — frames the chapter-17 / chapter-21 El-compounds within the P-document's divine-name-revelation chronology.

*(iv) **El Olam*** at Gen 21:33 (here — Avraham at Be'er Sheva). The fourth and final patriarchal El-compound, paired with the explicit YHWH apposition (*YHWH El Olam* — the same appositional construction as the *YHWH El Elyon* of Gen 14:22). The pattern's completion at the chapter-21 cultic-site is editorially deliberate: the four El-compounds across four sanctuary-sites stage the patriarchal religious geography as a four-fold El-cult, with each El-compound localized to a different site, and with YHWH identified appositionally with each compound at the integration-points (Gen 14:22; Gen 21:33).

**The religious-historical reconstruction.** The patriarchal El-compound pattern is a load-bearing datum for the modern religious-historical reconstruction of the patriarchal religion as a *West-Semitic El-religion locally diversified*, with each sanctuary-site preserving a distinct El-compound divine name corresponding to that site's pre-Israelite high-god cult. The Yahwistic editor then harmonizes each El-compound with YHWH through the appositional construction (*YHWH El Elyon* at Gen 14:22; the implicit identification at Gen 16:13; the divine self-disclosure *ani El Shaddai* at Gen 17:1; *YHWH El Olam* at Gen 21:33), producing the canonical text's *YHWH = the El of each site*.

**A. Alt** (*Der Gott der Väter* 1929) — the foundational monograph — first proposed reading the patriarchal *El-of-the-fathers* religion as a pre-Israelite West-Semitic cult-stratum distinct from the later YHWH-cult, with the El-compounds preserving authentic pre-Israelite divine-naming. Alt's reconstruction is updated and refined in **F. M. Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973, ch. 1 (*The Religion of the Patriarchs*) — the canonical modern statement: the patriarchal narratives preserve genuine pre-Israelite El-religion, with the El-compounds (*El Elyon*, *El Ro'i*, *El Shaddai*, *El Olam*, *El Bethel* at Gen 31:13 and 35:7, *El Elohei Yisrael* at Gen 33:20, *El the God of your father* at Gen 46:3) reflecting the local-sanctuary differentiation of a common West-Semitic El-cult. Cross's reconstruction is reinforced by the Ugaritic textual material (the El-of-Ugarit as the head of the Canaanite pantheon, with epithets distributed across the corpus); the **Deir ʿAlla** inscription's *El* and *shdyn*; the **Karatepe** inscription's *El qoneh ʾerets*; the **Hittite** *Elkunirsa* (a West-Semitic loan); the **Phoenician** *Elioun* preserved in Philo of Byblos' Sanchuniathon-fragment. The wider monograph-tradition: **M. S. Smith** *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism* 2001; **J. Day** *Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan* 2000; **M. Köckert** *Vätergott und Väterverheissungen* 1988 — the critical-revisionist treatment that pushes back on some of Alt's specifics while retaining the general framework; **R. de Vaux** *The Early History of Israel* 1978, pp. 271-287 — the Catholic-scholarly classical treatment.

**The Yahwistic harmonization.** The chapter-21 / chapter-14 parallel is editorially significant. Both verses (Gen 14:22, Gen 21:33) deploy the same appositional construction (*YHWH El X*) at the integration-point: a non-Israelite El-compound is named, and YHWH is appositionally identified with it. The Gen 14:22 instance is on the lips of Avram swearing to Melchizedek (a non-Israelite priest of El Elyon); the Gen 21:33 instance is on the lips of Avraham at his own cultic-site-establishment. The parallel marks the editor's deliberate harmonization-strategy: the patriarchal-El-compounds are identified with YHWH at every integration-point, preserving the El-compounds visible while assimilating them to the Yahwistic cult.

**The *olam* lexeme.** *Olam* (עוֹלָם) is one of the Hebrew Bible's most semantically contested lexemes. The standard lexical positions: **HALOT** ii.798-799 glosses *olam* as *remote time, antiquity, hidden time, indefinite future, perpetuity*; the lexical core is *long duration, indefinite extent in time* rather than the abstract *eternity* of the Greek philosophical tradition. **BDB** 761-763 gives the same range: *long duration, antiquity, futurity*. The semantic-development summary: (i) the **past register** — *olam* as *the long-distant past, remotest antiquity* (Gen 6:4 *gibborim asher me-olam* — *the mighty men of old, from antiquity*; Deut 32:7 *zekhor y'mot olam* — *remember the days of old*; Isa 51:9 *me'olam dorot olamim* — *from of old, from generations of old*; Job 22:15); (ii) the **future register** — *olam* as *long duration into the future, indefinite future* (Exod 21:6 the slave's *avadeo l-olam* — *he shall serve him forever*, where the lexeme clearly does not entail metaphysical eternity but indefinite life-of-master duration; Deut 23:4-7 the *l-olam* exclusion of Ammonites and Moabites from the assembly, where the practical duration is unspecified but indefinite); (iii) the **eternity register** — *olam* as *eternity, perpetuity in an abstract sense* (Ps 90:2 *me-olam ad-olam atah El* — *from everlasting to everlasting you are El*; Isa 40:28 *Elohei olam YHWH* — *YHWH is the everlasting Elohim*; Dan 12:2 the *chayei olam* — *life everlasting*). The eternity register is the *latest* of the three to develop and is contested as overreading of the lexeme's earlier-period sense.

**The Gen 21:33 register.** The Gen 21:33 *El Olam* is editorially undeterminable between the three registers on philological grounds alone. The cross-corpus parallels at **Ps 90:2** (*me-olam ad-olam atah El*) and **Isa 40:28** (*Elohei olam YHWH*) and **Jer 10:10** (*Elohim chayim u-melekh olam*) deploy *El* / *Elohim* + *olam* in the late-period register (Psalm 90, Deutero-Isaiah, Jeremiah are all post-monarchic-or-exilic compositions). The Gen 21:33 occurrence is the *earliest* canonical instance of the *El + olam* construct. Modern critical scholarship is divided: **Cross** (1973, p. 50) reads the patriarchal *El Olam* as preserving the early-West-Semitic register — *El of the long-distant time* — with the post-exilic *El of eternity* sense as a downstream development; **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 351-352) and **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 149) preserve the *Everlasting El* English (matching ASV) as preserving the temporal-duration sense without committing to the abstract-eternity reading; **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 92-94) and **NJPS** *the Everlasting God*; the **NIV** modernization to *Eternal God* imports the abstract-eternity sense and is critiqued as overreading. The WoH translation adopts the *Everlasting El* default, preserving the temporal-duration sense and rejecting the abstract-eternity reading; the El-compound transliteration *El Olam* is preserved in apparatus.

**Translation.** *YHWH, the Everlasting El* preserves ASV's *Everlasting* (temporal-duration sense; rejects the abstract-eternity overreading) and substitutes *El* for ASV's generic *God* (matching the WoH practice with *El Elyon*, *El Ro'i*, *El Shaddai*). The translation choice (a) preserves the El-compound pattern visible to the English reader; (b) preserves the appositional construction *YHWH El X* parallel to Gen 14:22; (c) follows the modern critical philological reading of *olam* (temporal duration, not abstract eternity); (d) avoids the NIV-style overreading. The fully transliterated alternative (*YHWH, El Olam*) is editorially defensible but is not adopted as the default; the partial-transliteration *Everlasting El* preserves the compound visibly while keeping the *olam* element rendered in English.

**Cross-corpus *appliesTo* expansion of related entries.** This entry does not expand other entries' *appliesTo* directly, but cross-references three: (i) [qara-b-shem-yhwh](#qara-b-shem-yhwh) (already covers GEN-21:33 — the *qara b-shem YHWH* invocation-formula; no expansion needed); (ii) [eshel-tamarisk-cultic-site](#eshel-tamarisk-cultic-site) (new v2.18.0 entry — the cultic-marker at the same verse); (iii) [yhwh-el-elyon-apposition](#yhwh-el-elyon-apposition) (Gen 14:22 entry — the structural parallel at the chapter's pattern-opening).

**Modern scholarly literature.** **A. Alt** *Der Gott der Väter* 1929 (the foundational monograph; reprinted in *Essays on Old Testament History and Religion* 1968); **F. M. Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973, ch. 1 (*The Religion of the Patriarchs*, pp. 1-75 — the canonical modern statement); **M. S. Smith** *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism* 2001 (Oxford); **J. Day** *Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan* 2000 (JSOTSup 265); **M. Köckert** *Vätergott und Väterverheissungen* 1988 (the critical-revisionist treatment); **R. de Vaux** *The Early History of Israel* 1978, pp. 271-287; **R. Albertz** *A History of Israelite Religion in the Old Testament Period* 1994, vol. 1, pp. 23-39; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 351-352; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 149; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 92-94; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 95-96. For the *olam* lexeme specifically: **HALOT** ii.798-799; **BDB** 761-763; **TDOT** vol. 10, pp. 530-545 (*olam* by H. D. Preuss); **E. Jenni** *Das hebräische *olam* im AT* (ZAW 64, 1952 and 65, 1953) — the classic two-part monograph-treatment.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the divine name *El Olam* and the appositional construction *YHWH El Olam* are grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the patriarchal El-compound pattern across Gen 14:18-22 / 16:13 / 17:1 / 21:33 is documentary-attested; (c) the religious-historical reconstruction is the standard modern-critical reading documented in Alt 1929, Cross 1973, Smith 2001, Day 2000, and the wider monograph-tradition; (d) the *olam* lexical-semantic range is documented in HALOT, BDB, TDOT, and Jenni 1952-53; (e) the Yahwistic-harmonization reading of the appositional construction is mainstream. Project-specific synthesis (any specific Wheel of Heaven reading of the patriarchal El-cult beyond the mainstream religious-historical reconstruction) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: tamarisk (tree)
terms.standardEnglish: tamarisk tree (ASV); tamarisk (NRSV/NJPS); grove (KJV)
terms.wohChoice: tamarisk
terms.rationale:
**Gen 21:33 contains the first of three Hebrew Bible occurrences of the lexeme *eshel*.** *Va-yita eshel bi-Be'er Sheva va-yiqra-sham b-shem YHWH El Olam* — *and he planted a tamarisk at Be'er Sheva, and called there on the name of YHWH, El Olam*. The *eshel* is the cultic-marker of the Avrahamic Be'er-Sheva site, paired with the *qara b-shem YHWH* invocation-formula and the *YHWH El Olam* divine name. The verse stages the only formal Avraham-cultic-site-establishment in the cycle that combines all three elements (cultic-marker + invocation + divine name).

**The lexeme's botanical identification.** *Eshel* (אֶשֶׁל) is securely identified as the **tamarisk** (genus *Tamarix*, most likely *Tamarix aphylla* — the leafless tamarisk, sometimes called *T. articulata*, the species native to the Negev and the Sinai), a desert-adapted tree well-suited to arid wadi-and-spring environments. The identification is supported by (i) the cognate Akkadian *ašlu* (tamarisk) and Arabic *athl* (tamarisk) — the cognate-philological evidence; (ii) the geographical-ecological match between the tamarisk's habitat (saline soils, desert wadis, the Negev region) and the Be'er-Sheva site; (iii) the standard botanical-survey identifications in Y. Felix *Plants and Animals of the Mishna* 1983 and *Nature and Land in the Bible* 1992; **M. Zohary** *Plants of the Bible* 1982, pp. 116-117; **F. Nigel Hepper** *Encyclopedia of Bible Plants* 1992, pp. 199-200. Earlier translations (KJV's *grove*) misread the lexeme; modern translations uniformly read *tamarisk*. The botanical identification is mainstream scholarly consensus.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The lexeme is a botanical hapax-cluster — only three Hebrew Bible occurrences:

*(i)* **Gen 21:33** (here — Avraham plants the *eshel* at Be'er Sheva).

*(ii)* **1 Sam 22:6** — *v-Sha'ul yoshev ba-Giv'ah tachat ha-eshel ba-Ramah v-chanito b-yado* — *Saul was sitting in Gibeah, under the tamarisk on the height, with his spear in his hand*. The verse places Saul under a specific *the eshel* (with the definite article — a named landmark-tree) at his royal-court site at Gibeah. The tamarisk is here a king-under-the-sacred-tree site, with the wider HB pattern of *judges-and-leaders-under-sacred-trees* (Judg 4:5 Deborah under the palm; Judg 6:11, 6:19 Gideon under the oak; 1 Sam 14:2 Saul under the pomegranate at Migron).

*(iii)* **1 Sam 31:13** — *va-yiqchu et-atzmoteihem va-yiqberu tachat ha-eshel b-Yavesh va-yatzumu shiv'at yamim* — *they took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk at Yavesh, and fasted seven days* (the Yavesh-Gilead men's recovery of Saul and his sons' bodies after the Mount Gilboa defeat). The Saul-burial site under the *eshel* completes the Saul-and-the-eshel pair (1 Sam 22:6 the royal-court site; 1 Sam 31:13 the burial site), and the *seven days of fasting* attached to the burial-tree marks the *eshel* as a cultic-mourning site.

The three-fold distribution makes the *eshel* a marked HB botanical lexeme, with cultic-or-royal associations at each of the three occurrences.

**The *plant + cult-site + invocation* pattern.** The verb at Gen 21:33 is *va-yita* (the qal imperfect waw-consecutive of *nata* — *to plant*). Avraham *plants* the tamarisk; he does not encounter a pre-existing tree at the site. The deliberate-cultic-tree-planting register is editorially significant: across the patriarchal-narrative pattern, sacred-tree-sites are normally pre-existing landmarks (Gen 12:6 *elon Moreh* — the oak/terebinth of Moreh at Shechem, a pre-existing oracle-tree; Gen 13:18, 14:13, 18:1 *elonei Mamre* — the oaks of Mamre, the pre-existing Avraham-residence site; Gen 35:4 the oak at Shechem under which Ya'aqov buries the foreign gods; Gen 35:8 the *Allon Bakhut* — *oak of weeping* — under which Rivkah's nurse Deborah is buried; Josh 24:26 the oak at Shechem under which Joshua sets up the witness-stone). The Avraham-tamarisk at Be'er Sheva is the only patriarchal sacred-tree-site that is *deliberately planted by the patriarch himself* rather than pre-existing. The verb's distinctive use is editorially deliberate: Avraham's act founds the site as his own cultic-marker, in contrast with the other sacred-tree-sites' inheritance-status.

**The wider Hebrew Bible sacred-tree-cultic-marker pattern.** The sacred-tree-and-cultic-site association recurs across the HB. The *elon / elah / allon* (oak / terebinth) family: Gen 12:6 (*elon Moreh*); Gen 13:18, 14:13, 18:1 (*elonei Mamre*); Gen 35:4, 35:8; Josh 24:26; Judg 4:5 (Deborah's palm; the *tomer Devorah* — slightly different lexeme but the same pattern); Judg 6:11, 6:19 (the oak at Ophrah); Judg 9:6, 9:37 (the *elon mutzav* — *the oak of the pillar* — at Shechem); 1 Sam 10:3 (the oak of Tabor); 2 Sam 18:9-14 (Avshalom's oak); 1 Kgs 13:14 (the oak at Bethel); Hos 4:13 *tachat alon v-livneh v-elah ki-tov tzilah* — *under oak and poplar and terebinth, because their shade is good* — the prophetic-indictment of sacred-tree-cult-sites. The wider lexeme-cluster (*eshel* / *elon* / *elah* / *allon* / *livneh* / *tomer* / *rimon* — tamarisk / oak / terebinth / oak / poplar / palm / pomegranate) covers the canonical HB sacred-tree-cultic-marker corpus. The pattern is documented by **M. Pope** *Song of Songs* (Anchor Bible 1977 / 1985), with the *sacred-tree-and-pole* discussion at pp. 211-227 (Pope's classical treatment of the sacred-tree-as-cultic-marker across the wider ANE corpus); **C. Frevel** *Aschera und der Ausschliesslichkeitsanspruch YHWHs* 1995 (the asherah-cult and the sacred-tree-association); **S. M. Olyan** *Asherah and the Cult of Yahweh in Israel* 1988; **J. M. Hadley** *The Cult of Asherah in Ancient Israel and Judah* 2000. The pattern is well-attested in the wider ANE: the sacred-tree-pole motif in Mesopotamian temple-art (the Assyrian sacred-tree friezes at Ashurnasirpal II's Nimrud palace; the Phoenician sacred-tree motifs); the Egyptian *ished*-tree of Heliopolis; the Ugaritic *qdš*-tree iconography.

**The lens-relevance and editorial discipline.** The sacred-tree-cultic-marker pattern is editorially load-bearing: the patriarchal narratives consistently locate divine-encounter and cult-establishment at sacred-tree-sites, with the lexeme-cluster (oak / terebinth / tamarisk / palm) as the standard markers. The pattern is part of the West-Semitic pre-Israelite religious-stratum that the patriarchal narratives preserve and that the Yahwistic editor harmonizes with the YHWH-cult (per the *yhwh-el-olam* entry on the wider El-compound religious-historical reconstruction). The reading is mainstream in modern critical scholarship (Pope 1985, Frevel 1995, Olyan 1988, Hadley 2000, the wider monograph-tradition); project-specific WoH synthesis on the sacred-tree-cult is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

**Translation.** *Tamarisk* is preserved (ASV's *tamarisk tree* is slightly redundant — the lexeme is itself the tree-name — but ASV is preserved as the WoH baseline). KJV's *grove* is rejected as a translation-error (the lexeme is a specific tree, not a tree-cluster). NJPS / NRSV *tamarisk* (without the redundant *tree*) is editorially cleaner; ASV's *tamarisk tree* is retained for baseline-consistency.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 351-352; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 149; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 95-96; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 92-94; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 107; Speiser *Anchor Bible Genesis* 1964, pp. 158-159. For the botanical-identification: Felix 1983, 1992; Zohary 1982; Hepper 1992. For the sacred-tree-cultic-marker reading: Pope *Song of Songs* (Anchor Bible 1977/1985); Frevel 1995; Olyan 1988; Hadley 2000.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *eshel* and its cognate-philological identification with the tamarisk are documentary; (b) the three-fold cross-corpus distribution (Gen 21:33; 1 Sam 22:6; 1 Sam 31:13) is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the verb *va-yita* (planted) is grammatically explicit; (d) the sacred-tree-cultic-marker pattern across the wider HB lexeme-cluster (*eshel* / *elon* / *elah* / *allon* / *livneh* / *tomer*) is documented in the cited monograph-tradition; (e) the wider ANE sacred-tree-iconography is documented in the cited scholarly literature. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: (he) tested, tried, put to the test
terms.standardEnglish: tested (NJPS/NRSV); did prove (ASV); did tempt (KJV archaic)
terms.wohChoice: tested
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:1 opens with the chapter's defining narrator-comment:** *v-ha-Elohim nisah et-Avraham* — *and the Elohim tested Avraham*. The verb is the piel of *n-s-h* (test, try, put to the test, prove), the standard Hebrew Bible lexeme for divine testing of human covenantal-faithfulness. The narrator-comment is editorial discipline of the highest order: by naming the entire pericope a *test* before it begins, the narrator preempts the reader's misreading of the command of v 2 as a sincere demand for child-sacrifice. The reader knows from v 1 that what follows is a test; Avraham does not. The dramatic-irony of the chapter is structurally generated by this opening narrator-comment.

**The lexeme and its cross-corpus distribution.** The piel of *n-s-h* is concentrated in the testing-of-Israel motif of the Pentateuch and the early historical books: **Exod 15:25** (the bitter-waters at Marah — *sham nisahu* — *there he tested them*); **Exod 16:4** (the manna — *l-ma'an anasenu ha-yelekh b-torati im-lo* — *that I may test them, whether they will walk in my law or not*); **Exod 20:20** (Sinai post-theophany — *l-va'avur nasot etkhem ba ha-Elohim* — *for the Elohim has come to test you*); **Deut 8:2** (the wilderness-forty-years recap — *l-ma'an anot'kha l-nasot'kha la-da'at et-asher bi-l'vavkha* — *to humble you and to test you, to know what was in your heart*); **Deut 8:16** (parallel — manna as testing-instrument); **Deut 13:4** (the false-prophet legislation — *ki m'naseh YHWH Elohekhem etkhem* — *for YHWH your Elohim is testing you*); **Deut 33:8** (the Levi-blessing — *asher nisito b-Masah trivehu al-mei Merivah* — recalling the *Masah / Merivah* incident); **Judg 2:22, 3:1, 3:4** (the post-Joshua nations-left-to-test-Israel motif — *l-nasot bam et-Yisrael* — *to test Israel by them*); **Ps 26:2** (the supplicant's plea — *b'chaneni YHWH v-naseni* — *examine me, YHWH, and test me*); **2 Chr 32:31** (the Hezekiah-and-the-Babylonian-envoys notice — *azavo ha-Elohim l-nasoto la-da'at kol bi-l'vavo* — *the Elohim left him to test him, to know all that was in his heart*). Gen 22:1 is the **first occurrence of the divine-testing piel in the Hebrew Bible** and the only patriarchal-narrative instance; the motif is otherwise concentrated in the Mosaic and post-Mosaic corpus.

**The theological problem of divine testing.** The verse generates the classical theological crux: *if the Elohim is omniscient, what does the test produce?* The crux is one of the most-discussed in the entire Hebrew Bible reception-history. The principal scholarly positions, presented in parallel and none preempted by this entry:

*(a) The test is for Avraham's benefit, not the deity's information.* **Maimonides** (*Guide of the Perplexed* III.24) reads the *aqedah* as a pedagogical demonstration: the test is *l-hodi'a* — *to make known* — to humanity (not to the deity) the limit of Avraham's love-and-fear. The Hebrew *l-ma'an eda* (Deut 8:2's *that I may know*) is anthropomorphic-narrative idiom, not a metaphysical claim about divine cognition. Maimonides's reading is the foundational Jewish-philosophical resolution and survives in mainstream rabbinic and modern-Orthodox theology.

*(b) The test discloses what was previously latent.* The classical Christian patristic and medieval scholastic position (Augustine, Aquinas *ST* I q.22, II-II q.97) distinguishes the deity's foreknowledge of the outcome from the test's function of *disclosing* the latent disposition in a publicly-visible form. The disposition was always present; the test makes it manifest. The position is theologically conservative on divine omniscience.

*(c) The test is a real epistemic event for the deity.* The modern philosophical-theology position of *open theism* (Pinnock, Sanders, Boyd) and some Process-theology readings (Cobb, Griffin) treat the test as genuinely producing new divine knowledge — the deity does not know in advance what Avraham will do. The position takes Gen 22:12's *ata yadati* (*now I know*) literally and rejects the Maimonidean and patristic resolutions. The position is a minority in the wider theological literature but is documentary in the modern philosophical-theology corpus.

*(d) The narrative-anthropomorphism reading (modern critical default).* **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 150), **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 354-356), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 102), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 102): the *nisah* and the *ata yadati* are narrative-anthropomorphism, not metaphysical claims. The text presents the deity in human-narrative register; the reader is not asked to reconcile the narrative's anthropomorphism with a metaphysical doctrine of omniscience. The chapter is a *story* in the strict literary sense, and the story requires the test-and-discover narrative grammar. The reading is the modern critical default and is the position best supported by the literary-narrative analysis of the chapter as a whole.

**Scholarly literature on the *aqedah* and the testing-problem.** **S. Spiegel** *The Last Trial: On the Legends and Lore of the Command to Abraham to Offer Isaac as a Sacrifice* (translated 1967) — the foundational survey of the rabbinic and medieval reception, including the *Akedah* liturgical tradition and the Crusade-era poetic reworkings. **J. D. Levenson** *The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son* (1993) — the foundational modern-critical synthesis of the *aqedah* and the wider HB *beloved-son* motif (Yitzchaq, Yosef, the firstborn-of-Israel; cross-corpus to the Christian reception). **J. D. Levenson** *The Universal Horizon of Biblical Particularism* (1998) — the wider methodological framework. **P. R. Davies and B. D. Chilton** *The Aqedah: A Revised Tradition History* (CBQ 1978) — the source-critical reconstruction of the chapter's redactional layers. **W. Brueggemann** *Genesis* (Interpretation 1982), pp. 187-194 — the testing-and-disclosure reading. **Sarna**, **Westermann**, **Wenham**, **Hamilton** as cited above for the modern critical synthesis. The chapter is also the locus classicus of the philosophical-reception tradition: **S. Kierkegaard** *Fear and Trembling* (1843; the *teleological suspension of the ethical*); **E.
