Auerbach** *Mimesis* (1946) ch. 1 (the canonical comparison with Homer's recognition-scene of Odysseus); **J. Derrida** *The Gift of Death* (1995; the Levinasian-Kierkegaardian reading). These reception-traditions are noted as downstream-not-preempted by this entry; the entry itself stays in the mainstream scholarly register of the modern critical Genesis commentary tradition.

**The article-bearing *ha-Elohim*.** The verse uses *v-ha-Elohim* (definite-article-bearing) rather than bare *Elohim* or *YHWH* as the subject of *nisah*. Across the chapter the divine-name distribution is editorially load-bearing: *ha-Elohim* (vv 1, 3, 9) for the test-commanding figure; *Elohim* without article (v 8 Avraham's reply; v 12 the *yere Elohim* certification); *YHWH* (vv 11, 14 twice, 15, 16) for the staying-of-the-hand, the place-name, and the oath. The classical source-critical reading (Gunkel, von Rad, Westermann) treats the divine-name distribution as a fingerprint of the E source overlaid with a J supplement (vv 11b-18 the YHWH section). The WoH *elohim-as-translation* entry handles the article-bearing form by rendering *the Elohim*; this entry adds the distributional observation to the apparatus.

**Translation.** The piel *nisah* is rendered *tested* (NJPS, NRSV, modern critical default), updating ASV's archaic *did prove*. KJV's *did tempt* (archaic English where *tempt* means *test*) is semantically misleading in modern English and is rejected.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *n-s-h* in the piel is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the four scholarly positions on the testing-problem are documented in the cited literature; (d) the WoH translation choice (*tested*) is the modern critical default and matches NJPS/NRSV. Project-distinctive WoH synthesis on the testing-problem is reserved for the wiki and long-form material downstream; the entry stays in mainstream Genesis-commentary register.

terms.literal: your only one / your unique one / your beloved
terms.standardEnglish: your only son (ASV); your favored one (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: your only son
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:2, 12, 16 each use *yechidkha* (your only one) of Yitzchaq.** The lexeme *yachid* (יָחִיד) — single, only, unique, beloved — is the chapter's recurring designation of Yitzchaq across the three key moments: the initial command (v 2 *kach-na et-binkha et-yechidkha asher ahavta*); the staying-of-the-hand certification (v 12 *v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidekha mimeni*); the oath-confirmation (v 16 *v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidekha*). The three occurrences mark the chapter's structural turns.

**The narrative-internal tension.** Yitzchaq is not Avraham's only son. **Yishma'el was born at Gen 16** and has been expelled but is not dead (Gen 21:8-21; Yishma'el is alive in the narrative world at Gen 22, and Avraham knows it — there is no death-notice for Yishma'el between Gen 21 and Gen 22). The *yechid* lexeme therefore cannot be read flatly as *only-existing-son*. The modern critical readings:

*(a) Yechid in the covenant-line sense.* **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 151), **Wenham** (*WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 105), **Hamilton** (*NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 105): Yitzchaq is the *only-one* of the covenantal-promise line, the only-one through whom the Avrahamic promise of nation-and-blessing will pass (per Gen 17:19-21 and Gen 21:12 — *ki b-Yitzchaq yiqare l'kha zera* — *for through Yitzchaq shall your seed be called*). Yishma'el is alive but has been removed from the promise-line at Gen 21:12-13. The *yechid* is the *only-one-of-the-promise*, not the *only-one-in-existence*.

*(b) Yechid as affective-emotional intensifier.* **Levenson 1993** pp. 28-34: the LXX renders *ton agapēton* (the beloved), and the affective sense — *your beloved one* — is supported by the parallel use at Prov 4:3 (*ki ben hayiti l-avi rakh v-yachid lifnei imi* — *for I was a son to my father, tender and beloved before my mother*) and Jer 6:26 (*evel yachid asi lakh* — *make a mourning as for an only-son*). The affective register is the dominant biblical-Hebrew sense of *yachid* — beloved-because-unique, treasured-because-irreplaceable.

*(c) Yechid in the firstborn-of-Sarah sense.* A combined reading: Yitzchaq is *only-one* relative to his mother Sarah (the only son she will ever bear, given her age), the *only-one* through whom the promise will pass, and the *beloved-one* in the affective register. The combined reading is the modern critical synthesis; the verse does not require disambiguation.

**Cross-corpus distribution of *yachid*.** The lexeme is rare and load-bearing. **Judg 11:34** — Jephthah's daughter, *bito v-hi yechidah* — *his daughter, and she was his only-one* — the parallel-only-child-to-be-sacrificed narrative, with the *yechidah* feminine form. The Jephthah-Yitzchaq parallel is one of the chapter's most cited cross-corpus pairings (Spiegel 1967, Levenson 1993, Trible *Texts of Terror* 1984). **Ps 22:21** (MT 22:21 / English 22:20) — *hatzilah me-chereb nafshi mi-yad-kelev yechidati* — *deliver my life from the sword, my only-one from the power of the dog* — *yechidah* as soul / life. **Ps 35:17** — *hashivah nafshi mi-sho'eyhem mi-k'firim yechidati* — parallel use of *yechidah* as soul/life. **Zech 12:10** — *v-safdu alav k-mispid al-ha-yachid* — *they shall mourn for him as one mourns for an only-son* — the major NT-reception verse (cited at John 19:37 in the crucifixion-scene; one of the foundational pierced-one prophecies in the Christian reception-tradition). **Prov 4:3** and **Jer 6:26** as cited above.

**The downstream reception.** The Vulgate *unigenitum* (only-begotten) carries the Johannine *monogenes* echo (John 3:16 *ton huion ton monogenē*); the Christian reception-tradition reads the *yechid* of Gen 22 as the typological prefiguration of the only-begotten Son (Hebrews 11:17-19 makes the typology explicit). The Islamic reception (Quran 37:101-113) does not name the son in the binding-narrative; the early Islamic tafsir tradition is divided between Yitzchaq and Yishma'el, with the Yishma'el identification becoming dominant in classical Sunni tradition (Firestone *Journeys in Holy Lands* 1990 on the Islamic reception-history). All three downstream receptions are flagged-not-preempted by this entry.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 150-151; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 357-358; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 104-106; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 104-108; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, pp. 162-163; Levenson 1993 ch. 3; Spiegel 1967.

**Translation.** ASV's *your only son* is preserved (the standard English smoothing toward the covenant-line sense). The narrative-internal Yishma'el tension is carried in the per-verse commentary. NJPS's *your favored one* (which leans toward the affective sense) is editorially defensible but is not adopted; the WoH choice prefers ASV's preservation of the *only-one* literal-equivalent over the affective smoothing.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *yachid* and its three Gen 22 occurrences are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable; (c) the three modern critical readings of the Yishma'el-tension are documented in the cited literature; (d) the downstream Christian and Islamic receptions are documented; (e) the WoH translation choice (*your only son*) is the standard English equivalent and matches ASV.

terms.literal: land of (the) Moriah
terms.standardEnglish: the land of Moriah (ASV/NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: the land of Moriah
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:2 names the destination of the *aqedah* journey:** *lekh-lekha el eretz ha-Moriah* — *go forth to the land of Moriah*. The noun *Moriah* is a hapax in the Pentateuch — the place-name recurs only once more in the entire Hebrew Bible, at **2 Chr 3:1**: *va-yachel Shlomoh livnot et-beit YHWH bi-Yrushalayim b-har ha-Moriah asher nir'ah l-David aviv* — *Solomon began to build the house of YHWH in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah, where [YHWH] had appeared to David his father*. The Chronicler's identification of *har ha-Moriah* with the Jerusalem Temple Mount is the foundation of the Jewish (Targumim, Rashi, Maimonides) and the wider rabbinic tradition identifying the binding-site with the later Temple.

**The textual variants.** The MT *ha-Moriah* is supported by the Targumim (Onkelos *ar'a pulchana*, *land of worship*; Pseudo-Jonathan explicitly identifies with the Temple Mount) and by the Vulgate (*in terram visionis* — *into the land of vision* — reading the place-name as a derivation of *r-'-h* / *to see*, anticipating the *yir'eh* / *yera'eh* wordplay of v 14). **The LXX reads *eis tēn gēn tēn hypsēlēn*** — *into the high land* — which suggests either a different Vorlage (e.g., *ha-marom* — the high — or a Hebrew word the LXX translators did not recognize as a place-name) or an interpretive gloss. **The Samaritan Pentateuch reads *eretz ha-Moreh*** — *the land of Moreh* (consonantal variant ה / י) — connecting the site to the *elon Moreh* of Shechem at Gen 12:6 and supporting the Samaritan tradition's identification of the binding-site with **Mount Gerizim**, near Shechem. The textual variant is the consonantal foundation of the Jewish-Samaritan dispute over the binding-site's location, paralleling the wider Jerusalem-Gerizim dispute (John 4:20 the Samaritan woman to Jesus on the same dispute).

**The modern critical positions.**

*(a) The Chronicler's identification is ancient tradition.* **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 151) and the conservative-evangelical commentary tradition (Wenham 1994 p. 105 with cautious agreement; Hamilton 1995 p. 109) accept the 2 Chr 3:1 identification as preserving an ancient tradition connecting the Avraham-narrative site with the later Temple. The reading is the dominant Jewish-traditional position, with extensive support in the targumic and rabbinic literature.

*(b) The Chronicler is retrojecting.* **Westermann** (*Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 357) and **Speiser** (*AB Genesis* 1964, p. 163) take the modern critical caution: the place-name *Moriah* is a free-floating proper name in Gen 22, and the Chronicler is retrojecting the later Jerusalem-cultic site onto the patriarchal narrative. The Chronicler's reading is the *first* attested identification, not necessarily the *original* one. The position is the modern critical default outside the conservative-evangelical and Jewish-traditional commentary traditions.

*(c) The Samaritan reading is the original.* A minority position (J. Macdonald *The Theology of the Samaritans* 1964; A. D. Crown *The Samaritans* 1989) reads the Samaritan *eretz ha-Moreh* as preserving the original consonantal text, with the MT's *ha-Moriah* a Judean-tendentious vocalization tying the patriarchal site to the Jerusalem Temple. The position is held by a small portion of the modern critical literature and is generally rejected by the broader scholarship on text-critical grounds (the MT vocalization is supported by the wider Masoretic tradition; the Samaritan reading is the variant, not the base text).

**The Islamic reception** does not engage the Moriah-Temple-Mount identification directly. The Quran 37:101-113 does not name the binding-site; the early Islamic tafsir tradition is divided, with some classical commentators (al-Tabari, Ibn Kathir) identifying the site with Mina near Mecca, others with the Levant. The Dome of the Rock (built 691-692 CE on the Temple Mount) is identified by some Islamic traditions with the binding-site (with Yishma'el as the bound son in the dominant Sunni reading); the wider question of the Temple-Mount-and-Dome-of-the-Rock site-identification is a major topic in Islamic-Jewish-Christian comparative scholarship. The reception-history is noted as downstream-not-preempted.

**The lexical-etymological foundation.** *Moriah* (מֹרִיָּה) is most naturally read as a *mem*-preformative noun from the root *r-'-h* (to see), giving the sense *the seen-place / the place-of-vision*. The Vulgate's *terra visionis* (*land of vision*) preserves this etymology. The chapter's r-'-h wordplay-engine (vv 4, 8, 13, 14) is consistent with this etymology — the place-name *Moriah* anticipates the chapter's seeing-and-being-seen pattern. An alternative etymology derives *Moriah* from *y-r-h* (to throw, point, teach, give oracle), giving *the place-of-oracle*; this etymology connects *Moriah* to *Moreh* (the *elon Moreh* of Gen 12:6) and supports the Samaritan reading. A third etymology (more speculative) derives from a compound *mor-yah* (*the myrrh of YHWH*) — generally rejected on philological grounds. The first etymology (*r-'-h*) is the modern scholarly default.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 150-151; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 356-357; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 105-106; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 108-109; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, p. 163; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, p. 328; Levenson 1993 on the Temple-Mount-and-Aqedah typology in later Jewish tradition.

**Translation.** ASV's *the land of Moriah* is preserved. The MT-fidelity policy (per *malakh-yhwh-messenger*, *al-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant*, etc.) supports preserving the Masoretic place-name over the LXX-gloss or the Samaritan-variant. The temple-mount identification and the textual-variant dispute are carried in the per-verse commentary and in this glossary entry.

The claim_type is *inferred* (per Editor's call) because (a) the place-name *Moriah* itself is grammatically explicit, but (b) the Temple-Mount identification rests on the Chronicler's 2 Chr 3:1 datum, which is itself a downstream reading rather than an explicit Gen 22 statement, and (c) the modern critical position is divided between accepting the Chronicler's identification and reading it as retrojection. The *inferred* claim_type signals that the identification is a defensible scholarly reading consistent with the canonical Hebrew Bible's own cross-reference, but not a grammatically explicit feature of Gen 22 itself.

terms.literal: that-which-goes-up / ascending-offering / whole-burnt-offering
terms.standardEnglish: burnt-offering (ASV/KJV); burnt offering (NRSV); whole burnt offering (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: burnt-offering
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22 contains the first sustained narrative use of *olah* in the Hebrew Bible**, recurring six times across the chapter: vv 2 (the command — *v-ha'alehu sham l-olah* — *and offer him there for a burnt-offering*); 3 (Avraham's preparation — *va-y'vaqa atzei olah* — *and he split the wood for the burnt-offering*); 6 (the wood loaded onto Yitzchaq — *atzei ha-olah* — *the wood of the burnt-offering*); 7 (Yitzchaq's question — *v-ayeh ha-seh l-olah* — *but where is the lamb for a burnt-offering*); 8 (Avraham's answer — *Elohim yir'eh-lo ha-seh l-olah* — *Elohim will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt-offering*); 13 (the substitution — *va-ya'alehu l-olah tachat b'no* — *and offered him up for a burnt-offering in the stead of his son*). The six-fold repetition is editorially load-bearing — the chapter's vocabulary is saturated with the sacrificial-cultic register before the substitution-pivot at v 13.

**The lexeme and its cultic-technical sense.** *Olah* (עֹלָה) derives from the root *a-l-h* (to go up, ascend) — the noun is the feminine participle, *that-which-goes-up*. The cultic-technical sense is the **whole-burnt-offering**: the sacrifice entirely consumed by fire on the altar, with the smoke ascending to the deity (hence the name). The *olah* is distinguished from the *zevach sh'lamim* (the peace-offering, partially consumed and partially eaten by the worshipper); from the *chatat* (the sin-offering, with specific atonement-function and a different consumption-rule); from the *asham* (the guilt-offering, with restitution-function); and from the *minchah* (the grain-offering, vegetable rather than animal). The *olah* is the principal sacrifice-class of Lev 1 and the Priestly material.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The lexeme is the highest-frequency sacrificial term in the Hebrew Bible (over 280 occurrences). The foundational legislation is **Lev 1:3-17** (the *olah* legislation — cattle, sheep, birds — the three-fold animal-categorization with the species-specific ritual details); **Lev 6:1-6** (English 6:8-13, the *torat ha-olah* — the priestly-instructional layer); **Num 15** (the cereal-and-libation accompaniments); **Num 28-29** (the daily, sabbath, festival *olah* calendar). Pre-Sinai narrative uses: **Gen 8:20** (Noach's *olah* after the flood); **Gen 22** (the *aqedah*, this chapter); **Exod 10:25, 18:12, 20:24, 24:5, 32:6, 35:16, 38:1** (the wilderness *olah* references). Post-Sinai pre-Solomonic narrative uses: Judg 6:26 (Gideon's altar); 11:31 (Jephthah's vow — *v-ha'alitihu olah*, the second *olah* + human-sacrifice narrative); 13:16, 19-20 (Manoah's *olah*); 1 Sam 6:14-15 (the Beth-Shemesh return-of-the-ark *olah*); 1 Sam 7:9-10 (Samuel's *olah* at Mizpah); 1 Sam 13:9-12 (Saul's premature *olah* at Gilgal); 1 Kgs 3:4 (Solomon's thousand-*olah* at Gibeon); 1 Kgs 8 (the Temple dedication). The *olah* is also the principal sacrificial form in the prophetic-cult-critique corpus: Isa 1:11 (the *olah* of rams), Amos 5:22 (*if you offer me *olot* I will not accept them*), Hos 6:6 (*chesed chafatzti v-lo zevach* — *I desire steadfast-love and not sacrifice*), Mic 6:6-7 (*ha-erkav b-olot* — *shall I come before him with burnt-offerings?*).

**The chapter's first-narrative-use status.** Gen 22 is the **first narrative use of *olah* with a human victim** in the Hebrew Bible and is the **second narrative use of *olah* overall** (after Gen 8:20). The chapter is therefore the foundational textual instance of the *olah* as a marked-sacrificial-class before its codification in Lev 1. The reader of the canonical text encounters *olah* here, before the Sinai legislation; the lexeme's force is generated by the narrative use, not by the cultic-legal definition. The literary-canonical reading (Alter 2004 p. 109; Sarna 1989 pp. 151-152) emphasizes the chapter's pre-cultic-legislative status — Avraham is asked for an *olah* before the cultic-legal system that defines *olah* has been promulgated. The Sinai legislation will later prohibit human-sacrifice (Lev 18:21, 20:2-5 the Molech-prohibition; Deut 12:31, 18:10; the corpus's foundational anti-child-sacrifice polemic); Gen 22's pre-Sinai test stages the very prohibition that the later legislation will codify.

**The ANE comparative material.** Child-sacrifice in the wider ANE is documented at the Phoenician *molk* / *mlk* sites (the Carthaginian Tophet at Salambo, with the burial-urns of cremated infants; Stager and Wolff *JBL* 1984; Smith *The Memoirs of God* 2004); the wider Levantine evidence is debated (Day *Molech: A God of Human Sacrifice in the Old Testament* 1989; Heider *The Cult of Molek: A Reassessment* 1985). The *molk* term is itself the Hebrew *molech* of Lev 18:21 et passim. The wider HB anti-child-sacrifice polemic (2 Kgs 16:3 Ahaz; 21:6 Manasseh; 23:10 Josiah's reform at the Topheth in the valley of Ben-Hinnom; Jer 7:31; 19:5; 32:35; Ezek 16:20-21, 20:26, 31; 23:37-39; Mic 6:7) treats the practice as a Canaanite-syncretistic abomination. The *aqedah*'s pre-Sinai non-completion of the human-*olah* is, on one mainstream reading (Levenson 1993; Sarna), the foundational textual repudiation of the practice — Avraham is tested, and the test ends with the substitution of the ram, marking the prohibition the later legislation will codify.

**Translation choices.** The ASV *burnt-offering* is preserved as the WoH baseline. The NJPS *whole burnt offering* is editorially defensible (more explicit about the totally-consumed character) but is rejected for ASV-baseline consistency. The KJV/LXX/Vulgate tradition (*holokautōma* / *holocaustum*) is not used in modern English translation register and is rejected. The literal *ascending offering* (preserving the *a-l-h* etymology) is editorially defensible for verse-by-verse pedagogical use but is rejected for the chapter's translation register.

**Scholarly literature.** Milgrom *Leviticus 1-16* (Anchor Bible 1991) on the *olah* legislation, pp. 133-178; Levine *Leviticus* (JPS 1989) pp. 5-12; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 151-152; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 357; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 105-106; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 105; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 109. On child-sacrifice and the ANE comparative material: Day 1989; Heider 1985; Stager and Wolff 1984; Levenson 1993; Smith 2004.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *olah* and its six-fold chapter-occurrence are grammatically explicit; (b) the cultic-technical sense is documented in Lev 1 and the wider Priestly corpus; (c) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable; (d) the chapter's first-narrative-use status is a textual fact; (e) the ANE comparative material is documented in the cited monograph-tradition; (f) the WoH translation choice (*burnt-offering*) matches ASV and the broader English-translation tradition.

terms.partOfSpeech: prep + def article + noun + def article + ordinal
terms.literal: on the day the third
terms.standardEnglish: on the third day (ASV/NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: on the third day
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:4 places the *aqedah*'s narrative-climax marker:** *ba-yom ha-shlishi va-yisa Avraham et-eynav va-yar et-ha-maqom me-rachoq* — *on the third day Avraham lifted up his eyes and saw the place from afar*. The temporal formula *ba-yom ha-shlishi* — *on the third day* — is a Hebrew Bible stylized-formulaic marker for theophany, decisive-event, or transition; the modern critical literature reads it as a literary-narrative convention rather than as a literal chronometric datum.

**Cross-corpus distribution.** The *third-day* formula recurs across the canon as a marker of decisive-event:

*(i)* **Exod 19:11, 15, 16** — the Sinai theophany (*v-hayu n'khonim la-yom ha-shlishi ki ba-yom ha-shlishi yered YHWH l-einei kol-ha-am al-har Sinai* — *be ready by the third day, for on the third day YHWH will come down upon Mount Sinai before the eyes of all the people*). The Sinai-theophany third-day is the parallel-most-cited cross-reference for Gen 22.

*(ii)* **Hos 6:2** — *y'chayenu mi-yomayim ba-yom ha-shlishi y'qimenu v-nichyeh l-fanav* — *after two days he will revive us, on the third day he will raise us up and we shall live before him*. The keystone cross-corpus verse for the *third-day-resurrection* motif; the prophetic reception is foundational to the NT-resurrection-typology that follows.

*(iii)* **Esth 5:1** — *va-y'hi ba-yom ha-shlishi va-tilbash Esther malkhut* — *on the third day Esther put on her royal robes* — the moment of the queen's decisive intervention.

*(iv)* **Jonah 1:17 (MT 2:1)** — *va-y'hi Yonah bi-m'ei ha-dag shloshah yamim u-shloshah lailot* — *Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights* — the three-days-and-three-nights as cited at Matt 12:40 as the typological parallel to the resurrection.

*(v)* **Gen 31:22** (Laban learns of Jacob's flight on the third day); **Gen 34:25** (the third-day-of-circumcision-pain at Shechem); **Gen 40:20** (Pharaoh's birthday on the third day, the restoration of the cup-bearer and execution of the baker); **Gen 42:18** (Joseph releases his brothers from prison on the third day).

*(vi)* **Josh 2:16** (Rahab to the spies — *hide yourselves there three days*); **Josh 3:2** (the crossing of the Jordan after three days); **Judg 14:14** (Samson's riddle un-resolved on the third day); **2 Sam 1:2** (the Amalekite messenger comes to David on the third day); **1 Kgs 12:5, 12** (Rehoboam's third-day-response to the elders); **2 Kgs 20:5, 8** (Hezekiah's healing on the third day); **Ezra 8:32, 10:8-9**.

The wider distribution makes the *third-day* a stylized formulaic marker rather than a literal chronometric datum.

**The modern critical reading.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 152: the third-day is a literary-narrative convention. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 358: the *dritter Tag* is a stylized topos of the decisive-event narrative. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 107: the third day functions as the narrative-pause moment that intensifies the dramatic-tension. **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 110: the third-day is a *stylized device for the moment of decisive arrival*. The wider monographic literature on the *third-day* motif: **Goppelt** *Typos* 1939/1982 (the foundational typological-NT treatment); **B. M. Bokser** *The Origins of the Seder* 1984 ch. 3 (the Jewish-liturgical reception); **G. Vermes** *Scripture and Tradition in Judaism* 1973 on the rabbinic-targumic reception of the *aqedah* third-day.

**The NT-resurrection reception.** The early Christian tradition reads the third-day formula prefiguratively. The principal NT-occurrences: **1 Cor 15:4** (*kai hoti egēgertai tē hēmera tē tritē kata tas graphas* — *and that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures*); **Matt 12:40** (the Jonah-three-days-and-three-nights typology); **Luke 24:7, 21, 46** (the *third-day* as the explicit resurrection-day formula); **Acts 10:40**. The patristic-reception (Origen, Augustine, Tertullian) reads the Gen 22 *third-day* as the typological prefiguration of the resurrection (Yitzchaq's *bound-but-not-slain* on the third day as the typological parallel to the *crucified-but-raised* on the third day). The reception is documented in **R. Daly** *The Origins of the Christian Doctrine of Sacrifice* 1978 and **D. Lerch** *Isaaks Opferung christlich gedeutet* 1950. The reception-tradition is flagged-not-preempted by this entry; the WoH translation reads the third-day in its mainstream Hebrew-Bible literary-narrative-convention register, leaving the NT-typology as downstream-reception.

**The Jewish-liturgical reception.** The *Akedah* is read on Rosh Hashanah (the second day) in the Jewish liturgical year, with extensive *piyyutim* (liturgical poems) on the third-day theme; the *zechut avot* (merit of the fathers) tradition treats Avraham's third-day-arrival as the foundational moment of the merit-imputation that the Rosh Hashanah liturgy invokes (Spiegel 1967; Levenson 1993 chs 8-10). The Jewish reception is flagged-not-preempted; the WoH translation reads the third-day in its Hebrew-Bible literary register, leaving the liturgical reception as downstream.

**Translation.** ASV's *on the third day* is preserved. The formula is grammatically explicit and is rendered literally. The literary-narrative-convention status is carried in commentary.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *ba-yom ha-shlishi* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable; (c) the literary-narrative-convention reading is the modern critical default; (d) the NT-resurrection-typology and the Jewish-liturgical reception are documented downstream-receptions, flagged-not-preempted; (e) the WoH translation choice is ASV-baseline literal.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb, qal and niphal imperfect 3ms of r-'-h
terms.literal: he will see / he will be seen (or: appear)
terms.standardEnglish: will provide / shall be provided (ASV providential register); will see / shall be seen (literal r-'-h register)
terms.wohChoice: will provide / shall be provided (translation); will-see / will-be-seen (commentary apparatus carries the wordplay)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22 is structured around a sustained wordplay on the root *r-'-h* (to see).** The wordplay is the chapter's theological-narrative engine and unites the sight-and-providence motifs across the chapter's keystone moments.

**The five-fold *r-'-h* axis.**

*(i)* **v 4** — *va-yisa Avraham et-eynav va-yar et-ha-maqom me-rachoq* — *Avraham lifted up his eyes and saw the place from afar*. The qal active of *r-'-h*; Avraham as subject. The first *seeing*.

*(ii)* **v 8** — *Elohim yir'eh-lo ha-seh l-olah b'ni* — *Elohim will see for himself / provide for himself the lamb for a burnt-offering, my son*. The qal active 3ms imperfect of *r-'-h* with the *lamed* + reflexive — *will see for himself* / *will provide for himself*. The English *provide* (Latin *pro-videre*, *to see in advance*) accidentally preserves the etymological connection between *seeing* and *providing*, but modern English readers do not hear *see* in *provide*. The Hebrew preserves the seeing-equals-providing identity unambiguously.

*(iii)* **v 13** — *va-yisa Avraham et-eynav va-yar v-hineh-ayil achar ne'echaz ba-s'vakh b-qarnav* — *Avraham lifted up his eyes and saw, and behold, a ram caught in the thicket by his horns*. The qal active of *r-'-h*; Avraham as subject; parallel-formulation to v 4. The second *seeing*. The chapter's narrative-arc is structurally arc-of-Avraham-seeing: he sees the place (v 4), the deity will see (v 8), he sees the substitute (v 13).

*(iv)* **v 14a** — *va-yiqra Avraham shem-ha-maqom ha-hu YHWH yir'eh* — *Avraham called the name of that place YHWH-yir'eh* (*YHWH will see / YHWH will provide*). The qal active 3ms imperfect; YHWH as subject. The place-name preserves the active *seeing-and-providing* sense.

*(v)* **v 14b** — *asher ye'amer ha-yom b-har YHWH yera'eh* — *as it is said to this day, on the mountain of YHWH it shall be seen / he shall appear / it shall be provided*. The niphal 3ms imperfect of *r-'-h*; the subject is ambiguous (*it* — the providence? *he* — YHWH himself in theophany?). The passive / mediopassive form opens to multiple readings (cf. the *va-yera YHWH* theophany-formula at Gen 12:7 et passim — see [va-yera-yhwh-theophany](#va-yera-yhwh-theophany) for the niphal of *r-'-h* as theophany-formula).

**The active/passive contrast.** The chapter's wordplay-axis pivots on the active/passive contrast: at v 8 and v 14a the verb is qal active (*yir'eh*, *will see*), with the deity as subject; at v 14b the verb is niphal passive/mediopassive (*yera'eh*, *will be seen* / *will appear*), with the place / the deity as ambiguous subject. The shift from active to passive enacts the chapter's theological move: the deity who *sees-and-provides* (v 8) is the same deity who *will be seen / will appear* at the place (v 14b). The seeing-and-being-seen identity is the theological engine — the place becomes a *seeing-place* because the deity who sees was seen there.

**The translation problem.** Modern English does not preserve the *see-equals-provide* identity that Hebrew *r-'-h* carries. Three translation strategies circulate:

*(a) ASV's *provide* / *be provided*.* Preserves the providential register; loses the literal *seeing* sense. The ASV/KJV tradition (KJV at v 14: *Jehovah-jireh ... In the mount of the LORD it shall be seen*) is internally inconsistent — KJV preserves *seen* at v 14b but uses *provide* at v 8.

*(b) Literal *see / be seen* throughout.* Preserves the wordplay-axis; loses the providential-narrative sense in modern English. The reader must reconstruct the *seeing-equals-providing* identity from the commentary.

*(c) Combined: *see-for-himself* / *will appear*.* Preserves both senses by literal-equivalent translation. Editorially defensible but creates English awkwardness.

The WoH translation adopts strategy (a) — ASV's *provide* / *be provided* — for translation register, with the literal wordplay-axis carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary. The choice is consistent with the WoH practice of preserving the ASV baseline at translation level while carrying philological apparatus separately. The wordplay-axis is one of the most-cited Hebrew Bible wordplay-features and is documented across the scholarly literature.

**Scholarly literature.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 152-153 — the *r-'-h* wordplay as *the chapter's central structural device*. **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 110-111 — the *seeing-and-being-seen* identity as theological-narrative engine. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 360-364 — the *Schauen* and *Erscheinen* contrast. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 108-110 — the wordplay as deliberate compositional device. **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 109-114 — the *r-'-h* axis as the chapter's structural-poetic device. **G. W. Coats** *Genesis: With an Introduction to Narrative Literature* 1983 on the *aqedah*'s narrative-poetic structure.

**Cross-corpus.** The active/passive *r-'-h* alternation recurs elsewhere as theophany-formula: *va-yera YHWH el-Avram* (Gen 12:7) and the patriarchal-theophany cluster (see [va-yera-yhwh-theophany](#va-yera-yhwh-theophany)). The combined *seeing-and-providing* sense of *r-'-h* with reflexive *lamed* is documented at 1 Sam 16:1 (Samuel sent to Bethlehem — *ra'iti b-vanav li melekh* — *I have seen / provided for myself a king among his sons*). The seeing-and-being-seen pattern is wider HB pattern (Ps 27:13; Job 19:25-27 — the famous *I shall see God*); the chapter's intensive use is its distinctive contribution.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the five-fold *r-'-h* axis is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the active/passive contrast at vv 8 + 14 is a feature of the pointed MT; (c) the wordplay-axis is the modern critical consensus and is documented in the cited literature; (d) the WoH translation choice (ASV-baseline *provide* / *be provided* with apparatus carrying the literal *see / be seen*) is editorially defensible and consistent with WoH practice.

terms.literal: (large) eating-knife / slaughter-knife / cleaver
terms.standardEnglish: knife (ASV/NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: knife
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:6, 10 contain two of the only three Hebrew Bible occurrences of the lexeme *ma'akhelet*.** The chapter's twice-use is editorially significant: the *ma'akhelet* is the chapter's named instrument of the (non-completed) slaughter. The lexeme is marked as belonging to the slaughter-and-large-blade register, distinct from the ordinary domestic-knife vocabulary.

**The lexeme.** *Ma'akhelet* (מַאֲכֶלֶת) is morphologically a *mem*-preformative feminine noun from the root *a-k-l* (to eat, consume). The literal sense is *the eating-instrument* / *the consuming-instrument* — the noun-form indicating an instrument that *brings about eating* (in the sense of cutting flesh into edible portions). The HALOT entry (i.585) reads *grosses Messer, Schwert* — *large knife, sword*; BDB (p. 38) reads *knife (for slaughtering and cutting up)*; the wider lexicographical tradition treats the lexeme as denoting a large slaughter-knife or cleaver, distinguished from the ordinary domestic *sakkin* (the term used in Prov 23:2 and the wider rabbinic-Hebrew register).

**The three-fold cross-corpus distribution.**

*(i)* **Gen 22:6** — *va-yiqach b-yado et-ha-esh v-et-ha-ma'akhelet* — *and he took in his hand the fire and the knife* (the loading-of-the-implements moment).

*(ii)* **Gen 22:10** — *va-yiqach et-ha-ma'akhelet li-sh'chot et-b'no* — *and he took the knife to slay his son* (the chapter's narrative-climax moment; the verb *sh-ch-t* — *to slaughter, slay* — is itself the cultic-technical sacrificial-slaughter verb of Lev 1:5, 11; 3:2; 4:24; 14:13, etc.).

*(iii)* **Judg 19:29** — *va-yachzeq ba-pilegesh va-y'natchehah la-atzameha li-shneim-asar n'tachim va-y'shalcheha b-khol gevul Yisrael* — *and he laid hold of the concubine, and divided her, [piece] by piece into twelve pieces, and sent her into all the borders of Israel*. The Levite's dismembering-knife in the Gibeah-concubine narrative; the second narrative use of *ma'akhelet* in the HB. The parallel is editorially load-bearing — the *ma'akhelet* is the instrument of two of the HB's most morally-disturbing narratives: the (averted) sacrifice of Yitzchaq and the (completed) dismemberment of the Levite's concubine. The cross-reference is noted in **Sarna** 1989 p. 152 and **Trible** *Texts of Terror* 1984 ch. 3.

*(iv)* **Prov 30:14** — *dor charvot shinav u-ma'akhalot m'tal'otav* — *a generation whose teeth are as swords and their jaw-teeth as knives* — the metaphorical use in the *Words of Agur* wisdom-poem; the *ma'akhalot* (plural) used metaphorically of the predatory-elite who consume the poor. The metaphorical-pejorative register is consistent with the lexeme's slaughter-and-large-blade core.

The three-fold distribution makes the lexeme a marked HB term; the two narrative occurrences (Gen 22, Judg 19) both name moments of (potential or actual) human-victim slaughter, and the wisdom-poem (Prov 30) uses the lexeme metaphorically of social-violence. The lexeme is therefore consistently associated with violent-large-blade-use rather than with ordinary domestic-cutting.

**The chapter's editorial use.** The chapter's choice of *ma'akhelet* rather than the more ordinary *sakkin* / *chereb* (the standard knife / sword vocabulary) is editorially deliberate. The narrative marks the instrument as specialized-slaughter equipment; the reader is meant to feel the lexeme's marked character. **Sarna** 1989 p. 152: *the rare term ma'akhelet, the slaughtering-knife, intensifies the dramatic horror*. **Wenham** 1994 p. 109: *the unusual word choice ma'akhelet suggests a specialized sacrificial implement*. **Alter** 2004 p. 110: *the rare slaughter-knife*. **Hamilton** 1995 p. 110: *the term ma'akhelet (knife) appears only here in the Pentateuch... the term often refers to a larger knife or even a sword*.

**Translation.** ASV's *knife* is preserved as the WoH baseline. The lexeme's marked-rare character is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary; the English *knife* is the standard lexical equivalent. NJPS and NRSV also read *knife*. A more specialized rendering (*cleaver*, *slaughter-knife*) is editorially defensible but creates a heightened-register effect the ASV-baseline avoids; the WoH choice preserves the ordinary English equivalent.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 152; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 109; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 110; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, p. 110; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 359; HALOT i.585; BDB p. 38; Trible *Texts of Terror* 1984 ch. 3.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *ma'akhelet* is grammatically explicit at the cited verses; (b) the three-fold cross-corpus distribution is verifiable; (c) the slaughter-and-large-blade register is documented in the cited lexicographical and commentary literature; (d) the WoH translation choice (*knife*) matches ASV and the broader English-translation tradition.

terms.literal: and he bound
terms.standardEnglish: bound (ASV/NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: bound
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:9 contains the only Pentateuchal occurrence of the verb *aqad* (to bind, truss) in the qal.** *Va-ya'aqod et-Yitzchaq b'no va-yasem oto al-ha-mizbeach mi-ma'al la-etzim* — *and he bound Yitzchaq his son, and laid him on the altar, upon the wood*. The verb is the chapter's name-giver in the post-biblical Jewish tradition: the entire pericope is called *Aqedat Yitzchaq* — *the Binding of Yitzchaq* — from this verb. The chapter's most famous moment is captured in vocabulary that occurs nowhere else in the Pentateuch.

**The lexeme.** *Aqad* (עָקַד) is the qal verb *to bind, truss, tie-up*. The morphologically-related noun *aqudim* (עֲקֻדִּים) — *striped, ringed, marked-with-bands* — recurs in the Hebrew Bible at **Gen 30:35, 39, 40** and **Gen 31:8, 10, 12** (Jacob's flock-breeding narrative with the *aqudim*, *nequdim*, *brudim* tri-color terms). The noun and the verb share the *q-d* binding-and-marking semantic core: to bind feet together produces visual-marking patterns on the bound animal; the *aqudim* are *bound-marked* animals (i.e., with the legs marked with circular-bands or stripes). The semantic-core is the binding-with-resulting-marking pattern.

**The qal verb's pentateuchal-hapax status.** The qal of *aqad* recurs nowhere else in the Pentateuch. The verb is a *true Pentateuchal hapax* — the unique-occurrence in the Torah of an otherwise wider-attested root. The root recurs (with the noun-form *aqudim*) in the Gen 30-31 Jacob-narrative as the morphologically-related but semantically-distinct *striped* sense; the qal verbal sense *to bind* is unique to Gen 22:9. Outside the Pentateuch, the verbal sense is not securely attested in biblical Hebrew (the root is wider-attested in post-biblical and Mishnaic Hebrew, where the verbal sense *to bind, truss* is standard; cf. **Mishnah Tamid 4:1** on the binding of the daily *tamid*-offering as *aqidah* — explicitly invoking the Gen 22 lexeme).

**The rabbinic-tradition reception.** The verb's uniqueness is editorially load-bearing in the rabbinic reception. The pericope is named *aqedat Yitzchaq* (*the binding of Yitzchaq*) from this verb (e.g., **Genesis Rabbah 56**; **b. Rosh Hashanah 16a, 26a**; **Mishnah Tamid 4:1** explicitly compares the daily *tamid*-offering's binding-procedure to the Gen 22 *aqedah*). The chapter's entire reception-history (the *zechut avot*, the *zechut aqedat Yitzchaq*, the *aqedah* as Rosh Hashanah liturgical centerpiece, the *Akedah piyyutim*) is built around the lexeme. **S. Spiegel** *The Last Trial* (1967) — the foundational survey of the rabbinic *aqedah*-tradition — takes the verb's uniqueness as the entry-point for the entire reception-tradition's development.

**The technical-specialized sense.** Modern commentators read the *aqad* verb as a technical-specialized sacrificial-binding term. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153: *the verb 'aqad', used here only, suggests a special technique reserved for sacrificial animals — the feet bound together in a particular way*. **Mishnah Tamid 4:1** describes the daily *tamid*-offering's binding-procedure: the front-right and rear-left legs bound together (forming the *aqedah*), the head-and-throat exposed for the *shechita* (slaughter-cut). The Mishnah explicitly cites Gen 22:9 as the precedent. **Levenson** 1993 ch. 8 reads the rabbinic identification of the daily *tamid* with the Avraham-Yitzchaq *aqedah* as the foundation of the *zechut aqedat Yitzchaq* (the *aqedah*-merit) Yom-Kippur-and-Rosh-Hashanah liturgical tradition. The lexeme's uniqueness is the philological-base of this entire reception-tradition. **Wenham** 1994 p. 109; **Hamilton** 1995 p. 110.

**Cross-corpus.** Outside the noun-form *aqudim* of Gen 30-31, the root does not recur in biblical Hebrew. The verb's wider Semitic cognate-distribution: Akkadian *eqēdu* (to bind, attached objects), Arabic *ʿaqada* (to bind, knot, contract), Syriac *ʿqad* (to bind, knot). The wider Semitic distribution supports the binding-and-knotting core-semantic.

**Translation.** ASV's *bound* is preserved. The lexeme's pentateuchal-hapax status is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary; the English *bound* is the standard lexical equivalent. A more specialized rendering (*bound-up*, *trussed*) is editorially defensible — *trussed* preserves the technical-binding register of the bound-feet sacrificial-procedure — but is rejected for ASV-baseline consistency. The WoH translation reads *bound*; the apparatus carries the specialized-sacrificial-binding sense.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 359; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 109; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 110; Spiegel *The Last Trial* 1967; Levenson *The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son* 1993 ch. 8; HALOT ii.873 on the *q-d* root; Mishnah Tamid 4:1; Genesis Rabbah 56; b. Rosh Hashanah 16a, 26a.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *aqad* in the qal is grammatically explicit at Gen 22:9; (b) the pentateuchal-hapax status of the qal verbal form is a textual fact verifiable by lexical search; (c) the morphological-relationship to the noun *aqudim* of Gen 30-31 is documentary; (d) the rabbinic-tradition reception is documented in the cited literature; (e) the WoH translation choice (*bound*) matches ASV and the broader English-translation tradition.

terms.literal: Avraham, Avraham
terms.standardEnglish: Abraham, Abraham (ASV/NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: Avraham, Avraham
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:11 contains the chapter's pivot-moment doubled vocative:** *va-yiqra elav mal'akh YHWH min-ha-shamayim va-yomer Avraham Avraham va-yomer hineni* — *and the messenger of YHWH called to him out of the heavens and said, Avraham, Avraham. And he said, Here am I*. The doubled-name vocative is one of the Hebrew Bible's most editorially-marked formulas — it occurs at decisive theophanic/prophetic moments, marking the call as the foundational-vocational-call of the named figure.

**The four-fold canonical pattern.** The doubled-name divine vocation-call recurs at exactly four moments in the Hebrew Bible, naming the four foundational vocational-calls of the canonical narrative:

*(i)* **Gen 22:11** — *Avraham Avraham* (this chapter; the call that stays the hand; the climax of the *aqedah*).

*(ii)* **Gen 46:2** — *Ya'aqov Ya'aqov* (the night-vision at Be'er Sheva before the descent to Egypt — *va-yomer Elohim l-Yisrael b-mar'ot ha-laylah va-yomer Ya'aqov Ya'aqov va-yomer hineni* — the parallel-formulation, including the *hineni* response).

*(iii)* **Exod 3:4** — *Moshe Moshe* (the burning bush — *va-yiqra elav Elohim mi-tokh ha-s'neh va-yomer Moshe Moshe va-yomer hineni* — the parallel-formulation in precise lexical parallel to Gen 22:11 and Gen 46:2: *va-yiqra elav* + doubled name + *hineni*).

*(iv)* **1 Sam 3:10** — *Sh'mu'el Sh'mu'el* (the call of the prophet — *va-yavo YHWH va-yityatzev va-yiqra k-fa'am-b-fa'am Sh'mu'el Sh'mu'el va-yomer Sh'mu'el daber ki shomea avdekha* — the same *va-yiqra* + doubled name pattern; the response varies, but the formula's structural-parallel is unmistakable).

The four-fold pattern names the four foundational prophetic-vocational figures of the Pentateuch and the Former Prophets: Avraham (the patriarchal foundation), Ya'aqov (the Israel-name foundation), Moshe (the exodus foundation), Sh'mu'el (the monarchy-transition foundation). The pattern is editorially deliberate; the doubled-name marks the four canonical figures in whose vocation-narratives the entire Hebrew-Bible covenantal-history is rooted.

**The wider NT-and-rabbinic distribution.** The pattern recurs in the New Testament at **Acts 9:4 / 22:7 / 26:14** — *Saoul Saoul* (the Damascus-road call of Paul, in three Lukan parallel-formulations); **Luke 10:41** — *Martha Martha*; **Luke 22:31** — *Simon Simon*; **Luke 13:34 / Matt 23:37** — *Ierousalēm Ierousalēm*. The Lukan-corpus instances are consistent with the OT pattern (decisive-divine-call to the named figure or place); the doubled-vocative is one of the most editorially marked NT-features and is read by the NT-scholarship (Bovon *Luke* Hermeneia 2002 ad loc.; Marshall *NIGTC Luke* 1978) as deliberate-stylistic recall of the OT pattern.

**The rabbinic-tradition reading.** **Genesis Rabbah 56:7** reads the doubled-vocative *Avraham Avraham* as *l'shon chibah u-l'shon zeruz* — *an expression of affection and an expression of urgency*. The doubled call combines tenderness (the affective register; the deity calling by name as one calls a beloved child) with urgency (the imperative register; the call that demands immediate response). The rabbinic reading is preserved in **Rashi** ad loc. and in the wider midrashic tradition.

**Modern critical literature.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153: *the doubling of the name is a Hebrew Bible feature for moments of urgency and tenderness*. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 360: *the doubled name signals special-call*. **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 112: *the doubling of the name is found elsewhere only at Gen 46:2, Exod 3:4, 1 Sam 3:10 — each a moment of foundational vocation*. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110: *the doubled vocative marks the moment of revelation*. **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 110-111: *the doubled name marks the decisive vocational-call*. The wider monographic literature: **W. Brueggemann** *Genesis* 1982 on the *prophetic-call type-scene*; **N. Habel** *The Form and Significance of the Call Narratives* (ZAW 1965) — the foundational form-critical study of the call-narrative pattern.

**Cross-corpus and reception.** The doubled-name formula is editorially marked by the Masoretic *paseq* (the vertical-stroke separator that visually marks the doubled vocative at MT Gen 22:11 — *Avraham | Avraham* — the *paseq* itself indicating the textual-tradition's recognition of the doubling as a marked feature). The *paseq*-marking is documented in **Yeivin** *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980 §150 and in the standard Masoretic apparatus. The Masoretic preservation supports the modern critical reading of the doubling as editorially-deliberate.

**Translation.** The doubled vocative is grammatically explicit; no translation choice is required at the language-level. ASV's *Abraham, Abraham* is preserved with the WoH transliteration *Avraham, Avraham*. The doubled vocative's marked-canonical-call status is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, p. 360; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 112; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 110-111; Brueggemann *Genesis* 1982; Habel ZAW 1965; Genesis Rabbah 56:7; Yeivin *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the doubled vocative is grammatically explicit at the four cited verses; (b) the canonical-vocation pattern is verifiable by lexical-narrative search; (c) the rabbinic-tradition reading is documented; (d) the modern critical convergence is documented in the cited literature; (e) the WoH translation choice preserves the doubled vocative literally.

terms.literal: behold-me / here-I-am
terms.standardEnglish: Here am I (ASV); Here I am (NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: Here am I
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22 contains a threefold *hineni* structuring the chapter's three calls.** The *hineni* response — *behold-me / here-I-am* — is the Hebrew Bible's prophetic-response-formula, the standard reply of the called figure to a vocational-call. Gen 22's threefold use is one of the chapter's most editorially-marked structural devices.

**The chapter's threefold *hineni*.**

*(i)* **v 1** — to Elohim's initial call (*va-yomer elav Avraham va-yomer hineni* — *and he said to him, Avraham, and he said, Here am I*). The first *hineni* — the response to the deity's call that opens the test.

*(ii)* **v 7** — to Yitzchaq's call (*va-yomer Avi va-yomer hineni v'ni* — *and he said, my father, and he said, Here am I, my son*). The middle *hineni* — the response to the son's call, the moment of greatest dramatic-tension (Yitzchaq does not yet know what is happening; Avraham's *hineni* to the son is the most affectively-weighted moment in the chapter).

*(iii)* **v 11** — to the messenger's call (*va-yiqra elav mal'akh YHWH min-ha-shamayim va-yomer Avraham Avraham va-yomer hineni* — *and the messenger of YHWH called to him from the heavens and said, Avraham Avraham. And he said, Here am I*). The third *hineni* — the response to the messenger's call that stays the hand and concludes the test.

The threefold-pattern is editorially-deliberate. **Sarna** 1989 p. 150: *the threefold hineni gives the chapter its structural-rhythmic backbone*. **Alter** 2004 p. 109: *the three hineni-responses, each in a different vocative direction, anchor the chapter's three-fold structure: the test commanded, the son's anguished question, the staying of the hand*. **Wenham** 1994 p. 105: *the three responses mark the chapter's three movements*.

**The cross-corpus prophetic-vocation distribution.** The *hineni* response-formula recurs across the Hebrew Bible as the standard reply of the called figure to a vocational-call. The cluster includes the foundational prophetic-vocation calls:

*(i)* **Gen 27:1, 18** — Yitzchaq to the (presumed) Esav at the blessing-scene; the parental-vocational *hineni*.

*(ii)* **Gen 31:11** — Ya'aqov to the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in the dream at Paddan-Aram: *va-yomer elai mal'akh ha-Elohim ba-chalom Ya'aqov va-omar hineni*.

*(iii)* **Gen 37:13** — Yosef to Ya'aqov's call to go to the brothers: *va-yomer lo hineni*.

*(iv)* **Gen 46:2** — Ya'aqov at Be'er Sheva (the parallel to Gen 22:11): *va-yomer hineni*.

*(v)* **Exod 3:4** — Moshe at the burning bush (the parallel to Gen 22:11): *va-yomer hineni*.

*(vi)* **1 Sam 3:4, 6, 8, 16** — Sh'mu'el's four-fold *hineni* (the prophet-call at Shiloh; the first three *hineni*-responses are to YHWH whom Sh'mu'el mistakes for Eli, the fourth is to Eli explicitly).

*(vii)* **2 Sam 1:7** — the Amalekite to David: *va-omar hineni*.

*(viii)* **Isa 6:8** — the prophet's iconic call-response: *va-eshma et-qol Adonai omer et-mi eshlach u-mi yelekh-lanu va-omar hineni sh'lacheni* — *and I heard the voice of the Lord saying, whom shall I send, and who will go for us? And I said, Here am I, send me*. The classic prophetic-vocational *hineni*; Isaiah's response is the most-cited NT-and-rabbinic instance of the formula.

The wider distribution makes *hineni* the canonical Hebrew Bible prophetic-response-formula. **Sarna** 1989; **Westermann** 1985 pp. 358-360; **Hamilton** 1995 pp. 105-112; **Wenham** 1994 p. 105; **Brueggemann** *Genesis* 1982; **Habel** ZAW 1965; **Greene** *The Role of the Messenger and Message in the Ancient Near East* 1989. The formula is also the foundational element of the **prophetic call type-scene** (Habel 1965; Long *The Problem of Etiological Narrative* 1968), the form-critical category that names the call-narratives of the Hebrew Bible: Moshe (Exod 3-4), Gideon (Judg 6), Sh'mu'el (1 Sam 3), Sha'ul (1 Sam 9-10), Yirm'yahu (Jer 1), Yechezkel (Ezek 1-3), Y'sha'yahu (Isa 6).

**Translation.** ASV's *Here am I* is preserved as the WoH baseline. The archaic word-order (*Here am I* rather than *Here I am*) preserves the demonstrative-emphatic register of the Hebrew *hineh* + 1cs suffix; NJPS and NRSV's *Here I am* smooths to modern English natural-order but loses the demonstrative-emphasis. The literal *Behold me* is most-explicit on the *hineh* demonstrative-particle but is too archaic for translation-register. The WoH choice preserves the ASV-archaic-emphatic order.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the threefold *hineni* of Gen 22 is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus prophetic-vocational distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the call-narrative-pattern is the modern form-critical consensus; (d) the WoH translation choice preserves ASV-baseline.

terms.literal: now I (have) known
terms.standardEnglish: now I know (ASV/NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: now I know
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:12 contains the chapter's most theologically-contested phrase:** *al-tishlach yadkha el-ha-na'ar v-al-ta'as lo m'umah ki atah yadati ki-yere Elohim atah v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidkha mimeni* — *do not lay your hand upon the lad, neither do anything to him, for now I know that you fear Elohim, seeing you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me*. The phrase *atah yadati* — *now I know* — generates the classical theological-problem of divine testing: if the deity is omniscient, what does the test produce? The crux is one of the most-discussed in the entire Hebrew Bible reception-history.

**The lexeme and the grammatical fact.** *Atah* is the adverb *now*; *yadati* is the qal perfect 1cs of *y-d-a* (to know). The grammatical sense is unambiguous: *now I have known* / *now I know* — the perfect verb expressing a completed act-of-knowing that is now present. The verse is grammatically explicit; the translation requires no choice at the language-level. The theological-problem is generated by the *content* of the verse, not by the translation.

**The four principal scholarly positions on the testing-and-knowing problem.**

*(a) Maimonidean / pedagogical reading.* **Maimonides** *Guide of the Perplexed* III.24 reads the test as pedagogical-demonstration: the test is *l-hodi'a* — *to make known* — to humanity (not to the deity) the limit of Avraham's love-and-fear of the deity. The *atah yadati* is anthropomorphic-narrative idiom; the deity already-knew, and the *atah yadati* names the disclosure-to-the-world of what the deity already knew. The position is the foundational Jewish-philosophical resolution of the divine-foreknowledge-and-test problem. **Aquinas** *ST* I q.14 a.13 reaches a parallel resolution: divine foreknowledge is non-temporal and does not exclude human freedom; the test discloses what was always known to the deity in its eternal mode of knowing.

*(b) Patristic / disclosure reading.* **Augustine** *De Genesi ad Litteram* and **Origen** *Homilies on Genesis* VIII read the test as disclosing the latent disposition in publicly-visible form. The disposition was always-present in Avraham; the test makes it manifest. The reading is theologically-conservative on divine omniscience and is the dominant patristic and Eastern-Orthodox resolution.

*(c) Open-theism / real-epistemic reading.* The modern philosophical-theology position of *open theism* (Pinnock *The Openness of God* 1994; Sanders *The God Who Risks* 1998; Boyd *God of the Possible* 2000) reads the test as genuinely producing new divine knowledge. The deity does not know in advance what Avraham will do. *Atah yadati* is read literally — the *now* and the *I have come to know* are taken at face value as a temporal-epistemic event. Some Process-theology readings (Cobb-Griffin *Process Theology* 1976; Hartshorne *Omnipotence and Other Theological Mistakes* 1984) reach a parallel conclusion through different metaphysics. The position is a minority in the wider theological literature but is documentary in modern philosophical-theology.

*(d) Narrative-anthropomorphism reading (modern critical default).* **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 361-362; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 113; **Brueggemann** *Genesis* (Interpretation 1982), p. 192: the *atah yadati* is narrative-anthropomorphism; the text presents the deity in human-narrative register and the reader is not asked to reconcile the narrative's anthropomorphism with a metaphysical doctrine of omniscience. The chapter is a *story* in the strict literary sense, and the story requires the test-and-discover narrative-grammar. Westermann: *the verse speaks of divine knowing in the only way the narrative can — as a temporal-epistemic event within the story; the metaphysical question is not the narrative's concern*. The position is the modern critical default and is the best-supported reading at the literary-narrative level.

**The wider scholarly literature.** **W. Brueggemann** *Genesis* (Interpretation 1982), pp. 187-194 — *the verse is the theological engine of the chapter; the disclosure-of-relationship rather than the disclosure-of-information is the verse's point*. **J. D. Levenson** *The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son* 1993, pp. 125-142 — the *atah yadati* as the chapter's *christological-typological* engine in the patristic reception (Yitzchaq's not-being-slain as the type of the not-yet-slain Beloved Son; the *atah yadati* as the deity's verdict-on-Avraham as the typological prefiguration of the verdict-on-Christ). **G. von Rad** *Genesis* (OTL 1972), pp. 244-245 — *the divine knowledge in this verse is relational-knowing, not informational-knowing*. **A. Heschel** *The Prophets* 1962 ch. 13 — the *pathos* of the deity as the theological framework within which divine *learning* is intelligible; the deity's emotional-relational-engagement with the human partner is constitutive of divine experience.

**The Kierkegaardian existential reading.** **S. Kierkegaard** *Fear and Trembling* (1843) addresses the *aqedah* as the locus classicus of the *teleological suspension of the ethical*. The reading is not directly about the *atah yadati* problem (Kierkegaard's focus is the *binding of the ethical universal under the religious individual*), but it is the most-cited philosophical-reception of the chapter and is flagged as downstream-not-preempted by this entry. The wider continental-philosophical reception (Levinas, Derrida *The Gift of Death* 1995, Caputo) takes the *aqedah* as the foundational scene of the *gift-against-economy* and the *responsibility-to-the-other*; the reception is rich and is noted as downstream.

**The Islamic reception.** The Quranic version (Sura 37:101-113) does not contain the *atah yadati* formula; the Quranic narrative's emphasis is on the obedience-and-submission of the patriarch and the son (the son explicitly consents in the Quranic version), with the deity's verdict expressed not as *now I know* but as *you have confirmed the vision* (Sura 37:105). The Quranic reception's emphasis on the *vision* (the *ru'ya*) parallels the Hebrew chapter's *r-'-h* wordplay-axis (see [yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay](#yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay)); the reception is noted as downstream-not-preempted.

**Translation.** ASV's *now I know* is preserved. The phrase is grammatically explicit; no translation choice is required. The theological-crux is reception-material, not translation-material. The WoH choice preserves the literal phrase and lets the apparatus carry the four scholarly positions on the testing-and-knowing problem.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 361-362; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 113; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, p. 164; Brueggemann *Genesis* 1982, pp. 187-194; Levenson *The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son* 1993; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972, pp. 244-245; Heschel *The Prophets* 1962; Maimonides *Guide* III.24; Aquinas *ST* I q.14, q.22, II-II q.97; Pinnock 1994; Sanders 1998; Boyd 2000; Kierkegaard *Fear and Trembling* 1843; Derrida *The Gift of Death* 1995.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the phrase *atah yadati* is grammatically explicit; (b) the four scholarly positions on the testing-and-knowing problem are documented in the cited literature; (c) the WoH translation choice preserves the literal phrase; (d) the theological-crux is reception-material, presented in parallel without preempting any of the four positions. Project-distinctive WoH synthesis on the testing-and-knowing problem is reserved for the wiki and long-form material downstream.

terms.literal: a ram behind / a ram after
terms.standardEnglish: a ram behind him (ASV); a ram, after this / afterward (alternative); a ram caught (NJPS smoothing); one ram (LXX/Syriac variant)
terms.wohChoice: a ram behind him (MT-fidelity)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:13 contains the chapter's substitution-pivot:** *va-yisa Avraham et-eynav va-yar v-hineh ayil achar ne'echaz ba-s'vakh b-qarnav va-yelekh Avraham va-yiqach et-ha-ayil va-ya'alehu l-olah tachat b'no* — *and Avraham lifted up his eyes and saw, and behold, a ram behind him caught in the thicket by his horns; and Avraham went and took the ram and offered him up for a burnt-offering in the stead of his son*. The verse contains two distinct philological cruxes: the *ayil* (ram) lexeme as substitution-pivot, and the textually-contested *achar* (behind / after / another).

**The substitution-pivot.** The verse is the chapter's narrative-climax: the (averted) human-sacrifice is replaced by an animal-substitution. The *ayil* (ram) — the standard HB term for the male sheep, ritually-significant as one of the principal *olah*-class sacrificial animals (per Lev 1:10 *im min-ha-tzon korbano min-ha-k'savim o min-ha-izim l-olah* — *if his offering is from the flock, from the sheep or from the goats, for a burnt-offering*). The *ayil* is the standard *olah*-animal of the wider HB sacrificial system (Lev 5:15, 18; 6:6; 9:2-4; 16:3-5 — the Yom Kippur ritual with two rams; 23:18; Num 5-7, 15, 23, 28-29; Ezek 43-46; Ezra 8:35, 10:19). The chapter's substitution of *ayil* for the human victim is therefore not a generic-animal substitution but an *olah*-class substitution: the same sacrificial-class is preserved across the substitution-pivot.

**The substitutionary-sacrifice theology.** The substitution-pivot is the foundation of the rabbinic *zecher aqedat Yitzchaq* (*memorial of the binding of Yitzchaq*) tradition and the wider *zechut avot* (*merit of the fathers*) tradition. The rabbinic reading (Genesis Rabbah 56:9; b. Rosh Hashanah 16a) treats the ram as the substitutionary-sacrifice that establishes the perpetual-merit of Avraham's faithfulness; the *shofar* (ram's-horn) of the Rosh Hashanah liturgy is connected to the *qarnei ha-ayil* (the ram's horns) of Gen 22:13 (the *shofar*-blast on Rosh Hashanah invokes the *aqedah*-merit). The reception-tradition is documented in **Spiegel** *The Last Trial* 1967 chs 3-5; **Levenson** *The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son* 1993 chs 7-10; **Vermes** *Scripture and Tradition in Judaism* 1973 on the *Aqedah* in the targumic and rabbinic literature.

**The Christian vicarious-atonement reception.** The patristic, medieval, and Reformation Christian tradition reads the substitution-pivot as the typological foundation of the doctrine of vicarious atonement: the ram substitutes for Yitzchaq as Christ substitutes for humanity. The reception is foundational from **Hebrews 11:17-19** onward (the *aqedah* as the type of the *not-finally-slain* Beloved Son, *logisamenos hoti kai ek nekrōn egeirein dynatos ho theos* — *reasoning that God is able to raise even from the dead*); through **Origen** *Homilies on Genesis* VIII; **Augustine** *Sermons*; the medieval Glossa Ordinaria; the Reformation atonement-theology (Luther, Calvin, the wider Protestant tradition). The reception is documented in **R. Daly** *The Origins of the Christian Doctrine of Sacrifice* 1978; **D. Lerch** *Isaaks Opferung christlich gedeutet* 1950; **Levenson** 1993. The reception is flagged-not-preempted by this entry; the WoH translation reads the substitution-pivot in its mainstream Hebrew-Bible cultic-substitution register, leaving the Christian-typological reception as downstream.

**The Islamic reception.** The Quranic version (Sura 37:107) names the substitution as *dhibhin azim* (*a mighty sacrifice*); the early Islamic tafsir tradition treats the ram as a special-divine-provision (in some traditions, the same ram that Habil/Abel offered at the foundational Genesis 4 sacrifice). The reception is noted as downstream-not-preempted.

**The textual variant *achar* / *echad*.** The MT reads *achar* (אַחַר) — *behind* / *after* — supported by the **Samaritan Pentateuch** and by **Onkelos** (*targum*: *batar* — *after / behind*). A significant variant-tradition reads *echad* (אֶחָד) — *one* — supported by the **LXX** (*kai idou krios heis* — *and behold, one ram*), the **Peshitta** (Syriac: *chad* — *one*), and the **Vulgate** (*unum arietem* — *one ram*; though the Vulgate also reflects *post tergum suum* — *behind his back* — in some manuscript traditions). The variant rests on the consonantal confusion *resh* / *dalet* (ר/ד) — the two letters are visually similar in the Hebrew square-script and are a frequent textual-variant locus across the HB textual tradition.

**The modern critical positions on the variant.**

*(a) MT *achar* original.* **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, p. 113-114: the MT *achar* (*behind*) is the lectio difficilior and is preserved as original. The variant *echad* (*one*) is read as a scribal-smoothing toward the more-expected enumeration. The position is the modern critical default outside the LXX-philological-reconstruction school.

*(b) LXX *echad* original.* **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964, p. 163; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 360-361 (with cautious endorsement): the LXX-Syriac *echad* (*one*) is the original reading; the MT *achar* (*behind*) is a scribal-emendation. The position is held by the LXX-philological-reconstruction school and is documented in the textual-critical literature (Tov *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 2012, pp. 235-236; Würthwein *The Text of the Old Testament* 1995).

*(c) Unresolvable variant.* **Skinner** *ICC Genesis* 1910, p. 331; **Driver** *The Book of Genesis* 1904, p. 219: the variant is unresolvable on textual-critical grounds; both readings are defensible.

**The WoH choice.** The WoH translation preserves the MT *achar* (*behind*), consistent with the established WoH textual-conservative practice (see [al-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant](#al-roi-and-halom-elohim-variant) for the parallel-policy on the Gen 16:13 MT-preservation; the wider WoH policy is MT-fidelity with variants noted in apparatus). The English *behind him* preserves the locative-spatial sense; the textual-variant *echad* (*one*) is noted in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 153; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 360-361; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, p. 110; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 113-114; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, p. 163; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, p. 331; Driver *The Book of Genesis* 1904, p. 219; Tov *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 2012; Würthwein 1995; Spiegel 1967; Levenson 1993; Daly 1978; Lerch 1950; Vermes 1973.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *ayil* and *achar* are grammatically explicit; (b) the substitution-pivot is the chapter's narrative-fact; (c) the *resh / dalet* textual variant is documented in the textual-critical literature; (d) the modern critical positions on the variant are documented; (e) the WoH translation choice (MT *achar* preserved) is consistent with established WoH textual-conservative practice; (f) the rabbinic, Christian, and Islamic reception-traditions are documented and flagged-not-preempted.

terms.partOfSpeech: divine name + verb (qal active / niphal passive)
terms.literal: YHWH-will-see / on-the-mountain-of-YHWH-it-shall-be-seen
terms.standardEnglish: Jehovah-jireh ... in the mount of Jehovah it shall be provided (ASV/KJV); Adonai-yireh ... on the mount of the LORD there is vision (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: YHWH-yir'eh ... on the mountain of YHWH it shall be provided
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:14 names the place of the *aqedah* and preserves the chapter's r-'-h wordplay-axis:** *va-yiqra Avraham shem-ha-maqom ha-hu YHWH yir'eh asher ye'amer ha-yom b-har YHWH yera'eh* — *and Avraham called the name of that place YHWH-yir'eh; as it is said to this day, on the mountain of YHWH it shall be seen / he shall appear / it shall be provided*. The verse is the chapter's etiological-closing-formula and unites the place-name with the chapter's wordplay-engine.

**The place-name *YHWH-yir'eh*.** The verbal element *yir'eh* is the qal active 3ms imperfect of *r-'-h* (to see) — *YHWH will see / YHWH will provide*. The active sense unifies *seeing* and *providing*: the deity who *sees* the situation *provides for* it. The place-name preserves the active-providential register from v 8 (*Elohim yir'eh-lo ha-seh* — *Elohim will see for himself / provide for himself the lamb*); the v 14a place-name explicitly retains the same verb-form *yir'eh*, marking the linguistic-typological connection.

**The closing-formula *b-har YHWH yera'eh*.** The closing-formula introduces a key shift: *yera'eh* is the **niphal** 3ms imperfect of *r-'-h* — *it shall be seen* / *he shall appear* / *it shall be provided* (mediopassive). The niphal is the same form used in the patriarchal-theophany-formula *va-yera YHWH* (see [va-yera-yhwh-theophany](#va-yera-yhwh-theophany)), where YHWH *appears* / *is-seen* to the patriarchs. The shift from active *yir'eh* (v 14a) to passive *yera'eh* (v 14b) enacts the chapter's deepest theological move: the deity who *sees-and-provides* (active) is the same deity who *will-appear / will-be-seen* (passive) at the place. The seeing-and-being-seen identity is the engine of the chapter's wordplay-axis.

**The translation-choice.** The English language does not preserve the *see-equals-provide* identity that Hebrew *r-'-h* carries (see [yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay](#yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay)). The ASV/KJV tradition reads *Jehovah-jireh ... In the mount of the LORD it shall be provided / seen* (KJV mixes provided / seen; ASV preserves *provided* at both); NJPS reads *Adonai-yireh ... On the mount of the LORD there is vision* (preserving the vision-register at the close); NRSV reads *the LORD will provide ... On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided*. The WoH translation preserves the transliterated place-name *YHWH-yir'eh* and reads *it shall be provided* at the closing-formula, matching ASV-baseline with WoH-YHWH-preservation. The wordplay-axis is carried in this glossary entry and in [yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay](#yireh-yerah-hreh-wordplay) above.

**The temple-mount identification.** The place-name is connected by the Chronicler at **2 Chr 3:1** to the Jerusalem Temple-Mount (*be-har ha-Moriah asher nir'ah l-David aviv* — *on Mount Moriah, where [YHWH] had appeared to David his father*). The Chronicler explicitly reuses the same r-'-h root in the niphal (*nir'ah* — *appeared / was-seen*), marking the cross-reference between Gen 22:14b's *b-har YHWH yera'eh* and the David-and-Temple-Mount-appearance tradition (2 Sam 24:16-25; 1 Chr 21:15-30). The temple-mount identification is the foundation of the Jewish (Targum Pseudo-Jonathan to Gen 22:14 explicitly identifies *har ha-Moriah* with the Temple-Mount) and the wider rabbinic tradition. The Christian and Islamic traditions also engage the identification (see [eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification](#eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification) for the wider scholarly-and-receptional debate).

**The etiological-formula *asher ye'amer ha-yom*.** The phrase *asher ye'amer ha-yom* — *as it is said to this day* — is a Hebrew Bible etiological-formula marking the place-or-saying as preserved in the narrator's contemporary tradition. The formula recurs at Gen 22:14; 1 Sam 5:5; 2 Sam 18:18; etc. The narrator marks the place-name as a continuing-tradition in the narrator's time, providing a temporal-stratification clue for the redactional dating of the verse. **Sarna** 1989 p. 154 on the etiological-formula; **Westermann** 1985 pp. 363-364; **Hamilton** 1995 pp. 113-114.

**The Samaritan reading.** The Samaritan Pentateuch preserves the place-name *YHWH-yir'eh* at v 14a and reads *b-har Elohim* at v 14b (with *Elohim* for *YHWH*). The Samaritan tradition identifies the binding-site with Mount Gerizim (per [eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification](#eretz-ha-moriah-temple-mount-identification)). The Samaritan reading is the variant; the MT is preserved as base-text.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, pp. 153-154; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 363-364; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 110-111; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 113-114; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, pp. 163-164; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004, pp. 110-111; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910, pp. 331-332; Spiegel 1967; Levenson 1993.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the place-name *YHWH-yir'eh* and the closing-formula *b-har YHWH yera'eh* are grammatically explicit; (b) the active/passive contrast of *yir'eh / yera'eh* is a feature of the pointed MT; (c) the Chronicler's 2 Chr 3:1 cross-reference is documentary; (d) the modern critical reading of the etiological-formula and the wordplay-axis is documented; (e) the WoH translation choice preserves the transliterated place-name and matches ASV-baseline at the closing-formula.

terms.partOfSpeech: prep + 1cs suffix + verb (niphal perfect 1cs) + noun construct + divine name
terms.literal: by myself I (have) sworn — utterance of YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: By myself have I sworn, saith Jehovah (ASV); By myself I swear, the LORD declares (NJPS); By myself I have sworn, says the LORD (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: By myself I have sworn, says YHWH
terms.rationale:
**Gen 22:16 contains the chapter's climactic divine-oath-formula:** *va-yomer bi nishbati n'um-YHWH ki ya'an asher asita et-ha-davar ha-zeh v-lo chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidekha* — *and he said, By myself I have sworn, says YHWH, because you have done this thing and have not withheld your son, your only son*. The verse is dense with editorially-marked formulae: the divine-self-oath *bi nishbati*, the prophetic-utterance formula *n'um-YHWH* (its first occurrence in canonical narrative), and the *chasakhta et-binkha et-yechidekha* echo of v 12.

**The divine-self-oath formula *bi nishbati*.** *Bi* is the prep + 1cs suffix (*by me*); *nishbati* is the niphal perfect 1cs of *sh-b-a* (to swear). The formula *bi nishbati* — *by myself I have sworn* — is the classical HB designation of the divine-self-oath: the deity, having no superior by which to swear, swears by himself. The formula is rare and structurally-load-bearing across the canon.

**The cross-corpus distribution of *bi nishbati*.**

*(i)* **Gen 22:16** (this verse — the first occurrence, the foundational divine-self-oath in canonical narrative).

*(ii)* **Isa 45:23** — *bi nishbati yatza mi-pi tzedakah davar v-lo yashuv ki li tikhra kol berekh tishava kol lashon* — *by myself I have sworn, from my mouth has gone forth a word in righteousness, and shall not return, that to me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear*. The cosmic-eschatological self-oath; the *every-knee-shall-bow* clause is cited at **Rom 14:11** and **Phil 2:10-11** as the foundational NT-text of the universal-confession-of-Christ — the Pauline reception of the divine-self-oath as the basis of the eschatological-confession.

*(iii)* **Jer 22:5** (against the house of David — *bi nishbati n'um YHWH*); **Jer 49:13** (against Bozrah — *bi nishbati n'um YHWH*); **Jer 51:14** (against Babylon — *nishba YHWH tz'va'ot b-nafsho* — sworn by his own life, a parallel-formulation).

*(iv)* **Amos 6:8** (against the pride of Jacob — *nishba Adonai YHWH b-nafsho n'um YHWH Elohei tz'va'ot* — sworn by his own life).

*(v)* **Isa 62:8** (the restoration-oath — *nishba YHWH bi-y'mino u-vi-z'roa uzo* — sworn by his right hand and by the arm of his strength).

The formula is concentrated in the prophetic-corpus (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Amos) and is foundational to the prophetic-utterance register. Gen 22:16 is the chronological-canonical first occurrence and is therefore the foundational instance for the entire formula's HB distribution.

**The Hebrews 6:13-14 reception.** **Hebrews 6:13-14** explicitly cites Gen 22:16-17 as the locus of the divine self-oath: *tō gar Abraam epangeilamenos ho theos, epei kat' oudenos eichen meizonos omosai, ōmosen kath' heautou, legōn ei mēn eulogōn eulogēsō se kai plēthynōn plēthynō se* — *for when God made the promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself, saying: 'Surely I will bless you and multiply you'*. The Hebrews-citation is the foundational NT-treatment of the divine-self-oath as the theological-engine of the divine-promise-bond. The reception is documented in **W. Lane** *Hebrews* (WBC 1991) pp. 152-156; **H. Attridge** *Hebrews* (Hermeneia 1989) pp. 179-184; **P. Ellingworth** *Commentary on Hebrews* (NIGTC 1993). The reception is flagged-not-preempted by this entry; the WoH translation reads the formula in its mainstream HB-prophetic register, leaving the Hebrews-reception as downstream.
