**The theological category of the divine-self-oath.** **F. M. Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973, ch. 11 on the divine-self-oath as the foundational covenantal-binding form; **D. R. Hillers** *Treaty-Curses and the Old Testament Prophets* 1964 on the wider treaty-and-oath ANE comparative material; **M. Weinfeld** *Deuteronomy and the Deuteronomic School* 1972 ch. 3 on the Deuteronomic oath-and-treaty formulae; **J. Lust** *Cult and Sacrifice in Daniel and Hebrews* in Vermeylen ed. 1989 on the Hebrews-reception of the divine-self-oath. The wider monographic literature: **G. Giesen** *Die Wurzel sb' 'schwören'* 1981; **T. Pola** *Die ursprüngliche Priesterschrift* 1995.

**The prophetic-utterance formula *n'um YHWH*.** The construct noun *n'um* (utterance, oracular-declaration) derives from a verb *n-'-m* (to utter, declare oracularly) used almost exclusively in the participial-prophetic register. The formula *n'um YHWH* — *utterance of YHWH* / *declares YHWH* — is the standard prophetic-utterance formula across the HB, recurring approximately 365 times across the canon, overwhelmingly in the prophetic corpus (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, the Twelve). **Gen 22:16 is the chronological-canonical first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible** — the formula's narrative-debut. After Gen 22:16, the next occurrences are in the prophetic corpus (Num 14:28; 1 Sam 2:30; 2 Sam 23:1 — David's last words; 2 Kgs 9:26, 19:33, 22:19 — concentrated in the early prophetic-narrative); the formula then proliferates across the major and minor prophets.

**The lexeme *n'um*.** **HALOT** ii.658-659; **BDB** p. 610: *utterance, oracular-declaration*. The lexeme is morphologically a passive-participle of *n-'-m* (to utter), nominalized into a construct-noun. The lexeme's restricted distribution (almost exclusively in the *n'um YHWH* / *n'um Adonai* / *n'um Adonai YHWH* prophetic-formulae) marks it as a specialized-oracular term. **R. Rendtorff** *Studies in Deuteronomy* 1985 ch. 4 on the *n'um YHWH* formula's prophetic-narrative distribution; **M. Tsevat** *The Meaning of the Book of Job and Other Biblical Studies* 1980 ch. 4 on the *n'um*-and-oracle register.

**The verse's structural-position.** The *bi nishbati n'um YHWH* combination marks the chapter's verdict-and-blessing as a maximally-binding divine-pronouncement: the divine-self-oath (the strongest form of divine-commitment) combined with the prophetic-utterance-formula (the strongest form of divine-speech-act). The chapter's blessing (vv 17-18) is therefore the chapter's most-binding divine-promise, and the formula's first-canonical-occurrence status marks the *aqedah* as the locus of the foundational divine-self-oath of the Hebrew Bible.

**Translation.** ASV's *By myself have I sworn, saith Jehovah* is updated to *By myself I have sworn, says YHWH* (modern English natural-order; WoH YHWH-preservation per established practice). The *bi nishbati* is preserved as *by myself I have sworn* (the standard English equivalent); *n'um YHWH* is preserved as *says YHWH* (ASV-baseline equivalent). The formula's chronological-canonical first-occurrence status is carried in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989, p. 154; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985, pp. 363-364; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994, pp. 111-112; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995, pp. 114-117; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964, p. 165; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 11; Hillers *Treaty-Curses* 1964; Weinfeld *Deuteronomy and the Deuteronomic School* 1972; Lane *Hebrews* (WBC) 1991; Attridge *Hebrews* (Hermeneia) 1989; HALOT ii.658-659; BDB p. 610.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *bi nishbati* is grammatically explicit at Gen 22:16; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable; (c) the Hebrews 6:13-14 reception is textually-documented and flagged-not-preempted; (d) the *n'um YHWH* formula's chronological-canonical first-occurrence status at Gen 22:16 is verifiable; (e) the WoH translation choice preserves the literal formula and matches ASV-baseline with WoH-YHWH-preservation.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun construct plural + proper name / gentilic adjective
terms.literal: sons of Het / the Hittite
terms.standardEnglish: the children of Heth (ASV/KJV); the Hittites (NJPS/NRSV/RSV); Ephron the Hittite
terms.wohChoice: the sons of Het (and Ephron the Hittite at v 10 where the gentilic *ha-Chitti* is explicit)
terms.rationale:
**Genesis 23 designates Avraham's land-transfer counterparts seven times as *bnei Het* (vv 3, 5, 7, 10, 16, 18, 20) and once as *Efron ha-Chitti* (v 10 — the gentilic-adjective).** The chapter's recurring designation raises the long-standing scholarly debate over the relationship between the *bnei Het* of Genesis 23 and the 2nd-millennium BCE Anatolian Hittite empire of Hattusa.

**The lexical-philological data.** *Het* (חֵת) is named at Gen 10:15 as the second son of Kena'an in the Table of Nations (after Sidon) — placing the *bnei Het* genealogically within the Canaanite peoples-list. The HB attests *bnei Het* / *Chitti* / *Chittim* across the canon at: Gen 10:15 (Table of Nations); Gen 23 *passim*; Gen 26:34-35 + 27:46 (Esau's Hittite wives — *Yehudit bat Be'eri ha-Chitti* and *Bosmat bat Eilon ha-Chitti*); Gen 36:2 (Esau's wife-list); Num 13:29 (the spies' report — *ha-Chitti v-ha-Yvusi v-ha-Emori yoshev ba-har*); Deut 7:1 + 20:17 (the seven-nations conquest-list); Josh 1:4 (the maximal land-claim — *kol eretz ha-Chittim*); Judg 1:26 (Luz in *eretz ha-Chittim*); Judg 3:5 (the unconquered peoples); 1 Sam 26:6 (*Achimelekh ha-Chitti*); 2 Sam 11 *passim* (*Uriah ha-Chitti*); 1 Kgs 10:29 + 11:1 (Solomon's Hittite wives and the *malkhei ha-Chittim*); 1 Kgs 15:5 (the *Uriah ha-Chitti* memorial); 2 Kgs 7:6 (the Aramean panic, *malkhei ha-Chittim*); Ezek 16:3, 45 (Jerusalem's mother *ha-Chittit*); 2 Chr 1:17 + 8:7.

**The two-Hittite problem.** Modern scholarship distinguishes two distinct historical referents for the HB *Chitti / Chittim*:

*(a) The 2nd-millennium BCE Anatolian Hittite Empire* — the imperial polity centered at Hattusa in central Anatolia, attested in cuneiform sources from ca. 1650-1180 BCE, speaking Indo-European Hittite (*Nesili*), with the Old/Middle/New Hittite kingdom periodizations and the well-documented international correspondence of the Amarna and Boğazköy archives. **T. Bryce** *The Kingdom of the Hittites* 1998 / 2nd ed. 2005 and *The Trojans and Their Neighbours* 2006 are the standard reference-works; **I. Singer** numerous articles on Hittite Anatolia and the Hittite-Egyptian correspondence; **H. A. Hoffner** *Hittite Myths* 1990 / 2nd ed. 1998 and *The Laws of the Hittites* 1997.

*(b) The 1st-millennium BCE Neo-Hittite / Luwian successor-states* — the Syro-Anatolian polities (Carchemish, Hamath, Aleppo, Sam'al, Til-Barsip, Tabal, Que, etc.) that survived the late-Bronze-Age collapse, attested in Neo-Assyrian sources as *māt Ḫatti* (*the land of Hatti*) for the wider Syro-Levantine region. **D. Hawkins** *Corpus of Hieroglyphic Luwian Inscriptions* 2000; **T. Bryce** *The World of the Neo-Hittite Kingdoms* 2012. The Assyrians regularly use *Ḫatti* as a regional designation for greater Syria-Palestine (cf. 2 Kgs 7:6 *malkhei ha-Chittim* paired with *malkhei Mitsrayim*).

*(c) Indigenous Canaanite *Heth*-people* — the modern critical consensus reads the *bnei Het* of Gen 10:15, 23, 26-27 as an indigenous Canaanite population (named for the eponymous *Het*, son of Kena'an), distinct from both (a) and (b), and only secondarily conflated with the Anatolian Hittites by later editorial/redactional identification. The Gen 23 *bnei Het* are part of the patriarchal-narrative ethnographic landscape — Hebron-area inhabitants — not Anatolian imperial subjects.

**The modern critical consensus.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 159-160: the *bnei Het* of Gen 23 are not the Anatolian Hittites; they are an indigenous Canaanite Hebron-area population whose name happens to coincide with the Anatolian polity's. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 371-372: the *bnei Het* are *Bewohner Palästinas* (inhabitants of Palestine) and the Anatolian-Hittite identification is a later conflation. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 124: the *bnei Het* are *a Hebron-based clan* and the Anatolian-Hittite reading is *unlikely*. **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 124-126: the *bnei Het* are *indigenous Canaanites*; the Anatolian identification is *secondary*. **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 170-172: notes the *philological coincidence* of the names but does not commit to historical identification.

**The minority position.** A minority of scholars (Gurney 1952 *The Hittites*; older Albrightian readings; some conservative-evangelical commentators) read the Gen 23 *bnei Het* as Anatolian-Hittite enclaves resident in patriarchal-period Canaan. The position rests primarily on the ANE-comparative-legal parallels: **M. R. Lehmann** *BASOR* 129 (1953) *Abraham's Purchase of Machpelah and Hittite Law* argued that the chapter's legal-procedure (the field-with-cave conferment, the precise-price-stipulation, the witnessed-transfer) reflects 2nd-millennium Hittite real-estate law. The Lehmann thesis was popular in the 1950s-60s but has been substantially deflated by subsequent scholarship: **G. M. Tucker** *JBL* 85 (1966) *The Legal Background of Genesis 23* argues that the chapter's legal-form is more consistent with 1st-millennium Neo-Babylonian and Neo-Assyrian conveyance-formulae than with 2nd-millennium Hittite law; **R. Westbrook** *JSS* 16 (1971) *Purchase of the Cave of Machpelah* concurs. The Tucker/Westbrook position is the modern critical default; the Lehmann thesis is rejected as a misidentification.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the literal Hebrew *bnei Het* as 'sons of Het' (rather than collapsing to the modern English convention 'Hittites') at the seven occurrences of *bnei Het* in the chapter (vv 3, 5, 7, 10, 16, 18, 20). At v 10, the gentilic-adjective *ha-Chitti* (which is itself a derived form) is rendered as 'the Hittite' (the standard English convention for the gentilic). The choice preserves the lexical-form distinction in the MT (collective-noun-phrase *bnei Het* vs. gentilic-adjective *ha-Chitti*) and remains neutral on the Anatolian-vs-Canaanite identification question — the philological data alone (an eponymous Canaanite *Het* in the Table of Nations) is preserved in the translation, and the wider historical-comparative debate is held in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Cross-corpus scope.** This entry governs only the Gen 23 occurrences. The wider HB *Chitti / Chittim* corpus (Gen 10:15, 26:34, 27:46, 36:2, Num 13:29, Deut 7:1, 20:17, Josh 1:4, Judg 1:26, 3:5, 1 Sam 26:6, 2 Sam 11, 1 Kgs 10-11, 15:5, 2 Kgs 7:6, Ezek 16:3, 45, 2 Chr 1:17, 8:7) is forward-referenced and will be added to *appliesTo* as those chapters are translated. The chapter-23 occurrences alone are recorded here.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 159-160; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 371-372; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 124; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 124-126; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 170-172; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 113-115; T. Bryce *The Kingdom of the Hittites* 2005; T. Bryce *The Trojans and Their Neighbours* 2006; T. Bryce *The World of the Neo-Hittite Kingdoms* 2012; H. A. Hoffner *Hittite Myths* 1998; H. A. Hoffner *The Laws of the Hittites* 1997; I. Singer (multiple articles); D. Hawkins *Corpus of Hieroglyphic Luwian Inscriptions* 2000; M. R. Lehmann *BASOR* 129 (1953); G. M. Tucker *JBL* 85 (1966); R. Westbrook *JSS* 16 (1971); O. R. Gurney *The Hittites* 1952.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexemes *bnei Het* and *ha-Chitti* are grammatically explicit; (b) the Gen 10:15 Table-of-Nations placement is direct documentary evidence for the Canaanite-eponymous reading; (c) the modern critical consensus (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton, Speiser) is documented; (d) the Lehmann/Anatolian-Hittite-law-parallel thesis and its modern critical rejection (Tucker, Westbrook) are documented; (e) the translation choice (literal *sons of Het* + gentilic *the Hittite*) preserves the MT lexical-form distinction and is neutral on the historical-identification debate.

terms.literal: prince of Elohim / one-lifted-up of Elohim
terms.standardEnglish: a mighty prince (ASV/KJV; superlative-reading); a prince of God (literal-theological); a mighty prince (NJPS, NRSV — superlative)
terms.wohChoice: a prince of Elohim (literal MT preservation)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 23:6 contains the Hittites' formal honorific address to Avraham:** *shema'enu adoni n'si Elohim atah b-tokhenu* — *hear us, my lord; you are a prince of Elohim among us*. The honorific is grammatically a construct phrase: *n'si* (construct of *nasi*, *one-lifted-up* / *prince* / *chieftain*; from *n-s-'*, *to lift up*) + *Elohim*. The construct relationship admits multiple readings, generating a long-standing translational dispute.

**The four principal readings.**

*(a) Literal-theological — *a prince of Elohim*.* The construct is read as a genitive of possession or origin: Avraham is *one-lifted-up belonging-to / by Elohim* — one chosen, favored, or designated by the deity. The reading is preserved in **Targum Onqelos** (*rav min qodam YHWH* — *great from before YHWH*); the **Vulgate** (*princeps Dei* — *prince of God*); the **Peshitta** (*ris min Aloho* — *chief from God*). The reading is theologically-direct: the Hittites recognize Avraham as a divinely-elevated figure.

*(b) Superlative-Elohim reading — *a mighty prince* / *an exalted prince*.* The reading takes *Elohim* as a Hebrew superlative-marker — the so-called *divine-superlative* construction, a frequent Semitic idiom where divine-name elements function as intensifying-modifiers rather than as genuine theological references. The classical philological treatment is **D. W. Thomas** *Vetus Testamentum* 3 (1953) *A Consideration of Some Unusual Ways of Expressing the Superlative in Hebrew* — the foundational study. Thomas surveys the divine-name-superlative across the HB: *harerei El* (Ps 36:7, *mighty mountains* — *mountains of God*); *arzei El* (Ps 80:11, *goodly cedars* — *cedars of God*); *charadat Elohim* (1 Sam 14:15, *a very great trembling* — *trembling of God*); *ruach Elohim ra'ah* (1 Sam 16:14, *an evil spirit* — *spirit-of-Elohim evil*; though here the divine-superlative reading is contested); etc. The construction is well-attested and the reading is the **modern critical default** for Gen 23:6. ASV/KJV render *a mighty prince*; NJPS *a mighty prince*; NRSV *a mighty prince*; Sarna 1989 endorses the superlative-reading; Westermann 1985 endorses; Speiser AB 1964 *exalted chief*.

*(c) Polytheist-context reading — a Hittite-spoken designation.* The reading attends to the speaker-context: the Hittites are addressing Avraham in their own religious idiom. The *Elohim* invoked by the Hittites need not be the deity of Avraham — it may be the local pantheon, or the gods generally, or the Hittite gods specifically. The reading preserves the literal *prince of (the) Elohim* but reframes the referent. The reading is held by **Cassuto** (in his Genesis commentary) and by the older Jewish-tradition exegetes (Ibn Ezra notes the polytheist-context possibility); among modern scholars, the reading is less dominant than the superlative-reading but is documented in **N. Wyatt** *Religious Texts from Ugarit* 2002 on the ANE-polytheist context of patriarchal honorifics.

*(d) Diplomatic-honorific reading — *your excellency* / *your highness*.* The reading takes the construct as a fixed-formula honorific, parallel to ANE diplomatic-correspondence titles (e.g., the Amarna *šarru rabu* — *great king*), with the *Elohim*-element having grammatical/intensifying function but no specific theological content. **Sarna** 1989 p. 157 endorses this reading as essentially synonymous with the superlative-reading: the honorific is a *diplomatic courtesy* parallel to modern *your excellency*. **Westermann** 1985 p. 374 likewise.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the literal *a prince of Elohim*, following readings (a) and (c). The choice is consistent with the established WoH policy on *Elohim*-bearing phrases: where the MT preserves the lexeme *Elohim*, the WoH translation preserves it. The superlative-reading (b) and the diplomatic-formula reading (d) — both currently dominant in modern critical scholarship — would collapse the *Elohim*-lexeme into an English intensifier (*mighty*, *exalted*) and would silently re-import the theological-smoothing that the WoH project's standing policy is to avoid. The Reader-Note: the modern critical consensus on the superlative-reading is documented and is defensible; the WoH choice is to preserve the lexical-MT and let the multiple readings live in the apparatus.

**The downstream effect on the chapter.** The literal reading (*prince of Elohim*) marks the Hittites' recognition of Avraham's standing as deriving from his divine-relationship — a recognition consistent with the Gen 12:3 nations-blessing formula and with the patriarchal-narratives' wider pattern of foreign-recognition of YHWH (Avimelekh at Gen 20-21; later, Ya'aqov-Lavan; later, Yosef-Pharaoh). The superlative-reading would flatten the recognition into a generic courtesy. The choice between the two readings is therefore not merely lexical but interpretive — the WoH choice supports the patriarchal-narrative pattern of foreign-recognition of the patriarch's divine-standing.

**Cross-corpus.** *Nasi* (prince, chieftain, one-lifted-up) recurs ~130 times across the HB, with concentrated distribution in Numbers (the tribal *n'si'im* — chieftains of the twelve tribes; Num 1:16, 2:3 and *passim*; 7:2-83), Ezekiel (the *nasi*-as-Davidic-prince figure; Ezek 34:24, 37:25, 44:3, 45:7-9, *passim*), and the patriarchal narratives (Gen 17:20 — the twelve princes of Yishma'el; Gen 23:6 — this verse; Gen 25:16 — the twelve princes of Yishma'el restated; Gen 34:2 — Shechem ben-Chamor *nasi ha-aretz*). The construct *n'si Elohim* is **hapax** in the HB — Gen 23:6 is the unique occurrence. The hapax-status reinforces the editorial-distinctiveness of the verse's honorific.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 157; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 374; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 126; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 130-131; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 171; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 114; D. W. Thomas *VT* 3 (1953); Cassuto *Genesis* II; HALOT i.726-728 (s.v. *nasi*); BDB pp. 672-673; N. Wyatt *Religious Texts from Ugarit* 2002.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construct phrase *n'si Elohim* is grammatically explicit at Gen 23:6; (b) the hapax-status of the construct in the HB is verifiable; (c) the four parallel readings (literal-theological, superlative-Elohim, polytheist-context, diplomatic-honorific) are all documented in the modern critical literature; (d) the divine-name-superlative construction is well-attested (Thomas 1953); (e) the WoH translation choice (literal *prince of Elohim*) is consistent with established WoH policy on *Elohim*-bearing phrases and preserves the lexical MT.

terms.literal: cave of the Makhpelah / cave of the doubling
terms.standardEnglish: the cave of Machpelah (ASV/KJV/NJPS/NRSV); the double cave (LXX *to spēlaion to diploun*)
terms.wohChoice: the cave of Makhpelah
terms.rationale:
**The chapter's keystone toponym — the cave Avraham purchases at vv 9, 17, 19, 20 (and recurring at Gen 25:9, 49:30, 50:13 as the patriarchal-burial-site).** The Hebrew *me'arat ha-Makhpelah* contains a notable feature: *ha-Makhpelah* carries the definite article *ha-* attached to what appears to be a proper-name, suggesting either an originally-descriptive term that has been crystallized into a toponym, or a proper-name that has retained the article (a feature shared with a small set of other HB toponyms — *ha-Negev*, *ha-Bashan*, *ha-Galil*).

**The etymological dispute.**

*(a) The LXX-rabbinic *doubling* etymology.* The LXX renders *to spēlaion to diploun* — *the double cave* — reading *Makhpelah* from the root *k-p-l* (to double, to fold). The rabbinic tradition develops the *doubling* reading into the interpretation that the cave is a *double-chambered* cave (two chambers one above the other; **b. Eruvin 53a**: *bayit u-aliyah al gabav* — *a house and an upper-chamber above it*) — or alternatively, that the cave is large enough to accommodate the *doublets* of patriarchal-couples buried there (**Genesis Rabbah 58:8**: the doubled-couples Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, Ya'aqov + Le'ah; the rabbinic tradition further adds Adam + Chavah as the foundational-couple). The reading is reception-attested and the *doubling*-derivation is philologically defensible (the root *k-p-l* is well-attested in the doubling sense).

*(b) The proper-name *Makhpelah* etymology unrecoverable.* The MT vocalization treats *ha-Makhpelah* as a proper place-name with the article, suggesting an already-existing toponym whose original etymology may be lost. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 157; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 376; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 128; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 134-135. The modern critical default is to treat the toponym as a place-name proper, with the LXX-rabbinic *doubling* etymology as a later folk-etymological development.

*(c) The geographical-descriptive etymology — *the place of the portion* / *the divided field*.* A minority reading takes *Makhpelah* in a geographical-topographical sense — the *doubled* / *divided* terrain of the field. The reading is less dominant but is held by **Speiser** AB 1964 p. 172.

**The Hebron-tomb identification.** The Genesis text places the cave *al-pnei Mamre hi Chevron b-eretz Kena'an* (Gen 23:19; cf. 25:9, 49:30, 50:13) — before Mamre, which is Hebron, in the land of Canaan. The location is therefore unambiguously identified with the Hebron-area site. The post-biblical identification of the cave with the present-day *Me'arat ha-Makhpelah* / *al-Haram al-Ibrahimi* (the Cave of the Patriarchs / Sanctuary of Abraham) at Hebron is supported by: **Josephus** *Jewish Antiquities* I.237 and *Jewish War* IV.532 (the Herodian-period structure already standing at the site in the 1st century CE; Josephus describes the marble monuments); the Herodian-period enclosure-walls (still extant — the largest Herodian-construction outside Jerusalem); uninterrupted Jewish, Christian, and Muslim tradition from the Second Temple period to the present. The archaeological identification of the cave-chamber beneath the Herodian enclosure is supported but not definitively confirmed; the Herodian enclosure is direct documentary evidence for the 1st-century-BCE identification of the site as the patriarchal-tomb.

**The cross-corpus patriarchal-burial chain.** The cave is named or referenced as the patriarchal-burial-site at:
- **Gen 23 (this chapter)** — Avraham purchases the cave from Efron ha-Chitti; Sarah is the first interred.
- **Gen 25:9-10** — Avraham is buried in the cave by Yitzchaq and Yishma'el (*va-yiqberu oto Yitzchaq v-Yishma'el banav el-me'arat ha-Makhpelah... ha-sadeh asher qanah Avraham me-et bnei Het*).
- **Gen 49:29-32** — Ya'aqov's burial-instructions, the most extensive reference: he is to be buried *im-avotai el ha-me'arah asher b-sdeh Efron ha-Chitti b-me'arat ha-sadeh asher b-Makhpelah asher al-pnei Mamre b-eretz Kena'an asher qanah Avraham et-ha-sadeh me-et Efron ha-Chitti la-achuzat qaver*. Ya'aqov names the previously-interred: Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, and his own first wife Le'ah (*v-shamah qavarti et Le'ah*).
- **Gen 50:13** — Ya'aqov is buried in the cave by his sons after the Egyptian-period mourning.

The pattern: the cave is the foundational patriarchal-acquisition of land in Kena'an (the first piece of the promised-land actually purchased and held in title by the patriarchs) and the burial-site for three patriarchal couples — Avraham + Sarah, Yitzchaq + Rivkah, Ya'aqov + Le'ah. The cross-corpus chain is editorially-deliberate; the chapter's careful legal-formalization of the title-transfer is the necessary precondition for the multi-generational burial-tradition.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *me'arat ha-Makhpelah* as *the cave of Makhpelah*, treating *Makhpelah* as a proper-name (per the modern critical default — Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton). The English drops the article on *Makhpelah* (following ASV/KJV/NJPS/NRSV convention), since modern-English proper-names do not naturally carry the article. The *doubling* etymology is documented in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary but is not chosen for the surface-translation, since (a) the modern critical default is to treat the toponym as a proper-name; (b) the surface-translation should not preempt the etymological-debate; (c) the rabbinic *doubling* reading is reception-material and is preserved in the apparatus.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 157; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 376; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 128; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 134-135; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 172; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 114-115; Josephus *Jewish Antiquities* I.237; Josephus *Jewish War* IV.532; A. Mader *Mambre* 1957; L. Vincent and J. Mackay *Hébron, le Haram el-Khalîl: Sépulture des Patriarches* 1923; Genesis Rabbah 58:8; b. Eruvin 53a.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the toponym *me'arat ha-Makhpelah* is grammatically explicit; (b) the LXX-rabbinic *doubling* etymology is documentary and philologically-defensible; (c) the modern critical default (proper-name with lost-etymology) is documented; (d) the Hebron-tomb identification is supported by Josephus, the Herodian-period archaeology, and uninterrupted post-biblical tradition; (e) the cross-corpus patriarchal-burial chain (Gen 23, 25, 49, 50) is documentary; (f) the WoH translation choice (proper-name treatment, ASV-baseline) preserves the lexical-MT and is consistent with the modern critical default.

terms.literal: for silver full / for full silver
terms.standardEnglish: for the full price (ASV/RSV); at the full price (NJPS); for the full price (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: for the full price
terms.rationale:
**Gen 23:9 contains Avraham's specific stipulation that he will purchase the cave at the *full* price — not as a gift, and not at a discount:** *yitten-li et-me'arat ha-Makhpelah asher-lo asher b-qtzeh sadehu b-kesef male yittnennah li b-tokhekhem la-achuzat qaver* — *let him give me the cave of Makhpelah which is his, which is at the end of his field; for the full price let him give it to me in your midst for a possession of a burying-place*. The phrase *b-kesef male* — *for full silver* — is a legal-symbolic formula carrying technical-juridical weight.

**The legal-symbolic function.** The chapter's narrative-tension (vv 4-15) is generated by the contrast between Efron's repeated offers to *give* the field (vv 11, 15 — *natatti lakh*, the *legal-fiction* of gift) and Avraham's insistence on *purchasing* it (vv 9, 13 — *b-kesef male*, *full silver*). The exchange is not merely a polite-negotiation but a formal-legal disposition: by paying the full price (rather than accepting a gift, or paying a token-price, or accepting at a discount), Avraham establishes an unambiguous and irrevocable transfer of title — one that cannot later be reclaimed by Efron's heirs or successors on the grounds of an incomplete transaction.

**The ANE legal-comparative background.** The *full-price* formula is well-attested in ANE land-transfer documents:
- **Old Babylonian sale-tablets** regularly include *kaspu gamru* (*full silver*) as the technical-legal seal of irrevocable transfer (Akkadian *gamru* — *complete, finished*; cognate to Hebrew *gamar*). The formula appears in Old Babylonian sale-formulae from Sippar, Nippur, and the wider Old Babylonian corpus (Schorr 1913 *VAB* V; San Nicolò 1922 *Babylonische Rechtsurkunden*).
- **Neo-Babylonian and Neo-Assyrian sale-tablets** continue the formula: *kaspu gamru tadin / mahir* (*the full silver has been given / received*) is the standard closing-clause of the conveyance-document.
- **Nuzi real-estate texts** contain parallel formulae, though the Nuzi-corpus emphasizes the *tidennutu* (antichretic-loan) form rather than the outright-sale form.
- **Aramaic Elephantine papyri** preserve parallel formulations in the 5th-century-BCE Jewish-military-colony documents.

The foundational modern study is **R. Westbrook** *JSS* 16 (1971) *Purchase of the Cave of Machpelah* — Westbrook reads Gen 23 as a legal-formal text whose conveyance-procedure reflects 1st-millennium Neo-Babylonian/Neo-Assyrian practice. **G. M. Tucker** *JBL* 85 (1966) *The Legal Background of Genesis 23* classifies the entire chapter as a *dialogue-deed* — a literary-form derived from the formal-conveyance-document genre. **H. Petschow** *MVAG* (1965) on Neo-Babylonian sale-formulae provides the comparative-Akkadian background. **K. Veenhof** various studies on Old Babylonian sale-tablets.

**The cross-corpus *full-silver-payment* parallels.**
- **1 Chr 21:24-25** (David refusing Ornan/Araunah's offer of the threshing-floor as a gift; David insists on paying *b-kesef male*): *lo ki qano eqneh b-kesef male ki lo essa asher l'kha l-YHWH v-ha'aloti olah chinam* — *no, but I will surely buy it for the full price, for I will not take what is yours for YHWH, nor offer a burnt-offering at no cost*. The 1 Chronicles passage is the clearest cross-corpus parallel to Gen 23: David, like Avraham, insists on the full-price-purchase rather than accepting a gift, for the same legal-symbolic reason (unambiguous title-transfer of cultically-significant land — in David's case, the future Temple-Mount). **Sarna** 1989 p. 158 explicitly draws the Gen 23 / 1 Chr 21 parallel.
- **Jer 32:9-12** (Jeremiah's symbolic field-purchase at Anatot during the Babylonian siege — the formal-legal procedure with weighed-silver and witnessed-deed): *va-eshqolah lo et ha-kesef shiv'ah sh'qalim va-asarah ha-kesef* — *and I weighed out to him the silver, seventeen shekels of silver*. Jeremiah's purchase is the most-elaborated HB legal-conveyance-procedure, with the deed-and-witnesses formality (vv 10-12) the closest formal-parallel to Gen 23.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *b-kesef male* as *for the full price* — preserving the legal-technical sense rather than the lexically-literal *for full silver*. The choice is consistent with ASV/RSV/NJPS/NRSV convention, all of which render in the legal-technical register (*full price* / *full money* / *at the full price*). The lexically-literal *for full silver* would obscure the legal-symbolic function and would be misleading-archaic to modern readers (since pre-coinage silver-bullion is not modern-readers' default conception of *money*). The cross-corpus parallel at 1 Chr 21:24 and the parallel-formula at Jer 32 are documented in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 157-158; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 374-375; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 126-127; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 131-133; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 171-172; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 114; G. M. Tucker *JBL* 85 (1966); R. Westbrook *JSS* 16 (1971); H. Petschow *MVAG* (1965); K. Veenhof studies on Old Babylonian sale-tablets; M. Schorr *Urkunden des altbabylonischen Zivil- und Prozessrechts* (VAB V, 1913); M. San Nicolò *Babylonische Rechtsurkunden* 1922; M. R. Lehmann *BASOR* 129 (1953) — for the older Hittite-law-parallel thesis, now rejected.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *b-kesef male* is grammatically explicit at Gen 23:9; (b) the ANE-comparative legal-background (Old Babylonian *kaspu gamru*; Neo-Babylonian and Neo-Assyrian conveyance-formulae) is documentary; (c) the cross-corpus parallel at 1 Chr 21:24-25 and the formal-procedure parallel at Jer 32:9-12 are documentary; (d) the modern critical reading (Westbrook 1971, Tucker 1966) of Gen 23 as a legal-formal text is the consensus position; (e) the WoH translation choice (*for the full price*) preserves the legal-technical sense and matches ASV/RSV/NJPS/NRSV convention.

terms.literal: silver passing for / to the merchant
terms.standardEnglish: current money with the merchant (ASV/KJV); at the going merchants' rate (NJPS); according to the merchants' weights (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: current with the merchant
terms.rationale:
**Gen 23:16 records Avraham's payment to Efron in the pre-coinage commercial-currency formula:** *va-yishqol Avraham l-Efron et-ha-kesef asher diber b-oznei bnei Het arba me'ot shekel kesef over la-socher* — *and Avraham weighed out to Efron the silver of which he had spoken in the ears of the sons of Het, four hundred shekels of silver, current with the merchant*. The phrase *kesef over la-socher* is a technical-commercial formula for standardized-weight silver-bullion.

**The pre-coinage commercial-economy.** Coinage was not introduced in the ancient Near East until the late 7th / early 6th century BCE (Lydia, the earliest coins). Through the entire patriarchal-period and Iron-Age HB chronology, silver functioned as bullion-money — valued and exchanged by weight rather than by face-value. The standard weight-unit was the *shekel* (*shekel* derives from *sh-q-l*, *to weigh* — the verb *va-yishqol* in this verse uses the same root). The chapter's verb *va-yishqol* (*and he weighed*) is grammatically and lexically transparent in the pre-coinage system: silver was *weighed out*, not *counted*.

**The *over la-socher* formula.** *Over* is the qal active participle of *a-b-r* (*to pass*); *socher* is the participial noun *merchant / trader* (from *s-ch-r*, *to go about, trade*). The phrase literally reads *passing for the merchant* — that is, *current* in the sense of *accepted-by-merchants* / *of merchant-standard*. The phrase identifies the silver as conforming to the inter-regional commercial-standard weight, as distinct from local or domestic weights which might differ from market to market. The standardized-merchant-weight ensured that the transaction's value was unambiguous and recognized across the trade-network.

**ANE-comparative background.** The standardized-merchant-weight concept is well-attested in the ANE economic record:
- **Old Babylonian and later Mesopotamian merchant-weight standards** are documented in the Babylonian *kaspu sa libbi* (*silver of the heart* — silver of guaranteed purity) and parallel formulae.
- **Egyptian Late-Bronze records** preserve the *deben* and *kite* weight-system with merchant-standard weights.
- **Standardized stone weights** (often inscribed with the weight-unit and the merchant's or temple's seal) are recovered archaeologically from sites across the ANE; significant collections from Lachish, Megiddo, Gezer, and the wider Iron-Age Levant. The standardized-weight-stones are direct material evidence for the merchant-standard-weight system.
- The Hebrew expression *even shleymah va-tzedeq* (*a full and just stone*, Deut 25:15) and the prophetic-indictment of false-weights (Amos 8:5; Mic 6:11; Prov 11:1, 16:11, 20:10, 23) attest the cultural-importance of accurate-weighing in the Iron-Age commercial-economy.

**The *over la-socher* formula is rare in the HB.** The formula recurs at:
- **Gen 23:16** (this verse) — Avraham's purchase of the cave.
- **2 Kings 12:5** (the Jehoash temple-repair fund — *kol kesef ha-qadashim asher yuva veit YHWH kesef over* — *all the silver of the dedicated-things that is brought into the house of YHWH, silver that is current*). The 2 Kings parallel uses the abbreviated form *kesef over* (without *la-socher*); the parallel confirms the formula's commercial-currency function.

The formula is more constrained than the broader *full-silver* formula (*b-kesef male*) — *over la-socher* specifies merchant-standard-weight; *b-kesef male* specifies full-price.

**Cross-corpus pre-coinage land-transactions.** Other HB pre-coinage land-transactions:
- **Gen 33:19** — Ya'aqov purchases land at Shechem from the *bnei Chamor* for *me'ah qesitah* — *a hundred qesitah*. The *qesitah* is an archaic weight-unit attested only at Gen 33:19, Josh 24:32, Job 42:11 (see existing glossary entry *kesitah* from Job 42).
- **2 Sam 24:24** = **1 Chr 21:25** — David purchases the threshing-floor of Aravnah/Ornan: *50 shekel kesef* (2 Sam) / *600 shekel zahav b-mishqal* (1 Chr). The numbers vary; the *b-mishqal* (*by weight*) at 1 Chr 21:25 explicitly notes the weighing.
- **Jer 32:9-12** — Jeremiah's purchase at Anatot for *shiv'ah sh'qalim va-asarah ha-kesef* — 17 shekels of silver, the most-elaborated HB conveyance-procedure (see *kesef-male* entry above).

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *over la-socher* as *current with the merchant* — preserving the ASV/KJV convention. The lexically-literal *passing for the merchant* would be obscure to modern readers; the modern-technical *at the merchants' weight* (NRSV) is accurate but loses the formula's quasi-currency register. The compromise *current with the merchant* (ASV) captures the formula's sense: the silver is *current* (commercially-accepted, in-circulation) *with* (in the eyes/use of) merchants. The technical sense (standardized-merchant-weight bullion) is documented in this glossary entry and in the per-verse commentary.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 162-163; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 377; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 127-128; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 135-136; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 172; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 115; R. Westbrook *JSS* 16 (1971); G. M. Tucker *JBL* 85 (1966); HALOT ii.778 (s.v. *socher*); BDB pp. 695-696.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *over la-socher* is grammatically explicit at Gen 23:16; (b) the pre-coinage silver-bullion-by-weight economic-system is documentary (archaeologically and textually attested across the ANE); (c) the cross-corpus parallel at 2 Kgs 12:5 confirms the formula's commercial-currency function; (d) the wider HB pre-coinage land-transaction parallels (Gen 33:19, 2 Sam 24:24, 1 Chr 21:25, Jer 32:9-12) are documentary; (e) the WoH translation choice (*current with the merchant*) preserves the ASV-baseline and captures the formula's standardized-merchant-weight sense.

terms.literal: and it stood / was-established / was-confirmed ... for / as a possession-by-purchase
terms.standardEnglish: were made sure to Abraham for a possession (ASV/KJV); passed to Abraham as his possession (NJPS); were transferred as a possession (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: were made sure to Avraham as a possession
terms.rationale:
**Gen 23:17-18 (and the recapitulating v 20) contains the chapter's formal legal-deed conferment-formula:** *va-yaqem sdeh Efron asher ba-Makhpelah asher lifnei Mamre ha-sadeh v-ha-me'arah asher-bo v-khol-ha-etz asher ba-sadeh asher b-khol-g'vulo saviv l-Avraham l-miqnah l-eynei bnei Het b-khol ba'ei sha'ar iro* — *and the field of Efron which was in Makhpelah, which was before Mamre — the field, and the cave which was in it, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all its border round about, were made sure to Avraham as a possession in the sight of the sons of Het, of all that went in at the gate of his city*. v 20 recapitulates: *va-yaqem ha-sadeh v-ha-me'arah asher-bo l-Avraham la-achuzat qaver me-et bnei Het*.

**The verb *va-yaqem*.** *Va-yaqem* is the qal imperfect 3ms waw-consecutive of *q-w-m* (*to stand, arise, be-established*). The standard sense is *and-it-arose / stood / was-established*. In the formal-legal context of vv 17-18 and 20, the verb takes the technical-juridical sense *and-it-was-confirmed / was-made-effective / passed-to-the-buyer-by-confirmed-deed*. The verb's force is the *standing* / *being-established* of the property in the buyer's title. The qal of *q-w-m* with an inanimate-property subject and the *l-X l-miqnah* construction is the technical legal-conveyance idiom.

**The noun *miqnah*.** *Miqnah* is the noun *acquisition-by-purchase, possession-acquired* (from *q-n-h*, *to acquire*; distinct from *miqneh* — *livestock, herd-acquisition*, though they share the root). The lexeme is well-attested in legal-property contexts: **Lev 25:16, 51** (in the Jubilee laws — *miqnat-nepheshot* for slave-purchase); **Jer 32:11-16** (the Jeremiah-land-deed at Anatot — *sefer ha-miqnah*, *the deed of purchase* — the most-elaborated HB legal-conveyance pericope); **Ezek 7:13** (the *qoneh u-mokher*, buyer-and-seller language). The *l-miqnah* construction in Gen 23:18 (and the parallel *la-achuzat qaver* in v 20) is the **legal-status terminus** — the property is *as a confirmed-acquisition-by-purchase*.

**The conferment-formula as form-critical type.** The phrase *va-yaqem ... l-Avraham l-miqnah l-eynei bnei Het b-khol ba'ei sha'ar iro* constitutes the conveyance-document's closing-clause: the property's *standing* in the buyer's title, *as a confirmed-acquisition*, *in the sight of* the witnesses (*l-eynei bnei Het*) and *in the presence of all who entered the gate of his city* (*b-khol ba'ei sha'ar iro*). The witnessed-transfer-formula and the city-gate as the formal-juridical venue (cf. Ruth 4:1-12 — Boaz's redemption-transaction at the city-gate, the most-elaborated HB city-gate-transaction-scene) are both standard ANE legal-formal features.

**The chapter as legal-narrative.** The chapter has been analyzed as a *dialogue-deed* — a literary form derived from formal-conveyance documents — by **G. M. Tucker** *JBL* 85 (1966) *The Legal Background of Genesis 23*. Tucker reads the entire chapter (the negotiation-narrative + the conferment-formula at vv 17-18, 20) as a literary-stylization of the formal-conveyance-deed genre, with the negotiation-dialogue replacing the document's witness-clause-and-stipulations and the conferment-formula at vv 17-18, 20 replacing the document's standing-clause. The classification has been broadly accepted in modern critical scholarship (Westermann, Sarna, Wenham, Hamilton, Speiser all draw on Tucker's form-critical analysis).

**ANE-comparative parallels.** The conveyance-document genre is well-attested across the ANE:
- **Old Babylonian sale-tablets** preserve the standard tripartite structure: object-of-sale + price + standing-clause (*kankuhu / kankakkuhu* — *its seal / sealed-deed*).
- **Neo-Babylonian sale-tablets** preserve the elaborated form with witnesses, scribe, date, and standing-clause.
- **Neo-Assyrian sale-tablets** preserve the *kanīku ša šarri* form.
- **Aramaic Elephantine papyri** (5th-century BCE Jewish-military-colony) preserve the legal-deed form with the *bayt zilī* (*house of mine*) standing-clause.
- **Hittite legal-deeds** (the older Lehmann 1953 *BASOR* thesis on Gen 23 as Hittite-law-parallel, since rejected — see *bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question* above) preserve a parallel structure with the *tuppi* (*tablet*) conveyance-document.

**The recurring *va-yaqem ... l-miqnah* at vv 17, 18, 20 (the three-fold repetition).** The chapter repeats the conferment-formula three times — vv 17-18 (the full enumeration) and v 20 (the recapitulating summary). The three-fold repetition is editorially-deliberate: the legal-conveyance is so important to the patriarchal-narratives' theological structure (the first piece of the promised-land actually purchased and held in title) that the conferment is restated for emphasis. **Sarna** 1989 pp. 162-163 notes the editorial-emphasis.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *va-yaqem ... l-miqnah* as *were made sure to Avraham as a possession* — preserving the ASV/KJV convention of *were made sure*. The translation choice between ASV's *made sure*, NJPS's modernizing *passed*, and NRSV's *were transferred as a possession* affects whether the reader perceives the legal-deed technical layer. The ASV *were made sure* preserves the *standing / confirmation* sense of *q-w-m* and captures the formal-juridical register. The NJPS *passed* loses the *standing* sense. The NRSV *were transferred as a possession* is accurate but flattens to a modern conveyance-vocabulary that loses the *standing* register.

**The claim_type discussion.** The entry is held as `claim_type=direct` rather than `inferred` on the following grounds: (a) the verbs *q-w-m* qal and the noun *miqnah* are grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the conferment-formula's structural-position (the chapter's recurring closing-clause at vv 17-18 and 20) is editorially-deliberate; (c) the form-critical reading (Tucker 1966, Westbrook 1971) of the chapter as a legal-narrative is the modern critical consensus and is not project-distinctive synthesis; (d) the ANE-comparative legal-deed parallels are documentary (the Old Babylonian, Neo-Babylonian, and Aramaic Elephantine corpora) — though the precise legal-tradition-of-origin (Hittite vs. Neo-Babylonian) remains debated. The legal-form classification itself is `direct` (scholarly-consensus); only the *which-ANE-tradition* sub-question is contested. The Editor flags the claim_type call for Reviewer attention in the escalation report.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 162-163; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 377-378; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 128-129; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 136-138; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 172-173; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 115-116; G. M. Tucker *JBL* 85 (1966) *The Legal Background of Genesis 23*; R. Westbrook *JSS* 16 (1971) *Purchase of the Cave of Machpelah*; H. Petschow *MVAG* (1965); M. R. Lehmann *BASOR* 129 (1953); M. Greenberg *JAOS* 82 (1962) on Hittite-law parallels (Greenberg supports the Lehmann thesis cautiously); HALOT ii.601-602 (s.v. *miqnah*); BDB pp. 889-890.

The claim_type is *direct* — see the discussion above.

terms.literal: in the image of Elohim / in the likeness of Elohim
terms.standardEnglish: in his image / in his likeness
terms.wohChoice: in their image / in their likeness (singular-they)
terms.rationale:
**Project-wide policy decision.** The Hebrew noun *Elohim* is grammatically plural in form but conventionally takes singular verbs and singular possessive suffixes throughout the canonical Hebrew Bible. The standard English translation tradition (ASV, KJV, NRSV, NJPS, etc.) renders the singular suffixes with singular masculine pronouns ('he,' 'his,' 'him'). The Wheel of Heaven translation adopts the **singular-they policy** for the image-and-likeness verses and their immediate narrative context — rendering *b-tzalmo* as 'in their image' (not 'in his image') and singular-verb forms as 'they' (not 'he') when the subject is *Elohim* as divine creative agent.

**Rationale.** Modern English uses singular-they as the natural pronoun for an entity whose grammatical number is ambiguous or plural-with-singular-verb-form. The Hebrew *Elohim* is exactly such an entity — plural in form, singular in syntactic agreement. The traditional 'his' rendering chooses one side of the Hebrew grammatical-singular/lexical-plural tension; the singular-they rendering preserves the tension. This is methodologically symmetric with the existing project policy on the *nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael* verb (MT-fidelity to whatever the Hebrew preserves) — the Hebrew preserves a plural-noun-with-singular-verb feature; the English preserves it via singular-they.

**The Genesis 1:26 → 27 grammatical shift.** Gen 1:26 uses 1st-person plural cohortative *na'aseh* ('let us make') with 1st-person plural suffixes *b-tzalmenu* ('in our image'), *ki-dmutenu* ('according to our likeness'). v 27 then shifts to 3rd-person masculine singular: *bara* ('he created'), *b-tzalmo* ('in his image'), *oto* ('him'). The shift is in the source Hebrew, not in modern translation. The shift has been read variously as:
- Plural of majesty / royal we (traditional Christian apologetic)
- Plural of self-deliberation (Cassuto, Wenham)
- Divine council (Cross 1973 *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic*; Smith 2001 *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism*; Heiser 2015 *The Unseen Realm*)
- Plural of intensification (Joüon-Muraoka §136d)

**Scope.** The singular-they policy applies specifically to:
- *b-tzelem Elohim* / *b-tzalmo* / *bi-dmut Elohim* — the image-and-likeness language
- The immediate narrative-unit pronouns when Elohim is the divine creative agent in these contexts

It does **NOT** apply to:
- Every Elohim-with-singular-verb construction across the corpus (which would be a maximal-cascade rewrite)
- Quotative-introductory verbs (e.g. *va-yomer Elohim* 'and Elohim said')
- Contexts where the singular reading is the natural English (e.g. *halakh et-ha-Elohim* 'walked with the Elohim')

**Cross-corpus scope (this entry's appliesTo):**
- Gen 1:27 (the founding image-verse)
- Gen 5:1-2 (the toledot recapitulation)
- Gen 9:6 (the post-flood image-grounded blood-prohibition)
- Future occurrences as encountered: Exod 31:13 (*lada'at ki ani YHWH m'qadishkhem*) etc. are NOT in scope; the scope is *tzelem-Elohim* / *dmut-Elohim* image-language specifically.

**Cross-reference.** See `elohim-as-translation` (the article-bearing transliteration policy) and the deferred *hit'u Elohim* plural-verb construction at Gen 20:13 (commentary-only, no entry — a related but distinct grammatical case).

terms.partOfSpeech: imperative + politeness particle + noun + preposition + noun + 1cs suffix
terms.literal: put, please, your hand under my thigh
terms.standardEnglish: Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh (ASV/KJV); Put your hand under my thigh (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Put, I pray you, your hand under my thigh
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:2 and 24:9 contain the chapter's most-discussed ANE-comparative gesture: the oath-by-the-thigh.** Avraham commissions his servant for the bride-quest with the formal gesture *sim-na yadkha tachat yereki* — *put, please, your hand under my thigh* — and the servant executes the gesture at v 9 (*va-yasem ha-eved et yado tachat yerekh Avraham adonav va-yishava lo*). The same gesture recurs only once more in the Hebrew Bible: **Gen 47:29**, where Ya'aqov commissions Yosef for the burial-oath (*sim-na yadkha tachat yereki v-asita imadi chesed v-emet al-na tiqberani b-Mitsrayim*). The two HB occurrences share an editorially-deliberate pattern: both are *deathbed-or-late-life* commissioning-oaths from a patriarchal father-figure to an authorized agent on a matter the principal cannot personally accomplish (Avraham cannot travel to Aram-Naharaim; Ya'aqov cannot personally choose his burial-site in Kena'an).

**The euphemistic content.** The Hebrew noun *yarekh* (יָרֵךְ) means *thigh* in the literal anatomical sense (e.g., Gen 32:26 — Ya'aqov's wrestling-injury *kaf yereko*; Num 5:21 — the *sotah*'s thigh in the bitter-water trial), but in the legal-formulaic register of the two oath-passages it is widely read as a euphemism for the *genital area* — the seat of generative power and of the patriarchal-seed-covenant. The reading is mainstream: **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 163; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 384; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 141; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 139-140; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 117; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 178. The euphemism's logic is that the oath touches the source of the patriarch's descendants — the very seed-line whose continuation the oath is meant to secure (the bride-quest at Gen 24; the patriarchal-burial-in-the-promised-land at Gen 47).

**Two specialized treatments.** **Malul 1985** (*VT* 35, 'More on *Pahad Yitzhaq* and the Oath by the Thigh', pp. 192-200) argues for the genital-touch reading and proposes the parallel to the *pachad Yitzchaq* (*the Dread/Thigh of Yitzchaq*) at Gen 31:42, 53 — reading *pachad* as a thigh-related cultic-juridical term and connecting the oath-gesture to the deity-witnessed-by-the-fertility-organ pattern. The *pachad* connection is debated but the genital-touch reading itself is widely accepted. **Hill 1967** (*JBL* 86, 'New Light on the Oath of Genesis 24:2', pp. 198-201) develops the genital-touch reading with attention to the formal-juridical register and the witnessing-by-the-life-source structure.

**The ANE-comparative gap.** The oath-on-fertility-symbol pattern is widely attested in the ANE: oaths sworn by the cult-statue's genitals (Mesopotamian), by the king's sceptre (across the ANE), by the bull's testicles (some Greek and Roman texts), by sacred trees and altars. **But the specific gesture of touching another person's genital area to swear an oath is RARE in the extant ANE textual corpus.** The Hittite, Old Babylonian, Mari, Ugaritic, and Egyptian corpora preserve oath-formulae and oath-objects extensively, but the *touch-the-thigh* gesture as a formal-juridical act is not directly paralleled in any single extant ANE text. The two HB occurrences (Gen 24:2/9 and Gen 47:29) are the principal documentary attestation. The gesture's logic is comprehensible within the wider ANE oath-on-fertility-symbol pattern, but its specific form is editorially distinctive to the patriarchal-narrative tradition.

**The Targum and rabbinic interpretation.** **Targum Onkelos** renders *tachat yereki* literally (*tchot yarki*) without euphemistic gloss. **Rashi** at Gen 24:2 cites **Pirqei de-Rabbi Eliezer 16** and reads *yerekh* as referring to the circumcised member — *because that is the first commandment that came upon him* (i.e., because the patriarch's covenant-marker is the *brit milah*, the oath touches the covenantal-organ). Rashi's reading is the canonical rabbinic-medieval gloss and matches the modern critical genital-touch reading on the practical-anatomical level; it adds the explicit circumcision-covenant logic. **Ibn Ezra** at Gen 24:2 takes a non-euphemistic position, reading *yerekh* as the literal thigh and the gesture as *seated submission* of the servant to the master (the hand-under-the-thigh as a posture of subordination). Ibn Ezra's reading is the principal medieval-Jewish alternative but is now a minority position.

**The Christian-medieval reception.** The Christian-medieval interpretive tradition almost uniformly reads *yerekh* as the literal thigh, often allegorizing the gesture toward Christ's descent-from-Avraham's-loins (Heb 7:5 *those descending out of the loins of Avraham*, where the Greek *osphys* — *loins* — was the Septuagint's standard rendering of *yerekh* at Gen 46:26, Exod 1:5, Judg 8:30). The Heb 7:5 reception preserves the seed-line covenantal logic without the euphemistic-gesture content. The modern critical reading restores the euphemistic content the medieval tradition smoothed away.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *sim-na yadkha tachat yereki* as *Put, I pray you, your hand under my thigh* — preserving the ASV/KJV literal-with-Hebrew-euphemism. The choice matches the ASV register and follows the modern critical convention of preserving the Hebrew euphemism rather than disambiguating in the translation. The genital-touch reading and the *Gen 47:29 / pachad Yitzchaq* cross-references are surfaced in the commentary. The apparatus-explicit gloss option ('put your hand under my thigh (i.e., upon the generative organ)') was rejected as editorially-intrusive; the literal preserves the Hebrew register.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The gesture-formula governs Gen 24:2 (Avraham to servant), Gen 24:9 (servant executing the gesture), and Gen 47:29 (Ya'aqov to Yosef — the burial-oath, also the second occurrence of the *chesed v-emet* formula; cross-reference to the *chesed-v-emet-steadfast-love-and-truth* entry).

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 163; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 384; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 141; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 139-140; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 117; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 178; Malul 1985 *VT* 35 pp. 192-200; Hill 1967 *JBL* 86 pp. 198-201; Freedman 1976 *JBL* 95 (*Pillars, Stones, and Sacred Stones*, comparative); HALOT s.v. *yarekh*; BDB s.v. *yarekh* p. 437. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the gesture-formula is grammatically explicit in the MT at all three verses; (b) the genital-touch euphemistic reading is the modern critical consensus across the standard commentaries; (c) the ANE-comparative gap (the oath-on-fertility-symbol pattern widespread, the specific genital-touch gesture rare in extant ANE texts) is documentary fact; (d) the cross-corpus appliesTo (Gen 24:2, 24:9, 47:29) is verifiable by lexical search.

terms.literal: YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens
terms.standardEnglish: the LORD, the God of heaven (ASV/KJV/NRSV); the LORD, God of heaven (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:3 contains the first occurrence in the Hebrew Bible of the formula *YHWH Elohei ha-shamayim v-Elohei ha-aretz* — *YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth* — and the first occurrence of the bare *Elohei ha-shamayim* construction.** The formula recurs at v 7 within the chapter (without the *v-Elohei ha-aretz* second member) and then disappears from Genesis. Across the rest of the canonical Hebrew Bible, the *Elohei ha-shamayim* phrase is **concentrated in post-exilic and late-period literature**, raising the well-known source-critical question whether the Gen 24 occurrence signals late composition or is a context-sensitive idiom available across the corpus.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The bare *Elohei ha-shamayim* construction recurs at: **Jonah 1:9** (Jonah's confession to the sailors — *ivri anokhi v-et-YHWH Elohei ha-shamayim ani yare*); **Ezra 1:2** (Cyrus's decree as quoted in Ezra — *YHWH Elohei ha-shamayim natan li*); **Ezra 5:11** (Aramaic *Elah shemaya v-arqa*); **Ezra 5:12** (Aramaic *Elah shemaya*); **Ezra 6:9-10** (Aramaic, in Darius's response); **Ezra 7:12, 7:21, 7:23** (Aramaic, in Artaxerxes's authorization); **Neh 1:4-5** (Nehemiah's prayer *Elohei ha-shamayim* + *El ha-shamayim ha-gadol v-ha-nora*); **Neh 2:4, 2:20** (Nehemiah-before-Artaxerxes); **Dan 2:18-19** (Aramaic *Elah shemaya*); **Dan 2:37, 2:44** (Aramaic); **Ps 136:26** (the closing verse of the Great Hallel, *hodu l-El ha-shamayim* — partial). The cross-corpus pattern is striking: the construction is concentrated in **Persian-period Aramaic and Hebrew literature** (Ezra-Nehemiah, Daniel, post-exilic Psalms, with Jonah likely Persian-period and Gen 24 the only patriarchal-narrative occurrence).

**The late-period-formula reading.** Westermann (*Genesis 12-36*, Hermeneia, 1985, pp. 384-385) reads the Gen 24 occurrence as evidence of late composition: the formula is a Persian-period stock-phrase for the supreme deity in international-political contexts (Cyrus, Darius, Artaxerxes all use it of YHWH in their decrees), and its appearance in the patriarchal narrative is anachronistic. Hamilton (*NICOT Genesis 18-50*, 1995, pp. 138-139) treats the Gen 24 occurrence as similarly late and notes the chapter's other late-features (the developed prayer-language, the bride-quest type-scene's literary sophistication, the *eved* as protagonist). The late-period reading is the modern critical default for Gen 24 as a whole: the chapter is often dated to the post-exilic period in critical scholarship (Van Seters 1975, Carr 1996, Schmid 1999) — though the dating remains debated.

**The diplomatic-context reading.** Sarna (*JPS Genesis*, 1989, p. 164) reads the Gen 24 occurrence as a **diplomatic-foreign-context idiom**: Avraham is commissioning a servant for a journey into a foreign land (Aram-Naharaim) where the local cult is not YHWHistic, and the formula *YHWH the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth* establishes YHWH's universal sovereignty in the cosmographic-binitarian register that foreign audiences would recognize. The reading is supported by the recurrence at v 7 in Avraham's continued commissioning-speech: the formula is concentrated in the foreign-context-framing speeches, not in the narrative-prose or in the patriarchal-internal dialogue. On this reading, the formula is *not* a late-composition marker but a context-sensitive register-shift available to the patriarchal narrator. Wenham (*WBC Genesis 16-50*, 1994, p. 140) leans toward Sarna's reading. Alter (*Five Books of Moses*, 2004, p. 117) notes the formula's distinctive register but does not commit on dating.

**The cosmographic-binitarian structure.** The doubled construct *Elohei ha-shamayim v-Elohei ha-aretz* — *the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth* — is structurally distinctive: it pairs the celestial and terrestrial domains under a single divine sovereignty. The doubled-construct pattern recurs in late-period material (Ezra 5:11 Aramaic *Elah shemaya v-arqa*; Dan 5:23 Aramaic *Mara di-shmaya*; Tob 7:17 *despota tou ouranou kai tēs gēs* in the LXX) and is read in modern scholarship as a Persian-period cosmographic formula for the supreme deity. The bare *Elohei ha-shamayim* (without the *v-Elohei ha-aretz* second member) is the more common late-period form; the doubled form at Gen 24:3 is the most cosmographically-developed instance in the patriarchal narratives.

**The Elohim-policy interaction.** The WoH translation policy on *Elohim* with the article (see *elohim-as-translation*) is to render the article-bearing form literally — 'the Elohim'. The Gen 24:3 formula's *Elohei ha-shamayim* uses *Elohei* as construct ('the Elohim of...'), not as the absolute with article. The WoH rendering preserves the construct: *the Elohim of the heavens* (rather than 'God of heaven' as in ASV/NRSV). The plural *Elohim* is rendered with the singular-they policy where applicable (image-and-likeness contexts; see *tzelem-elohim-image-plurality*) but in the *YHWH Elohei ha-shamayim* formula the verb-agreement is implicit (no finite verb in the phrase), so the singular-they question does not arise at the syntactic level. The translation reads as standard scholarly English with the article-preserving *the Elohim of the heavens*.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders the formula as *YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens and the Elohim of the earth* (v 3) and *YHWH, the Elohim of the heavens* (v 7). The choice preserves the article-bearing construct per established WoH Elohim-policy and matches the literal Hebrew register. The cosmographic-binitarian structure and the late-period-formula vs. diplomatic-context dating-question are surfaced in the commentary.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The construction governs Gen 24:3 (the doubled form), Gen 24:7 (the bare form). Future cross-corpus occurrences (Jonah, Ezra, Nehemiah, Daniel) will be referenced when those books are translated; the cross-corpus distribution is documented here but the *appliesTo* for this entry is limited to the Genesis occurrences.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 384-385; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 164; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 140; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 138-139; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 117; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 178; Van Seters *Abraham in History and Tradition* 1975 (on the chapter's dating); Carr *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996; Schmid *Erzväter und Exodus* 1999; Niehr 1990 *Der höchste Gott* (on the Persian-period high-god formulae); Knauf 1990 *Kein Land der Verheissung* (on the patriarchal-narrative chronology). The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the two modern critical positions on the dating-question (late-composition per Westermann/Hamilton vs. diplomatic-context per Sarna/Wenham) are mainstream scholarly readings, both documented; (d) the article-bearing construct-rendering matches established WoH Elohim-policy.

terms.source: וְהָיָה הַנַּעֲרָ אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ ... אֹתָהּ הֹכַחְתָּ לְעַבְדְּךָ
terms.translit: v-haya ha-na'arah asher omar eleha ... otah hokhachta l-avdekha
terms.literal: let it come to pass: the young woman to whom I say ... her you have appointed for your servant
terms.standardEnglish: let it come to pass, that the damsel to whom I shall say ... let the same be she that thou hast appointed for thy servant (ASV/KJV); let the girl to whom I shall say ... be the one whom you have appointed for your servant (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: Let it come to pass that the young woman to whom I shall say ... let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:12-14 contains the first direct prayer of a non-patriarch in the Hebrew Bible** (Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 165). The servant — unnamed in the chapter; identified in some Jewish-tradition strata with Eliezer of Damascus from Gen 15:2, though the chapter itself preserves the anonymity editorially — addresses YHWH directly with a structured petitionary prayer (*va-yomar YHWH Elohei adoni Avraham haqreh-na lefanai ha-yom va-aseh-chesed im adoni Avraham*) followed by a sign-seeking test-formula (vv 13-14). The two-part structure — invocation + test-condition — is editorially-deliberate and inaugurates a recurring HB sub-genre of *divinatory-test prayer*.

**The first-non-patriarch-prayer status.** Prior to Gen 24:12, direct divine address in Genesis is concentrated in patriarchal figures (Avram at 15:2-3, 8; 17:18; 18:23-32; 20:7 implicit) and in YHWH-initiated dialogue. The servant's prayer at 24:12-14 is the **first in-narrative direct address to YHWH by a figure who is not a patriarch and not a prophet**. Sarna notes the editorial significance: the chapter authorizes prayer-as-direct-address for figures outside the patriarchal-prophetic circle, establishing the *general-servant prayer-access* pattern that becomes standard in the later HB. **Greenberg** *Biblical Prose Prayer* 1983 pp. 7-12 reads the verse as the foundational instance of HB prose-prayer (distinct from cultic-psalmic prayer), with the four constituent elements present: (i) divine address-formula; (ii) historical-anchoring phrase (*Elohei adoni Avraham*); (iii) petition (*haqreh-na lefanai ha-yom*); (iv) appeal to divine virtue (*va-aseh-chesed*). **Miller** *They Cried to the Lord* 1994 pp. 100-105 extends the analysis to the sign-seeking sub-structure at vv 13-14.

**The sign-seeking test-formula.** The conditional structure at v 14 — *v-haya ha-na'arah asher omar eleha ... v-amrah ... otah hokhachta l-avdekha* (*let it come to pass: the young woman to whom I shall say ... and she shall say ... let her be the one whom you have appointed for your servant*) — is a **divinatory sign-test** in the formal sense: a future-conditional whose fulfillment will be read as direct divine designation. The structure recurs in modified form across the HB:
- **Judg 6:36-40** — Gideon's fleece-test. Two-part conditional sign-test: *if you will save Israel by my hand, behold I will put a fleece of wool on the threshing floor; if there be dew on the fleece only and on all the ground dry, then I shall know...* (Judg 6:36-37); the parallel second test inverts the condition (vv 39-40). The Gideon test is the canonical HB sign-seeking pericope.
- **1 Sam 14:8-10** — Jonathan's omen at Michmash. Two-part conditional: *if they say thus to us, 'Wait until we come to you,' then we will stand still in our place ... but if they say thus, 'Come up unto us,' then we will go up' (vv 9-10)*. Jonathan reads the Philistine response as divinely-designated battle-go-ahead.
- **Gen 24:12-14** is the inaugural instance — earlier in the canonical sequence than the Gideon and Jonathan parallels, and providing the template for the sub-genre.

**The chesed-link to Avraham.** The servant's prayer-form is editorially-tight: he names YHWH as *Elohei adoni Avraham* (not as his own deity), petitions for *chesed im adoni Avraham* (the *chesed-v-emet* virtue-pair; see *chesed-v-emet-steadfast-love-and-truth*), and reads the test-result as *kI-asita chesed im-adoni* (v 14b). The chesed-vocabulary saturates the prayer (4x in vv 12-14, plus v 27 and v 49 in the wider chapter), and the entire prayer is structured around the servant's claim that YHWH's chesed-toward-Avraham is the operative covenantal logic. The servant prays not as an individual-religious-subject but as an authorized-agent of Avraham's covenantal relation. The pattern is the **agent-prayer** sub-genre — recurring at Gen 32:10-13 (Ya'aqov's prayer at the Jabbok, also chesed-loaded), Exod 32:11-14 (Moses's intercession on Sinai), Num 14:13-19 (Moses again at Kadesh), 1 Sam 12:23 (Samuel's covenantal-prayer-duty), etc.

**The chapter as prayer-narrative.** The chapter is the most prayer-dense single chapter in Genesis: the servant prays at 24:12-14 (the test-formula), 24:21 (silent observation as the test runs), 24:26-27 (the prostrate-blessing after Rivkah's family-disclosure), 24:42-44 (the recitation of the prayer to Lavan and Bethuel), 24:48 (the second prostrate-blessing), 24:52 (the third prostrate-blessing after the family's assent). The recitation-block at vv 34-49 is itself a public-recitation of the prayer-test, transforming the private petitionary act into a witnessed-juridical narrative — the prayer becomes part of the diplomatic case for Rivkah's release.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders the prayer literally — invocation, petition, and test-formula preserved without apparatus-intrusive gloss. The first-non-patriarch-prayer status and the test-formula sub-genre cross-references (Judg 6, 1 Sam 14) are surfaced in the v 12 commentary.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The prayer-formula governs Gen 24:12, 24:13, 24:14 (the prayer + test in the narrative), and forward-references the recitation-block at Gen 24:42, 24:43, 24:44 (the public recitation). The sign-seeking sub-genre's later instances (Judg 6:36-40, 1 Sam 14:8-10) are documented in the rationale but not in the *appliesTo* — they are governed by their own future entries when those books are translated.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 165; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 385-386; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 141-142; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 142-144; Greenberg *Biblical Prose Prayer* 1983 pp. 7-12; Miller *They Cried to the Lord* 1994 pp. 100-105; Balentine *Prayer in the Hebrew Bible* 1993 pp. 60-65; Reventlow 1986 *Gebet im Alten Testament*. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the prayer-formula is grammatically explicit; (b) the first-non-patriarch-prayer status is a documentary fact verifiable by reading the prior Genesis chapters; (c) the sign-seeking sub-genre's cross-corpus distribution (Gen 24, Judg 6, 1 Sam 14) is verifiable by structural-comparative reading; (d) the four-element prose-prayer analysis is Greenberg's standard form-critical framework.

terms.literal: chesed and truth / loyal-love and faithfulness / steadfast-love and dependability
terms.standardEnglish: lovingkindness and truth (KJV); mercy and truth (ASV); steadfast love and faithfulness (NRSV); kindness and faithfulness (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: chesed and truth (preserving chesed untranslated; rendering emet as 'truth')
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:27 introduces the foundational HB virtue-pair *chesed v-emet* — *chesed and truth* — at the servant's doxological-blessing after Rivkah identifies herself as Bethuel's daughter (*va-yomer barukh YHWH Elohei adoni Avraham asher lo-azav chasdo va-amito me-im adoni*). The pair recurs at v 49 in the servant's appeal to Lavan and Bethuel (*v-atah im-yeshkhem osim chesed v-emet et-adoni*). The two occurrences in Gen 24 inaugurate one of the most cross-corpus-reachable virtue-formulae in the entire Hebrew Bible — the central HB ethical-and-divine virtue-pair, present from the patriarchal narratives through the Pentateuch, the historical books, the Psalter, the wisdom corpus, and the prophets.

**The cross-corpus distribution.** The pair *chesed v-emet* (or with reversed order, *emet v-chesed*) recurs at: **Gen 24:27, 24:49** (the chapter's two occurrences); **Gen 32:11** (Ya'aqov's prayer at the Jabbok — *qatonti mi-kol ha-chasadim u-mi-kol ha-emet asher asita et-avdekha*); **Gen 47:29** (Ya'aqov to Yosef — *v-asita imadi chesed v-emet al-na tiqberani b-Mitsrayim* — the burial-oath, paired with the oath-by-the-thigh; cross-reference to *yad-tachat-yarekh-oath-gesture*); **Exod 34:6** (the canonical YHWH self-revelation formula at Sinai — *YHWH YHWH El rachum v-chanun erekh apayim v-rav-chesed v-emet*); **Josh 2:14** (the spies' oath to Rachav — *naphshenu tachteykhem la-mut im lo tagidu et-davarenu zeh v-haya ba-tet YHWH lanu et-ha-aretz v-asinu imakh chesed v-emet*); **2 Sam 2:6** (David to the men of Yavesh-Gilad — *v-atah ya'as YHWH imakhem chesed v-emet*); **2 Sam 15:20** (David to Ittai the Gittite — *chesed v-emet*); **Ps 25:10** (the alphabetic Psalm — *kol-orchot YHWH chesed v-emet*); **Ps 40:11-12** (*emunatkha u-teshu'atkha ... chasdekha va-amitkha*); **Ps 57:4** (*yishlach me-shamayim v-yoshi'eni ... yishlach Elohim chasdo va-amito*); **Ps 61:8** (*chesed v-emet man yintzeruhu*); **Ps 85:11** (*chesed v-emet nifgashu tzedeq v-shalom nashaqu* — the chesed-emet-tzedek-shalom four-virtue tableau); **Ps 86:15** (the Exodus 34:6 formula reused); **Ps 89:15** (*tzedeq u-mishpat mekhon kisekha chesed v-emet yeqadmu fanekha*); **Ps 138:2** (*al-chasdekha v-al-amitekha*); **Prov 3:3** (*chesed v-emet al-ya'azvukha*); **Prov 14:22** (*chesed v-emet chorshei tov*); **Prov 16:6** (*b-chesed v-emet yekhupar avon*); **Prov 20:28** (*chesed v-emet yitzeru-melekh*); **Hos 4:1** (*ki ein emet v-ein chesed v-ein da'at Elohim ba-aretz* — the prophetic indictment); **Mic 7:20** (*titen emet l-Ya'aqov chesed l-Avraham* — the closing of Micah, citing the patriarchal-promise tradition). The formula thus runs continuously from the patriarchal narratives (Gen 24, 32, 47), through the canonical Sinai-self-revelation (Exod 34:6), into the historical books (Joshua, Samuel), the Psalter (Ps 25, 40, 57, 61, 85, 86, 89, 138), the wisdom corpus (Prov 3, 14, 16, 20), and the prophets (Hos 4:1, Mic 7:20). Twenty-plus principal occurrences across the canon.

**The lexeme *chesed*.** *Chesed* (חֶסֶד) is the central HB virtue-noun, with no single English equivalent. The classical lexicographies render variously: KJV *lovingkindness* (Coverdale's neologism, attempting to capture the loyal-love sense); ASV *mercy* (older Christian-traditional, narrower than the Hebrew); NRSV *steadfast love* (modern critical default, after Glueck); NJPS *kindness* / *faithful kindness* (modernizing but still partial); some scholarly literature *loyal-love*, *covenant-loyalty*, *covenant-faithfulness*. The lexeme's semantic core covers: (a) loyal-action toward a covenantal partner; (b) gracious-action exceeding what is owed; (c) the disposition or character-trait that grounds (a) and (b). **Glueck** *Das Wort Hesed im alttestamentlichen Sprachgebrauche als menschliche und göttliche gemeinschaftgemässe Verhaltungsweise* (1927; ET 1967 *Hesed in the Bible*) is the foundational monograph: chesed as *covenant-loyalty in the wider sense, the action-disposition of one party in a relationship toward the other party*. Glueck's covenantal reading was the modern critical default for fifty years. **Sakenfeld** *The Meaning of Hesed in the Hebrew Bible: A New Inquiry* (HSM 17, 1978) refines Glueck: chesed is not strictly contractual-covenant-bound but is the action of a more-powerful party on behalf of a less-powerful party in an established relationship, where the more-powerful party could have declined to act and the less-powerful party has no formal-legal claim. The Sakenfeld refinement is the modern critical consensus. **Clark** *The Word Hesed in the Hebrew Bible* (JSOT Sup 157, 1993) extends the corpus-comprehensive analysis with statistical and semantic-field methodology. **Britt** *Choosing the Right Word: Hesed and 'Faithful Love' in Translation* (2003) covers the translation-history.

**The lexeme *emet*.** *Emet* (אֱמֶת) is from the root *aman* (*to be firm, reliable, faithful*) and means *firmness, dependability, faithfulness, truth*. The standard English *truth* captures only part of the semantic range — *emet* is closer to *dependable-truth, reliable-truth, trustworthiness, faithfulness*. The pair *chesed v-emet* is thus a hendiadys of *loyal-action + dependability* — the two together name a single covenantal-virtue cluster: the loyal-action that is reliably-extended-over-time. **Moberly** *NIDOTTE* I:427-433 (on aman/emet); **HALOT** s.v. *emet*; **BDB** s.v. *emet* p. 54.

**The Exod 34:6 keystone.** The pair's canonical-formula status is set by **Exod 34:6** — the YHWH self-revelation at Sinai after the Golden Calf — where YHWH proclaims his thirteen-attributes (in the rabbinic count) with *rav-chesed v-emet* (*abounding in chesed and emet*) at the structural center. The Exod 34:6 formula is the most-cited divine-character verse in the HB, recurring in modified form at Num 14:18, Neh 9:17, Ps 86:15, 103:8, 145:8, Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, Nah 1:3 — the *Thirteen Middot* tradition in Jewish liturgy. The Gen 24:27 prayer's *chesed v-emet* invocation thus prefigures the Sinai self-revelation: the servant addresses YHWH in the very terms YHWH will use to self-reveal at Sinai. The structural-thematic anticipation is editorially-deliberate.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves *chesed* untranslated throughout the corpus (per established WoH lexeme-preservation policy on theologically-loaded terms with no single English equivalent — parallel to the *nefesh*, *Elohim*, *YHWH*, *Torah* preservations), and renders *emet* as *truth* per ASV. The pair is thus rendered *chesed and truth* at Gen 24:27, 24:49, 32:11, 47:29, etc. The choice avoids the ASV/KJV *lovingkindness* (Coverdale's invented compound, semantically partial), the NRSV *steadfast love* (capturing only the loyal-love dimension), and the NJPS *kindness* (narrower than the Hebrew). The preserved-untranslated *chesed* signals the reader to consult the apparatus; the Sakenfeld/Glueck philological background is given there.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The pair governs the two Gen 24 occurrences (vv 27, 49). The wider cross-corpus distribution (Gen 32:11, 47:29, Exod 34:6, Josh 2:14, 2 Sam 2:6 and 15:20, the Psalter, the wisdom corpus, Hos 4:1, Mic 7:20) is documented in the rationale for forward-reference; the *appliesTo* will be expanded as those passages are translated. The entry as inaugurated here governs the chesed-emet-pair-as-formula across all future occurrences; chapter-by-chapter additions will expand the *appliesTo* without modifying the rationale.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 166-167; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 385-386; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 142-143; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 146-147; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 119; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 178-179; Glueck *Das Wort Hesed* 1927 (ET *Hesed in the Bible*, 1967); Sakenfeld *The Meaning of Hesed in the Hebrew Bible* HSM 17 (1978); Clark *The Word Hesed in the Hebrew Bible* JSOTSup 157 (1993); Britt *Choosing the Right Word* (2003); Moberly NIDOTTE I:427-433; HALOT s.v. *chesed* and *emet*; BDB s.v. *chesed* p. 338 and *emet* p. 54; TDOT V:44-64.
