The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the pair is grammatically explicit at every cited verse; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Glueck/Sakenfeld/Clark scholarly tradition is the modern critical consensus; (d) the Exod 34:6 keystone-formula and the *Thirteen Middot* tradition are documentary; (e) the lexeme-preservation translation choice matches established WoH policy on theologically-loaded untranslatable terms.

terms.partOfSpeech: prepositional phrase + verb qal perfect 3ms + noun (subject)
terms.literal: from YHWH the matter has gone forth
terms.standardEnglish: The thing proceedeth from the LORD (ASV); This thing comes from the LORD (NRSV); The matter was decreed by the LORD (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: The matter has come forth from YHWH
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:50 contains Lavan and Bethuel's keystone response to the servant's recitation: *me-YHWH yatza ha-davar lo nukhal daber elekha ra o-tov* — *the matter has gone forth from YHWH; we cannot speak to you bad or good*.** The formula is a divine-providence acknowledgement: the family-decision is recognized as already determined by YHWH's action through the prior events (the servant's prayer, the well-side test, Rivkah's qualifying response), and the family's role is reduced from active-choosing to ratification of an already-decreed outcome. The phrase is rare in the HB; the construction *me-YHWH yatza ha-davar* (*from YHWH the matter has gone forth*) does not recur exactly elsewhere, but its constituent register and its theological function place it within a small cluster of HB divine-providence-acknowledgement formulae.

**The closest constructional parallel: Isa 45:23.** The verb *yatza* (qal perfect of *y-tz-'* — *to go out, go forth, issue, proceed*) with a divine-source preposition (*mi-* + divine name or pronoun) recurs at **Isa 45:23** — *bi nishbati yatza mi-pi tzedakah davar v-lo yashuv* (*by myself I have sworn, the word has gone forth from my mouth in righteousness, and shall not return*). The Isaianic verse is the structural counterpart: a *davar* (matter/word) has *yatza* (gone forth) from the divine source. The Gen 24:50 formula compresses this into the third-person acknowledgement: not *the word has gone forth from my mouth* (divine self-declaration), but *the matter has gone forth from YHWH* (human acknowledgement of the divine origin). The two verses share the *yatza* + *davar* core construction and the theological logic of irreversible divine speech-act.

**The wider providence-acknowledgement cluster.** The HB contains a small but distinctive cluster of formulae in which a human (often a non-Israelite) acknowledges divine sovereignty over an outcome that has already manifested: **Gen 31:24** (Lavan to Ya'aqov — Lavan's dream-warning, the second narrative occurrence of Lavan accepting a divine constraint); **Gen 41:38-39** (Pharaoh to Yosef — *can we find such a one as this, a man in whom the spirit of Elohim is?*); **Exod 8:15, 8:19, 9:27** (Pharaoh's hardening-and-acknowledgement sequence); **Num 23-24** (Balaam's repeated acknowledgement that he can speak only what YHWH puts in his mouth); **Josh 2:9-11** (Rachav's confession to the spies); **1 Sam 24:17-20** (Saul to David); **Dan 2:47, 3:28-29, 4:34-37, 6:26-27** (Nebuchadnezzar and Darius). The pattern is structural: a non-Israelite (or a wavering Israelite) figure acknowledges YHWH's sovereignty over an outcome they cannot resist or modify. Lavan and Bethuel's Gen 24:50 response is the patriarchal-narrative inaugural instance.

**The Lavan-before-Bethuel order.** The verse opens with *va-ya'an Lavan u-Bethuel va-yomru* — *and Lavan and Bethuel answered and said* — placing Lavan (the brother) before Bethuel (the father). The order is editorially-significant and has generated discussion. **Sarna** (*JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 167) reads the order as evidence of Lavan's primacy in the household-economy, comparing the **Nuzi marriage-contracts** (HSS V, JEN, AASOR 16) in which the bride's brother appears as the agnate-protector and the principal-agent in the marriage negotiation, while the father plays a secondary or absent role. The Nuzi parallels were extensively developed in mid-twentieth-century scholarship (Speiser 1955 *AASOR* 13; Gordon 1937 *Biblical Archaeologist*; later critically reviewed by Greengus 1975 *JAOS* 95 and Eichler 1989 *JANES* 18). The brother-as-agnate-protector reading remains a defensible interpretation of the Lavan-before-Bethuel order, though the Nuzi parallels are now treated more cautiously than in the 1950s-60s. **An alternative reading** treats the order as redactional: the chapter sets up Lavan as a recurring character in the cycle (Gen 29-31 — Ya'aqov's later twenty-year household-service in Lavan's house), and the editorial highlighting at v 50 prefigures Lavan's future narrative role. **A third reading** notes that Bethuel disappears from the chapter after v 50 (the *al-tabit* — *we cannot speak to you bad or good* — is the last he says; from v 51 forward the family-actors are Lavan and the mother and the brother and the mother again at vv 53, 55) and reads the order as a literary signal that Bethuel is already in retirement or recently deceased; some critical scholars (Westermann p. 387) propose that the *u-Bethuel* in v 50 is a textual interpolation, the v 50 response originally Lavan-only.

**The 'cannot speak bad or good' formula.** The phrase *lo nukhal daber elekha ra o-tov* — *we cannot speak to you bad or good* — is a HB merism: the speaker can render no judgment in either direction. The formula recurs at: **Gen 31:24, 31:29** (Lavan's two later occurrences of the *daber ra o-tov* idiom — Lavan apparently has a habit of this construction); **Num 24:13** (Balaam to Balak — *lo ukhal la'avor et-pi YHWH la'asot tovah o ra'ah mi-libi*); **2 Sam 13:22** (Avshalom — *lo-diber Avshalom et-Avshalom l-ra v-ad-tov*). The merism's logic: an outcome divinely-determined leaves no human judgment-space; the speaker can only ratify.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders the formula as *The matter has come forth from YHWH* — matching ASV's *the thing proceedeth from the LORD* in the literal register, modernizing the verb to *has come forth* (rather than the awkward *proceedeth*). The choice preserves the *yatza* perfect-aspect (the matter *has gone forth*, the action already complete) and the *from-YHWH* divine-source preposition. The Lavan-before-Bethuel order question is surfaced in the v 50 commentary; the entry does not pre-empt the question.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The formula governs Gen 24:50 — the verse's unique occurrence in this exact form. The wider providence-acknowledgement cluster (Gen 31, 41, Exod 8-9, Num 23-24, Josh 2, 1 Sam 24, Dan 2-6) is documented in the rationale; the closest constructional parallel (Isa 45:23) will be governed by its own future entry. The *appliesTo* is intentionally narrow.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 167; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 387; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 144-145; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 147-148; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 121; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 181; Speiser 1955 *AASOR* 13 (on the Nuzi parallels); Gordon 1937 *Biblical Archaeologist* (on the brother-as-agnate-protector); Greengus 1975 *JAOS* 95 (critical review of the Nuzi parallels); Eichler 1989 *JANES* 18 (further critical review); Selman 1976 *Tyndale Bulletin* 27 (on the patriarchal-Nuzi comparative project). The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the *yatza* + *davar* construction is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the wider providence-acknowledgement cluster is documented in the standard commentaries; (d) the Lavan-before-Bethuel order question is presented as an open editorial question, not as a project-distinctive synthesis.

terms.source: אֲחֹתֵנוּ אַתְּ הֲיִי לְאַלְפֵי רְבָבָה וְיִירַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו
terms.translit: achotenu at hayi l-alfei revavah v-yiyrash zar'ekh et sha'ar son'av
terms.partOfSpeech: blessing-formula (vocative + imperative + numerical compound + verb + noun-phrase)
terms.literal: our sister, you, become to thousands of ten-thousands, and may your seed possess the gate of those who hate it
terms.standardEnglish: Our sister, be thou the mother of thousands of ten thousands, and let thy seed possess the gate of those that hate them (ASV); May you, our sister, become thousands of myriads (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: Our sister, may you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:60 contains the matriarchal-fertility blessing-formula: *achotenu at hayi l-alfei revavah v-yiyrash zar'ekh et sha'ar son'av* — *our sister, may you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them*.** The verse is the family's send-off blessing to Rivkah as she departs with the servant for Kena'an. The formula is editorially-distinctive in two respects: (i) it is the **canonical matriarchal counterpart to the patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad** (dust / stars / sand — see *patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad*); (ii) it contains an **explicit verbal cross-reference to Gen 22:17** — Avraham's blessing at the *aqedah* — establishing a structural-literary inclusio across the Abraham cycle.

**The cross-reference to Gen 22:17.** The second half of the verse — *v-yiyrash zar'ekh et sha'ar son'av* (*may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them*) — verbally echoes Gen 22:17's *v-yiyrash zar'akha et sha'ar oyvav* (*may your seed possess the gate of his enemies*). The verbal echo is exact at the structural level: *yiyrash zar'X et sha'ar Y*, with the X-pronoun shifted from masculine-suffix to feminine-suffix (Avraham → Rivkah) and the Y-noun shifted from *oyvav* (*his enemies*) to *son'av* (*those who hate them* — the *them* anteceding to *zar'ekh*'s implied collectivity). The editorial deliberateness is mainstream: **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 169; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 387; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 122; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 148-149. The Avraham-cycle structural function: the bride enters the patriarchal family under a blessing that mirrors the patriarch's own foundational blessing, transferring the seed-promise from the masculine-patriarchal to the feminine-matriarchal register.

**The numerical-compound *l-alfei revavah*.** The phrase *l-alfei revavah* — *to thousands of ten-thousands* — is a HB numerical-multiplicity expression. *Eleph* (אֶלֶף) is *thousand*; *revavah* (רְבָבָה) is *ten-thousand / myriad*. The construct *alfei revavah* yields *thousands of ten-thousands*, equivalent to *myriads of myriads* or in modern English *millions*. The compound is rare: the only other HB occurrences of *alfei revavah* are at **Num 10:36** (the Ark's resting-formula — *shuvah YHWH rivvot alfei Yisrael*, with the order reversed); **Deut 33:17** (Moses's blessing of Joseph — *rivvot Efrayim v-hem alfei Menasheh*, with the order reversed and the construct distributed); **Dan 7:10** (Aramaic — *alef alfin yeshamshuneh v-ribbo ribbavan qadamohi yequmun* — the heavenly court of myriads). The Gen 24:60 instance is the **unique HB occurrence of the construct *l-alfei revavah* in a matriarchal-fertility blessing**.

**The matriarchal-counterpart-to-patriarchal-triad structural framing.** The patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad (Gen 13:16 dust; Gen 15:5 stars; Gen 22:17 sand) is the canonical masculine-patriarchal multiplicity-formula. The matriarchal counterpart at Gen 24:60 uses a different lexical register — *alfei revavah* rather than *ka-afar / ka-kokhavim / ka-chol* — but accomplishes the same theological function. The lexical distinction is editorially-meaningful: the masculine-patriarchal formulae use *cosmographic-natural* similes (dust, stars, sand), while the matriarchal formula uses a *numerical-arithmetic* expression. Trible (*God and the Rhetoric of Sexuality* 1978 pp. 27-31) reads the distinction as part of the HB's broader gendered-blessing-rhetoric: masculine-patriarchal blessings are framed in terms of cosmographic-spatial extension; matriarchal blessings are framed in terms of numerical-generational descent. The reading is not universally accepted but it documents the lexical-pattern fact.

**The Ruth 4:11-12 recurrence — the matriarchal-blessing as canonical form.** The formula's matriarchal-canonical status is confirmed by its explicit reuse at **Ruth 4:11-12** — the marriage of Boaz and Ruth, with the elders and people at the gate of Bethlehem blessing Ruth with the explicit invocation of the matriarchs: *yiten YHWH et-ha-ishah ha-ba'ah el-betkha k-Rachel u-k-Le'ah asher banu sh'tehem et-bet Yisrael v-aseh chayil b-Efratah u-q'ra-shem b-Beit Lechem* (*may YHWH make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and Le'ah, who together built the house of Israel; may you do worthily in Efratah and proclaim a name in Bethlehem*). The Ruth blessing extends the matriarchal-blessing-formula by naming Rachel and Le'ah as the canonical referents and by including Rivkah implicitly through the patriarchal-matriarchal genealogy. The Ruth 4:12 second half — *v-yhi vetkha k-veit-Peretz asher yaledah Tamar l-Yehudah* — extends the matriarchal-formula to Tamar, completing the canonical four-matriarch + Tamar tableau that underwrites the Davidic genealogy. The Ruth recurrence is the canonical confirmation of the matriarchal-blessing as a HB literary form.

**The patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad and the *iyrash sha'ar* sub-formula.** The *iyrash sha'ar* (*possess the gate*) sub-formula at Gen 24:60b is a verbal-military idiom for conquest of city-gates — the city-gate as the city's juridical-and-defensive center, possession of the gate as metonymic for possession of the city. The sub-formula recurs at Gen 22:17 (the patriarchal-blessing-formula cited above), at Mic 1:9 (the prophetic-judgment formula *higi'ah el-sha'ar ami*), and in the wider HB military-rhetoric (Judg 5:11). The Gen 22:17 and Gen 24:60 paired-occurrence establishes the formula as a structural element of the patriarchal-cycle's covenant-promise vocabulary.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *l-alfei revavah* as *to thousands of ten thousands* — matching ASV's literal preservation of the numerical compound, with the obsolete *be thou the mother of* (ASV) replaced by *may you become*. The *iyrash sha'ar son'av* second half is rendered *may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them*, matching ASV with the *son'av* (3ms suffix anteceding to *zar'ekh*'s implied collectivity) preserved as *them*. The matriarchal-blessing-formula's distinctiveness, the Gen 22:17 cross-reference, and the Ruth 4:11-12 canonical-recurrence are surfaced in the v 60 commentary.

**The patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad entry is NOT extended to Gen 24:60.** The triad entry is keyed to the masculine-patriarchal cosmographic-similes; the Gen 24:60 formula uses a distinct lexical register (*alfei revavah* + *iyrash sha'ar*) and merits its own entry as the matriarchal-canonical counterpart. The two entries together cover the patriarchal-and-matriarchal seed-multiplicity vocabulary.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The formula governs Gen 24:60 — the verse's unique HB occurrence as a matriarchal-fertility blessing. The Ruth 4:11-12 recurrence is documented in the rationale; the Ruth verses will be governed by their own entries when the book of Ruth is translated. The Gen 22:17 cross-reference is documented; the Gen 22:17 second half is already governed by the *patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad* entry. The *iyrash sha'ar* sub-formula cross-references are documented but not in *appliesTo*.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 169; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 387; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 146-147; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 148-149; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 122; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 181; Trible *God and the Rhetoric of Sexuality* (OBT, 1978) pp. 27-31; Fewell and Gunn *Gender, Power, and Promise* 1993; Bach *Women in the Hebrew Bible* 1999; Frymer-Kensky *Reading the Women of the Bible* 2002. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the Gen 22:17 cross-reference is verifiable by lexical comparison; (c) the Ruth 4:11-12 matriarchal-blessing-formula recurrence is verifiable by reading; (d) the *l-alfei revavah* HB-distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (e) the structural framing as matriarchal-counterpart-to-patriarchal-triad is documentable from the lexical-pattern facts without requiring synthesis.

terms.literal: to suach in the field [hapax verb, six readings]
terms.standardEnglish: to meditate in the field (ASV/KJV/Vulgate); to walk in the field (NRSV); to commune with himself in the field (some modern); to converse in the field (LXX)
terms.wohChoice: to meditate in the field (ASV/Vulgate default; flagged as hapax)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:63 contains the chapter's lexical hapax: Yitzchaq's evening-time activity is described as *la-suach ba-sadeh lifnot arev* — *to suach in the field at the turning of evening*.** The infinitive *la-suach* (לָשׂוּחַ) is from a root *s-w-ch* or *s-y-ch* (the root-identification itself uncertain) and is a HB hapax legomenon — the verb-form does not recur elsewhere in the canonical Hebrew Bible. The verse has generated **six principal readings** across the ancient versions, the rabbinic tradition, and modern philology.

**The six readings.**

**(1) 'to meditate' — Vulgate, Ibn Ezra, ASV, KJV, ESV.** The Vulgate renders *ad meditandum*; the ASV/KJV follow. The reading takes *suach* as cognate to *siach* (שִׂיחַ — *meditation, complaint, musing*) and reads Yitzchaq's evening-activity as contemplative-meditative. Ibn Ezra at Gen 24:63 supports this reading. The 'meditate' rendering is the modern-English-traditional default.

**(2) 'to converse / to chatter / to occupy oneself in thought' — LXX *adoleschēsai*.** The LXX *adoleschēsai* (ἀδολεσχῆσαι) means *to talk idly, to chatter, to spend time in conversation* (LSJ s.v.). The reading takes *suach* as related to *siach* (שִׂיחַ — *speech, talk, complaint*) in the speaking-register. The LXX reading is the earliest documentary interpretation of the hapax.

**(3) 'to pray' — Targum Onkelos, Rashi, the rabbinic-canonical reading.** Targum Onkelos renders *l-tzala'ah* (*to pray*). **Rashi** at Gen 24:63 cites the targumic reading and develops it into the canonical rabbinic interpretation: **b. Berakhot 26b** reads Yitzchaq's *la-suach* as the institution of the afternoon *Minchah* prayer-service — *Yitzchaq tiken tefillat minchah* (Yitzchaq instituted the afternoon prayer). The reading is paired in the rabbinic tradition with Avraham instituting the morning *Shacharit* (from Gen 19:27 *va-yashkem Avraham ba-boqer*) and Ya'aqov instituting the evening *Ma'ariv* (from Gen 28:11 *va-yiphga ba-maqom*). The Berakhot 26b passage is the canonical-liturgical etiology for the three-times-daily prayer-cycle of Rabbinic Judaism. The rendering Yitzchaq's *la-suach* as *to pray* is thus the canonical Jewish-liturgical reading.

**(4) 'to converse / to commune' — some modern English; NRSV margin.** Modern English translations including NJPS and the NRSV margin render *suach* as *converse* or *commune* — picking up the LXX-reading's speech-register but smoothing toward a meditative-contemplative register more compatible with modern English. **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 182 renders *to commune with himself*.

**(5) 'to complain / lament' — some modern philological.** Some modern lexicographies (BDB ambivalent; HALOT noting the difficulty) connect *suach* to *si'ach* in its complaint-lament register (the *siach* of Ps 55:3, 64:2 — *meditation* that shades into *complaint*). The reading would frame Yitzchaq's evening-activity as a lament — perhaps a continued mourning for Sarah (cf. v 67 *va-yinacheim Yitzchaq acharei imo*) or for Hagar's earlier expulsion (cf. Gen 25:11 *Yitzchaq dwelt by Be'er Lachai Ro'i*, the Hagar-encounter site). The lament-reading is a minority modern position.

**(6) 'to wander / to walk about' — modern philological; NRSV.** The NRSV renders *to walk in the field*. The reading takes *suach* as a variant of *sh-w-ch* (*to walk, go about, wander*) — comparing Akkadian *šâ?u* (to walk) and Aramaic cognates. The reading is supported by some modern philological treatments and is the NRSV's translation-choice. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 388 notes the philological uncertainty without committing to one reading.

**(A rejected seventh reading: 'to make water / urinate' — vulgar.)** A medieval reading (not in the canonical commentaries but recurring in some marginal-rabbinic strands) connects *la-suach* to *sh-w-ch* in the bodily-function register (cf. the rabbinic-Hebrew euphemism *shichah* for relieving oneself). The reading is rejected by all standard modern commentaries and noted only as a curio.

**The hapax-status fact.** The verb *la-suach* in this exact form (qal infinitive of s-w-ch / s-y-ch with the field-context) does not recur in the canonical Hebrew Bible. The related noun *siach* (meditation, complaint, talk) is well-attested across the Psalter (Ps 55:3, 64:2, 102:1, 104:34, 119:97, 119:99, 119:148) and the wisdom corpus (Job 7:13, 9:27, 10:1, 15:4, 21:4, 23:2), but the verb-form at Gen 24:63 is unique. The hapax-status is what generates the six-reading uncertainty.

**The Yitzchaq-instituted-Minchah tradition.** The rabbinic reading from b. Berakhot 26b is the most consequential interpretation: it makes Gen 24:63 the canonical-liturgical etiology for the afternoon prayer-service. The institution is doctrinally-significant in Rabbinic Judaism: the *Minchah* is one of the three-times-daily prayer-services, and its patriarchal-etiology at Gen 24:63 grounds the rabbinic liturgical-pattern in patriarchal-narrative precedent. The reading is liturgically-canonical but philologically-speculative — the hapax does not unambiguously support the pray-reading over the meditate-reading. The rabbinic tradition's choice of the pray-reading reflects the liturgical-etiological function rather than a purely philological argument.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *la-suach* as *to meditate* — the ASV/KJV/Vulgate default. The choice preserves the contemplative-meditative register that is closest to the Hebrew *siach*-cluster's semantic field, neutral on the meditate-vs.-pray-vs.-converse-vs.-wander question, and accessible to the modern English reader. The six readings and the Berakhot 26b afternoon-prayer-institution tradition are surfaced in the v 63 commentary. The 'meditate' choice does not preempt the rabbinic 'pray' reading — the commentary documents both.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The hapax governs Gen 24:63 — the only HB occurrence of the verb-form. The related *siach* noun-occurrences (Ps 55, 64, 102, 104, 119; Job 7, 9, 10, 15, 21, 23) are documented in the rationale; they are governed by their own future entries when those passages are translated.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 169; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 388; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 147; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 150-151; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 122; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 182; HALOT s.v. *suach / siach*; BDB s.v. *suach* p. 962; b. Berakhot 26b (the rabbinic-canonical Minchah-institution); Rashi at Gen 24:63; Ibn Ezra at Gen 24:63; Heinemann *Prayer in the Talmud* 1977. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the hapax-status is a documentary fact verifiable by lexical search; (b) the six readings are documented across the standard ancient and modern versions (Vulgate, LXX, Targum Onkelos, ASV/KJV, NRSV, NJPS, Speiser, Westermann); (c) the Berakhot 26b rabbinic-canonical Minchah-institution reading is a documentary fact of the rabbinic tradition; (d) the WoH translation choice (ASV/Vulgate *meditate*) is defended as the default that preserves the *siach*-cluster register without preempting the rabbinic alternative.

terms.literal: a beqa its weight / ten of gold their weight
terms.standardEnglish: of half a shekel weight ... of ten shekels weight of gold (ASV/KJV); a half-shekel in weight ... ten shekels in weight (NRSV); weighing a half-shekel ... weighing ten shekels (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: of half a shekel weight ... of ten shekels weight of gold
terms.rationale:
**Gen 24:22 contains the chapter's pre-coinage bullion-by-weight inventory: the servant gives Rivkah a golden nose-ring of *beqa mishqalo* (*half-shekel its-weight*) and two bracelets of *asarah zahav mishqalam* (*ten of gold their-weight*).** The verse documents two weight-units of the pre-coinage HB economy: *beqa* (the half-shekel) and the implied *shekel* (the standard weight-unit, here for gold). The transaction is the chapter's only weight-measurement instance and connects forward to the wider HB pre-coinage bullion-economy vocabulary (cf. *kesef-over-la-socher-merchant-weight-bullion-formula* at Gen 23:16 for the parallel weight-unit transaction at the Machpelah purchase).

**The *beqa* unit.** *Beqa* (בֶּקַע) is the **half-shekel** weight-unit. The lexeme is rare in the HB: the only other clear occurrence is at **Exod 38:26** — *beqa la-gulgolet machatzit ha-shekel b-shekel ha-qodesh* (*a beqa per head, half-a-shekel by the sanctuary-shekel*) — the half-shekel-per-head census-tax at the tabernacle-construction. The Exod 38:26 verse explicitly identifies the *beqa* as *machatzit ha-shekel* (*half of the shekel*), confirming the half-shekel weight-equivalence. The lexeme thus has two principal HB occurrences (Gen 24:22 and Exod 38:26), with the Exodus passage providing the explicit equivalence-formula.

**The *shekel* unit.** *Shekel* (שֶׁקֶל) is the standard HB weight-unit, attested across the Pentateuch (Gen 23:15-16, Exod 21:32, 30:13, 38:24-29, Lev 5:15, 27:3-25, Num 3:47-50, 18:16, Deut 22:19, 22:29, Josh 7:21, 1 Sam 9:8, 2 Sam 14:26, 18:11-12, 1 Kgs 10:16-17, 1 Chr 21:25, 29:4, 2 Chr 9:15-16, Neh 5:15, 10:33, Ezek 4:10, 45:12, Amos 8:5, Zech 11:12-13). The shekel's mass is debated — different shekel-standards (the sanctuary-shekel of Exod 30:13, the royal-shekel of 2 Sam 14:26, the merchant-shekel) coexisted; the standard-shekel mass is approximately 11.4 grams based on archaeological weight-stone evidence from Iron Age sites (Kletter 1998 *Economic Keystones* — the standard monograph). The Ekron-stones and the Judahite weight-stones inscribed *PYM*, *NTzP*, *BQA* (with the *beqa* explicitly attested in epigraphic inventory) confirm the multi-unit pre-coinage weight-system.

**The pre-coinage bullion-economy.** The HB patriarchal-narratives and the wider pre-Persian-period HB economy operated by **weighed bullion** rather than by coinage. Coinage was introduced in the Persian period (the *daric* of Darius I, c. 515-485 BCE; Greek coinage from c. 700 BCE in Lydia and the Greek poleis but slower to reach the Levant); the HB pre-Persian economy used weighed silver and gold by the *shekel* / *mina* / *talent* system. The *kesef over la-socher* formula at Gen 23:16 (see *kesef-over-la-socher-merchant-weight-bullion-formula*) — *silver passing for the merchant* — names the bullion-weight standard used in commercial transactions. The Gen 24:22 transaction is structurally parallel: weighed-gold (*asarah zahav mishqalam* — *ten of gold their-weight*) given as a betrothal-gift in the bullion-economy register.

**The betrothal-gift context.** The nose-ring (*nezem*) and bracelets (*tzemidim*) at v 22 are read in modern scholarship as **betrothal-gifts** — formal-juridical objects marking the initiation of the marriage-negotiation. The pattern is attested across the ANE: Mesopotamian *terhatu* (bride-price); Egyptian betrothal-gifts in the *šm.t* contracts; Aramaic Elephantine marriage-contracts (TAD B2.6, B3.8) preserve betrothal-gift inventories with similar gold-weight specifications. The Gen 24:22 weight-specification (*beqa* + *asarah*) is the formal-juridical content: the gift's value is recorded in weight-of-precious-metal because the gift is a quasi-contractual element of the marriage-negotiation. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 165 and **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 386-387 read the v 22 weight-specification in this light.

**The numerical-arithmetic of the gift.** The half-shekel nose-ring + two ten-shekel bracelets totals 20.5 shekels of gold ≈ 234 grams (using the standard 11.4g shekel-mass). At modern gold-prices the equivalent is roughly USD 18,000-20,000 (varying with gold-market); the value in the Late-Bronze-or-Iron-Age economy is correspondingly substantial. The gift's value is editorially-meaningful: the servant's transaction is significant-but-not-excessive, framed within the pre-coinage gift-economy norms. The vv 53 secondary gifts (silver vessels, gold vessels, garments, additional precious gifts to Lavan and the mother) escalate the value but the v 22 initial gift establishes the formal-juridical opening of the negotiation.

**Translation choice.** The WoH translation renders *beqa mishqalo* as *of half a shekel weight* and *asarah zahav mishqalam* as *of ten shekels weight of gold* — matching ASV/KJV. The 'half a shekel' rendering for *beqa* uses the explicit Exod 38:26 equivalence; the alternative would be transliterated *beqa* (preserving the lexeme), but the ASV practice of glossing the rare *beqa* with the explicit half-shekel-equivalent matches the established WoH practice of preserving the *shekel* as an English-loanword while glossing the rarer subordinate units. The weight-units' pre-coinage-economy context and the betrothal-gift function are surfaced in the v 22 commentary.

**Cross-corpus appliesTo.** The weight-units govern Gen 24:22 (the chapter's instance). The cross-reference to Exod 38:26 (the *beqa* equivalence) is documented in the rationale; the Exodus verse will be governed by its own future entry. The wider HB shekel-distribution is documented; future weight-unit verses (Gen 33:19 *qesitah*, the shekel-occurrences across the Pentateuch and the Prophets) will be governed by their own entries. The Gen 23:16 *kesef over la-socher* parallel weight-unit transaction is governed by the existing *kesef-over-la-socher-merchant-weight-bullion-formula* entry.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 165; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 386-387; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 143; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 145; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 118; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 179; Powell 1992 *ABD* VI:903-908 ('Weights and Measures'); Kletter 1998 *Economic Keystones: The Weight System of the Kingdom of Judah* (JSOTSup 276); Kletter 2004 ('Coinage Before Coins?' *Levant* 36); Stern 1982 *Material Culture of the Land of the Bible in the Persian Period*; HALOT s.v. *beqa* and *shekel*; BDB s.v. *beqa* p. 132 and *shekel* p. 1053. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the weight-units are grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the Exod 38:26 explicit *beqa = machatzit ha-shekel* equivalence is a documentary fact; (c) the pre-coinage bullion-economy is the modern critical consensus historical-economic context; (d) the betrothal-gift function is documented in the standard commentaries and supported by the ANE-comparative marriage-contract evidence; (e) the Iron Age shekel-mass calibration is documented in Kletter 1998 and corroborated by the epigraphic-weight-stone evidence including the inscribed *BQA* / *NTzP* / *PYM* weight-stones.

terms.source: וַיֶּעְתַּ֨ר יִצְחָ֤ק לַֽיהוָה֙ לְנֹ֣כַח אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ ... וַיֵּעָ֤תֶר לוֹ֙ יְהוָ֔ה
terms.translit: va-ye'tar Yitzchaq la-YHWH l-nokhach ishto ... va-ye'ater lo YHWH
terms.literal: and Yitzchaq entreated YHWH on behalf of his wife ... and YHWH was entreated of him
terms.standardEnglish: and Isaac entreated YHWH for his wife ... and YHWH was entreated of him (ASV/KJV); pleaded (NRSV); pleaded ... and YHWH responded (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and Yitzchaq entreated YHWH for his wife ... and YHWH was entreated of him (ASV-baseline, preserving the qal/niphal entreat-and-being-entreated symmetry)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:21 is the first HB occurrence of the verbal root *atar* (עָתַר) in the *entreat / intercede* prayer-register.** The verse stages a stylistically deliberate qal-niphal symmetry: Yitzchaq *va-ye'tar* (qal — *entreated*) YHWH, and YHWH *va-ye'ater* (niphal — *was entreated of, allowed himself to be entreated*) him. The qal-and-niphal pairing on the same root in adjacent clauses is unusual Hebrew prose-style and is a stylistic marker for the prayer-and-answer pattern: petition uttered, petition received. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 179 notes the stylistic device explicitly; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 414 reads the niphal as the *answer*-clause, completing the petition-answer movement. The Yitzchaq-Rivkah pregnancy follows the petition by twenty years (cf. Gen 25:20 — Yitzchaq is forty at marriage; Gen 25:26 — he is sixty at the twins' birth), and the *atar* prayer is editorially located at the chronological pivot.

**The *atar* root vs. other HB prayer-roots.** The HB has a layered prayer-vocabulary, each root with its own register:

- *atar* (עָתַר) — *entreat, intercede, plead* — the **intercessory** prayer-root, used heavily when a prayer is being offered for someone else or against a specific affliction. The root carries an *entreating-pressure* sense (BDB s.v. *atar* p. 801: 'to plead, entreat, supplicate'; HALOT s.v.: 'to entreat, pray, supplicate, particularly in intercession'). The niphal *ne'tar* (passive: *be entreated*) means *to allow oneself to be persuaded by intercession* — the divine response-form to *atar*-petitions.
- *hithpallel* (הִתְפַּלֵּל) — the standard HB **prayer**-verb, the canonical Hebrew word for 'pray' (see existing `hithpallel-first-prayer-occurrence` entry at Gen 20:7, 17). The lexeme's primary register is the formal petition / prayer-service register.
- *darash* (דָּרַשׁ) — *seek, inquire of* — the **oracle-consultation** prayer-root (see the companion *darash-yhwh-seeking-oracle* entry, Gen 25:22). Used heavily in the prophetic-consultation register: *to seek YHWH* through an oracular channel.
- *qara b-shem YHWH* (קָרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה) — *call on the name of YHWH* (see existing entry covering Gen 4:26 / 12:8 / 13:4 / 21:33) — the **cultic-invocation** formula.
- *t'fillah* / *t'chinnah* / *l-chalot pne YHWH* — additional nominal and verbal registers, each with subtler semantic ranges.

The Yitzchaq-Rivkah *atar* prayer is editorially-specific: it is *intercessory* (Yitzchaq prays for Rivkah, not for himself) and *afflictive-context* (the petition addresses Rivkah's barrenness). The lexical choice is registerially-precise.

**Cross-corpus distribution of *atar*.** The root has a focused distribution across the HB, concentrated in three principal contexts:

(i) **The Moses-plagues cycle in Exodus.** **Exod 8:4-5** (Moses *atar* to YHWH against the frog-plague at Pharaoh's request: *ha'tiru el-YHWH* — *entreat YHWH*); **Exod 8:24-26** (the swarm-plague: Moses *atar* and the swarms depart); **Exod 9:28** (the hail-plague: *ha'tiru el-YHWH* — Pharaoh's request); **Exod 10:17-18** (the locust-plague: *atar* and the locusts depart). The *atar*-plagues pattern is a stylistic-feature: each plague-cycle includes Pharaoh's request for *atar*-intercession and Moses' compliance, the petition-answer movement matching the Gen 25:21 qal-niphal symmetry.

(ii) **The Judges and Samuel cycles.** **Judg 13:8** (Manoah *atar* to YHWH for further instructions about the promised son — the Samson birth-narrative; the structural parallel to Gen 25:21 is editorially-deliberate: both are barrenness-intercession-followed-by-divine-birth-promise scenes). **2 Sam 21:14** (after the burial of Saul's seven sons, *va-ye'ater Elohim la-aretz* — *Elohim was entreated for the land*, the niphal divine-response form). **2 Sam 24:25** (David's altar at the threshing-floor: *va-ye'ater YHWH la-aretz* — closing the plague-and-altar narrative). **1 Chr 5:20** (the trans-Jordanian tribes' war: *va-ye'atru lo* — *YHWH was entreated of them*).

(iii) **The Chronicler and Ezra.** **2 Chr 33:13, 19** (the Manasseh prayer-tradition: *va-yitpalel elav va-ye'ater lo* — *and he prayed to him, and he was entreated of him* — the famous Manasseh repentance-narrative). **Ezra 8:23** (the *atar*-fast before the return: *va-natzumah va-n'vakshah me-Eloheinu al-zot va-ye'ater lanu*).

The distribution-pattern is consistent: *atar* names intercessory-petition in afflictive contexts (plague, barrenness, war, divine-anger, sin), with the niphal-passive marking the divine-response. The lexeme is the HB's signature *intercession*-vocabulary distinct from the broader *pray* and the more formal *seek-oracle* registers.

**The *l-nokhach ishto* clause.** The phrase *l-nokhach ishto* (לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ — *on behalf of / opposite / facing his wife*) is grammatically opaque. *L-nokhach* (from *nokhach* — *over against, opposite, in front of*) can be read either spatially (*facing his wife*, suggesting Yitzchaq prayed in her physical presence) or prepositionally (*on behalf of her*, the standard intercessory-direction). The ASV *for his wife* takes the second reading; some modern translations (Alter 2004 p. 130: *facing his wife*) preserve the spatial sense. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 179 reads the spatial-intercession sense: Yitzchaq prays with Rivkah present, the prayer joining husband and wife in shared petition. The image is editorially significant: the patriarch prays not in private but in the presence of the petition's beneficiary. The translation preserves the ASV idiom *for his wife* with the spatial-presence sense noted in commentary.

**The barrenness-and-prayer pattern across Genesis.** The Hebrew Bible's matriarchal-barrenness pattern repeats across three generations: Sarah (Gen 11:30, 16:1, 17:17, 18:11-15, 21:1-7), Rivkah (Gen 25:21), Rachel (Gen 29:31, 30:1-2, 30:22-24). Each follows the same narrative-shape: announced barrenness, extended waiting, divine response, miraculous birth. The Gen 25:21 instance is the second of the three. **Rachel's** *va-yizkor Elohim et-Rachel va-yishma eleha Elohim* (Gen 30:22 — *Elohim remembered Rachel and Elohim heard her*) preserves the same petition-answer movement using different vocabulary (*zakhar* + *shama* in place of *atar* qal + niphal). The three-fold matriarchal pattern is structurally deliberate.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 178-179; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 413-414; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 175-176; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 175-176; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 130; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 194-195; Greenberg *Biblical Prose Prayer* 1983 (the standard monograph on HB prayer-vocabulary; the *atar* root treated at pp. 7-8, 19-21); Wagner *Beten und Klagen* 1971 (German treatment of the HB prayer-vocabulary; *atar* discussed in section on intercessory-prayer); HALOT s.v. *atar* and BDB s.v. *atar* p. 801. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the root *atar* is grammatically and lexically explicit; (b) the qal-niphal symmetry at Gen 25:21 is grammatically explicit; (c) the cross-corpus distribution across Exodus, Judges, Samuel, Chronicles, and Ezra is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the prayer-vocabulary-stratification (atar vs. hithpallel vs. darash vs. qara b-shem) is mainstream HB philology (Greenberg 1983; Wagner 1971); (e) the matriarchal-barrenness-pattern reading is documented in Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, and Hamilton.

terms.partOfSpeech: qal infinitive construct of *darash* + direct-object marker + divine name
terms.literal: and she went to seek / inquire of YHWH
terms.standardEnglish: and she went to inquire of YHWH (ASV/KJV/NRSV); and she went to inquire of the LORD (NIV); and she went to inquire of YHWH (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and she went to inquire of YHWH (ASV-baseline; the canonical English rendering of the oracle-consultation register)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:22 contains the *first HB occurrence of *darash YHWH** in the oracle-consultation prayer-register.** Rivkah, distressed by the intrauterine struggle of the twins, *va-telekh li-drosh et-YHWH* — *and she went to inquire of YHWH*. The construction *darash et-YHWH* (with the direct-object marker *et*) governs the lexeme's central oracle-consultation register: a deliberate, located act of seeking divine guidance through an authorized channel (priest, prophet, oracle-site). The Rivkah-inquiry is the foundational HB scene of female-prophetic-consultation, and the verse is editorially-load-bearing as the source-text for the *darash YHWH* lexical-tradition that runs across the entire HB historiographic and prophetic corpus.

**The *darash* root and its registers.** *Darash* (דָּרַשׁ) has three principal HB registers: (i) the **oracle-consultation** register (*darash YHWH* / *darash Elohim* — *seek YHWH / Elohim*), the load-bearing prophetic-consultation lexeme; (ii) the **investigative** register (*darash et-ha-davar* — *seek out the matter*, in legal-judicial contexts, e.g., Deut 13:14, 17:4, 17:9, 19:18); (iii) the **wisdom-seeking** register (*darash chochmah* — *seek wisdom*; *darash et-YHWH* in the post-exilic devotional-religious sense, e.g., Ps 14:2 / 53:3, 1 Chr 28:9). The Gen 25:22 instance is the first oracle-consultation occurrence; the lexeme's other registers postdate the patriarchal narratives.

**Cross-corpus distribution of *darash YHWH* in the oracle-consultation register.** The lexeme recurs as a canonical phrase across the entire HB historiographic and prophetic corpus. Principal occurrences:

- **Gen 25:22** — the first occurrence (here).
- **Exod 18:15** — Moses' justification-of-the-judicial-system response to Jethro: *ki yavo elai ha-am li-drosh Elohim* — *the people come to me to inquire of Elohim*. The verse establishes the Mosaic-judicial-oracular role.
- **1 Sam 9:9** — the canonical **lexical-historical note**: *l-fanim b-Yisrael ko amar ha-ish b-lekhto li-drosh Elohim lekhu v-nelkha ad-ha-ro'eh ki la-navi ha-yom yiqare l-fanim ha-ro'eh* — *Formerly in Israel, when a man went to inquire of Elohim, he would say, 'Come, let us go to the seer'; for he who is now called a Prophet (navi) was formerly called a Seer (ro'eh)*. The verse is the canonical-HB note on the *ro'eh*/*navi* lexical-historical shift and the foundational reference-point for understanding the prophetic-institution's pre-monarchic *darash*-consultation form.
- **1 Sam 28:6-7** — Saul's distress before the Endor seance: *va-yishal Sha'ul ba-YHWH v-lo anahu YHWH ... va-yomer Sha'ul l-avadav baqshu li eshet ba'alat ov v-elkha eleha v-edreshenah-bah* — *and Saul inquired of YHWH, but YHWH did not answer him ... and Saul said to his servants, Seek for me a woman who is a medium, that I may go to her and inquire of her*. The text uses the *darash* root for both the legitimate-YHWH-consultation (frustrated) and the illegitimate-medium-consultation (sought as substitute). The Saul scene is the canonical-HB cautionary on consultation-channel-legitimacy.
- **1 Kgs 14:5** — the Ahijah-Jeroboam scene: Jeroboam's wife is sent to *li-drosh me-imo davar* about the sick child. The first monarchic-period *darash* through a named prophet.
- **1 Kgs 22:5-8** — the famous Jehoshaphat-Ahab scene before Ramoth-Gilead: *drosh-na ka-yom et-davar YHWH* (Jehoshaphat to Ahab — *inquire today of the word of YHWH*); Ahab assembles the 400 prophets; Micaiah is summoned with Ahab's lament *sneitiv ki lo-yitnabe alai tov ki im-ra* — *I hate him for he never prophesies good concerning me, but evil*. The scene is the canonical-HB illustration of the *darash YHWH* legitimate-prophetic-channel vs. the court-prophet-corruption tension.
- **2 Kgs 1:2-16** — the Ahaziah Baal-Zebub scene: *lekhu dirshu b-Baal Zevuv elohei Eqron* — *go inquire of Baal-Zebub the god of Ekron*; Elijah's three-fold rebuke *ha-mi-b'li-ein-Elohim b-Yisrael atem holkhim li-drosh* — *is it because there is no Elohim in Israel that you are going to inquire?*. The scene is the canonical-HB illustration of the Yahwistic-vs.-foreign-deity *darash*-channel distinction.
- **2 Kgs 3:11** — the Jehoram-Jehoshaphat-Edom expedition: *ein-poh navi la-YHWH v-nidresha et-YHWH me-oto* — *is there not a prophet of YHWH, that we may inquire of YHWH through him?* — leading to the Elisha-oracle.
- **2 Kgs 22:13-18 / 2 Chr 34:21-26** — the Josiah-reform Huldah-consultation: *lekhu dirshu et-YHWH ba'adi u-v'ad ha-am u-v'ad kol-Yhudah* — *go inquire of YHWH for me and for the people and for all Judah*. The senior royal officials are sent to the prophetess Huldah; the consultation produces the Josiah-reform oracle. The Huldah-consultation is the second-named-female-prophetic-consultation scene of the HB, structurally paralleling Rivkah's Gen 25:22 inquiry across the canonical arc.
- **Jer 21:2** — the Zedekiah-Jerusalem-siege consultation: *drosh-na ba'adenu et-YHWH ki Nevukhadretzar melekh-Bavel nilcham aleinu*. The siege-and-inquiry scene; Jeremiah's negative-oracle response.
- **Jer 37:7** — the second Zedekiah-Jeremiah inquiry, with the same negative-oracle outcome.
- **Ezek 14:1-11; 20:1-3** — the elders-of-Israel inquiries during the exilic period; YHWH's refusal to be inquired-of by the idolatrous elders. The post-exilic *darash YHWH* takes on additional moral-conditional qualification.

The lexeme's distribution is corpus-spanning and the consistent register is **prophetic-oracle-consultation through an authorized channel**: priest, prophet, prophetess, oracle-site. The Rivkah-inquiry is the lexically-foundational instance.

**Where did Rivkah go to inquire?** The MT is editorially-silent on the consultation-location: *va-telekh li-drosh et-YHWH* names the action but not the channel. The textual silence is itself the editorial-point. The major interpretive traditions divide:

(a) **The rabbinic-canonical reading: the yeshivah of Shem or Eber.** **Targum Pseudo-Jonathan** at Gen 25:22 reads Rivkah's destination as the beit-midrash of Shem; **Genesis Rabbah 63:6** locates the inquiry at the yeshivah of Shem; **Rashi** at Gen 25:22 follows Genesis Rabbah. The rabbinic reading harmonizes the patriarchal narrative with the rabbinic-canonical chain-of-tradition: Shem (Noah's son, conventionally surviving into the patriarchal period in rabbinic chronology) serves as the prophetic-channel before the institution of the Levitical priesthood.

(b) **The patriarchal-altar-site reading.** Some medieval commentators (Ibn Ezra at Gen 25:22; some modern conservative commentators) read Rivkah as inquiring at one of the named patriarchal altar-sites — Be'er Lachai Ro'i (Gen 25:11), Hebron / Mamre (Gen 18), or Beer-Sheva (Gen 21:33). The reading is geographically plausible (Yitzchaq's residence at Be'er Lachai Ro'i is named immediately at v 11) but not textually-explicit.

(c) **The modern critical reading: the consultation-channel is editorially-silent.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 180 and **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 415 read the silence as deliberate: the patriarchal-period narrative does not specify the consultation-channel because no institutional channel is yet established. The reading preserves the editorial-deliberateness of the textual silence and resists harmonization with later institutional structures.

(d) **Direct-divine-encounter reading.** Some readers (mostly devotional and not modern-critical) take the v 23 *va-yomer YHWH lah* (*and YHWH said to her*) as a direct unmediated divine response, suggesting Rivkah received the oracle without an institutional channel — in which case *li-drosh* names the petition-act without specifying a location. The reading is grammatically possible but minority.

The WoH translation preserves the textual silence and the four-reading interpretive-distribution is documented in commentary.

**NT reception at Rom 9:10-12.** Paul's *Romans 9:10-13* reads the Gen 25:23 oracle (*the elder shall serve the younger*) as a paradigm of **divine election prior to action**: *kai ou monon de, alla kai Rebekka ex henos koitēn echousa Isaak tou patros hēmōn ... hina hē kat' eklogēn prothesis tou Theou menē* — *and not only this, but Rebekah also having conceived by one, even by Isaac our father ... that the purpose of God according to election might stand*. The Pauline reading takes the v 23 oracle as election-of-corporate-peoples (Edom and Israel) and developmentally the Calvinist tradition reads it as foundational predestination-of-individuals; the Arminian and the modern Catholic readings hold to the corporate-peoples interpretation. The reception is flagged here as downstream of the Hebrew text and treated extensively in the companion *rav-yaavod-tzair-younger-displaces-elder* entry (Gen 25:23). The Gen 25:22 *darash* is the petition-act that precedes the oracle; the oracle's content is treated in the v 23 entry.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 180; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 415; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 176; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 176-177; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 130; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 195; Wagner *Beten und Klagen* 1971 (HB prayer-vocabulary, *darash* discussion); Greenberg *Biblical Prose Prayer* 1983; Long *The Reign and Rejection of King Saul* 1989 (on the 1 Sam 28 *darash* dispute); HALOT s.v. *darash* and BDB s.v. *darash* p. 205; Genesis Rabbah 63:6; Rashi at Gen 25:22; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan at Gen 25:22. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *darash* is grammatically explicit; (b) the first-occurrence status at Gen 25:22 is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the cross-corpus distribution across the Pentateuch, the Former Prophets, the Latter Prophets, and the Writings is verifiable; (d) the 1 Sam 9:9 lexical-historical note is a documentary fact of the HB; (e) the four-reading interpretive distribution (rabbinic-Shem, patriarchal-altar, critical-silence, direct-divine-encounter) is documented in the standard commentaries; (f) the Rom 9:10-12 NT reception is documentary but flagged-not-preempted.

terms.partOfSpeech: adjective + qal imperfect + adjective (the syntax governs the subject-ambiguity)
terms.literal: and great / elder shall serve little / younger (subject-object order grammatically ambiguous)
terms.standardEnglish: and the elder shall serve the younger (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS dominant); and the elder, the younger shall serve (NJPS footnote; minority)
terms.wohChoice: and the elder shall serve the younger (ASV-dominant; the editorial-deliberate subject-ambiguity preserved in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:23 is the chapter's oracular keystone, and the verse is editorially-load-bearing across multiple traditions: the founding-oracle of the Esav-Ya'aqov rivalry, the source-text of the Pauline-election doctrine, and the foundational HB instance of the *younger-displaces-elder* narrative pattern that runs through the entire Genesis arc.**

**Three distinct issues at the verse.**

**(a) The ketiv/qere on *goyim* (the opening clause).** The MT ketiv reads גיים (*gyym* — defective plural; ambiguous pointing); the qere reads גוֹיִם (*goyim* — *nations*, the standard plural-form with full waw). The qere is the standard ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS practice and the dominant scholarly reading. The defective ketiv has generated multiple interpretive proposals: (i) **scribal lapse** — the simplest reading; (ii) **deliberate paronomasia with *ge'im*** (*proud / haughty ones*) — Targum Ps-J's reading, taking the defective ketiv as a hint at the proud-character of the two nations; (iii) **graphic-similarity ambiguity** — Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 180 cites **Aptowitzer** on the possible hint at *gyym* meaning *peoples* in a register distinct from the political *goyim*; (iv) **rabbinic-midrashic reading** — *b. Avodah Zarah 11a* reads the defective ketiv as a hint at the two great proud-empires Rome and Persia, foreshadowing the post-biblical political enmities. The WoH translation follows the qere *goyim* per standard MT practice and surfaces the ketiv-variant in commentary.

**(b) Subject-ambiguity at *v-rav ya'avod tza'ir* (the closing clause).** This is the verse's editorially-deliberate ambiguity and the most consequential translation-decision of the chapter. The Hebrew word-order leaves the subject grammatically ambiguous: *rav* (*great / elder / older*) and *tza'ir* (*little / younger*) are both nominative-form adjectives functioning as substantives; the verb *ya'avod* (*shall serve*, qal imperfect 3ms) does not disambiguate subject from object. Hebrew word-order is generally Verb-Subject-Object (VSO) but adjectival-subject-and-object pairs flanking a verb can be read in either direction.

**Reading 1 (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS dominant):** *the elder shall serve the younger* (*rav* = subject, *tza'ir* = object). On this reading the oracle predicts a reversal of the natural primogeniture: the older twin (Esav) will serve the younger twin (Ya'aqov). The reading is supported by (i) Hebrew word-order convention (subject typically precedes object after the verb in fronted-construction Hebrew prose); (ii) the narrative outcome of Gen 25-36 (Esav sells the birthright, loses the blessing, settles in Edom; Ya'aqov receives both birthright and blessing, becomes Israel); (iii) the LXX reading *ho meizōn douleusei tō elassoni* — *the greater shall serve the lesser* — unambiguously supports Reading 1; (iv) the rabbinic-canonical reading (Targum Onkelos, Targum Ps-J, Rashi, Ibn Ezra, Genesis Rabbah 63:7) all read R1.

**Reading 2 (NJPS footnote; Speiser AB; minority modern critical):** *the elder, the younger shall serve* (*rav* = object, *tza'ir* = subject). On this reading the oracle predicts the more conventional outcome: the younger twin will serve the older. The reading is supported by (i) the Hebrew syntactic possibility — adjectival-pair constructions can in principle reverse subject-object; (ii) **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196 reads R2 explicitly, arguing that the ambiguity is editorial and the conventional outcome would be R2; (iii) **NJPS** retains R1 in the main text but footnotes R2 as a textually-defensible alternative. R2 is a minority reading and not the modern critical consensus.

**Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 181 documents both readings explicitly and presents R1 as the dominant. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 415 reads the ambiguity as 'a real possibility in the Hebrew' and notes that the narrative outcome supports R1 but does not foreclose R2. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 176 reads R1 with brief acknowledgment of R2. **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 177-178 reads R1.

The WoH translation follows R1 (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS dominant) and surfaces R2 in commentary as the minority position.

**(c) The Pauline reception at Romans 9:10-13.** Paul cites the verse at **Rom 9:12** as the proof-text for divine election prior to action: *kai ou monon de, alla kai Rebekka ex henos koitēn echousa Isaak tou patros hēmōn (11) mēpō gar gennēthentōn mēde praxantōn ti agathon ē phaulon, hina hē kat' eklogēn prothesis tou Theou menē, (12) ouk ex ergōn all' ek tou kalountos, errēthē autē hoti ho meizōn douleusei tō elassoni* — *and not only this, but Rebekah also having conceived by one, by Isaac our father (11) for the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or bad, in order that the purpose of God according to election might stand, (12) not of works, but of him that calls, it was said to her, The elder shall serve the younger*. Paul follows the LXX wording (R1: *ho meizōn douleusei tō elassoni*) and reads the oracle as the foundational HB demonstration of divine election prior to merit.

The Pauline reception has generated two principal interpretive traditions:

(i) **The Calvinist / Reformed tradition** reads Rom 9:10-13 as the foundational predestination text: divine election operates at the individual-soteriological level, prior to and independent of human action. Calvin *Institutes* III.21-24 develops the reading; the Westminster Confession of Faith III, the canons of Dort. The Calvinist reading takes the verse as supporting double-predestination (election to salvation; reprobation to damnation).

(ii) **The Arminian / Wesleyan / modern Catholic tradition** reads Rom 9:10-13 as election-of-corporate-peoples, not individual-soteriological predestination: the *meizōn / elassoni* names the corporate destinies of Edom and Israel as nations, not the individual soteriological status of Esav and Ya'aqov as persons. The reading is supported by the original Genesis 25:23 context (the verse opens with *shnei goyim b-vitnekh u-shnei l'umim mi-me'ayikh* — *two nations are in your womb, and two peoples shall be separated from your bowels* — which frames the oracle as corporate-political). The modern Catholic exegetical tradition (e.g., the **Jerome Biblical Commentary**, the **Catholic Study Bible**) reads the corporate-peoples interpretation; the Eastern Orthodox tradition similarly. Modern critical NT scholarship (e.g., **Cranfield** *Romans* ICC, **Dunn** *Romans 9-16* WBC, **Käsemann** *Commentary on Romans*) largely sides with the corporate-peoples reading.

**The Pauline reception is flagged-not-preempted.** The WoH apparatus surfaces both interpretive traditions (Calvinist-predestinarian; Arminian-corporate-peoples) and does not adjudicate. The Rom 9:12 citation belongs to NT-reception history; the Gen 25:23 verse itself is editorially-confined to the patriarchal-political register (the *two nations* / *two peoples* framing of the opening clauses).

**The younger-displaces-elder pattern across Genesis.** Gen 25:23 is the foundational HB instance of the *younger-displaces-elder* narrative pattern that recurs across the entire Genesis arc. The pattern's instances:

- **Abel (younger) over Cain (elder)** — Gen 4:1-5; YHWH regards Abel's offering but not Cain's. The first instance, ending in violence.
- **Yitzchaq (younger) over Yishma'el (elder)** — Gen 17:18-21, 21:9-13; the covenant-line passes through Yitzchaq, not the firstborn Yishma'el. The second instance.
- **Ya'aqov (younger) over Esav (elder)** — Gen 25:23 (the oracle here), 25:29-34 (the birthright-sale), 27 (the blessing-deception). The third instance and the load-bearing-keystone of the pattern.
- **Yosef (younger) over the elder brothers** — Gen 37, 41-50; the eleventh of twelve sons rises to second-in-Egypt and saves the family. The fourth instance, structurally most-elaborated.
- **Ephraim (younger) over Manasseh (elder)** — Gen 48:13-20; Ya'aqov deliberately crosses his hands in the blessing of Yosef's sons, placing the firstborn-blessing on the younger Ephraim. The fifth instance, most explicitly-staged.
- **Perez (younger) over Zerach (elder)** — Gen 38:27-30; the twin-birth of Tamar's sons re-stages the Gen 25:24-26 twin-birth scene, with the younger Perez breaking through ahead of the elder Zerach. Perez becomes the ancestor of David (Ruth 4:18-22). The sixth instance.

The pattern is **structurally-deliberate** across the Genesis arc and is editorially-coordinated with the Gen 25:23 oracle. The HB then extends the pattern into the post-Genesis material: **Moses (younger)** over Aaron (elder); **David (youngest of eight)** over his elder brothers (1 Sam 16-17); **Solomon (younger)** over Adonijah (elder) in the 1 Kgs 1-2 succession; **Jeremiah** over the established prophetic-priestly families. The pattern's HB function is to assert *election over primogeniture* — the covenantal-political principle that divine selection operates by criteria other than birth-order.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 178-181 (the standard balanced philological-and-thematic treatment); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 414-416; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 176-177; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 177-178; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 195-196 (the Reading 2 minority); Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 131; Cranfield *Romans 9-16* ICC 1979 (NT reception, corporate-peoples reading); Dunn *Romans 9-16* WBC 1988; Käsemann *Commentary on Romans* 1980; Bell *Provoked to Jealousy* 1994 (Pauline-reception study); Genesis Rabbah 63:7; Rashi at Gen 25:23; Targum Onkelos and Targum Pseudo-Jonathan at Gen 25:23. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the ketiv/qere on *goyim* is a documentary MT feature; (b) the subject-ambiguity at *v-rav ya'avod tza'ir* is grammatically explicit and is documented in the standard commentaries; (c) the modern critical consensus on R1 (vs. Speiser's minority R2) is documented; (d) the Pauline reception at Rom 9:10-13 is a documentary NT fact, flagged-not-preempted in the apparatus; (e) the younger-displaces-elder pattern across the Genesis arc is verifiable by structural-narrative analysis and is mainstream in modern Genesis scholarship.

terms.source: וְיָד֤וֹ אֹחֶ֙זֶת֙ בַּעֲקֵ֣ב עֵשָׂ֔ו וַיִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמ֖וֹ יַעֲקֹ֑ב
terms.translit: v-yado ochezet ba-aqev Esav va-yiqra shmo Ya'aqov
terms.partOfSpeech: proper noun + noun (heel) + denominative verb root (supplant)
terms.literal: and his hand grasped at the heel of Esav, and his name was called Ya'aqov / heel-grasper
terms.standardEnglish: and his hand had hold on Esau's heel; and his name was called Jacob (ASV/KJV); ... his name was called Jacob (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and his hand had hold on Esav's heel; and his name was called Ya'aqov (ASV-baseline, with both the heel and the supplant etymologies surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:26 contains the first explicit Ya'aqov-name-etymology: *v-yado ochezet ba-aqev Esav va-yiqra shmo Ya'aqov* — *and his hand grasped at Esav's heel, and his name was called Ya'aqov*.** The name *Ya'aqov* (יַעֲקֹב) is paronomastically linked to *aqev* (עָקֵב — *heel*): Ya'aqov is the *heel-grasper*, named at the moment of his birth from the gesture of grasping his elder twin's heel. The verbal-form *Ya'aqov* (impf 3ms of a verbal root *aqav* / *aqab*) is etymologically connected to the noun *aqev* via the shared consonantal root *ʿ-q-b*. The name's double-meaning — *heel* (the body-part) and *supplant / overreach / cheat* (the denominative verb) — is editorially-load-bearing across the entire Ya'aqov-narrative.

**The two-fold etymology.**

(a) **The *heel*-etymology (Gen 25:26).** The name's first explicit etymological grounding is in the *aqev* (heel) imagery of the birth-grasp. *Ya'aqov* on this reading means *heel-grasper* or *one-who-follows-at-the-heel* — a positive or at least neutral name. The naming is performed by Yitzchaq (the verb *va-yiqra shmo* is impersonal 3ms in MT, but the canonical-rabbinic reading identifies Yitzchaq as the namer; cf. Rashi at Gen 25:26 citing Genesis Rabbah 63:8).

(b) **The *supplant*-etymology (Gen 27:36; Hos 12:4).** The name's second etymological reach is to the verbal root *aqav* (denominative from *aqev*) in the *supplant / overreach / cheat* sense. The denominative-verb register is realized programmatically at two later HB verses:

- **Gen 27:36** — Esav's lament after the blessing-deception: *va-yomer ha-khi qara shmo Ya'aqov va-ya'aqveni zeh paamayim et-b'khorati laqach v-hineh atah laqach birkhati* — *and he said, Is it not rightly that his name is called Ya'aqov, for he has supplanted me these two times: he took away my birthright, and behold, now he has taken away my blessing*. Esav's etymology-of-the-name explicitly connects *Ya'aqov* with *va-ya'aqveni* (qal impf with 1cs suffix — *he has supplanted me*) and identifies the *supplant* sense as the name's operative meaning. The Gen 27:36 etymology is the canonical-HB internal-text-explicit derivation of the supplant-sense from the heel-name.

- **Hos 12:4** — the Hosea-prophet's retrospective: *ba-beten aqav et-achiv u-v-ono sarah et-Elohim* — *in the womb he supplanted his brother, and in his strength he strove with Elohim*. The Hosea passage doubles the etymology: the supplant-action at the womb-grasp (Gen 25:26) and the strive-with-Elohim at Penuel (Gen 32:25-33, where the name is changed to *Yisra'el* — *one who strives with El*). Hosea reads the two namings as a sequenced moral-trajectory: Ya'aqov is named for grasping, renamed for striving; the prophet's framing is admonitory.

**The narrative-deployment of the supplant-etymology.** The *supplant* register is not narrated at Gen 25:26 — the verse uses only the neutral *heel*-imagery. The supplant-sense is *activated* in the following narrative-units:

- **Gen 25:27-34** — the birthright-sale; Ya'aqov *supplants* by taking advantage of Esav's hunger. The action is staged as Ya'aqov's first *aqav*-act.
- **Gen 27:1-40** — the blessing-deception; Ya'aqov supplants by deceiving the blind Yitzchaq. The action is the second and most-elaborate *aqav*-act. Esav's lament at v 36 makes the etymology explicit.
- **Gen 31:1-21** — the departure from Lavan with the maximized flocks; some readings of the speckled-and-striped flocks deceive Lavan via genealogically-creative breeding (cf. *aqod* / *naqod* — striped / spotted; the lexical-play between the breeding-vocabulary and the Ya'aqov-name is editorially-deliberate).
- **Gen 32:25-33** — the night-wrestle at Penuel; the *aqav*-trajectory culminates in the wrestling-match where Ya'aqov is renamed *Yisra'el* (*one who strives with El*) and is left limping (the heel-injury inverts the heel-grasping birth-gesture: Ya'aqov, the heel-grasper, is wounded in the *kaf yerekh* — *socket of the thigh* — and walks with a limp thereafter).

The full Ya'aqov-arc is structurally framed by the *aqev* / *aqav* etymological-trajectory: from heel-grasp (birth) through serial-supplant-acts (birthright, blessing, Lavan-flocks) to the wrestling-rename and the resulting limp (Penuel). The Gen 25:26 instance is the lexically-foundational moment.

**Historical-onomastic context.** The name *Ya'aqov* or *Ya'aqov-el* is attested in 2nd-millennium-BCE Egyptian and Akkadian sources outside the Hebrew Bible:

- **Egyptian Hyksos king Ya'qub-har / Ya'qub-her** (c. 17th century BCE; preserved on scarabs and seals). The name is the West-Semitic *Ya'aqov-her* or *Ya'aqov-El*. The Hyksos king's existence outside the HB is documentary evidence that *Ya'aqov* was a West-Semitic patriarchal-onomastic name in the period.
- **Thutmose III topographical lists** (15th century BCE; the Karnak relief) include the place-name *y-ʿ-q-b-ʾ-r* — likely *Ya'aqov-el* — among the conquered Levantine sites. The place-name is interpreted as *Ya'aqov-El* — *(may) El protect / (the place of) Ya'aqov-El*. **Albright** (*BASOR* 81, 1941, 'The Land of Damascus Between 1850 and 1750 BC') treats the name in the Levantine-onomastic context.
- **Cuneiform attestations.** The name *Ia-aḫ-qu-ub-el* and variants are attested in Old Babylonian and Mari documents in the 18th century BCE.

The historical-onomastic evidence places *Ya'aqov* as a documented West-Semitic name in the patriarchal period. The HB Ya'aqov-narrative is grounded in a real West-Semitic name-stock; the etymology (heel / supplant) is the HB-internal narrative-theological interpretation of an existing name, not an ad-hoc invention. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 182 and **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417 treat the historical-onomastic background; **Albright** 1941, **Mendenhall** 1958 *BASOR* 152 ('Mari'), and **Pardee** 2002 *Ritual and Cult at Ugarit* are the standard reference-works.

**The Yitzchaq-Esav-Ya'aqov age-symbolism at Gen 25:26.** The verse closes with the chronological note *v-Yitzchaq ben shishim shanah b-ledet otam* — *and Yitzchaq was sixty years old when she bore them*. The chronological-note frames the twin-birth as occurring twenty years after Yitzchaq's marriage to Rivkah (Gen 25:20 — Yitzchaq is forty at marriage; the *atar*-prayer at v 21 spans the twenty-year barrenness). The age-detail is editorially-deliberate: it situates the twins' birth chronologically and stages the *atar*-prayer (v 21) as a twenty-year intercession, structurally paralleling the Sarah-twenty-five-year-wait of the previous generation (cf. Gen 12:4 — Avram is seventy-five at the Lekh-Lekha call; Gen 21:5 — Avraham is one hundred at Yitzchaq's birth; the twenty-five-year-wait).

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 177-178; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 178-179; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 131; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196; Albright 1941 *BASOR* 81; Mendenhall 1958 *BASOR* 152; Pardee 2002 *Ritual and Cult at Ugarit*; HALOT s.v. *aqav* and *Ya'aqov*; BDB s.v. *aqav* and *aqev* p. 784; Genesis Rabbah 63:8; Rashi at Gen 25:26; Hosea 12:4 commentary in Wolff *Hosea* Hermeneia 1974. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexical link *Ya'aqov* / *aqev* is morphologically explicit; (b) the supplant-etymology is HB-internal-explicit at Gen 27:36 and Hos 12:4; (c) the historical-onomastic attestations (Hyksos king, Thutmose III topographical lists, OB cuneiform) are documentary-evidence; (d) the narrative-deployment of the supplant-trajectory across Gen 25-32 is verifiable by structural-narrative analysis; (e) the age-chronology at Gen 25:20, 25:26 is grammatically explicit.

terms.source: וַיְהִ֣י עֵשָׂ֗ו אִ֛ישׁ יֹדֵ֥עַ צַ֖יִד אִ֣ישׁ שָׂדֶ֑ה וְיַעֲקֹב֙ אִ֣ישׁ תָּ֔ם יֹשֵׁ֖ב אֹהָלִֽים
terms.translit: va-yhi Esav ish yodea tzayid ish sadeh v-Ya'aqov ish tam yoshev ohalim
terms.strongs: H376 (ish) + H6718 (tzayid) + H7704 (sadeh) + H8535 (tam) + H3427 (yashav) + H168 (ohel)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun-pair characterization (Esav: skilled-hunter / man-of-field; Ya'aqov: tam / dwelling-in-tents)
terms.literal: and Esav was a man knowing hunting, a man of the field; and Ya'aqov was a tam man, dwelling in tents
terms.standardEnglish: Esau was a skillful hunter, a man of the field; and Jacob was a quiet man, dwelling in tents (ASV); ... a plain man (KJV); ... a quiet man (NRSV); ... a mild man (NJPS); ... a simple man / a man complete (some modern critical)
terms.wohChoice: Esav was a man skillful in hunting, a man of the field; and Ya'aqov was a quiet man, dwelling in tents (ASV-baseline; the four-reading apparatus on *tam* surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:27 stages the twin-character-contrast formula that grounds the entire Esav-Ya'aqov rivalry across the Genesis arc.** Two parallel three-element characterizations: Esav as *ish yodea tzayid ish sadeh* (a man knowing hunting, a man of the field) — the wilderness-pastoralist-hunter; Ya'aqov as *ish tam yoshev ohalim* (an *ish tam*, dwelling in tents) — the home-dwelling type. The contrast is editorially-load-bearing: the two brothers are characterized at v 27 in formulae that justify the parental-favoritism at v 28 (Yitzchaq loves Esav for his venison; Rivkah loves Ya'aqov) and that establish the narrative-grammar of all subsequent Esav-Ya'aqov interactions.

**The *tam* lexeme — the chapter's translation-crux.** The Hebrew *tam* (תָּם) is the chapter's load-bearing translation-decision. The lexeme admits **four principal readings**, each with a substantial lexical-and-scholarly defense:

**Reading 1: *quiet, mild, peaceable* (ASV / KJV revised / NRSV / NJPS).** The dominant English-tradition rendering. The reading takes *tam* in its *family-character* register — Ya'aqov as the home-dwelling, mild-tempered, non-aggressive brother as contrasted with Esav the hunter. The reading is conventional but is a **smoothing** of the lexeme's actual semantic range; it does not pick up the *tamim*-cluster's moral-integrity register active across the HB. The ASV's *quiet* is preserved as the WoH baseline.

**Reading 2: *plain, simple, straightforward* (KJV original).** The 1611 KJV rendering. *A plain man* takes *tam* in its *uncomplicated character* register — Ya'aqov as straightforward in disposition. The reading is the dominant 17th-century English-tradition reading and is preserved in some conservative modern translations. The *plain* rendering is closer to the lexeme's *uncompounded / unmixed* sense than the smoother *quiet* but loses the *integrity* register that the lexeme primarily carries elsewhere in the HB.

**Reading 3: *blameless, perfect, complete* (NRSV adjusted; modern critical).** Some modern translations (notably the NRSV at *the perfect / blameless* register; certain modern commentators) render *tam* in its *moral-integrity* register. The reading aligns Ya'aqov's characterization with the *tamim* of Gen 6:9 (Noah: *tzadik tamim hayah b-dorotav* — *a righteous man, blameless in his generations*), Gen 17:1 (Avraham: *hithallekh lefanai v-heyeh tamim* — *walk before me and be blameless*, treated in the existing `tamim-walk-before-god-covenant` entry), and Job 1:1 (*ish tam v-yashar yere Elohim v-sar me-ra* — *a man tam and upright, who feared Elohim and turned from evil*, treated in the existing `tom-v-yashar` entry). On Reading 3, Ya'aqov is characterized as *morally-integral* / *covenantally-whole* at v 27 — the same register applied to Noah, Avraham, and Job. The reading is exegetically-deep and produces an **immediate moral-tension** with the immediately-following narrative (vv 29-34: Ya'aqov manipulates the famished Esav; ch 27: Ya'aqov deceives the blind Yitzchaq). The tension is **the narrative's editorial-point**: Ya'aqov is named *tam* at v 27 and then narrated as *aqav* (supplanter) at vv 29-34 and ch 27. The moral-integrity register is in tension with the supplant-acts; the tension is the Ya'aqov-character's signature.

**Reading 4: *innocent, naive* (some modern critical, especially against the supplant-action).** Some modern critical commentators (e.g., **Fokkelman** *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 p. 87) read *tam* in a third register: *innocent / naive*, with the meaning that Ya'aqov *appears* uncomplicated while in fact harboring the trickster-disposition. The reading is exegetical-narrative-critical rather than lexical-foundational and is a minority. The reading frames the v 27 *tam* as ironic narrator-voice: the narrator names Ya'aqov *tam* as a setup for the immediately-following *aqav*-action, deflating the *tam* character at the very moment of its assertion.

**Scholarly distribution across the four readings.**

- **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 180-181 reads *tam* as *quiet, sedentary* (Reading 1) while noting the cross-reference to *tamim* in commentary.
- **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417 reads *tam* as carrying both the quiet-character and the integrity registers; presents both R1 and R3.
- **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 177-178 reads *tam* as *blameless* (R3) and develops the moral-tension argument.
- **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 179-180 reads *tam* as *peaceful, ordered* (R1+R3 hybrid).
- **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 131 reads *tam* as *a simple man* (R2-leaning).
- **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196 reads *tam* as *retiring*, characterizing Ya'aqov's sedentary nature.
- **Fokkelman** *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 pp. 86-87 develops the ironic-narrator reading (R4).

The modern critical scholarship is genuinely divided; the WoH translation takes the ASV-baseline R1 (*quiet*) for the main text and surfaces the four-reading apparatus in commentary.

**The *tamim*-cluster cross-reference.** The lexical-family *t-m-m* (תָּמַם) governs a connected vocabulary across the HB:

- **Adjective *tam* and *tamim*** — covered by the existing `tamim-walk-before-god-covenant` entry (Gen 17:1, primary). The cluster names *whole, complete, integral, unblemished, blameless* across cultic (Exod 12:5 Passover lamb; Lev 1:3 *olah*), covenantal-ethical (Gen 6:9 Noah; Gen 17:1 Avraham; Deut 18:13; Josh 24:14), psalmist (Ps 15:2, 18:24, 18:33, 101:2, 101:6, 119:1), and wisdom-corpus (Prov 11:5, 11:20, 20:7, 28:18) registers.
- **Adverb *tom* + adjective *yashar*** — covered by the existing `tom-v-yashar` entry (Job 1:1, 1:8, 2:3 primary). The pair names *complete-and-upright* / *blameless-and-upright* character.
- **Adjective *tam*** at Gen 25:27 (here) — the patriarchal-narrative occurrence; structurally-located between the Gen 17:1 (Avraham *tamim*) and Job 1:1 (Job *tam v-yashar*) instances of the lexeme.

The v 27 *ish tam* is editorially-located in the lexical-cluster as the patriarchal-narrative instance of the moral-integrity register. The cross-reference to the existing entries is documented in commentary.

**The *ish sadeh* / *yoshev ohalim* contrast.** The parallel non-*tam* element of the formula is the **dwelling-locale contrast**: Esav is *ish sadeh* (a man of the field, the open-country) and Ya'aqov is *yoshev ohalim* (one who dwells in tents). The contrast is editorially-deliberate and is a standard ANE characterization-pair: the wilderness-pastoralist-hunter vs. the home-dwelling-tent-pastoralist. The contrast is **not** the contrast between nomad and settled-agriculturalist — both brothers are pastoralist; the difference is *where* and *how* they dwell within the pastoralist register. Esav's *ish sadeh* aligns him with the *pere adam* / *Yishma'el* register (the wilderness-pastoralist mode-of-life of the Yishma'el oracle at Gen 16:12 and the closing-formula at Gen 25:18 — see existing `pere-adam-wild-ass-prophecy` entry). Ya'aqov's *yoshev ohalim* aligns him with the patriarchal-tent-dweller register (Avraham at Gen 18:1 *yoshev petach ha-ohel*; the patriarchal *ohel* as the dwelling-locale). The locale-contrast structurally **subordinates** Esav to the Yishma'el-line type and **aligns** Ya'aqov with the Avraham-Yitzchaq-line type. The chapter is structurally setting up the covenant-line transmission to Ya'aqov.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 180-181; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 177-178; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 179-180; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 131; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196; Fokkelman *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 pp. 86-87; HALOT s.v. *tam* and *tamim*; BDB s.v. *tam* p. 1070 and *tamim* p. 1071; Brueggemann *Genesis* Interpretation 1982 (on the moral-integrity register and the Ya'aqov-character question); Levenson *Sinai and Zion* 1985 (on the wider covenantal-ethical lexical cluster). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *tam* is grammatically explicit; (b) the four-reading apparatus is documented across the standard ancient and modern English versions; (c) the *tamim*-cluster cross-references to Gen 17:1, Gen 6:9, and Job 1:1 are verifiable by lexical search; (d) the *ish sadeh* / *yoshev ohalim* contrast is grammatically explicit and is documented in the standard commentaries; (e) the structural alignment with the Yishma'el-line (Esav) and the Avraham-line (Ya'aqov) is editorial-narrative-critical analysis with broad scholarly support.

terms.source: אַדְמוֹנִ֔י כֻּלּ֖וֹ כְּאַדֶּ֣רֶת שֵׂעָ֑ר ... מִן־הָאָדֹ֤ם הָאָדֹם֙ הַזֶּ֔ה ... עַל־כֵּ֥ן קָרָֽא־שְׁמ֖וֹ אֱדֽוֹם
terms.translit: admoni kulo k-aderet sear ... min ha-adom ha-adom ha-zeh ... al-ken qara shemo Edom
terms.partOfSpeech: adjective + noun + noun (v 25); doubled-adjective + demonstrative (v 30); naming-formula + proper noun (v 30)
terms.literal: ruddy, all of him like a hairy mantle ... from this red red [stuff] ... therefore his name was called Edom (Red)
