terms.standardEnglish: ruddy, all over like a hairy mantle ... that same red pottage ... therefore was his name called Edom (ASV/KJV)
terms.wohChoice: ruddy, all over like a hairy mantle (v 25) ... that same red pottage / that red, red stuff (v 30) ... therefore his name was called Edom (v 30; ASV-baseline; the double-name-play apparatus surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**The Esav/Edom/Seir name-cluster at Gen 25 is one of the HB's most-elaborated multi-name-etymology compressions, distributed across two verses (v 25 at birth; v 30 at the lentil-stew episode) with three distinct paronomastic links.** The verse-pair stages a foundational HB etymology for the Edomite peoples (Esav's descendants) and the Seir mountain-range (the Edomite tribal-territory).

**The three name-links.**

**(a) *admoni* (v 25) → *Edom* (v 30) — the *red* link.** Esav emerges *admoni* (אַדְמוֹנִי — *ruddy, reddish*) at birth (v 25); the *admoni* is paronomastically linked to *adom* (אָדֹם — *red*) via the shared root *ʾ-d-m*. The *admoni* foreshadows the explicit naming-formula at v 30: *va-yomer Esav el-Ya'aqov hal'iteni na min ha-adom ha-adom ha-zeh ... al-ken qara shemo Edom* — *and Esav said to Ya'aqov, Feed me, I pray, with that same red red [stuff] ... therefore was his name called Edom*. The v 30 explicit etymology connects the lentil-stew's *red color* to the name *Edom* (*Red*). The double-link — *admoni* at birth, *adom* at the stew — structurally brackets the Esav-naming-arc across vv 25-30.

**(b) *sear* (v 25) → *Seir* — the *hairy* link.** Esav emerges *k-aderet sear* (*like a hairy mantle*) at birth (v 25); *sear* (שֵׂעָר — *hair*) is paronomastically linked to *Seir* (שֵׂעִיר — the proper-noun toponym of the Edomite mountain-range). The link is not made explicit at v 25 (the v 25 verse names only the *sear* / *hairy* feature, not the Seir-place-name) but is **deferred** to the wider Esav-Edom-Seir compound-identity established across the Genesis arc:

- **Gen 32:4** — Ya'aqov sends messengers *artzah Seir sdeh Edom* — *to the land of Seir, the field of Edom* — explicitly equating Seir and Edom as Esav's territory.
- **Gen 33:14, 16** — Esav returns *l-Seira* — *to Seir*.
- **Gen 36:8-9** — *va-yeshev Esav b-har Se'ir Esav hu Edom* — *and Esav dwelt in Mount Seir; Esav is Edom* — the explicit equation.
- **Gen 36:20** — the Horites: *bnei Se'ir ha-Hori yoshvei ha-aretz* — the pre-Edomite inhabitants of Seir.
- **Deut 2:4-5, 2:12, 2:22** — the Mosaic-period Seir-Edom identification.

The *sear* (hairy) feature at v 25 thus seeds the eventual Seir (hairy-mountain) toponym-identification — though the v 25 text does not yet make the link explicit. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 181 reads the v 25 *sear* as the paronomastic foreshadow of the Seir-toponym; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 416 similarly.

**(c) Esav's name itself — the opaque etymology.** The name *Esav* (עֵשָׂו) is etymologically opaque in the standard lexicons. **BDB** s.v. *Esav* p. 796 derives it from an unattested root *asah* in the sense *to make / form*; **HALOT** s.v. *Esav* marks the etymology as 'origin uncertain'; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 416 notes the absence of a clear etymology. The v 25 narrative does not provide an etymology for the name *Esav* itself — the verse instead loads two paronomastic foreshadows (*admoni* for Edom; *sear* for Seir) onto the elder twin's birth-description without grounding the name *Esav* itself. The editorial-effect is the *multiple-name-etymology compression*: the elder twin is named *Esav*, but his *secondary* names (*Edom*, *Seir*) carry the paronomastic-etymological work.

**The v 30 *hal'iteni* hapax.** The lentil-stew episode at v 30 contains a HB **hapax legomenon**: the verb *hal'iteni* (הַלְעִיטֵנִי — *feed-me / cram-me*), qal/hiphil imperative of the root *l-ʿ-ṭ* with first-person suffix. The root *l-ʿ-ṭ* does not recur in the Hebrew Bible. The verb is attested in **Talmudic Aramaic and Mishnaic Hebrew** in the force-feed register (esp. of cattle being force-fed; see *m. Shab.* 24:3; *b. Shab.* 155b). The lexeme's force-feed register at Gen 25:30 stages Esav's exhaustion as a desperate-quantity request: *cram me with that red red stuff* — an inelegant verb-choice that the narrator deploys to characterize Esav's appetite-driven character. The hapax-status is editorially-deliberate: the narrator uses a lexically-marked verb to mark Esav's character-defining moment. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 181 and **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 178 note the hapax-status; the ASV smoothing *feed me, I pray you* loses the force-feed register but preserves the petition-form.

**The doubled *ha-adom ha-adom* construction.** The phrase *min ha-adom ha-adom ha-zeh* (*from this red red [stuff]*) is grammatically an **emphatic iterative redoubling**. The doubled adjective in Hebrew prose conveys intensification: *very red*, *exceedingly red*, *red, red*. The construction parallels other HB emphatic-doublings (*tov tov* — *very good*; *me'od me'od* — *exceedingly*; *naqi naqi* — *clean clean*). The doubling here **(i)** intensifies the red-color description (the lentils' striking visual quality); **(ii)** emphasizes Esav's exhaustion (his speech is staccato, redundant — the language of a famished man); **(iii)** stages the *adom* paronomasia twice at the cusp of the *Edom* naming-formula. The triple-deployment is editorially-deliberate. **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 132 renders the doubling as *that red red stuff*, preserving the staccato; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196 renders *some of that red, red stuff*. The WoH translation preserves the ASV's *that same red pottage* in the main text and surfaces the doubling in commentary.

**The Edom-historical context.** The *Edom* peoples (the descendants of Esav per the Gen 36 genealogy) are well-attested in the ANE record. **Knauf** *Midian* 1988 and **Bartlett** *Edom and the Edomites* 1989 are the standard monographs. The Edomites are attested in (i) the Neo-Assyrian record from the 9th century BCE forward (Salmaneser III, Tiglath-Pileser III, Esarhaddon, and Ashurbanipal annals list Edomite tribute); (ii) the Egyptian record from the Late Bronze period (Papyrus Anastasi VI 4:11-13 from c. 1200 BCE mentions Edomite shasu-pastoralists entering Egypt); (iii) the Iron Age archaeological record at Buseirah (Bozrah, the Edomite capital), Tel el-Kheleifeh, Umm el-Biyara, and Tawilan. The Edomite kingdom is a real Iron Age polity south-east of Judah. The HB Esav-Edom narrative is the foundational-ancestral tradition for the Edomite peoples, structurally paralleling the Yishma'el-Arabian tradition (covered by the existing `twelve-princes-of-ishmael` entry).

**The structurally-deliberate Yishma'el / Edom parallelism.** Genesis 25 stages the **two collateral-genealogies** of the Avrahamic line at the chapter's structural opening and middle: the Keturah-line (vv 1-6, the southern-Arabian Midianite-line tribes), the Yishma'el-line (vv 12-18, the northern-Arabian twelve-tribe-confederation), and the Yitzchaq-line (vv 19-34, the covenantal line). The Esav-Edom etiology at vv 25, 30 then **seeds a fourth collateral-genealogy** (the Edomite peoples, to be fully developed at Gen 36) within the Yitzchaq-line: the elder Yitzchaq-son splits off as the Edomite-ancestor, paralleling the Yishma'el split-off from the Avraham-line. The chapter is structurally building the collateral-genealogies framework that the wider Genesis arc will use to map the patriarchal-line relationships to the surrounding Iron-Age peoples.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 180-181; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-418; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 177-178; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 179-181; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 131-132; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 196-197; Knauf *Midian* 1988; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* 1989; Beit-Arieh *Tel Aroer* 2007; HALOT s.v. *Esav*, *Edom*, *Seir*, *hal'iteni*; BDB s.v. *adom* p. 10 and *Esav* p. 796; Genesis Rabbah 63:8, 63:13; Rashi at Gen 25:25, 25:30. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *admoni* / *Edom* and *sear* / *Seir* paronomasias are morphologically explicit; (b) the *Esav* etymology's opacity is documentary-lexicographic fact (BDB, HALOT); (c) the *hal'iteni* hapax-status is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the doubled *ha-adom ha-adom* construction is grammatically explicit; (e) the Esav-Edom-Seir compound-identity at Gen 36:8-9 is HB-internal-explicit; (f) the Edomite-historical-archaeological attestation is documentary.

terms.source: מִכְרָ֥ה כַיּ֛וֹם אֶת־בְּכֹֽרָתְךָ֖ לִֽי ... וַיִּמְכֹּ֥ר אֶת־בְּכֹרָת֖וֹ לְיַעֲקֹֽב
terms.translit: mikhrah ka-yom et-b'khoratkha li ... va-yimkor et-b'khorato l-Ya'aqov
terms.partOfSpeech: noun (firstborn-status) + verb (sell) + verb (swear), in formal-legal exchange-formula
terms.literal: sell, today, your firstborn-status to me ... and he sold his firstborn-status to Ya'aqov
terms.standardEnglish: sell me first your birthright ... and he sold his birthright to Jacob (ASV/KJV); sell me your birthright first / sell me, as of this day, your birthright (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Sell me first your birthright ... and he sold his birthright to Ya'aqov (ASV-baseline; the four-step legal-form, the *bazah* contempt-register, and the Hebrews 12:16 reception surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:31-34 stages the foundational HB episode for the *bekhorah* (birthright / firstborn-status) institution. The four-verse legal-juridical sequence at vv 31-34, capped by the narrator's editorial verdict *va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah* (*and Esav despised the birthright*) at v 34, is the canonical HB scene-of-birthright-transfer between brothers and the source-text for the HB-wide birthright-institution.**

**The *bekhorah* lexeme and the firstborn-institution.** *Bekhorah* (בְּכֹרָה) is the abstract-noun of *bekhor* (firstborn) and names the **legal-juridical status of the firstborn son** within the patriarchal-period inheritance-system. The institution carries a cluster of legal-economic rights: the **double portion of the paternal inheritance** (formalized in Deut 21:15-17); the **patriarchal blessing** (transferred separately at Gen 27); the **headship of the family-line** and certain cultic responsibilities (priest-of-the-household in pre-Levitical periods, cf. Exod 22:29, Num 3:12-13, 8:17-18 — the firstborn-as-priestly substitution by Levites). The institution is **legally-transferable** between brothers under certain conditions; the Gen 25 transaction is the canonical HB instance of voluntary inter-brother birthright-transfer.

**The ANE legal context: the Nuzi parallels.** The Nuzi tablets (Late Bronze Age Hurrian-Akkadian legal documents from northern Mesopotamia, 15th-14th centuries BCE) document multiple inter-brother inheritance-transfer transactions structurally parallel to the Gen 25 sale. The most-cited parallel is **HSS V 67** (the *Tupkitilla / Kurpazah* tablet): a brother sells his inheritance-share to another brother for three sheep. The tablet's legal-form parallels the Gen 25 transaction in three significant elements: (i) the seller's voluntary act; (ii) the small-value consideration (three sheep at Nuzi; bread-and-lentil-stew at Gen 25); (iii) the legal-binding effect of the sale on subsequent inheritance distribution. **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 195-196 develops the Nuzi-parallel argument extensively and treats the Gen 25 transaction as documenting a real ANE legal-institution-form. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 182 cites Speiser's Nuzi-parallel and accepts the legal-form as plausible. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417 broadly affirms.

**Critical caution on the Nuzi parallels.** The Nuzi-parallels have been **substantially critiqued** in the second-generation modern critical scholarship. **Selman** 1976 *Comparative Customs of the Patriarchal Age* (Tyndale Bulletin) and **Van Seters** 1975 *Abraham in History and Tradition* (Yale) review the Speiser-era Nuzi-parallel arguments and find that some of the early-mid-20th-century parallels (including some of Speiser's *AB Genesis* readings) were **overdrawn** — the parallels were stronger in the Albright-era scholarly imagination than in the actual legal-document evidence. **Greengus** 1975 *JAOS* 95 and **Eichler** 1989 *JANES* 18 conduct detailed re-examinations of the Nuzi and Old Babylonian legal-document corpora and conclude that (i) the basic legal-form of brother-to-brother inheritance-transfer is well-attested in the ANE legal corpora; (ii) but the specific Nuzi-Gen 25 parallels are less precise than the early-Speiser arguments suggested; (iii) the institutional setting is plausible Late-Bronze / Iron-I West-Semitic legal-practice rather than a uniquely-Hurrian-Nuzi parallel. The modern critical position: the legal-form is **plausible-ANE-attested** but the specific Nuzi-tablet parallels are **less precise than once claimed**; the Gen 25 transaction documents a real-form ANE legal institution without requiring the specific Nuzi-Hurrian context.

**The four-step legal-juridical sequence at vv 31-33.** The Gen 25 transaction is staged as a **formal legal-act** in a four-step sequence:

(i) **Ya'aqov's request — the legal-immediacy formula.** *Va-yomer Ya'aqov mikhrah ka-yom et-bekhoratkha li* (v 31) — *and Ya'aqov said, Sell me today your birthright*. The phrase *ka-yom* (*as today, today, now*) is the **legal-immediacy marker** specifying that the transaction is effective immediately, with no deferred-effect. The construction recurs at v 33 (*hishav'ah li ka-yom* — *swear to me today*) and is the legal-formulary's repeated marker.

(ii) **Esav's response — the legal-justification clause.** *Va-yomer Esav hineh anokhi holekh la-mut v-lamah zeh li b'khorah* (v 32) — *and Esav said, Behold, I am about to die, and what profit shall the birthright do me?*. The clause is a **legal-justification for the sale**: Esav names a contingency (imminent death from hunger / from the wilderness-hunt) that renders the birthright valueless to him. The justification-clause is the legal-precondition for voluntary alienation-of-inheritance under standard ANE inheritance-law: the seller must articulate a reason for sale.

(iii) **Ya'aqov's oath-demand — the legal-binding instrument.** *Va-yomer Ya'aqov hishav'ah li ka-yom* (v 33a) — *and Ya'aqov said, Swear to me today*. The oath-demand is the **binding-instrument** that completes the legal-form: a sale-by-oath in the ANE legal-context is irrevocable. The niphal *hishav'ah* (imperative — *take an oath*) requires Esav to formally invoke the deity as witness to the transaction.

(iv) **Esav's oath and the legal-completion.** *Va-yishava lo va-yimkor et-b'khorato l-Ya'aqov* (v 33b) — *and he swore to him, and he sold his birthright to Ya'aqov*. The oath is sworn; the sale is completed (the verb *makhar* — *sell* — in the qal imperfect marking the irrevocable transfer). The legal-form is now complete.

The four-step sequence is **editorially-deliberate** and matches the formal-juridical sequence of an ANE inheritance-sale: request, justification, oath-demand, oath-and-completion. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 182 documents the four-step sequence explicitly; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 416 similarly.

**The closing *va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah* at v 34.** The transaction's narrative-closing clause — *va-Ya'aqov natan l-Esav lechem u-n-zid adashim va-yokhal va-yesht va-yaqam va-yelekh va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah* (v 34) — *and Ya'aqov gave Esav bread and pottage of lentils, and he ate and drank, and rose up, and went his way; so Esav despised the birthright*. The closing verb *va-yivez* (qal impf 3ms of *bazah*) carries an **editorial moral-verdict**: Esav *despised* the birthright. The treatment of the *bazah* verb is governed by the companion `va-yivez-esav-et-ha-bekhorah-despising-birthright` entry, which covers the narrator's moral-verdict, the Heb 12:16 NT reception (*bebēlos* — *profane*), and the rabbinic-tradition expansion (Genesis Rabbah 63:11). The two entries together govern the full Gen 25:31-34 sequence: this entry covers the four-step legal-form and the *bekhorah* institution; the companion entry covers the *bazah* verdict.

**Cross-corpus distribution of *bekhorah* and the birthright-institution.** The lexeme and the institution recur across the HB:

- **Gen 27:36** — Esav's lament re-thematizes the birthright-loss: *et-b'khorati laqach v-hineh atah laqach birkhati* — *he took away my birthright, and behold, now he has taken away my blessing*. The verse explicitly distinguishes *bekhorah* (firstborn-status, transferred at Gen 25:31-34) from *berakhah* (blessing, transferred at Gen 27).
- **Deut 21:15-17** — the **double-portion law for the firstborn**: a man with two wives, one loved and one hated, must give the firstborn-son of the unloved wife the *double-portion* (*pi shnayim* — *two-fold*) of the inheritance. The verse is the formal-legal codification of the firstborn-double-portion that the Gen 25 *bekhorah* names.
- **1 Chr 5:1-2** — the explicit-retrospective on Reuben's lost-birthright: *u-vnei Reuven bekhor Yisrael ki hu ha-bekhor u-v-challelo y'tzu'ei aviv nittnah b'khorato livnei Yosef ben-Yisrael v-lo l-hityaches la-bekhorah* — *and the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel — for he was the firstborn, but because he defiled his father's couch, his birthright was given to the sons of Yosef ben-Yisrael; nevertheless he is not to be reckoned in the genealogy according to the birthright*. The Chronicler's retrospective applies the *bekhorah* lexeme to Reuben's loss (the Gen 35:22, 49:3-4 narrative) and to Yosef's gain (the Gen 48:5 adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh). The verse is the canonical-HB internal note on the birthright-institution's transferability.
- **1 Sam 1-2** — the David-anointing-over-elder-brothers narrative; the *bekhorah* is not explicitly invoked but the structural pattern (younger over elder) recurs.
- **2 Chr 21:3** — the Jehoshaphat-inheritance distribution: the kingdom goes to the firstborn Jehoram, but with provisions for the other sons.

The institution's cross-corpus distribution confirms the **HB-systemic** character of the birthright-institution; the Gen 25 transaction is the canonical-narrative source-text for the institution's legal-form.

**The Yitzchaq-blessing distinction at Gen 27.** A crucial editorial distinction: the **Gen 25 transaction** transfers the *bekhorah* (firstborn-status, the legal-inheritance-position); the **Gen 27 transaction** transfers the *berakhah* (paternal blessing, the patriarchal-covenant-transmission). The two are distinguishable institutions: a son could in principle hold the birthright without the blessing or vice versa. The Esav-narrative tragically loses both, but at different moments and via different mechanisms. Gen 27:36 makes the distinction explicit (Esav's lament names the two separately). The Gen 25 sale is the first-of-two losses; the Gen 27 deception is the second-and-final loss.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 414-417; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 178-179; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 180-183; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 132; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 195-196 (the Nuzi-parallel argument); Selman 1976 *Tyndale Bulletin* (critical re-examination of the Nuzi parallels); Van Seters 1975 *Abraham in History and Tradition* (the critical case against early-Albright-era ANE parallels); Greengus 1975 *JAOS* 95; Eichler 1989 *JANES* 18; Tsevat 1958 *HUCA* 29 ('The Husband Veils a Wife', on the Nuzi marriage-customs); Hess 2007 *Israelite Religions*; Pressler 1993 *The View of Women Found in the Deuteronomic Family Laws*; HALOT s.v. *bekhor*, *bekhorah*, *makhar*, *shava*. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *bekhorah* lexeme is grammatically explicit; (b) the four-step legal-juridical sequence at vv 31-33 is grammatically explicit and is documented in the standard commentaries; (c) the Nuzi-parallel argument (Speiser) and its critical re-examination (Selman, Van Seters, Greengus, Eichler) are documented in mainstream ANE-legal scholarship; (d) the Deut 21:15-17 double-portion codification and the 1 Chr 5:1-2 transferability-precedent are HB-internal-explicit; (e) the *bekhorah* / *berakhah* distinction at Gen 27:36 is HB-internal-explicit; (f) the institutional setting is plausibly Late-Bronze / Iron-I West-Semitic legal-practice (the modern critical consensus position after Selman 1976).

terms.partOfSpeech: qal imperfect 3ms + direct-object marker + definite-article noun
terms.literal: and Esav despised / held in contempt the birthright
terms.standardEnglish: so Esau despised his birthright (ASV/KJV); thus Esau despised his birthright (NRSV); thus did Esau spurn the birthright (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: so Esav despised his birthright (ASV-baseline; the *bazah* contempt-verdict and the Heb 12:16 + rabbinic-tradition reception surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 25:34b — *va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah* — is the narrator's editorial moral-verdict closing the birthright-sale episode. The clause's verb *bazah* (בָּזָה — *despise, scorn, hold in contempt*) is a strong-negative valence and frames Esav's action as not merely transactional but as constitutive contempt for the institution.** The clause's load-bearing function is to interpret the preceding transaction (vv 31-33): the narrator does not let the legal-form stand neutral; the closing-verdict explicitly attributes contempt-of-the-birthright to Esav. The verb-choice is editorially-deliberate and is the source-text for the post-biblical Esav-as-profane-figure reception tradition.

**The *bazah* lexeme.** *Bazah* (בָּזָה) is the HB's primary verb for *despise, hold in contempt, treat as worthless*. The lexeme's distribution is broad across the HB:

- **Gen 25:34** (here) — Esav despising the birthright.
- **Num 15:31** — the apostate who despises the word of YHWH: *ki d'var YHWH bazah* — *because he has despised the word of YHWH*. The legal-cultic formula for high-handed sin.
- **1 Sam 10:27** — *u-vnei v-liyaal amru mah-yoshi'enu zeh va-yivzuhu* — *and the worthless men said, How shall this one save us? and they despised him* — the contempt of Saul by the *bnei beliyaal*.
- **1 Sam 17:42** — Goliath *va-yivez oto ki-hayah na'ar* — *and he despised him for he was a youth* — Goliath's contempt of David.
- **2 Sam 6:16** / **1 Chr 15:29** — Michal *va-tivez lo b-libah* — *and she despised him in her heart* — Michal's contempt of David's ecstatic dancing.
- **2 Kgs 19:21** / **Isa 37:22** — *bazah lekha la'agah lekha betulat bat-Tzion* — *the virgin daughter of Zion has despised you and laughed you to scorn* — the Hezekiah-anti-Sennacherib oracle.
- **Ps 22:7, 22:25, 51:19, 73:20, 102:18, 119:141** — psalmist's complaint and worship registers.
- **Prov 1:7, 14:2, 15:5, 23:9, 23:22, 30:17** — wisdom-corpus.
- **Mal 1:6** — the priests despising the name of YHWH: *bozei sh'mi* — *despisers of my name*.
- **Mal 1:7** — the polluted altar-bread: *shulchan YHWH nivzeh hu* — *the table of YHWH is despised*.
- **Isa 53:3** — the suffering servant: *nivzeh va-chadal ishim* — *despised and rejected of men*.
- **Zech 4:10** — *mi vaz l-yom k-tannot* — *who has despised the day of small things*.

The lexeme's consistent register is **strong-negative valuation**: to treat as worthless, beneath consideration, contemptible. The application of the verb to Esav at Gen 25:34 places his action in the strong-negative-valuation register — the narrator's editorial verdict is severe.

**The verb's compositional function at v 34.** The verb-tense is qal **imperfect** (*va-yivez*, with the vav-consecutive making it past-narrative). In Hebrew prose, the vav-consecutive imperfect is the standard narrative tense — but the verb's placement *after* the transactional-completion (*va-yashav*, *va-yelekh* — *and he rose, and he went*) carries the **summarizing-editorial-comment** function. The verb is not part of the action-narrative-sequence; it is the narrator's retrospective verdict on the action just completed. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 182 reads the clause as the narrator's editorial-verdict; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 416 similarly; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 132 reads the four staccato verbs *va-yokhal va-yesht va-yaqam va-yelekh* (*and he ate, and he drank, and he rose, and he went*) as deliberately staging Esav's animal-immediacy-of-appetite, closing with the editorial *va-yivez Esav et-ha-bekhorah*.

**NT reception at Hebrews 12:16-17.** The *va-yivez* verdict is taken up in the New Testament at **Heb 12:16-17**: *mē tis pornos ē bebēlos hōs Ēsau, hos anti brōseōs mias apedeto ta prōtotokia heautou; (17) iste gar hoti kai metepeita thelōn klēronomēsai tēn eulogian apedokimasthē, metanoias gar topon ouch heuren kaiper meta dakryōn ekzētēsas autēn* — *Lest there be any fornicator, or profane person, as Esau, who for one morsel of meat sold his own birthright. (17) For ye know that even when he afterward desired to inherit the blessing, he was rejected; for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears*. The Hebrews passage characterizes Esav as *bebēlos* (βέβηλος — *profane, unhallowed, irreligious*) on the explicit basis of the v 34 birthright-sale. The Greek *bebēlos* renders the *va-yivez* verdict into the early-Christian theological vocabulary as *profanity / unholiness* — Esav becomes the type-figure of the *profane* one who has shown contempt for the sacred (the *bekhorah* being read as containing the sacred-line-of-promise).

The reception extends across the patristic and medieval Christian tradition:
- **Origen** *Homilies on Genesis* XII reads Esav as the type of those who prefer the material to the spiritual.
- **Augustine** *De Civitate Dei* XV-XVI reads Esav as the type of the earthly-city in contrast to Ya'aqov's heavenly-city; the birthright-sale is the foundational moment of this typology.
- **Calvin** *Commentary on Genesis* and *Institutes* III reads Esav as evidence of the doctrine of reprobation.
- The **Lectionary** tradition pairs Gen 25:31-34 with Heb 12:16-17 across the Christian liturgical-year.

**Rabbinic reception at Genesis Rabbah 63:11.** The rabbinic tradition expands the *va-yivez* verdict into a series of moral failings. **Genesis Rabbah 63:11** lists five sins committed by Esav on the day of the birthright-sale: (i) he violated a betrothed maiden; (ii) he committed murder; (iii) he denied the resurrection of the dead; (iv) he denied the principle of divine providence; (v) he despised the birthright. The midrashic expansion takes the *va-yivez* verdict and **amplifies** it into a comprehensive characterological condemnation of Esav as the type-figure of the wicked-Edomite-ancestor. The expansion is interpretive-amplificatory rather than philological-tied; the v 34 text says only that Esav despised the birthright, but the rabbinic-tradition reads the verdict as evidence of comprehensive moral failure.

The rabbinic-tradition continues:
- **b. Bava Batra 16b** — the Esav-as-five-sins tradition.
- **Targum Pseudo-Jonathan** at Gen 25:34 — expands the five-sins reading into the targumic paraphrase.
- **Rashi** at Gen 25:34 — cites Genesis Rabbah 63:11 and the *bazah*-as-comprehensive-contempt reading.
- The medieval and modern rabbinic-canonical tradition broadly accepts the comprehensive-moral-failure reading of Esav.

**Modern critical reception.** Modern critical scholarship (Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton, Alter, Speiser) reads the *va-yivez* verdict more narrowly: the verse is the narrator's verdict on the *immediacy-of-appetite-over-long-term-promise* pattern. Esav is characterized as someone who values immediate satisfaction over deferred patriarchal blessing — the contempt-verdict targets this specific decision-making pattern, not a comprehensive moral failure. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 182 reads the v 34 verb-sequence (*va-yokhal va-yesht va-yaqam va-yelekh va-yivez*) as the narrator's staged-portrait of Esav's appetite-driven character; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 416-417 similarly.

**The reception is flagged-not-preempted.** The WoH apparatus surfaces both the NT reception (Hebrews 12:16-17 and the Christian-typological tradition) and the rabbinic reception (Genesis Rabbah 63:11 and the rabbinic-canonical comprehensive-moral-failure reading), and notes the modern critical alternative (the narrower appetite-over-promise reading). The Gen 25:34 text itself contains only the *bazah*-verdict on the birthright-decision; the larger characterological-condemnation readings are downstream-receptions and not preempted by the v 34 text alone.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 181-182; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 414-418; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 178-179; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 182-183; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 132; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 196; Attridge *Hebrews* Hermeneia 1989 pp. 369-372 (on Heb 12:16-17); Bauckham *Jude, 2 Peter* WBC 1983 (on the Jude-Esav typology); Lane *Hebrews 9-13* WBC 1991; Genesis Rabbah 63:11; b. Bava Batra 16b; Rashi at Gen 25:34; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan at Gen 25:34; Calvin *Commentary on Genesis* at 25:34; Origen *Homilies on Genesis* XII; Augustine *De Civitate Dei* XV-XVI; HALOT s.v. *bazah* and BDB s.v. *bazah* p. 102. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *bazah* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Heb 12:16-17 NT reception is documentary, flagged-not-preempted; (d) the Genesis Rabbah 63:11 rabbinic reception is documentary, flagged-not-preempted; (e) the modern critical alternative reading (appetite-over-promise rather than comprehensive moral failure) is mainstream and is documented in Sarna, Westermann, Wenham, Hamilton, Alter, Speiser.

terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3ms, vav-consec) + four direct-object nouns in apposition, each with 1cs suffix
terms.literal: and he kept my keeping / my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my teachings / laws
terms.standardEnglish: and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws (ASV/KJV); kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws (NRSV); kept my charge: my commandments, my laws, and my teachings (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws (ASV-baseline; the Sinaitic-retrojection / P-or-late-D layer-character of the four-noun cluster surfaced in apparatus and commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:5 contains one of the most striking source-critical anomalies in the patriarchal narratives: a fourfold legal-stipulation cluster — *mishmarti, mitzvotai, chuqotai, v-torotai* (*my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws / teachings*) — applied retrospectively to Avraham.** The cluster names the four primary categories of Pentateuchal legal-stipulation vocabulary, all of which properly belong to the Sinaitic / Mosaic legal-corpus (Exodus 19 onward), and applies them to a patriarch who in the surrounding narrative receives covenant promises but no enumerated law-code. The verse is the chapter's clearest documentary-source signal and is foundational to the modern critical reading of the patriarchal-narratives' Sinai-retrojection patterning.

**The four lexemes individually.**

*(i) **Mishmeret*** (מִשְׁמֶרֶת — *charge, watch, guard-duty, keeping*). From the root *sh-m-r* (*keep, guard, watch*). The noun's primary HB register is **Priestly cultic-duty**: the watchpost, the guard-station, the assigned cultic responsibility. The lexeme is concentrated in the Priestly source (P) and in the late-Deuteronomistic / Chronistic strata. Lev 8:35; 18:30; 22:9; Num 1:53; 3:7-8, 25, 28, 31-32, 36, 38; 4:27-28, 31-32; 8:26; 9:19, 23; 18:3-5, 8; 19:9; 31:30, 47 — the Priestly cultic-duty distribution. Deut 11:1 (the Deuteronomic-paranetic-deployment); 1 Kgs 2:3 (David's deathbed-charge to Solomon); 2 Kgs 11:5-7; Ezek 40:45-46; 44:8, 14-16; 48:11; Zech 3:7; Mal 3:14; Neh 12:9, 24, 45; 13:14, 30; 1 Chr 9:19, 23, 27; 12:30; 23:32; 25:8; 26:12; 2 Chr 7:6; 8:14; 13:11; 23:6; 35:2 — the broader cultic-administrative distribution. The application of *mishmeret* to Avraham at Gen 26:5 is the only occurrence in the patriarchal narratives and is editorially anomalous: Avraham is not depicted in the narrative as performing cultic-guard-duty in the Priestly-Levitical sense.

*(ii) **Mitzvot*** (מִצְוֹת — *commandments*, plural of *mitzvah*). From the root *tz-v-h* (*command*). The lexeme is the Pentateuch's primary term for **divine commandments delivered through Moses**. Major cluster-occurrences: Exod 15:26 (the post-Red-Sea-crossing assurance: *im-shamo'a tishma l-qol YHWH Elohekha... v-ha'azanta l-mitzvotav v-shamarta kol-chuqav* — the verse already linking *mitzvot* and *chuqim*); Exod 16:28; 20:6 (the Decalogue's second-commandment promise *l-ohavai u-l-shomrei mitzvotai*); 24:12 (the Sinai stone-tablets); Lev 4:2, 13, 22, 27; 5:17; 22:31; 26:3, 14-15; Num 15:22-23, 39-40; Deut *passim* (the Deuteronomic-paranetic *passim*-distribution: 4:2, 40; 5:10, 29, 31; 6:1-2, 17, 25; 7:9, 11; 8:1-2, 6, 11; 10:13; 11:1, 8, 13, 22, 27-28; 13:5, 19; 15:5; 19:9; 26:13, 17-18; 27:1, 10; 28:1, 9, 13-15, 45; 30:8, 10-11, 16; the Deuteronomic distribution is the densest in the HB). Cross-corpus: 1 Kgs 2:3; 3:14; 6:12; 8:58, 61; 9:6; 11:34, 38; 13:21; 14:8; 18:18; 2 Kgs 17:13, 16, 19, 37; 18:6; 21:8; 23:3; Ps 19:9; 78:7; 89:32; 112:1; 119 (the Torah-Psalm, 22 occurrences of *mitzvot*); 147:15; Prov 2:1; 3:1; 4:4; 6:20, 23; 7:1-2; 10:8; 13:13; 19:16; Eccl 8:5; 12:13; Isa 48:18; Jer 35:18; Dan 9:4-5; Ezra 9:10, 14; 10:3; Neh 1:5, 7, 9; 9:13-14, 16, 29, 34; 10:30, 32; 1 Chr 28:7-8; 29:19; 2 Chr 7:19; 8:13-15; 14:4; 17:4; 19:10; 24:20; 29:25; 30:6, 12; 31:21; 34:31; 36:14. The application of *mitzvot* to Avraham at Gen 26:5 is the only patriarchal-narratives occurrence and is editorially anomalous: the patriarchal-narrative Avraham receives covenant promises (Gen 12:1-3; 15; 17) but no enumerated *mitzvot* in the Mosaic-legal sense.

*(iii) **Chuqot*** (חֻקּוֹת — *statutes, decrees, ordinances*, plural of *chuqah*). From the root *ch-q-q* (*engrave, inscribe, decree*). The lexeme's primary register is **engraved-decree-law**, with secondary registers in **permanent-perpetual-statute** (the *chuqat olam* formula common in Priestly cultic-prescription: Lev 3:17; 7:36; 10:9; 16:29, 31, 34; 17:7; 23:14, 21, 31, 41; 24:3; Num 10:8; 15:15; 18:23; 19:10, 21). Major cluster-occurrences: Exod 12:14, 17, 24, 43; 13:10; 15:25-26; 18:16, 20; 27:21; 28:43; 29:9, 28; 30:21; Lev 3:17; 6:11, 15; 7:34, 36; 10:9, 11, 13-15; 16:29, 31, 34; 17:7; 18:3-5, 26, 30; 19:19, 37; 20:8, 22-23; 22:9; 23:14, 21, 31, 41; 24:3, 9; 25:18; 26:3, 15, 43, 46; Num 9:3, 12, 14; 10:8; 15:15-16; 18:8, 11, 19, 23; 19:2, 10, 21; 27:11; 30:17; 31:21; 35:29; Deut 4:1, 5-6, 8, 14, 40, 45; 5:1, 31; 6:1-2, 17, 20, 24-25; 7:11; 8:11; 10:13; 11:1, 32; 12:1; 16:12; 17:19; 26:16-17; 27:10; 28:15, 45; 30:10, 16. Cross-corpus: extensively distributed across the Historical Books, Psalms, and Chronicles in the Deuteronomistic / Chronistic strata. The application of *chuqot* to Avraham at Gen 26:5 is the only patriarchal-narratives occurrence and is editorially anomalous on the same grounds: Avraham receives covenant promises, not engraved-decree-statutes in the Mosaic-legal sense.

*(iv) **Torot*** (תּוֹרֹת — *teachings, instructions, laws*, plural of *torah*). From the root *y-r-h* (*shoot, throw, teach, instruct, direct*). The plural form *torot* (תּוֹרֹת) is morphologically distinguishable from the singular *torah* (תּוֹרָה) and is comparatively rare in the HB. Plural-form distribution: **Gen 26:5** (here, the only patriarchal-narrative occurrence); Exod 16:28 (the manna-and-Sabbath narrative: *ad-anah me'antem li-shmor mitzvotai v-torotai* — paired with *mitzvot*); 18:16, 20 (Yitro's advice: *v-hoda'ti et-chuqei ha-Elohim v-et-torotav* — paired with *chuqim*); Lev 26:46 (the cap of the Holiness Code: *eleh ha-chuqim v-ha-mishpatim v-ha-torot asher natan YHWH beino u-vein bnei Yisrael* — the explicit cap-formula); Isa 24:5 (the apocalyptic-pollution passage: *ki-avru torot chalfu-choq heferu brit olam* — the eschatological-pollution verse mentioning *torot*, *choq*, and *brit olam* together); Ezek 43:11 (the future-temple-vision: *kol-torato v-kol-chuqotav*); Dan 9:10 (the post-exilic confession: *l-lekhet b-torotav asher natan l-faneinu b-yad avadav ha-nevi'im*). The plural form's HB-rarity (10 occurrences total) places the Gen 26:5 application in a small editorial-cluster. The application to Avraham is the only patriarchal-narratives occurrence and is editorially anomalous: Avraham is not depicted as receiving multiple distinct legal *torot* in the narrative.

**The fourfold cluster's cross-corpus distribution.** The cluster of *mishmeret + mitzvot + chuqim + torot* as a unit recurs only in late-Deuteronomistic and post-exilic contexts:

- **Deut 11:1** — *v-ahavta et YHWH Elohekha v-shamarta mishmarto v-chuqotav u-mishpatav u-mitzvotav kol ha-yamim* — the Deuteronomic-paranetic deployment with *mishmeret + chuqot + mishpatim + mitzvot* (substituting *mishpatim* for *torot*).
- **Deut 26:17** — *YHWH he'emarta ha-yom lihyot l-kha l-Elohim v-la-lekhet bi-drakhav v-li-shmor chuqav u-mitzvotav u-mishpatav v-li-shmoa b-qolo* — the explicit Deuteronomic-covenant-acceptance formula (the verse-pair v 17-18 is the foundational *amartem-amarti* covenant-acceptance text).
- **Deut 28:1, 28:15, 28:45** — the blessings-and-curses formula deploying *mitzvot* with *chuqot* and *mishpatim*.
- **1 Kgs 2:3** — David's deathbed-charge to Solomon: *v-shamarta et-mishmeret YHWH Elohekha la-lekhet bi-drakhav li-shmor chuqotav mitzvotav u-mishpatav v-edevotav ka-katuv b-torat Moshe* — the explicit Deuteronomistic-Solomonic deployment of *mishmeret + chuqot + mitzvot + mishpatim + edot*, with *torat Moshe* invoked as the source-text.
- **1 Kgs 8:58** — Solomon's temple-dedication prayer: *l-lekhet b-khol-drakhav v-li-shmor mitzvotav v-chuqav u-mishpatav asher tziva et-avoteinu*.
- **1 Kgs 9:6** — the conditional clause: *im-shov tashuvun atem u-vneikhem me-acharai v-lo tishmru mitzvotai chuqotai asher natati lifneikhem*.
- **2 Kgs 17:13** — the Deuteronomistic-prophetic-summary at the fall of Samaria: *shuvu mi-darkheikhem ha-ra'im v-shimru mitzvotai chuqotai k-khol ha-torah asher tzivvti et-avoteikhem va-asher shalachti aleikhem b-yad avadai ha-nevi'im* — the explicit cluster-deployment.
- **2 Kgs 23:3** — Josiah's covenant-renewal: *li-shmor et-mitzvotav v-et-edvotav v-et-chuqotav b-khol-lev u-v-khol-nefesh*.
- **Neh 1:7-9; 9:13-14; 10:29-30** — the post-exilic covenant-renewal cluster-deployments.
- **1 Chr 22:13; 29:19** — the Chronicler's David-to-Solomon and Solomon-temple-charges, deploying the cluster.
- **2 Chr 7:17-19** — the Chronicler's parallel to the 1 Kgs 9 deployment.
- **2 Chr 33:8** — the Manasseh-restoration parallel.
- **Mal 3:22** (Eng 4:4) — the closing of the prophets: *zikhru torat Moshe avdi asher tziviti oto b-Chorev al-kol-Yisrael chuqim u-mishpatim* — the closing-of-the-Prophets explicit cluster-deployment.

The cluster's distribution is **concentrated in the late-Deuteronomistic, post-exilic, and Chronistic strata**, with the late-Deuteronomistic deployment being the densest. The application of the cluster to Avraham at Gen 26:5 is editorially anomalous in placing the late-Deuteronomistic / Chronistic vocabulary in a patriarchal-narrative context.

**The modern critical reading.** **Wellhausen** *Prolegomena zur Geschichte Israels* 1883/1885 reads Gen 26:5 as a deuteronomistic-or-priestly insertion into an earlier J-narrative; the verse's vocabulary is the editorial signature of the late stratum imposing the Sinaitic-legal vocabulary on the patriarchal narrative. **Skinner** *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 363-364 reads the verse as a late editorial gloss. **von Rad** *Genesis: A Commentary* (1956 OTL; ET 1961, 1972) reads the verse as a deuteronomistic-or-priestly Sinaitic-retrojection. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425-426 develops the reading in detail: *the verse's vocabulary is unmistakeably that of the late-Deuteronomistic / Priestly stratum; the four nouns belong properly to the Sinai-legal corpus and their application to Avraham is the editor's anachronistic projection of the Mosaic-legal framework backward onto the patriarch*. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 184-185 reads the verse as the chapter's most-flagged source-critical signal and surfaces the Sinaitic-retrojection-reading with caveats: *the four-noun cluster is striking; modern critical commentary reads it as a late Deuteronomistic gloss imposing the Sinai-legal framework on the patriarchal narrative; the rabbinic-traditional reading harmonizes by holding that Avraham observed the Torah by anticipation* (m. Qidd. 4:14; b. Yoma 28b). **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 189-190 notes the source-critical reading and presents the rabbinic-traditional alternative. **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 195-196 surfaces both readings. **Alter** *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 134 notes the late-vocabulary character of the verse and reads it as editorial smoothing.

**Friedman**'s documentary-source attribution.** **Friedman** *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 attributes Gen 26:5 to a late redactional layer, with the four-noun cluster identified as the redactor's vocabulary signature. The verse is one of the cleaner editorial-signature instances in the patriarchal narratives.

**The rabbinic-traditional reading.** The rabbinic-traditional alternative is that Avraham observed the entire Torah by anticipation. **m. Qidd. 4:14** (cited in **b. Yoma 28b**): *Avraham avinu... shamar ha-Torah kullah ad she-lo nittnah* — *our father Avraham... kept the entire Torah before it was given*. **Genesis Rabbah 64:4** expands the reading: Avraham observed not only the written Torah but also the rabbinic-derivative ordinances and the *eruv tavshilin*. **Rashi** at Gen 26:5: *mishmarti — gezerot l-harchaqah al ha-azharot she-ba-Torah ... mitzvotai — d'varim she-illu lo nikhtevu r'uyot hen l-tzivah... chuqotai — d'varim she-yetzer ha-ra u-mum'ot olam meshivin aleihem... torotai — l'rabot torah she-be-al peh*. The rabbinic-traditional reading harmonizes the anachronism by extending Avraham's observance to cover the full Mosaic-Sinaitic and Oral-Torah corpus by anticipation. The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline rendering and surfaces both the modern critical Sinaitic-retrojection reading and the rabbinic-traditional anticipation reading in apparatus.

**The verse's structural function in the Yitzchaq cycle.** The verse caps the YHWH-self-disclosure to Yitzchaq at vv 2-5, in which YHWH reissues the Avrahamic land-promise to Yitzchaq. The cluster-clause at v 5 — *eqev asher shama Avraham b-qoli va-yishmor mishmarti mitzvotai chuqotai v-torotai* — provides the *grounds* for the promise-reissue: Yitzchaq receives the promise *because Avraham obeyed*. The grounds-clause structurally parallels Deut 7:8 (the election-grounded-in-the-fathers' love: *ki me-ahavat YHWH etkhem... va-yotzi YHWH etkhem b-yad chazaqah*) and Deut 9:5 (the gift-grounded-in-the-fathers' merit: *u-vi-shvuat YHWH asher nishba la-avotekha l-Avraham l-Yitzchaq u-l-Ya'aqov*). The grounds-clause-structure is a Deuteronomic signature, and the fourfold cluster at Gen 26:5 is its patriarchal-narrative parallel.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline four-noun rendering — *my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws* — without flattening the cluster into a hendiadys or smoothing it into modern legal-paraphrase. The four nouns are preserved as a distinct cluster so that the source-critical anomaly is visible to the reader. The Sinaitic-retrojection reading and the rabbinic-traditional anticipation reading are documented in apparatus and not preempted. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the source-critical reading is mainstream modern-critical-commentary and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Wellhausen *Prolegomena zur Geschichte Israels* 1883/1885; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 363-364; von Rad *Genesis: A Commentary* OTL 1961/1972 pp. 268-269; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425-426; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 184-185; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 189-190; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 195-196; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 134; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 200; Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003; Knohl *The Sanctuary of Silence* 1995 (on the P / H stratification); Levinson *Deuteronomy and the Hermeneutics of Legal Innovation* 1997. Rabbinic-tradition sources: m. Qidd. 4:14; b. Yoma 28b; Gen Rab 64:4; Rashi at Gen 26:5. HALOT and BDB s.v. *mishmeret*, *mitzvah*, *chuqah*, *torah*. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the fourfold cluster is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of the cluster as a unit is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the cluster's concentration in late-Deuteronomistic / Priestly / post-exilic / Chronistic strata is documentary-attested in the standard source-critical commentaries; (d) the modern critical Sinaitic-retrojection reading is mainstream (Wellhausen, von Rad, Westermann, Sarna, Wenham, Hamilton, Alter, Friedman); (e) the rabbinic-traditional anticipation reading is documented in m. Qidd. 4:14, b. Yoma 28b, Gen Rab 64:4, and Rashi, and is flagged-not-preempted.

terms.source: וְהִנֵּ֤ה יִצְחָק֙ מְצַחֵ֔ק אֵ֖ת רִבְקָ֥ה אִשְׁתּֽוֹ
terms.translit: v-hineh Yitzchaq m-tzacheq et Rivkah ishto
terms.partOfSpeech: verb, piel participle masculine singular + direct-object marker + proper noun + noun-with-3ms-suffix
terms.literal: and behold, Yitzchaq was playing / sporting / fondling with Rivkah his wife
terms.standardEnglish: Isaac was sporting with Rebekah his wife (ASV/KJV); Isaac was fondling his wife Rebekah (NRSV/NJPS); Isaac was caressing Rebecca his wife (Alter)
terms.wohChoice: Yitzchaq was sporting with Rivkah his wife (ASV-baseline; the conjugal sense is determinative here and is surfaced in apparatus; the wordplay with Yitzchaq's own name is preserved by the bare *sporting* rendering)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:8 deploys the piel participle *m-tzacheq* in a clearly conjugal sense: Yitzchaq is observed *m-tzacheq*-ing with Rivkah his wife through a window by Avimelekh, and the observation is sufficient to disclose the marriage that Yitzchaq had been concealing under the wife-sister cover. The conjugal sense at Gen 26:8 is distinct from the contested Gen 21:9 piel-participle of the same lexeme (where Sarah saw Yishma'el *m-tzacheq* and the meaning is irreducibly disputed across four readings: see the companion `metzacheq-ishmael-debate` entry).** The two attestations are not in competition: they are distinct uses of a deliberately polysemous piel-participle. This entry documents the cross-corpus *m-tzacheq* polysemy and disambiguates the Gen 26:8 conjugal usage from the Gen 21:9 contested usage and from the wider HB distribution of the lexeme.

**The piel of *tzachaq* — the full polysemy.** The piel of *tz-ch-q* (intensive of the qal *laugh*) carries a wide semantic range across the HB: *play, jest, sport, mock, joke, laugh, caress, fondle, make-love, perform-for-others*. The full range is fully on display across the Genesis-and-beyond attestations.

*(a) Gen 17:17* — Avraham *va-yitzchaq* (qal — *laughed*) at the promise of Yitzchaq's birth. The qal stem is the lexical base; the piel is its intensification. The qal here is the laughter-of-incredulity.

*(b) Gen 18:12, 18:13, 18:15 (twice)* — Sarah *va-titzchaq* (qal — *laughed*) at the same promise; the threefold piel repetition at vv 13, 15 with YHWH catching her out: *lamah zeh tzachakah Sarah* — *why did Sarah laugh*; *va-tekhachesh Sarah lemor lo tzachakti ki yare'ah va-yomer lo ki tzachakt* — *and Sarah denied saying, I did not laugh, for she was afraid; and he said, No, but you did laugh*. The laughter-of-incredulity register.

*(c) Gen 19:14* — Lot's sons-in-law thought he was *k-m-tzacheq* (piel participle with comparative — *like one mocking / joking*) in his warning about Sodom's destruction. The piel here is in the *joking / not-serious-speaker* register: the sons-in-law treat Lot's warning as a joke.

*(d) Gen 21:6* — Sarah on Yitzchaq's birth: *tzchoq asah li Elohim kol-ha-shomea yitzchaq-li* — *Elohim has made laughter for me; all that hear will laugh with me*. The lexeme is the chapter's running paronomastic-name-play with *Yitzchaq*.

*(e) Gen 21:9* — Sarah saw Yishma'el *m-tzacheq* (piel participle, no direct object in MT; LXX adds *with Isaac her son*). The contested attestation. The four-reading debate is governed by the companion `metzacheq-ishmael-debate` entry: *playing* (modern critical default — Sarna, Alter, NJPS, Westermann); *playing-with-Isaac* (LXX expansion / NRSV); *mocking* (ASV/KJV — piel-harsher); *maximal rabbinic-tradition reading* (idolatry, sexual immorality, bloodshed — Gen Rab 53:11, b. Sotah 26b, Rashi).

*(f) Gen 26:8* (here) — Yitzchaq *m-tzacheq* (piel participle, with explicit direct object *et Rivkah ishto* — *with Rivkah his wife*). The conjugal-tenderness / love-making register. The piel here is in the *love-play* / *caressing / fondling / making-love* register. The contextual disambiguators are decisive: (i) the explicit direct object names the wife; (ii) the verb is observed through a window — a culturally-marked sight-line for intimate behavior; (iii) the observation is sufficient to disclose the marriage to Avimelekh, who immediately concludes *hineh ishtekha hi* (v 9 — *behold, she is your wife*). The visual sufficiency requires the *m-tzacheq* to be intimate-conjugal-recognition-disclosing; mere *playing* would not have been disclosive of the marriage. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 185: *the verb here clearly carries the sense of marital intimacy; the wordplay with Yitzchaq's name remains active*. **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 134: *the participle is in the love-play register; the bedroom-window sight-line and the disclosive force require the intimate sense*. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 425: *the piel here names erotic-marital play*. **NJPS** (1985) renders *fondling*; **NRSV** *fondling*; **NIV-2011** *caressing*; **CSB** *caressing*. The piel-conjugal reading is the modern critical consensus.

The Hebrew also activates the **paronomastic name-play**: *Yitzchaq m-tzacheq* — *Yitzchaq Yitzchaq-ing* / *the-laugher laughing*. The verb-name homophony is the chapter's continuation of the Gen 17-21 laughter-and-name-play arc (see the companion `yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern` entry). The conjugal sense and the name-play are *jointly* operative: the patriarch named *He-will-laugh* is observed *laughing* / *playing-erotically* with his wife. The compound is editorially dense.

*(g) Exod 32:6* — the golden-calf incident: *va-yashvu la-ekhol v-shato va-yaqumu l-tzacheq* — *and they sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play*. The piel-infinitive in the golden-calf context. The rabbinic-traditional reading and the Pauline reception (1 Cor 10:7 *neither be idolaters as some of them were, as it is written: the people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play* — Greek *paizein*) read the *l-tzacheq* in the **ritual-sexual-cultic** register: the calf-worship is accompanied by sexual-cultic-revelry. **Childs** *The Book of Exodus* OTL 1974 pp. 565-566 surfaces both the bare-revelry and the sexual-cultic readings.

*(h) Judg 16:25* — Samson made to *l-tzacheq* (piel-infinitive — *to perform, to make-sport, to entertain*) before the Philistines at the Dagon-temple. The piel here is in the **performance / spectacle** register: Samson is brought out as entertainment, an object of mockery, a performer. The Philistines' *l-tzacheq* deployment of Samson recalls (and inverts) the *bnei Yisrael b-Mitzrayim* / *l-tzacheq* / Samson-as-prisoner-performer register.

*(i) 2 Sam 2:14* — *y-sachaqu* (a parallel root *s-ch-q* — *laugh, sport*, semantically near-identical to *tz-ch-q*; the *s* / *tz* alternation is a Hebrew dialectal variant): *yaqumu na ha-ne'arim v-y-sachaqu l-faneinu* — *let the young men arise and play before us*. The piel-cohortative in the deadly-tournament-play register at the Avner-Yoav contest before Gibeon's pool.

*(j) Ezek 23:32 (?)*; Lam 1:7; Hab 1:10 — qal-stem occurrences in the laughter / mockery / triumph-laugh registers.

**The cross-corpus polysemy summarized.** The piel of *tz-ch-q* covers the semantic range: **(α) playful-laughter** (Gen 21:9 default; Gen 26:8 wordplay-element); **(β) mocking-joking** (Gen 19:14; ASV/KJV reading of Gen 21:9); **(γ) erotic-love-play** (Gen 26:8 determinative; Gen 39:14, 17 *l-tzacheq bi* in Potiphar's wife's accusation against Yosef); **(δ) ritual-sexual-cultic revelry** (Exod 32:6 rabbinic-traditional / Pauline reading); **(ε) performance-spectacle** (Judg 16:25); **(ζ) deadly-tournament-play** (2 Sam 2:14, parallel root). The piel is deliberately polysemous, and each attestation is disambiguated by context.

**The disambiguation principle.** The four-or-five contested attestations of piel *tzacheq* in Genesis (Gen 19:14; 21:9; 26:8; 39:14, 17) constitute a polysemy-cluster across the patriarchal-narratives. The polysemy is editorially-deliberate: the lexeme's semantic openness allows the same verb to carry mockery, play, erotic-tenderness, and accusation across distinct narrative moments. The **Gen 26:8 attestation is disambiguated by the explicit direct object** (*et Rivkah ishto*) and **by the disclosive-force of the visual observation**. The **Gen 21:9 attestation is *not* disambiguated** — the MT supplies no direct object, the visual observation is by Sarah (not by a non-Israelite king with an outsider's external-recognition frame), and the consequence (v 10's demand for expulsion) is itself the source of the lexeme's contested character. The two attestations are thus structurally distinct: Gen 26:8 is grammatically-determinate; Gen 21:9 is grammatically-indeterminate.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline *sporting* at Gen 26:8 — a euphemistic-archaic register that preserves the conjugal sense while keeping the wordplay-with-Yitzchaq's-name active. Modern explicit renderings (*fondling*, *caressing*, *making love with*) sharpen the conjugal sense at the cost of obscuring the name-play. The WoH choice is editorially conservative: the conjugal sense is documented in apparatus and in the commentary; the rendering preserves the lexical-textural connection to Yitzchaq's name. The modern critical alternative (NRSV / NJPS *fondling*; Alter *caressing*) is documented but not adopted.

**Cross-references.** See also the companion `metzacheq-ishmael-debate` entry for the Gen 21:9 contested usage; the `yitzchaq-name-etymology-laughter-pattern` entry (Gen 17-21) for the running laughter-and-name-play arc; and the Gen 39 sibling overlay entry `le-tzachek-mockery-piel-gen39` (Gen 39:14, 17, in the overlay `_translation-glossary.json`), which governs the *l-tzacheq bi/banu* attestation in Potifar's wife's accusation (range γ above) and back-references this entry by id — a true bidirectional sibling link per the ratified sibling-overlay pattern.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 185, 146; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 134, 103-104; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425, 338-339; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 190, 81-82; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 196, 78-79; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 200, 155-156; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 364, 322-323; Childs *The Book of Exodus* OTL 1974 pp. 565-566 (on Exod 32:6); HALOT s.v. *tz-ch-q* and BDB s.v. *tzachaq* p. 850. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the piel participle *m-tzacheq* is grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the cross-corpus polysemy of piel *tz-ch-q* is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the conjugal disambiguation at Gen 26:8 is mainstream modern-critical-consensus (Sarna, Alter, Westermann, NJPS, NRSV, NIV-2011, CSB); (d) the Gen 21:9 contested character is documented in the companion entry; (e) the polysemy-cluster framework is documentary-derivable from the cross-corpus distribution. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.source: וַיִּקְרָ֤א שֵֽׁם־הַבְּאֵר֙ עֵ֔שֶׂק ... וַיִּקְרָ֥א שְׁמָ֖הּ שִׂטְנָֽה ... וַיִּקְרָ֤א שְׁמָהּ֙ רְחֹב֔וֹת
terms.translit: va-yiqra shem ha-be'er Esek ... va-yiqra sh'mah Sitnah ... va-yiqra sh'mah Rechovot
terms.strongs: H6230 (Esek; from H6229 *asaq* — contend); H7856 (Sitnah; from H7853 *satan* — be hostile, accuse); H7344 (Rechovot; from H7337 *rachav* — be wide, broad)
terms.partOfSpeech: three place-name-naming-formulae, each verb (qal impf 3ms, vav-consec) + noun-with-suffix + proper-noun place-name
terms.literal: and he called the name of the well Esek (Contention) ... and he called its name Sitnah (Opposition / Hostility) ... and he called its name Rechovot (Broad-Places / Room)
terms.standardEnglish: Esek ... Sitnah ... Rehoboth (ASV/KJV); Esek ... Sitnah ... Rehoboth (NRSV); Esek ... Sitnah ... Rehoboth (NJPS); Contention ... Enmity ... Wide-Places (semantic-rendering, rare)
terms.wohChoice: Esek ... Sitnah ... Rechovot (transliterated, per WoH compound-place-name convention; the in-text etiologies translated literally so the *contention → opposition → room* progression is visible)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:20-22 stages a deliberate three-well place-name etymology triad in the Yitzchaq cycle: *Esek* (Contention), *Sitnah* (Opposition / Hostility), and *Rechovot* (Broad-Places / Room).** Each name is given an in-text etiology, and the three names together form a structural progression — *contention → opposition → room* — that traces Yitzchaq's transition from contested-water-rights conflict with the herdsmen of Gerar to the *making-room* (*hirchiv YHWH lanu*) that frames his arrival at Be'er Sheva. The triad is the chapter's keystone naming-cluster and the Yitzchaq-cycle's distinctive contribution to the patriarchal well-naming pattern (see also `be-er-sheva-folk-etymology` for the parallel Be'er Sheva double-etymology).

**(i) *Esek* (עֵשֶׂק) — v 20.** *Va-yiqra shem ha-be'er Esek ki hit'as'qu imo* — *and he called the name of the well Esek, because they contended with him*. The name derives from the verb *hit'as'qu* (hithpael of *a-s-q*), the cognate verb in the in-text etiology. The root *a-s-q* (*contend, strive, dispute*) is a hapax-or-near-hapax HB verbal-root (the noun *Esek* is also a hapax as a common noun; it occurs only as the place-name). **HALOT** s.v. *Esek* notes the lexeme's restriction to the Gen 26:20 attestation. The name names the disputed-well: the Gerar herdsmen contended with Yitzchaq's herdsmen for the water, and the contention itself supplies the name. The contention-register is **legal-economic** (water-rights litigation) rather than military: the dispute is verbal and territorial, not violent.

**(ii) *Sitnah* (שִׂטְנָה) — v 21.** *Va-yachp'ru be'er acheret va-yarivu gam aleha va-yiqra sh'mah Sitnah* — *and they dug another well, and they contended for that also, and he called its name Sitnah*. The name derives from the **root *s-t-n* (שׂ-ט-ן)** — the same root as ***ha-satan*** (הַשָּׂטָן, *the adversary / the accuser*) in Job 1-2, Zech 3, and 1 Chr 21:1. The root's primary register is **hostile-opposition / adversarial-accusation**: a *satan* in the HB ordinary-language sense is a *legal accuser* or *military adversary*. The noun *sitnah* (the abstract-noun feminine of *satan*) names the *act of accusation / hostile-opposition*. The lexeme has a sparse but significant HB distribution: **Gen 26:21** (here, the place-name); **Ezra 4:6** (the *sitnah al yoshvei Yehudah v-Yerushalayim* — the *accusation* sent against the inhabitants of Yehudah and Yerushalayim under Achashverosh; the only non-Gen-26 attestation of the abstract-noun). The verbal root *satan* (*to be hostile, to accuse, to oppose*) is itself sparsely-distributed but with notable theological loci: Ps 38:21; 71:13; 109:4, 20, 29; Zech 3:1 (the *ha-satan* opposing the high-priest Yehoshua); and the *ha-satan* — the personified-or-quasi-personified adversary — at Job 1:6-12; 2:1-7; Zech 3:1-2; 1 Chr 21:1. The cross-corpus *s-t-n* attestations are governed by separate glossary entries (the bnei-elohim entries at Job 1-2; the future Zechariah-and-Chronicles entries).

**The Sitnah / ha-satan cross-reference: flagged-but-not-conflated.** The Gen 26:21 *Sitnah* lexeme shares its root with the later-HB *ha-satan* (the adversarial-figure of Job 1-2, Zech 3, 1 Chr 21:1) but is **not the same lexeme**. The Gen 26:21 *Sitnah* is a **place-name** derived from the *legal-or-territorial-contention* register; the Job 1-2 *ha-satan* is a **definite-article-marked common-noun** (later evolving toward proper-noun status) in the **divine-council-prosecutor** register. The two attestations share the root but inhabit distinct semantic registers and distinct narrative-theological functions. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 186 notes the root-connection without conflating: *the place-name's root is identical to that of the later ha-satan; the place-name register is restricted to the territorial-contention sense*. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 426 similarly. The flagged-but-not-conflated treatment preserves the philological connection while preserving the editorial-register-distinction.

**(iii) *Rechovot* (רְחֹבוֹת) — v 22.** *Va-yatek mi-sham va-yachp'r be'er acheret v-lo ravu aleha va-yiqra sh'mah Rechovot va-yomer ki-atah hirchiv YHWH lanu u-farinu va-aretz* — *and he removed from there, and dug another well, and they did not contend for it; and he called its name Rechovot, and he said, For now YHWH has made-room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land*. The name derives from the root ***r-ch-v*** (*be wide, be broad, make-room*); the in-text etiology explicitly cites the verb *hirchiv* (hiphil — *made-room*). The plural-form *Rechovot* (literally *broad-places*) names the **room-and-fruitfulness register**: the *room-making* by YHWH is the conditions for the *fruitfulness* (the *u-farinu va-aretz* echo of the *p-r-h* / *piru u-rvu* fruitfulness-formula of Gen 1:28, 9:1, 17:6, 35:11). The name resolves the triad's progression: from *contention* (Esek) through *opposition* (Sitnah) to *room and fruitfulness* (Rechovot). The Gen 1:28 / 9:1 fruitfulness-formula echo is editorially deliberate: the Yitzchaq-arc resolves into the same multiplication-blessing that frames the primeval narrative and the patriarchal land-promise.

**The lexeme's cross-corpus distribution.** *Rechovot* as a toponym recurs at **Gen 10:11** (the Assyrian *Rechovot Ir* — *broad-places of the city*; the post-Babel city-name); **Gen 36:37** / **1 Chr 1:48** (the Edomite king *Sha'ul mi-Rechovot ha-nahar* — *Shaul of Rechovot of the River*; the Edomite king-list place-name). The HB *Rechovot* attestations are three distinct places (the Yitzchaq-well, the Assyrian city, the Edomite-river place); the recurring *rechov* / *r-ch-v* element is the *broad-place / open-square / wide-room* register, distinguishing the *rechov* from the narrow alley or street. The cross-corpus pattern confirms the lexeme's register as **space-of-room** / **open-area** in contradistinction to constraint or contention.

**The structural progression: contention → opposition → room.** The three-name triad is editorially deliberate. The progression traces the Yitzchaq-arc's pastoral-conflict-resolution: each well-digging is met with successively-named conflict (the conflict-names escalating from *contention* to *opposition*), until the third digging yields a well without conflict and the YHWH-attributed *room-making*. The pattern is structurally a **three-fold-progression-with-resolution**: a common biblical narrative-pattern (cf. Eliezer's three-stage encounter with Rivkah at Gen 24; the three signs at Exod 4; the three-fold prophetic-call patterns). The Yitzchaq-arc-progression is the patriarchal-cycle's example of the pattern applied to **territorial-pastoral conflict-resolution**.

**The deferred-name-naming as theological signature.** A second editorial feature: at each well, **Yitzchaq names the well after the conflict, not after a resolution**. Esek and Sitnah are names that **memorialize the conflict** rather than transcend it. Only at Rechovot does Yitzchaq name the well after a positive outcome (room / fruitfulness) attributed to YHWH. The naming-pattern is itself a form of **theological signature**: Yitzchaq does not gloss over the conflict-history; he names it. The pastoral-pacifist register (Yitzchaq does not fight back — he simply moves to dig another well) is editorially-deliberate. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 186: *the names memorialize the conflict; Yitzchaq's pastoral-pacifist response is editorially-foregrounded*. **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 191: *the triad encodes Yitzchaq's character — patient, non-confrontational, willing to move rather than fight*.

**Geographical identification.** The three wells have been variously identified with sites in the Negev / north-Negev region. **Esek** has been tentatively identified with *Wadi 'Asaq* (south of Gaza; the etymological echo); **Sitnah** has no widely-accepted identification; **Rechovot** has been tentatively identified with *Wadi Ruheibah* (about 30 km southwest of Beersheba; the etymological echo). The identifications are tentative and not load-bearing for the narrative. **Aharoni** *The Land of the Bible* (rev. ed. 1979) reviews the Negev-toponymic-identification literature.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 185-186; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425-426; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 190-192; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 197-199; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 135; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 201; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 365-366; Aharoni *The Land of the Bible* rev. ed. 1979 (for the Negev-toponym identification); HALOT s.v. *Esek*, *Sitnah*, *Rechovot*, and the verbal roots *a-s-q*, *s-t-n*, *r-ch-v*; BDB s.v. *Esek* p. 796, *Sitnah* p. 966, *Rechovot* p. 932. For the *s-t-n* / *ha-satan* root-but-not-conflation treatment: see Day *An Adversary in Heaven* 1988; Stokes *Satan: How God's Executioner Became the Enemy* 2019; the future companion entries at Job 1-2 and Zech 3.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the three name-naming-formulae are grammatically explicit in the MT; (b) the in-text etiologies (the *hit'as'qu* / *yarivu* / *hirchiv* cognate-verb-deployments) are grammatically explicit; (c) the *s-t-n* root-connection to *ha-satan* is documentary-lexicographic fact (HALOT, BDB) and is flagged-but-not-conflated (the place-name and the adversarial-figure share the root without sharing the semantic register); (d) the structural-progression reading (contention → opposition → room) is mainstream modern-critical-consensus; (e) the cross-corpus distribution of *Rechovot* as a toponym is verifiable; (f) the pastoral-pacifist character-reading of Yitzchaq is documented in Sarna, Wenham, and the standard commentaries.

terms.strongs: H408 (al — negative-particle) + H3372 (yare — fear, qal impf 2ms with jussive force) + H854 (et — with) + H595 (anokhi — I)
terms.partOfSpeech: negative-particle + verb (qal impf 2ms jussive) + causal-conjunction + preposition-with-2ms-suffix + 1cs-independent-pronoun
terms.literal: do-not fear, for with-you (am) I
terms.standardEnglish: fear not, for I am with thee (ASV/KJV); do not fear, for I am with you (NRSV); fear not, for I am with you (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: do not fear, for I am with you (ASV-baseline; the cross-corpus distribution and the divine-encouragement-formula character surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:24 contains the first patriarchal occurrence of *al-tira ki-itkha anokhi* — *do not fear, for I am with you* — in the canonical divine-encouragement-formula register. The formula is one of the Hebrew Bible's most distributionally extensive divine-speech-openers, recurring across the Torah, the Historical Books, the Prophets, and the post-exilic literature, with a particularly dense Isaian and Jeremianic-prophetic-call distribution.** This entry documents the formula's cross-corpus reach as a single editorial unit and governs its future applications across the patriarchal narratives and beyond.

**The formula's components.**

*(i) *Al-tira* — the negative-jussive opener.* The construction *al + tira* (negative-particle *al* + qal-imperfect 2ms of *yare* — *fear*) is the **prohibitive-jussive** form *do not fear*. The construction is distinguished from the **absolute-negative** *lo tira* (*you shall not fear*; e.g., Deut 7:18) by its **immediate-situational-prohibition force**: *al-tira* addresses a specific present anxiety, where *lo tira* expresses a general legal-prohibition. The *al-tira* form is the standard HB **divine-encouragement-opener** at moments of theophany or prophetic-call.

*(ii) *Ki-itkha anokhi* — the divine-presence-grounds clause.* The construction *ki + itkha anokhi* (*for + with-you (am) I*) supplies the **grounds-clause** for the prohibition-of-fear. The deity's presence (*itkha anokhi*) is the reason the addressee need not fear. The clause is **structurally inseparable from the formula**: the *al-tira* prohibition almost always travels with a presence-grounds clause or a related divine-self-disclosure.

**The cross-corpus distribution.**

*Patriarchal narratives.* **Gen 15:1** (the inaugural Avram-theophany at the *brit-bein-ha-betarim* vision: *al-tira Avram anokhi magen lakh skharkha harbeh me'od* — *fear not, Avram, I am a shield to you, your reward shall be exceedingly great*; the presence-grounds is the *magen* / shield-image rather than the *itkha anokhi* itself, but the formula-pattern is established); **Gen 21:17** (the Hagar-in-the-wilderness mal'akh-Elohim-encounter: *mah-lakh Hagar al-tir'i ki-shama Elohim el-qol ha-na'ar* — *what ails you, Hagar? fear not, for Elohim has heard the voice of the lad*; the presence-grounds is the divine-hearing); **Gen 26:24** (here — Yitzchaq at Be'er Sheva, the most-canonical patriarchal deployment with the *anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha al-tira ki-itkha anokhi* compound — see the companion `anokhi-elohei-X-avikha-ancestral-god-formula` entry); **Gen 35:17** (Rachel's midwife at Benjamin's birth: *al-tir'i ki gam-zeh lakh ben* — *fear not, for this also is a son to you*; the presence-grounds is the son-promise rather than the divine-presence, but the formula-pattern recurs); **Gen 46:3** (Ya'aqov at Be'er Sheva on the way down to Mitsrayim: *Anokhi ha-El Elohei avikha al-tira me-r'dah Mitsraymah ki l-goy gadol asimkha sham* — *I am the El, the Elohim of your father; do not fear to go down to Mitsrayim, for I will make of you a great nation there*; the compound formula with the ancestral-god self-disclosure).

*Exodus and wilderness.* **Exod 14:13** (Moses at the Red Sea: *al-tira'u hityatzvu u-r'u et-yeshu'at YHWH asher ya'aseh la-khem ha-yom* — *fear not, stand still and see the salvation of YHWH which he will work for you today*; the people-collective addressee); **Exod 20:20** (Moses at Sinai: *al-tira'u ki l-va'avur nasot et-khem ba ha-Elohim u-va'avur tihyeh yirato al-pneikhem l-vilti techeta'u* — *fear not, for Elohim has come to test you, and that his fear may be before you, that you not sin*); **Num 14:9** (Joshua and Caleb to the people: *al-tira'u et-am ha-aretz... va-YHWH itanu al tira'um* — *do not fear the people of the land... YHWH is with us, fear them not*; the presence-grounds clause); **Num 21:34** (YHWH to Moses on Og of Bashan: *al-tira oto ki b-yad'kha natati oto* — *fear him not, for I have delivered him into your hand*); **Deut 1:21** (Moses to the people on the land-conquest: *re ha-aretz... aleh resh ka-asher diber YHWH Elohei avoteikha lakh al-tira v-al-techat* — *see the land... go up, take possession, as YHWH the Elohim of your fathers has said to you; do not fear, and do not be dismayed*); **Deut 3:2** (Sihon and Og: *al-tira oto ki v-yad'kha natati oto*); **Deut 7:18** (the general-prohibition *lo tira* — note the *lo*-not-*al* form for the categorical-legal register); **Deut 20:1, 20:3** (the war-rules); **Deut 31:6, 31:8** (Moses to the people and to Joshua: the *chazak v-ematz al-tira'u v-al-tartzu* — *be strong and of good courage, fear not, and be not terrified*); **Deut 31:23** (YHWH to Joshua at the transition).

*Joshua and Judges.* **Josh 1:9** (the post-Moses YHWH-charge to Joshua: *halo tzivvitikha chazak v-ematz al-ta'arotz v-al-techat ki imkha YHWH Elohekha b-khol asher telekh*); **Josh 8:1** (the post-Ai instructions: *al-tira v-al-techat*); **Josh 10:8** (the Gibeon-coalition battle: *al-tira me-hem ki v-yad'kha natatim*); **Josh 10:25** (Joshua to his commanders); **Josh 11:6** (the Hazor-coalition: *al-tira mi-pneihem ki machar*); **Judg 4:18** (Yael to Sisera: *al-tira* — the ironic-treacherous deployment); **Judg 6:23** (the Gideon-call: *shalom lakh al-tira lo tamut* — *peace be to you, fear not, you shall not die*).

*Samuel and David.* **1 Sam 4:20** (Eli's daughter-in-law at the death of Eli: *al-tir'i ki ben yaladt* — *fear not, for you have borne a son*); **1 Sam 22:23** (David to Aviatar: *shvah iti al-tira ki asher y'vakesh et-nafshi y'vakesh et-nafshekha*); **1 Sam 23:17** (Yonatan to David: *al-tira ki lo timtza'akha yad Sha'ul avi* — *fear not, for the hand of Sha'ul my father shall not find you*; the inter-personal deployment with the *itkha-anokhi* presence-grounds-analog in v 16 *va-y'chazek et-yado be-Elohim*); **2 Sam 9:7** (David to Mephiboshet: *al-tira ki aso e'eseh imkha chesed*); **2 Sam 13:28** (Avshalom to his servants: the ironic-violent deployment); **2 Kgs 1:15** (the *mal'akh YHWH* to Eliyahu: *red oto al-tira mi-panav*); **2 Kgs 6:16** (Elisha to his servant at Dotan: *al-tira ki rabim asher itanu me-asher otam* — *do not fear, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them*); **2 Kgs 19:6** / **Isa 37:6** (Yeshayahu to Hizqiyahu: *al-tira mi-pnei ha-d'varim asher shamata*); **2 Kgs 25:24** (Gedalyah to the people).

*The major prophets — the formula's densest distribution.* **Isaiah** is the formula's densest concentration in the HB. **Isa 7:4** (Yeshayahu to Achaz: *al-tira u-l-vavkha al-yerakh*; the Imanu-El sign-context); **Isa 8:12** (the *al-tomru qesher* + *al-tira'u*); **Isa 10:24** (*al-tira ami yoshev Tziyon*); **Isa 35:4** (*al-tira'u hineh Eloheikhem*); **Isa 37:6** (par. 2 Kgs 19:6); **Isa 40:9** (the herald-of-Tziyon); **Isa 41:10** (the foundational deutero-Isaiah deployment: *al-tira ki imkha-ani al-tishta ki ani Elohekha* — *do not fear, for I am with you; do not be dismayed, for I am your Elohim*; the *imkha-ani* compound is the formula's classical-prophetic form); **Isa 41:13** (*al-tira ani azartikha*); **Isa 41:14** (*al-tir'i tola'at Ya'aqov*); **Isa 43:1, 43:5** (the Servant-Israel passages: *al-tira ki ge'altikha* / *al-tira ki itkha-ani*); **Isa 44:2, 44:8** (*al-tira avdi Ya'aqov*); **Isa 51:7** (*al-tira'u cherpat enosh*); **Isa 54:4** (*al-tira'i ki lo tevoshi*); **Isa 54:14** (the post-exilic-restoration passage). **Jeremiah**: **Jer 1:8** (the inaugural Jeremiah-call: *al-tira mi-pneihem ki-itkha ani l-hatzilekha*); **Jer 1:17** (par.); **Jer 10:5** (the idol-mockery passage); **Jer 30:10, 46:27-28** (the Israel-restoration passages); **Jer 40:9** (Gedalyah to the people, par. 2 Kgs 25:24); **Jer 42:11** (*al-tir'u mi-pnei melekh Bavel*). **Ezekiel**: **Ezek 2:6, 3:9** (the inaugural Ezekiel-call: *al-tira me-hem u-mi-divrehem al-tira*). **Lamentations**: **Lam 3:57** (the foundational Lamentations passage: *qaravta b-yom ekra'eka amarta al-tira*). **Daniel**: **Dan 10:12, 10:19** (Gavri'el to Daniel: the apocalyptic-deployment).

*Minor prophets and post-exilic.* **Hag 2:5** (*ka-davar asher karati it'khem b-tzeitkhem mi-Mitsrayim v-ruchi omedet b'tokh'khem al-tira'u*); **Zech 8:13, 8:15** (the post-exilic restoration: *al-tira'u techezakna y'deikhem*); **Mal 3:5** (the inversion); **Joel 2:21, 22** (*al-tir'i adamah... al-tir'u bahamot sadai*); **Neh 4:8** (Nehemiah to the builders).

**The cross-corpus pattern.** The formula's distribution traces a structural-pattern across the HB: it appears at **(α) moments of theophany or prophetic-call** (Gen 15:1; 26:24; Exod 3:6 — the burning bush; Isa 6, 41:10; Jer 1:8; Ezek 2-3; Dan 10); **(β) moments of military or political crisis** (Exod 14:13; Num 14:9, 21:34; Josh 8:1, 10:8; 2 Kgs 6:16, 19:6); **(γ) moments of inter-personal reassurance** (Gen 21:17, 35:17; 1 Sam 4:20, 23:17; 2 Sam 9:7); **(δ) moments of eschatological-restoration prophecy** (Isa 41-44; Jer 30, 46; Zech 8; Hag 2). The formula functions as a **divine-speech-marker** across these contexts: when YHWH (or a divine-messenger) speaks to an anxious human in the HB, the formula is the standard opener.

**Conrad and Nissinen on the formula's ANE context.** The formula has been studied as a cross-cultural ANE phenomenon. **Edgar W. Conrad** *Fear Not Warrior: A Study of 'al tira' Pericopes in the Hebrew Scriptures* (Brown Judaic Studies 75; Scholars Press 1985) is the foundational monograph on the formula. Conrad distinguishes the **warrior-encouragement** form (where the *al-tira* opens a war-oracle assuring the addressee of military victory; cf. the Mari-letters and the Neo-Assyrian prophecy-corpora) from the **patriarchal-encouragement** form (where the *al-tira* opens a covenant-renewal or land-promise-confirmation). The Gen 26:24 instance is a patriarchal-encouragement; the Isa 41-44 instances are a hybrid (warrior-encouragement re-purposed for the Servant-Israel). **Martti Nissinen** *Prophets and Prophecy in the Ancient Near East* (SBL Writings from the Ancient World 12; SBL 2003) documents the cross-cultural distribution: the formula is well-attested in **Neo-Assyrian prophecy-collections** (the *Esarhaddon-prophecies* and the *Ashurbanipal-prophecies*; e.g., the Esarhaddon-oracle *lā tapallah Aššurahiddin* — *do not fear, Esarhaddon*); in **Mari-letters** (the *lā tapallah* form recurring in Old-Babylonian prophetic-letters from the early 2nd millennium BCE); in **Egyptian royal-encouragement texts**. The HB formula sits within a well-documented ANE divine-encouragement-genre. The HB's editorial-contribution is the formula's **systematic deployment across the canonical theophany-and-prophetic-call narratives** as a structural marker of divine-speech-to-anxious-human.
