**The Gen 26:24 deployment.** The verse's full deployment — *anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha al-tira ki-itkha anokhi u-verakhtikha v-hirbeiti et-zar'akha ba'avur Avraham avdi* — combines four formulae: **(i)** the *anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha* ancestral-god self-disclosure formula (see the companion `anokhi-elohei-X-avikha-ancestral-god-formula` entry); **(ii)** the *al-tira ki-itkha anokhi* fear-not divine-encouragement formula (this entry); **(iii)** the *u-verakhtikha v-hirbeiti et-zar'akha* land-and-seed blessing formula (covered by the `zera-eretz-promise` and `patriarchal-seed-comparison-triad` entries); **(iv)** the *ba'avur Avraham avdi* Avraham-merit grounds-clause (linking back to the v 5 *eqev asher shama Avraham b-qoli* — see the companion `mishmeret-mitzvot-chuqim-torot-fourfold-covenant` entry). The verse is a **four-formula compound**, structurally one of the densest divine-self-disclosure verses in the patriarchal-narratives. The Yitzchaq-cycle's editorial density is a marker: Yitzchaq, the least-narratively-developed of the three patriarchs, receives the most-densely-formulaic divine-encouragement at the chapter's structural pivot.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Conrad *Fear Not Warrior* 1985 (the foundational monograph); Nissinen *Prophets and Prophecy in the Ancient Near East* 2003; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425-428; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 186-187; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 192-193; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 199-200; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 135; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 201; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 366-367; Greenberg *Ezekiel 1-20* AB 1983 (on the Ezekiel-call); Childs *Isaiah* OTL 2001 (on the Isaian distribution); Holladay *Jeremiah* Hermeneia 1986-1989 (on the Jeremiah-call); HALOT s.v. *yare*, *al*; BDB s.v. *yare* p. 431. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula's components are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the formula's ANE context (Mari-letters, Neo-Assyrian prophecy-collections) is documentary-attested and is documented in Conrad 1985 and Nissinen 2003; (d) the structural-function pattern (theophany / military-crisis / inter-personal / eschatological) is mainstream modern-critical-consensus; (e) the four-formula-compound character of Gen 26:24 is grammatically explicit. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: I (am) the Elohim of Avraham your father
terms.standardEnglish: I am the God of Abraham thy father (ASV/KJV); I am the God of your father Abraham (NRSV); I am the God of your father Abraham (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: I am the Elohim of Avraham your father (WoH preserves *Elohim* in transliteration per `elohim-as-translation`; the ancestral-god-formula's cross-corpus reach and the Alt 1929 reconstruction surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:24 contains the first canonical *anokhi Elohei X avikha* (I am the Elohim of X your father) ancestral-god self-disclosure formula in the patriarchal narratives. The formula identifies the speaking deity as the same deity who had a covenantal relationship with the addressee's father — establishing the deity's identity through ancestral-religious-continuity rather than through cosmological-creator predication.** The formula is the foundational HB form of what **Albrecht Alt** (1929 *Der Gott der Väter* — *The God of the Fathers*) reconstructed as the **patriarchal-period ancestral-god religion**: the pre-Mosaic religious-form in which each patriarchal-lineage worshipped the deity who had appeared to and made promises to that lineage's founding patriarch. The Gen 26:24 deployment is the textual-foundation for Alt's reconstruction.

**The formula's components.**

*(i) *Anokhi* — the emphatic 1cs-independent-pronoun.* The pronoun *anokhi* (as distinguished from the shorter *ani*) is the formula's standard opener in the divine-self-disclosure register. The *anokhi* form is the **archaic-or-poetic-or-emphatic** variant and is used in the HB's most-canonical divine-self-disclosure moments (Exod 20:2 — the Decalogue's *anokhi YHWH Elohekha asher hotzetikha me-eretz Mitsrayim* — *I am YHWH your Elohim, who brought you out of the land of Mitsrayim*; Deut 5:6 par.). The use of *anokhi* (rather than *ani*) at Gen 26:24 marks the formula as **canonical-divine-self-disclosure**, structurally analogous to the Decalogue-opener.

*(ii) *Elohei [name] avikha* — the construct-chain ancestral identification.* The construct-chain *Elohei [Patriarch-name] avikha* (*the Elohim of [Patriarch] your father*) identifies the deity through three nested specifications: **(a)** the divine-name *Elohei* (construct of *Elohim*); **(b)** the proper-name *Avraham* (the founding patriarch); **(c)** the kinship-term *avikha* (*your father*) re-establishing the genealogical-link. The construct-chain pattern is editorially distinctive: the deity is identified neither by self-naming (as in *Anokhi YHWH*) nor by cosmological-predicate (as in *Anokhi El-Shaddai*) but by **ancestral-relational genealogy**.

**The cross-corpus distribution.**

*Patriarchal narratives.* **Gen 26:24** (here — Yitzchaq at Be'er Sheva: *anokhi Elohei Avraham avikha*); **Gen 28:13** (Ya'aqov's ladder-vision at Bethel: *anokhi YHWH Elohei Avraham avikha v-Elohei Yitzchaq* — *I am YHWH, the Elohim of Avraham your father and the Elohim of Yitzchaq*; the **expanded two-generation** formula); **Gen 31:5** (Ya'aqov to Leah and Rachel: *Elohei avi hayah imadi* — *the Elohim of my father has been with me*; the indirect-deployment); **Gen 31:29** (Lavan to Ya'aqov: *Elohei avikhem emesh amar elai* — *the Elohim of your father spoke to me last night*); **Gen 31:42** (Ya'aqov to Lavan: *Elohei avi Elohei Avraham u-Pachad Yitzchaq* — *the Elohim of my father, the Elohim of Avraham, and the Pachad / Fear / Kinsman-of Yitzchaq*; the **expanded compound** formula with the otherwise-rare *Pachad Yitzchaq* divine-name); **Gen 31:53** (the Lavan-Ya'aqov covenant: *Elohei Avraham v-Elohei Nachor yishpetu beineinu Elohei avihem* — *the Elohim of Avraham and the Elohim of Nachor judge between us, the Elohim of their father*; the **collateral-ancestral** deployment, naming both Avraham's deity and Nachor's deity as potentially-distinct ancestral-god-figures); **Gen 32:10** (Ya'aqov before meeting Esav: *Elohei avi Avraham v-Elohei avi Yitzchaq* — *the Elohim of my father Avraham, and the Elohim of my father Yitzchaq*); **Gen 35:1** (YHWH to Ya'aqov: *kum aleh Bet-El... v-aseh sham mizbeach la-El ha-nireh elekha b-vorchakha mi-pnei Esav achikha* — *arise, go up to Bet-El... and make there an altar to the El who appeared to you when you fled from before Esav your brother*; the implicit ancestral-god-identification through the *appeared-to-you-before* clause); **Gen 35:3** (Ya'aqov: *El ha-oneh oti b-yom tzarati va-yhi imadi ba-derekh asher halakhti* — *El who answered me in the day of my distress and was with me in the way that I went*); **Gen 43:23** (Yosef's steward to the brothers: *Eloheikhem v-Elohei avikhem natan la-khem matmon b-amteach'oteikhem* — *your Elohim and the Elohim of your father has given you treasure*); **Gen 46:1, 46:3** (Ya'aqov at Be'er Sheva on the way down to Mitsrayim: v 1 *va-yizbach z'vachim l-Elohei aviv Yitzchaq*; v 3 *Anokhi ha-El Elohei avikha al-tira me-redah Mitsraymah* — *I am the El, the Elohim of your father; do not fear to go down to Mitsrayim*; the compound formula with *al-tira* — see the companion `al-tira-fear-not-divine-encouragement-formula` entry); **Gen 48:15** (Ya'aqov blessing Ephraim and Manasseh: *ha-Elohim asher hithalkhu avotai l-fanav Avraham v-Yitzchaq* — *the Elohim before whom my fathers Avraham and Yitzchaq walked*); **Gen 49:25** (the blessing of Yosef: *me-El avikha v-ya'azreka* — *from the El of your father, and he shall help you*); **Gen 50:17** (Yosef to his brothers: *Elohei avikha*).

*Exodus — the keystone deployment.* **Exod 3:6** (the burning bush — **THE foundational deployment of the formula**: *Anokhi Elohei avikha Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchaq v-Elohei Ya'aqov* — *I am the Elohim of your father, the Elohim of Avraham, the Elohim of Yitzchaq, and the Elohim of Ya'aqov*; the **triadic-patriarchal compound** that becomes the canonical-formula across the Pentateuch and beyond); **Exod 3:13-15** (the *anokhi Elohei avikha* re-deployment and the *YHWH Elohei avoteikhem Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchaq v-Elohei Ya'aqov ... zeh-shmi l-olam v-zeh zikhri l-dor dor* — the explicit divine-name-revelation tied to the ancestral-formula); **Exod 3:16** (the elders-of-Israel deployment: *YHWH Elohei avoteikhem nire'ah elai Elohei Avraham Yitzchaq v-Ya'aqov*); **Exod 4:5** (the formula's repetition for the sign-of-the-rod); **Exod 6:3** (the explicit P-or-late-P divine-name-revelation: *va-era el-Avraham el-Yitzchaq v-el-Ya'aqov b-El Shaddai u-shmi YHWH lo nodati la-hem* — *I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchaq, and to Ya'aqov as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them*; the source-critical-foundational verse for the documentary distinction between the P-source and the J-and-E-sources on the divine-name); **Exod 6:7-8** (the Exodus-promise: *v-laqachti et-khem li l-am v-hayiti la-khem l-Elohim... el-ha-aretz asher nasati et-yadi la-tet otah l-Avraham l-Yitzchaq u-l-Ya'aqov*); **Exod 33:1** (the post-Sinai re-deployment: *ha-aretz asher nishbati l-Avraham l-Yitzchaq u-l-Ya'aqov*).

*Deuteronomy and beyond.* **Deut 1:8, 1:11, 1:21, 4:1, 4:37, 6:3, 6:10, 6:18, 6:23, 7:8, 7:12-13, 8:18, 9:5, 10:11, 10:15, 10:22, 11:9, 11:21, 12:1, 13:7, 19:8, 26:3, 26:7, 26:15, 27:3, 28:11, 29:12, 30:5, 20, 31:7, 20-21, 34:4** — the Deuteronomic-paranetic-recurrence-cluster of *YHWH Elohei avoteikha*, *Avraham Yitzchaq v-Ya'aqov*, *ha-aretz asher nishba YHWH la-avotekha*. The Deuteronomic-cluster is the densest non-patriarchal-narrative deployment of the formula. Subsequent biblical books: **Josh 1:6, 18:3, 21:43-44, 24:2-3**; **1 Kgs 18:36** (Eliyahu at Karmel: *YHWH Elohei Avraham Yitzchaq v-Yisrael*); **2 Kgs 13:23**; **1 Chr 16:13, 29:18** (David's prayer: *YHWH Elohei Avraham Yitzchaq v-Yisrael avoteinu*); **2 Chr 30:6**; **Neh 9:7-8** (the Levitical confession); **Ps 47:10, 105:9** (the *brit Avraham v-shvuato l-Yitzchaq* compound).

**Alt's *Der Gott der Väter* (1929) and the patriarchal-ancestral-god-religion reconstruction.** **Albrecht Alt** in 1929 reconstructed the patriarchal-period religion on the basis of these ancestral-god-formulae. Alt's three foundational arguments:

*(i) The pre-Mosaic patriarchal religion is not YHWH-worship.* Per Exod 6:3 (the P-source's explicit divine-name-revelation), the divine-name YHWH is not known to the patriarchs; the patriarchs worship *El* under various designations (El-Shaddai, El-Elyon, El-Olam, El-Roi, El-Bet-El, El-Elohei-Yisrael, the Pachad-Yitzchaq, the Avir-Ya'aqov). The names are concentrated in the patriarchal narratives and become rare or absent after the Mosaic period.

*(ii) Each patriarchal-lineage worships an ancestral-god.* The recurring formula *Elohei [Patriarch] avikha* (*Elohim of [Patriarch] your father*) names the deity through ancestral-relationship, not through cosmological-predicate. The form is consistent with a **clan-based ancestral-religion**: each patriarchal-lineage has its own ancestral-god who appeared to and made promises to the lineage's founding patriarch, and the same deity continues to relate to the lineage through subsequent generations.

*(iii) The ancestral-god-formula has ANE West-Semitic parallels.* Alt argued from the Nabatean-period inscriptions (the *El of Pakeida*, *El of Obodas*) and from the Old-Babylonian and Mari-period prosopography for a wider West-Semitic ancestral-god-religion. The patriarchal-period Israelite religion was thus a regionally-typical clan-based ancestral-religion that subsequently merged into and was identified with the Mosaic YHWH-religion.

**Alt's reconstruction has been substantially refined in subsequent scholarship.** **Frank Moore Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* (1973, ch. 1) develops and partly revises Alt's reconstruction, arguing for the identification of the patriarchal *El* with the Ugaritic head-of-pantheon *El* and reading the *El*-titles as Canaanite-period El-cult variants. **William F. Albright** in subsequent decades argued for the historicity of the patriarchal-period traditions and accepted the broad outlines of Alt's reconstruction with chronological refinements. **John Van Seters** *Abraham in History and Tradition* (1975) and **Thomas Thompson** *The Historicity of the Patriarchal Narratives* (1974) substantially critique the historicity-claims, arguing that the patriarchal narratives reflect first-millennium-BCE Israelite conditions read back into the second-millennium. **Patrick D. Miller** *The Religion of Ancient Israel* (2000) and **Mark S. Smith** *The Early History of God* (1990, 2nd ed. 2002) develop the modern critical consensus: Alt's broad framework of a pre-Mosaic ancestral-god-religion is plausible, but the specific historicity of the patriarchal figures and the dating to the 2nd millennium BCE remain contested.

**The modern critical consensus position.** The cross-corpus distribution of the ancestral-god-formula is mainstream documentary-attested. The Alt 1929 reconstruction of the patriarchal-ancestral-god-religion is foundational but is subject to subsequent refinement and partial critique. The Exod 3 / Exod 6 explicit divine-name-revelation tradition (whereby the patriarchs' *Elohim* is retrospectively identified with YHWH) is the source-critical signature of the P-source (or late-P-source) editorial framework. The Gen 26:24 deployment is the **second-patriarchal deployment** of the formula (after Gen 26:24 itself's direct address to Yitzchaq), and is foundational for the subsequent Ya'aqov-cycle and Mosaic-cycle deployments.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline rendering *I am the Elohim of Avraham your father* (with *Elohim* in transliteration per the `elohim-as-translation` entry). The ancestral-god-formula's cross-corpus reach and the Alt 1929 reconstruction are documented in apparatus. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the ancestral-god-formula is mainstream modern-critical-consensus and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Alt *Der Gott der Väter* 1929 (Stuttgart: Kohlhammer; ET in *Essays on Old Testament History and Religion* 1966, pp. 1-77); Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1 (the Ugaritic-El-cult-identification revision); Albright *From the Stone Age to Christianity* 1940/1957; Van Seters *Abraham in History and Tradition* 1975; Thompson *The Historicity of the Patriarchal Narratives* 1974; Miller *The Religion of Ancient Israel* 2000; Smith *The Early History of God* 1990/2002; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 426-427; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 186; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 192-193; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 199; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 135; Childs *The Book of Exodus* OTL 1974 pp. 71-72 (on Exod 3:6); Propp *Exodus 1-18* AB 1999 pp. 191-194 (on Exod 3:6); Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 (on the P-source / J-source divine-name-distinction); de Pury *Promise to the Patriarchs* in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* V (1992).

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula's components are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Alt 1929 reconstruction is documentary and is documented in the standard *Old Testament Theology* and *Israelite Religion* literature; (d) the subsequent refinements (Cross, Albright, Van Seters, Miller, Smith) are documentary; (e) the modern critical consensus position is mainstream; (f) the Exod 3 / Exod 6 explicit divine-name-revelation tradition is HB-internal-explicit. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.strongs: H4786 (morah / morat — bitterness, grief) + H7307 (ruach — spirit, breath)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3fp, vav-consec) + construct-noun + noun + preposition-with-suffix + waw + preposition-with-suffix
terms.literal: and they were (a) bitterness of spirit to Yitzchaq and to Rivkah
terms.standardEnglish: and they were a grief of mind unto Isaac and to Rebekah (ASV); and they were a bitterness of spirit unto Isaac and to Rebekah (KJV); and they made life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah (NRSV); and they were a source of bitterness to Isaac and Rebekah (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and they were a bitterness of spirit to Yitzchaq and to Rivkah (KJV-baseline; the *morat-ruach* construct preserved as a fixed-phrase; the chapter-27 narrative-setup function surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 26:35 closes the chapter with the brief but editorially-load-bearing notice that Esav's two Chitti (Hittite) wives — Yehudit bat-Be'eri and Basemat bat-Elon (v 34) — were *morat ruach* (bitterness of spirit) to Yitzchaq and Rivkah. The notice is a narrative-setup verse for Gen 27 (the Yitzchaq-deathbed-blessing deception narrative): Rivkah's hostility to Esav and her stratagem for Ya'aqov are motivated in part by the wives, and her v 27:46 complaint to Yitzchaq — *qatzti b-chayyai mi-pnei b-not Chet* (*I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Chet*) — directly cites the v 26:35 notice.**

**The *morat ruach* lexeme.** The construct *morat ruach* (מֹרַת רוּחַ) is a fixed-phrase combining *morah* / *morat* (*bitterness, grief*; the construct-form of the abstract-noun from the root *m-r-r* — *be bitter*) with *ruach* (*spirit, breath, disposition*). The phrase as a fixed-construct is **virtually unique to Gen 26:35** in the HB. The related construct *mar nefesh* (*bitter of soul*) recurs more widely (Judg 18:25 — *anashim marei nefesh*; 1 Sam 1:10 — Channah *marat nafesh*; 1 Sam 22:2; 30:6; 2 Sam 17:8; Job 3:20; 7:11; 10:1; 21:25; Prov 31:6; Isa 38:15, 17; Ezek 27:31), and the related verbal expressions (*marar et-cha'yyei* — *embitter the life of*: Exod 1:14 — the Egyptians embittering Israelite life with hard labor; Ruth 1:20 — Naomi's *kara YHWH li mara*; the *mara* word-play) are well-attested. The *morat ruach* specifically is the chapter's distinctive variant.

**The *ruach* element.** The choice of *ruach* (rather than *nefesh* — *soul*) is editorially significant. The *ruach* in BH carries the **disposition / temperament / spirit-mood** register more than the *nefesh* (which carries the **life-force / personhood** register). The construct *morat ruach* names a **persistent dispositional-grievance** rather than a **soul-level affliction**. The choice is consistent with the verse's editorial function: the wives are an ongoing source of vexation, not a singular catastrophe. The lexeme **inverts** the more common HB *nakhat ruach* (*pleasantness of spirit*) and *qotzer ruach* (*shortness of spirit*; Exod 6:9 — Israel's response to Moses's reassurance) constructions, both of which use *ruach* in the dispositional-temperament register.

**The Gen 26:34-35 / Gen 27 narrative-link.** The closing-verse pair vv 34-35 is the chapter's **narrative-pivot toward Gen 27**: the v 34 wife-introduction and the v 35 *morat ruach* notice together establish the conditions for Rivkah's stratagem. The link is made explicit at **Gen 27:46**: *va-tomer Rivkah el-Yitzchaq qatzti b-chayyai mi-pnei b-not Chet im-loqeach Ya'aqov ishah mi-b'not Chet ka-eleh mi-b'not ha-aretz lamah li chayyim* — *and Rivkah said to Yitzchaq, I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Chet; if Ya'aqov takes a wife from the daughters of Chet such as these, of the daughters of the land, what good shall my life do me*. The v 27:46 complaint is **direct dialogue-quotation of the v 26:35 narrator's notice**: the narrator's *morat ruach* (the wives' effect on Yitzchaq and Rivkah) is reframed by Rivkah at v 27:46 as her own *qatzti b-chayyai* (I am weary of my life). The continuity is editorial-structural: the chapter-26 closing verses are not vestigial; they are the deliberate set-up for chapter 27's mother-and-son stratagem.

**The wife-list inconsistency: a flagged source-critical problem.** Esav's wife-list is given three different ways across Genesis: **(i) Gen 26:34** — Yehudit bat-Be'eri the Chitti, Basemat bat-Elon the Chitti; **(ii) Gen 28:9** (the Mahalat-addition: after the Yitzchaq-blessing-stratagem, Esav adds *Mahalat bat-Yishma'el achot Nevayot* — Mahalat daughter of Yishma'el, sister of Nevayot — to his existing wives); **(iii) Gen 36:2-3** (the Esav-Edom-genealogy wife-list: *Adah bat-Elon ha-Chitti v-Oholivamah bat-Anah bat-Tziv'on ha-Chivvi v-Basemat bat-Yishma'el achot Nevayot*). The three lists overlap partially: **Basemat bat-Elon** of Gen 26:34 and **Adah bat-Elon** of Gen 36:2 share the patronymic *bat-Elon ha-Chitti* but with different given-names; **Basemat bat-Yishma'el achot Nevayot** of Gen 36:3 corresponds to **Mahalat bat-Yishma'el achot Nevayot** of Gen 28:9 (same patronymic, different given-names); **Yehudit bat-Be'eri** of Gen 26:34 corresponds (less clearly) to **Oholivamah bat-Anah bat-Tziv'on ha-Chivvi** of Gen 36:2 (different patronymic and different ethnonym — Chivvi vs Chitti). The three-list inconsistency has occupied scholars from the patristic period onward. Rabbinic tradition (Genesis Rabbah 65:2; Rashi at Gen 36:2) harmonizes by identifying the differently-named wives as the same persons under different names (Basemat / Adah; Mahalat / Basemat). Modern critical scholarship (Wellhausen *Prolegomena* 1885; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 425-426; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 427, 558-560; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 187) reads the three lists as drawn from distinct sources or traditions: Gen 26:34 may be J-or-late-P (the *morat ruach* setup for Gen 27's J-or-E narrative); Gen 28:9 is a later editorial bridge; Gen 36:2-3 is from the P-or-late-P genealogical-source. The inconsistency is **editorially-unsmoothed**: the redactor preserved the three traditions without harmonization. **Friedman** *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 attributes Gen 26:34-35 to J (or to a J-supplement) and Gen 36 to P.

**The Chitti / Hittite identification.** The wives are designated *Chitti* (Hittite). The cross-corpus identification of the *Chittim* is the subject of a long-standing scholarly debate (covered by the `bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question` entry, anchored at Gen 23): the *Chittim* of Genesis are either (a) the Anatolian Hittites (the Late Bronze Age Hattusa-empire, attested in the Hittite cuneiform corpus c. 1650-1180 BCE), or (b) the *Hittites* of Canaan (a southern-Canaanite ethnonym, possibly unrelated or only loosely related to the Anatolian Hittites; cf. Num 13:29 — *ha-Chitti v-ha-Yevusi v-ha-Emori yoshev ba-har*), or (c) the *Neo-Hittites* of the post-Hattusa-collapse Syro-Hittite kingdoms (c. 1180-700 BCE; Carchemish, Hamath, Aleppo). The Genesis distribution favors **(b)** — the patriarchal-period *Chittim* are integrated with the Canaanite ethnonyms — though the question is contested. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 425-427 and **Hoffner** *Hittites and Hurrians* in *Peoples of Old Testament Times* (ed. Wiseman 1973) review the question. The v 26:34 attestation of the *Chittim* in the Negev / Be'er Sheva region is consistent with reading (b). The `bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question` entry's *appliesTo* is anchored at Gen 23 (the Hebron-Hittite trading-context); whether it should be extended to Gen 26:34-35 is editorially debatable. The conservative reading: the Gen 23 Hebron-Hittite trading-context (commercial-legal-institutions) and the Gen 26:34 Esav-Hittite-wife-context (ethnic-marriage-prohibition) are distinct attestations of the *Chittim*-question; both can be governed by the same entry, but the contexts are distinct. This entry **does not** apply the `bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question` entry at Gen 26:34 — the Editor's call is to flag the question for the Reviewer's consideration.

**The Hittite-wife ethnic-marriage-prohibition theme.** The Esav-Hittite-wife-marriage is part of a wider Genesis-and-beyond theme of **endogamy-vs-exogamy in patriarchal marriage**. The patriarchs' explicit instructions on inter-marriage: **Gen 24:3** (Avraham to Eliezer: *lo tiqach ishah liv'ni mi-b'not ha-Kena'ani asher anokhi yoshev b-qirbo* — *do not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Kena'ani in whose midst I dwell*); **Gen 27:46-28:1** (Rivkah and Yitzchaq to Ya'aqov: *lo tiqach ishah mi-b'not Kena'an*); **Gen 28:6** (Esav noting the instruction and his own contrary choice). The Esav-Hittite-wife-choice violates the patriarchal endogamy-instruction implicitly (Yitzchaq's instructions to Esav are not narrated, but Esav's parallel-non-compliance is structurally clear) and supplies the *morat ruach* of v 35. The theme is editorially-foundational for the wider Israelite endogamy-tradition culminating in the Deut 7:1-4 ethnic-marriage-prohibition and the Ezra 9-10 / Neh 13 post-exilic foreign-wife-controversy.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 186-187; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 427-428, 558-560; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 193, 322-324; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 200-201, 380-383; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 135; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 202; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 367, 425-426; Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003; Hoffner *Hittites and Hurrians* in *Peoples of Old Testament Times* (ed. Wiseman 1973); Hoffmeier *Israel in Egypt* 1996 and *Ancient Israel in Sinai* 2005; HALOT s.v. *morah*, *m-r-r*, *Chitti*; BDB s.v. *morah* p. 600 and *m-r-r* p. 600. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 65:2, 65:4 (on the Esav-wife-bad-behavior expansions); Rashi at Gen 26:34-35 and 36:2. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *morat ruach* construct is grammatically explicit; (b) the Gen 27:46 dialogue-quotation back-link is HB-internal-explicit; (c) the three-wife-list inconsistency is documentary and is documented in the standard source-critical commentaries; (d) the *Chittim*-question is documentary and is governed by the separate `bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question` entry; (e) the endogamy-vs-exogamy patriarchal-marriage theme is HB-internal-explicit. Project-specific synthesis is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.strongs: H3543 (kahah — be dim, faint, fail) + H5869 (ayin — eye) + H7200 (ra'ah — see)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3fp, vav-consec) + noun (dual-with-3ms-suffix) + preposition (privative-min) + infinitive construct
terms.literal: and his eyes were dim from seeing
terms.standardEnglish: his eyes were dim, so that he could not see (ASV/KJV result-clause smoothing); his eyes had grown too dim to see (NRSV); his eyes were too dim to see (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: his eyes were dim from seeing (ASV-baseline-adjacent; the privative-*min* preserved literally; the cross-corpus patriarchal-blindness-narrative-trigger formula surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:1 opens the blessing-deception narrative with the editorially-load-bearing clause *va-tikheina eynav me-r'ot* — *and his eyes were dim from seeing*. The clause is the narrative-mechanism by which the deception is made possible: Yitzchaq's blindness sets up Ya'aqov's tactile-deception via the goat-skins (vv 16, 22-23). The blindness-notice is not an incidental detail of patriarchal-aging; it is the chapter's structural-foundation for the deception-plot.**

**The *kahah* lexeme.** The verb *kahah* (כָּהָה) covers a semantic range from *be dim, dull, faint* through *grow weak* to *fail entirely*. The standard lexicons (BDB s.v. *kahah* p. 462; HALOT s.v. *khh*) document the verb across cultic (Lev 13:6, 21, 26, 28, 39, 56 — leprosy-spot fading), perceptual (here at Gen 27:1; Deut 34:7 of Mosheh; 1 Sam 3:2 of Eli; Zech 11:17), and dispositional (Isa 42:4 — the servant *lo yikheh v-lo yarutz* — *shall not faint nor be crushed*) registers. The Gen 27:1 deployment is the perceptual-failing-eyesight register.

**The privative-*min* construction.** The construction *me-r'ot* (*from seeing*) is the **privative-*min* of result/inability**: the eyes' dimness has the effect of preventing seeing. The construction is editorially-precise: the eyes are not simply *dim* (an absolute description) but *dim from seeing* (a functional description naming the resulting incapacity). The ASV's *his eyes were dim, so that he could not see* renders the privative-*min* as a consequence-clause; modern translations (NJPS, NRSV) smooth to *his eyes were too dim to see*. The WoH translation preserves the literal privative-*min* (*his eyes were dim from seeing*), which retains the construction's functional precision without overinterpreting toward either pure-physical (modern blindness) or pure-figurative (loss-of-perceptive-discernment) readings.

**The patriarchal-blindness-narrative-trigger formula across the HB.** The blindness-clause at Gen 27:1 is the first instance of a recurring HB pattern: blindness functions as a narrative-trigger for a *transferred-perception* or *transferred-authority* episode. The cross-corpus instances:

- **Gen 27:1** (here) — Yitzchaq's blindness triggers the Ya'aqov-blessing-deception; the blessing-transmission inverts the elder-to-younger order.
- **Gen 48:10** — *v-einei Yisrael kavdu mi-zoqen lo yukhal lir'ot* — *and the eyes of Yisrael were heavy from age, he could not see*. Ya'aqov's dim eyes at the Ephraim-Manasseh blessing-reversal echo Yitzchaq's at Gen 27:1; the verb *kavad* (be heavy) substitutes for *kahah* (be dim) but the construction (*mi-zoqen* — *from age*) parallels Gen 27:1's *me-r'ot*. The Ephraim-Manasseh blessing-reversal (Ya'aqov crosses his hands to bless the younger Ephraim over the elder Manasseh) structurally mirrors the Gen 27 Ya'aqov-Esav reversal — but with the crucial editorial difference that Ya'aqov's act is **deliberate** (he crosses his hands knowingly, vv 14-19) where Yitzchaq's was **deceived**. The two scenes are deliberately paired.
- **Deut 34:7** — *u-Moshe ben me'ah v-esrim shanah b-moto lo kahatah eyno v-lo nas lecho* — *and Mosheh was 120 years old at his death; his eye had not dimmed nor his vigor fled*. The verb *kahah* is the same as at Gen 27:1; the negation is editorially-marked — Mosheh, unlike Yitzchaq, does **not** suffer eye-dimming, which is the Deuteronomic-editor's tribute to Mosheh's continued-perceptive-capacity at the moment of his death-and-succession to Yehoshua.
- **1 Sam 3:2** — *v-Eli shokhev bi-mqomo v-einav hechelu kehot lo yukhal lir'ot* — *and Eli was lying in his place, and his eyes had begun to dim, he could not see*. Eli's blindness at the call-of-Shmuel scene parallels Yitzchaq's: the dim-eyed elder figure is the medium through which an authority-transition occurs (Eli to Shmuel, paralleling Yitzchaq's role as the medium through which the blessing-line transfers).
- **1 Sam 4:15** — *v-Eli ben tish'im u-shmoneh shanah v-einav qamah v-lo yakhol lir'ot* — Eli at 98, eyes *qamah* (set, fixed — i.e., motionless / cataract-like). The later instance uses a different verb but maintains the eyes-failing-at-authority-transition pattern.
- **1 Kgs 14:4** — *v-Achiyah lo yakhol lir'ot ki qamu einav mi-sevo* — *and Achiyah could not see, for his eyes were set from age*. The prophet Achiyah's blindness at the Yarov'am-wife scene; the blind prophet **sees through the deception** (Yarov'am's wife is disguised but Achiyah identifies her by YHWH-given-perception). The reverse-Gen 27 pattern: the blind prophet penetrates the disguise where the blind patriarch fails to.
- **Zech 11:17** — the *ro'eh ha-elil* (worthless-shepherd) oracle: *charev al-z'ro'o v-al-eyn y'mino z'ro'o yavosh tivash v-eyn y'mino kahoh tikheh* — *a sword upon his arm and upon his right eye; his arm shall be utterly withered, and his right eye shall be utterly dimmed*. The cognate-accusative *kahoh tikheh* parallels Gen 27:33's *va-yecherad charadah* construction (see companion entry); the *kahah* is here used as the punishment-of-a-failed-shepherd.

The cross-corpus pattern: **blindness functions narratively as the trigger for transferred-perception or transferred-authority**. The pattern is editorially-load-bearing across the patriarchal-and-deuteronomistic-historical narratives.

**The modern critical reading of Gen 27:1.** The standard commentaries (Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 188; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 436-437; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 204; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 211-212; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 138; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 207-208) uniformly read the blindness-clause as the narrative-mechanism enabling the deception. Sarna notes the editorial-parallel to Gen 48:10. Westermann develops the patriarchal-blindness-motif as a structural feature of the J-or-E source's narrative-method. Alter reads the blindness as part of the chapter's wider perception-and-deception-thematics: the chapter is preoccupied with the senses (sight, touch, smell, taste, hearing — all five senses are deployed in the deception-scene), and the blindness-clause sets up the chapter's sense-perception-failure framework.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline-adjacent rendering *his eyes were dim from seeing* (literal privative-*min*). The cross-corpus patriarchal-blindness-narrative-trigger formula and the Gen 48:10 / Deut 34:7 / 1 Sam 3:2 / 1 Kgs 14:4 cross-references are documented in commentary. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the blindness-narrative-trigger pattern is mainstream modern-critical-consensus and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 188; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 436-437; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 204; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 211-212; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 138 and *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 3 (on the senses-and-perception-deception thematics); Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 207-208; Fokkelman *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975; Sternberg *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative* 1985 (on the narrator's information-asymmetry); HALOT s.v. *khh*; BDB s.v. *kahah* p. 462. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *kahah* is grammatically explicit; (b) the privative-*min* construction is grammatically explicit; (c) the cross-corpus patriarchal-blindness-narrative-trigger pattern is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the editorial-parallel to Gen 48:10 is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the senses-and-perception-deception thematic-framework is standard modern-critical-reading.

terms.strongs: H4303 (matamim, matamot — savory-food, delicacies; from H2938 ta'am — taste)
terms.literal: savory-foods, delicacies, taste-pleasing-dishes
terms.standardEnglish: savoury meat (KJV); savory food (ASV/NRSV); a dish (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: savory food (ASV-baseline; the fixed-phrase recurrence at vv 4, 7, 9, 14, 17, 31 preserved; the blessing-meal ritual-context surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**The noun *matamim* (מַטְעַמִּים) is Gen 27's fixed-phrase for the blessing-meal — the savory dishes whose preparation, presentation, and consumption frame the patriarchal-blessing-transfer. The term recurs six times across the chapter (vv 4, 7, 9, 14, 17, 31), always in blessing-transfer context. The lexeme's chapter-bound concentration is editorially-deliberate: *matamim* is the chapter's signature-noun.**

**The lexeme and its etymology.** *Matamim* (also attested as *matamot*) derives from the root *t-ʿ-m* (טעם — *taste, perceive by tasting, savor*; cf. the noun *taam* — *taste, judgment, decree*, which extends from the sensory-taste register to the abstract-judgment register, as at 1 Sam 25:33 — David to Avigayil — *barukh tamekh* — *blessed be your discernment*). The noun-form *matamim* names dishes-of-taste — savory, palatable, prepared-for-pleasure. BDB s.v. *matam* p. 381; HALOT s.v. *matam, matamot*; both gloss as *tasty / delicious dishes*.

**Cross-corpus distribution.** The lexeme is rare in the HB, occurring outside Gen 27 only at:

- **Prov 23:3** — *al-tit'av l-matamotav v-hu lechem k'zavim* — *do not desire his delicacies, for they are deceitful food*. The warning against the *matamim* of the stingy host (cf. v 6 — *al-tilcham et-lechem ra eyin* — *do not eat the bread of one with an evil eye*); the wisdom-tradition's caution against accepting hospitality from the stingy.
- **Prov 23:6** — *al-tilcham et-lechem ra eyin v-al-tit'av l-matamotav* — *do not eat the bread of one with an evil eye, neither desire his delicacies*. The second deployment in the same Proverbs-cluster.

The Proverbs occurrences inflect the *matamim* lexeme with the **ambivalence-of-the-prepared-meal** register: the savory dishes can be a vehicle of genuine hospitality or of deception. The Gen 27 deployment shares the ambivalence: the *matamim* is presented as the medium of paternal-blessing but is in fact the medium of fraternal-deception.

**The 2 Sam 13:5-10 *biryot* parallel.** A closely-related ANE prepared-meal lexeme appears at **2 Samuel 13:5-10** in the Amnon-Tamar narrative: Amnon feigns illness and asks David to send Tamar to prepare *biryot* (בִּרְיוֹת — also from the prepared-food register; cf. *barah* — *eat food, especially food for the sick*) before his eyes. The parallel is editorially-marked: in both Gen 27 and 2 Sam 13, a prepared meal frames a deception (Ya'aqov deceives Yitzchaq for the blessing; Amnon deceives David and Tamar for the rape). The structural parallel is noted in modern-critical commentary (Alter *Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 2 on the pattern of *Type-Scenes* including the prepared-meal-as-deception-vehicle; Polzin *David and the Deuteronomist* 1993 on the 2 Sam 13 deployment).

**The blessing-meal as ANE ritual-context.** The prepared-meal as the **frame for patriarchal-blessing-transfer** is an ANE legal-and-ritual institution attested across the second-and-first millennium BCE Near East. The meal serves three functions: **(i)** it is the **occasion** of the blessing (the patriarch-son-or-elder dines, the relationship is performed); **(ii)** it is the **medium** of the blessing (the *nefesh* — the patriarchal vital-self — is engaged via the meal, which strengthens the patriarch's vitality before the blessing-utterance; cf. v 25's *va-yiqach lo yayin va-yesht* — *and he took wine and drank* — and the immediately-following blessing); **(iii)** it is the **witness** of the blessing (the consumed meal forms the legal-evidence of the relationship having been honored, structurally parallel to the covenant-meal at Gen 31:54 of Ya'aqov-Lavan, and to the *zevach todah* — thanksgiving-sacrifice-meal — of the wider Israelite cultic tradition). **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 188 develops the meal-as-blessing-context argument; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 437-438 likewise; **Mitchell** *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* 1987 (the standard-monograph on the HB blessing-lexicon) treats the meal-context across the patriarchal-blessing-corpus.

**The six-occurrence chapter-bound distribution.** The lexeme's deployment across Gen 27 traces the chapter's ritual-mechanics:

- **v 4** — Yitzchaq's request to Esav: *asei li matamim ka-asher ahavti* — *make me savory food such as I love*.
- **v 7** — Rivkah's recapitulation of the request to Ya'aqov: *havi'ah li tzayid v-asei li matamim* — *bring me venison and make me savory food*.
- **v 9** — Rivkah's substitute-instructions to Ya'aqov: *v-e'eseh otam matamim l-avikha* — *and I will make them savory food for your father*.
- **v 14** — the preparation: *va-ta'as imo matamim ka-asher ahev aviv* — *and his mother made savory food, such as his father loved*.
- **v 17** — the delivery: *va-titen et-ha-matamim v-et-ha-lechem* — *and she gave the savory food and the bread*.
- **v 31** — Esav's parallel-and-too-late offering: *va-ya'as gam-hu matamim* — *and he also made savory food*.

The six-deployment distribution is editorially-deliberate: the lexeme structures the chapter as *matamim-request → matamim-substitution → matamim-deception → matamim-too-late*. The narrator uses the fixed-phrase to mark each stage of the ritual-mechanics. The chapter's signature-noun is the chapter's narrative-spine.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline *savory food* across all six occurrences. The fixed-phrase recurrence and the blessing-meal ritual-context are documented in commentary. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the *matamim* fixed-phrase is mainstream modern-critical-attested and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 188-189; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 437-440; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 205-208; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 213-218; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 138-140 and *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 2 (the prepared-meal-as-type-scene); Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 207-211; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); Polzin *David and the Deuteronomist* 1993 (on the 2 Sam 13 parallel); HALOT s.v. *matam, matamot*; BDB s.v. *matam* p. 381. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *matamim* is grammatically explicit; (b) the six-occurrence chapter-distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Prov 23:3, 23:6 cross-references are HB-internal-explicit; (d) the 2 Sam 13:5-10 *biryot*-parallel is documented in mainstream modern-critical commentary; (e) the ANE blessing-meal ritual-context is documented in Mitchell 1987 and the standard commentaries.

terms.strongs: H5668 (ba-avur — for the sake of, that) + H1288 (barakh — bless, piel) + H5315 (nefesh — vital-self, soul, breath, life)
terms.partOfSpeech: preposition-with-particle + verb (piel impf 3fs with 2ms suffix) + noun (sf with 1cs suffix)
terms.literal: in order that my nefesh may bless you
terms.standardEnglish: that my soul may bless thee (ASV/KJV); so that I may give you my innermost blessing (NJPS); so that I may bless you (modern smoothing dropping *nefesh*)
terms.wohChoice: that my nefesh may bless you (transliteration of *nefesh* per project convention; the blessing-as-vital-self-transmission preserved; the patriarchal-blessing-as-ANE-speech-act surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27 contains the HB's most concentrated deployment of the *nefesh*-as-subject-of-blessing construction. The formula *ba-avur t-varekhekha nafshi* (*that my nefesh may bless you*) and its variants recur at vv 4, 19, 25, 31 — four times across the chapter, marking the four key moments of the blessing-meal-and-transfer sequence. The chapter's deployment of *nefesh* as the **agent** of blessing (rather than as the **object** that receives blessing, the more common HB pattern) is editorially-distinctive and is the chapter's most concentrated witness to the ANE-Israelite blessing-as-vital-self-transmission framework.**

**The *nefesh* lexeme and its semantic range.** *Nefesh* (נֶפֶשׁ) in BH covers a broad semantic range from *breath / throat* (the original-physiological register, as at Isa 5:14 — the *nefesh* of Sheol opens wide; Jonah 2:6 — water reaches the *nefesh*) through *life / living being* (Gen 2:7 — *nefesh chayah*; Lev 17:11 — *ki ha-dam hu ha-nefesh*) to *self / person / personhood* (the Pentateuchal legal corpus's *nefesh* in census-and-criminal-law contexts) and *vital-self / desiring-or-feeling-self* (the psalmist's *nafshi* in Pss 42, 103, 116, etc.). The lexeme is **emphatically NOT** equivalent to the post-Hellenistic Greek *psychē* / Latin *anima* / English *soul* in the dualistic-immortal-spirit sense; the *nefesh* is the embodied-vital-personhood, not a separable-immaterial-substance. **H. W. Wolff** *Anthropology of the Old Testament* 1974 (ET 1974 from *Anthropologie des Alten Testaments* 1973) chapter 2 (*nefesh — Needy Man*) is the standard modern-critical monograph on the lexeme's semantics; **Wolff** distinguishes the BH *nefesh* from later dualistic readings and reads the lexeme as naming the embodied-vital-self with particular emphasis on the desiring / needy / feeling dimension.

**The blessing-as-speech-act framework.** The HB patriarchal-blessing is not a wish or a prayer; it is a **performative speech-act** with binding-effect once-uttered. The framework is widely attested across the Genesis-blessing-corpus: Avraham's blessing of Yitzchaq is implied; Yitzchaq's blessing of Ya'aqov (here) and Esav (vv 39-40); Ya'aqov's blessing of Ephraim and Manasseh (Gen 48); Ya'aqov's blessing of the twelve sons (Gen 49); the parallel-blessings of the Pentateuch (Mosheh's blessing of the tribes at Deut 33; Bil'am's oracles at Num 23-24). **C. W. Mitchell** *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987) is the standard monograph; **Mitchell** distinguishes (i) **declarative blessing** (the speech-act that effects what it says), (ii) **invocational blessing** (the speech-act that calls on a deity to act), and (iii) **acclamational blessing** (the speech-act that recognizes blessing already conferred). The Gen 27 patriarchal-blessing is **declarative** in Mitchell's typology: Yitzchaq's words effect the blessing in their utterance, which is why the blessing cannot be revoked after the deception is discovered (v 33 — *gam-barukh yihye* — *indeed, he shall be blessed* — see companion entry on the blessing-irrevocability formula). The declarative-blessing-speech-act framework is foundational to the chapter's structural-mechanics.

**The *nefesh*-as-agent-of-blessing construction.** The Gen 27 fourfold-deployment of *t-varekhekha nafshi* / *t-varakhanni nafshekha* is editorially-load-bearing for the **blessing-as-vital-self-transmission** dimension of the framework. The construction's reading: the patriarch's *nefesh* — his embodied-vital-personhood, with particular emphasis on the desiring / feeling / volitional dimension — is the subject-and-source of the blessing-utterance. The blessing is not merely *spoken* by the patriarch but is *poured out* from his vital-self into the son's vital-self. The blessing-meal frame (see companion entry on *matamim*) is the ritual-mechanism by which the *nefesh* is **prepared**: the patriarch eats and drinks (v 25), satisfying his vital-self, which then has the **fullness-of-life-force** required to perform the declarative-blessing-speech-act. The framework is ANE-religiously-typical: the patriarch's vital-life-force is the resource from which the blessing draws.

**The four chapter-deployments.**

- **v 4** — Yitzchaq to Esav: *va-asei li matamim ka-asher ahavti v-havi'ah li v-okhelah ba-avur t-varekhekha nafshi b-terem amut* — *and make me savory food such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat, that my nefesh may bless you before I die*. The opening-deployment: the patriarch frames the meal as the precondition for the blessing-transfer.
- **v 19** — Ya'aqov to Yitzchaq: *anokhi Esav b'khorekha asiti ka-asher dibarta elai kum-na shevah v-okhlah mi-tzeidi ba-avur t-varakhanni nafshekha* — *I am Esav your firstborn; I have done as you spoke to me; arise, I pray, sit and eat of my venison, that your nefesh may bless me*. The deception-deployment: Ya'aqov repeats the patriarch's own formula back to him.
- **v 25** — Yitzchaq's instruction within the deception: *va-yomer ha-gishah li v-okhlah mi-tzeyd b'ni l-ma'an t-varekhekha nafshi* — *and he said, Bring it near to me, that I may eat of my son's venison, that my nefesh may bless you*. The blessing-meal-itself: Yitzchaq's *nefesh* is being fed before the blessing.
- **v 31** — Esav (too late) to Yitzchaq: *yaqum avi v-yokhal mi-tzeyd b'no ba-avur t-varakhanni nafshekha* — *let my father arise, and eat of his son's venison, that your nefesh may bless me*. The closing-deployment: Esav repeats the formula but the blessing has already been transmitted.

The four-deployment distribution traces the chapter's structural-mechanics: the *nefesh*-blessing-formula is requested (v 4), spoken-deceptively (v 19), enacted (v 25), and finally requested-too-late (v 31). The chapter's signature-formula is the chapter's narrative-spine.

**The cross-corpus *nefesh*-blessing-language.** The *nefesh*-as-agent-of-blessing is concentrated at Gen 27 but extends in attenuated form elsewhere:

- **Pss 103:1, 103:2, 103:22, 104:1, 104:35, 146:1** — *barekhi nafshi et-YHWH* — *bless YHWH, O my nefesh*. The reverse-direction deployment: the *nefesh* is the agent that blesses, but the object is YHWH rather than another human. The construction inverts the Gen 27 form but preserves the *nefesh*-as-subject-of-blessing register.
- **Job 31:30** — Job's negative-confession: *v-lo natati l-chato chiki lish'ol b-alah nafsho* — *I have not let my mouth sin by asking with a curse for his nefesh*. The *nefesh* as the object of curse/blessing-speech-acts.
- **Prov 11:25** — *nefesh berakhah t-dushan* — *the blessing-nefesh shall be made fat*. The wisdom-tradition's reverse-construction: the blessing-bestowing *nefesh* itself is enriched.

The cross-corpus deployment confirms the *nefesh* as the standard HB-and-wisdom-tradition agent-of-blessing-speech-acts, with the Gen 27 chapter being the patriarchal-narrative concentration-locus.

**The transliteration choice.** The WoH translation preserves *nefesh* in transliteration (rather than translating to English *soul*) for two reasons: **(i)** to maintain consistency with the project's wider *nefesh*-cluster glossary policy (see related entries on *nefesh-chayah*, *tselem-elohim-image-plurality* in the central glossary which preserves *nefesh* in apparatus); **(ii)** to preserve the lexeme's embodied-vital-personhood semantic range against the post-Hellenistic *soul*-dualism that the English-tradition *soul* introduces. The choice is **lens-quiet**: it does not preempt any WoH-specific reading; it tracks Wolff 1974 and the modern-critical lexicographic-consensus on the *nefesh*'s non-dualistic character.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Wolff *Anthropology of the Old Testament* 1974 ch. 2 (*nefesh — Needy Man*); Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 188; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 437-438; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 204-208; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 213-216; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 138; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 207-209; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); Westermann *Blessing in the Bible and the Life of the Church* 1978 (ET 1978); Pedersen *Israel: Its Life and Culture* I-II 1926 (the classic Scandinavian-school treatment of the *nefesh* and blessing-life-force); HALOT s.v. *nefesh*; BDB s.v. *nefesh* p. 659. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construction *t-varekhekha nafshi* is grammatically explicit; (b) the four-occurrence chapter-distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *nefesh* lexeme's embodied-vital-self semantics is documented in Wolff 1974 and the standard lexicons (HALOT, BDB); (d) the declarative-blessing-speech-act framework is documented in Mitchell 1987; (e) the cross-corpus *nefesh*-blessing-language is HB-internal-explicit; (f) the transliteration-policy follows the project's central-glossary *nefesh*-cluster convention.

terms.source: הַקֹּל֙ ק֣וֹל יַעֲקֹ֔ב וְהַיָּדַ֖יִם יְדֵ֥י עֵשָֽׂו
terms.translit: ha-qol qol Ya'aqov v-ha-yadayim y'dei Esav
terms.strongs: H6963 (qol — voice, sound) + H3290 (Ya'aqov) + H3027 (yad — hand) + H6215 (Esav)
terms.partOfSpeech: definite-article-noun + construct-noun + proper noun, doubled / definite-article-noun-dual + construct-noun + proper noun
terms.literal: the voice (is) the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands (are) the hands of Esav
terms.standardEnglish: The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau (ASV); The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau (KJV); The voice is the voice of Jacob, yet the hands are the hands of Esau (NRSV); The voice is the voice of Jacob, yet the hands are the hands of Esau (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: The voice is the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands are the hands of Esav (ASV-classic chiastic-doubling preserved; the chiastic-doublet structure surfaced in commentary as the chapter's central irony-formula)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:22 contains the chapter's narrative-rhetorical fulcrum: *ha-qol qol Ya'aqov v-ha-yadayim y'dei Esav* — *the voice is the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands are the hands of Esav*. The verse is the chapter's central irony-formula, the moment at which the deception's mechanism becomes verbally explicit even as Yitzchaq fails to draw the inference. The verse is one of the HB's most-celebrated chiastic-doublet constructions and one of the most editorially-marked sentences in the patriarchal-cycle.**

**The chiastic-doublet structure.** The verse displays a tightly-marked two-clause parallel structure:

A: *ha-qol* (the-voice) — definite-article-noun (singular)
B: *qol Ya'aqov* (voice-of Ya'aqov) — construct-state-noun + proper-noun

A': *v-ha-yadayim* (and-the-hands) — definite-article-noun (dual)
B': *y'dei Esav* (hands-of Esav) — construct-state-noun + proper-noun

The pattern is **A-B / A'-B'**: each clause opens with a definite-article-noun (subject) and closes with a construct-noun + proper-noun (predicate). The repetition of the article-construct pattern across the two clauses is **editorially-distinctive**: the narrator could have collapsed either clause to a possessive (*kol Ya'aqov v-y'dei Esav* — *Jacob's voice and Esau's hands*) but chose to preserve the article-construct doubling. The choice is the chapter's most-marked rhetorical decision. **Alter** *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 6 reads the verse as a paradigm-instance of the HB's syntactic-doubling-for-emphasis device; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 189-190 notes the chiastic structure as editorially-deliberate; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 438-440 develops the chiastic reading.

**The voice-hands chiasm and Isaac's failed-inference.** The chapter is preoccupied with **sense-perception failure**: Yitzchaq is blind (v 1; see companion entry); he attempts to verify through touch (v 21 — *gshah-na v-amushkha* — *come near that I may feel you*); he detects the voice as Ya'aqov's (v 22a) but the hands as Esav's (v 22b); he attempts to verify through smell (v 27 — *va-yarach et-rei'ach b'gadav* — *and he smelled the smell of his garments*); and ultimately through taste (v 25 — the *matamim*). The five senses are systematically deployed and systematically deceived. The voice-hands chiasm at v 22 is the **moment of clearest sense-evidence**: Yitzchaq verbally registers the contradiction (the voice is Ya'aqov's, the hands are Esav's) but **fails to draw the inference** that follows: the voice is the more-reliable sense-witness, hence the figure-before-him is Ya'aqov, not Esav. The narrator's irony is editorially-load-bearing: Yitzchaq names the deception's evidence without recognizing its meaning. The verse's structural function is to **stage the failure of inference** — to make the reader the witness to a perceptual-deception that the participant fails to penetrate.

**The rabbinic-reception of the verse (flagged, not preempted).** The verse's rabbinic-reception is dense and historically-influential. Two principal lines of midrashic reading:

- **b. Bava Batra 16b** — Esav's *qol* (voice / sound) is read as the *qol* of war-and-violence; Ya'aqov's *qol* is read as the *qol* of Torah-study and prayer. The rabbinic reading inverts the chapter's surface: the *qol Ya'aqov* names not just Ya'aqov's individual voice but the *Torah-and-prayer-voice* of the people-of-Israel, while the *yadei Esav* names Esau-as-Rome and the predatory-power of the gentile-empires. The reading is the foundation of the rabbinic Esav-as-Rome typology (developed across b. Avodah Zarah 2b, b. Sanhedrin 12a, Gen Rab 65:21, Lev Rab 13:5, and the Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 38).
- **Genesis Rabbah 65:20** — the *qol* of Ya'aqov is the *qol* heard at Sinai (*qol shofar* and *qol Elohim*); the *yadei* of Esav is the *yadei* of the persecuting-nations across history. The midrash reads the verse as a prophecy: Israel will be sustained by its *qol* (its Torah-study, its prayer) and threatened by Esau's *yadayim* (the empires' predatory-power). The reading is the source-text for the rabbinic *qol-Ya'aqov* / *yadei-Esav* typological-pair that recurs across medieval Jewish exegesis (Rashi at Gen 27:22; Ramban; the Maharal of Prague *Netzach Yisrael*).

The rabbinic-reception is **load-bearing** for subsequent reception-history but is **downstream** of the chapter's narrative-context. The WoH translation does **not** preempt the rabbinic reception: the chapter is rendered in its narrative-context, and the rabbinic-typological reading is flagged here as one of the verse's most-historically-significant downstream-receptions without being smuggled into the translation itself. The reception-history is the wiki's province, not the translation's.

**The Pauline-Christian reception (briefly).** Romans 9:13 cites Mal 1:2-3 (*Ya'aqov ahavti v-et-Esav saneti* — *Jacob I have loved, but Esau I have hated*) in a reading that runs back through the Gen 27 deception-narrative: Paul reads the Ya'aqov-over-Esav election as a paradigm of divine-sovereign-election independent of human-meritorious-action (since both brothers are unborn at Gen 25:23's prenatal-oracle). The Pauline reading is editorially-distant from the chapter's narrative-context — Paul is reading the chapter as a theological-allegory rather than as a narrative — but is the foundational-Christian reception of the Esav-Ya'aqov pair. The WoH translation does **not** preempt the Pauline reception; it is flagged here for completeness without being smuggled into the translation.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-classic chiastic-doubling *the voice is the voice of Ya'aqov, but the hands are the hands of Esav*. The chiastic-doublet structure and the sense-perception-failure framework are documented in commentary. The rabbinic and Pauline receptions are flagged-not-preempted. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the chapter's narrative-mechanics are mainstream modern-critical-attested and editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 189-190; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 437-440; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 207-208; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 218-220; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 140 and *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 6 (the chiastic-doubling-as-rhetorical-emphasis pattern); Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 209-211; Fokkelman *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 pp. 94-114 (the verse's chiastic-structure and sense-perception-thematics); Sternberg *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative* 1985 ch. 9 (the chapter's information-asymmetry); Polzin *The Bible's Drama* 1980; HALOT s.v. *qol*, *yad*; BDB s.v. *qol* p. 876 and *yad* p. 388. Rabbinic-tradition sources: b. Bava Batra 16b; Genesis Rabbah 65:20-21; Rashi at Gen 27:22; the Esav-as-Rome typology in Lev Rab 13:5, Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 38, and Maharal *Netzach Yisrael*. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the chiastic-doublet structure is grammatically explicit; (b) the article-construct repetition is morphologically marked; (c) the sense-perception-failure framework is documented in the standard commentaries (Alter, Sternberg, Fokkelman); (d) the rabbinic reception is HB-internal-cited and is flagged-not-preempted; (e) the Pauline reception (Rom 9:13 → Mal 1:2-3 → Gen 27) is HB-and-NT-internal-explicit and is flagged-not-preempted.
