terms.literal: and may the Elohim give you, of the dew of the shamayim and of the fatness of the eretz, and abundance of grain and new wine
terms.standardEnglish: And God give thee of the dew of heaven, and of the fatness of the earth, and plenty of grain and new wine (ASV); Therefore God give thee of the dew of heaven, and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of corn and wine (KJV); May God give you of the dew of heaven, and of the fatness of the earth, and plenty of grain and wine (NRSV); May God give you Of the dew of heaven and the fat of the earth, Abundance of new grain and wine (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: And may the Elohim give you of the dew of the skies, and of the fatness of the land, and abundance of grain and new wine (ASV-baseline preserved; *shamayim* and *eretz* per the central-glossary entries; the partitive-*min* and the patriarchal-fertility-blessing-formula surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:28 contains the chapter's central blessing-formula proper — the patriarchal-fertility-blessing that Yitzchaq utters over the figure-he-takes-to-be-Esav. The formula's elements (dew-of-the-skies, fatness-of-the-land, grain, new wine) constitute one of the HB's canonical agricultural-blessing word-pairs, recurring across the Deuteronomic-and-prophetic-corpus.**

**The formula's elements.**

**(i) *Tal ha-shamayim* — *the dew of the skies*.** The dew (*tal*) in BH agricultural context refers specifically to the heavy night-and-early-morning condensation of the eastern-Mediterranean coastal-and-hill-country climate, which supplements the seasonal rainfall and is foundational to the summer-fruiting cycle (esp. of grapes, olives, and figs) in a region where the rainfall is concentrated in the October-April rainy-season. The lexeme *tal* is consistently positive in the HB agricultural-blessing-vocabulary (cf. **Deut 33:13** — the Joseph-blessing's *u-mi-tal mi-mishmanei tachat* — *and from the dew, of the fatness from below*; **Deut 33:28** — *eretz dagan v-tirosh af-shamav ya'arfu tal* — *a land of grain and new wine; yes, his heavens drop down dew*; **Hos 14:6** — *ehyeh ka-tal l-Yisrael* — *I will be as the dew to Yisrael*; **Mic 5:6** — *v-hayah she'erit Ya'aqov ... k-tal me-et-YHWH* — *and the remnant of Ya'aqov shall be ... like dew from YHWH*; **Zech 8:12** — *ha-shamayim yitenu talam* — *the skies shall give their dew*). The negative-deployment (dew-withheld as covenant-curse) is rarer (**1 Kgs 17:1** — Eliyahu's drought-oracle: *im-yihyeh ha-shanim ha-eleh tal u-matar ki im l-fi devari* — *if there shall be dew or rain these years, except according to my word*; **Hag 1:10** — *al-ken aleikhem kal'u shamayim mi-tal* — *therefore the skies above you have withheld their dew*).

**(ii) *Shemanei ha-aretz* — *the fatness of the land*.** The construct-plural *shemanei* (from *mashman* / *shemen* — fat, oil, richness) names the agricultural-fertility-of-the-land: the rich-soil-conditions producing abundant olive-oil, grape-must, and grain. The lexeme is consistently agricultural-fertility-positive in the HB (cf. **Isa 25:6** — the eschatological-feast *mishteh shemanim mishteh shmarim shemanim m'mucha'yim shmarim m'zuqaqim* — *a feast of fat things, a feast of wines on the lees, of fat things full of marrow, of well-refined wines*; **Neh 9:35** — the post-exilic confession of having received *eretz r'chavat-yadayim ha-shemenah* — *a wide and fat land*).

**(iii) *Dagan v-tirosh* — *grain and new wine*.** The pair is one of the HB's most-canonical agricultural-blessing word-pairs. **Dagan** (דָּגָן — *grain*, primarily the staple-cereals of wheat and barley) and **tirosh** (תִּירוֹשׁ — *new wine, must*, the freshly-pressed-and-fermenting grape-juice in the early-fermentation stage) constitute the two principal-staples of the Iron-Age Israelite agricultural-economy. The pair recurs at **Deut 7:13** (*v-d-ganeikha v-tiroshekha v-yitzharekha* — *and your grain, and your new wine, and your fresh oil*; the canonical-three with oil added); **Deut 11:14** (the rainy-season-blessing); **Deut 12:17** (the tithe-locale); **Deut 14:23, 26** (the tithe-vocabulary); **Deut 18:4** (the priestly-portion); **Deut 28:51** (the covenant-curse-reversal); **Deut 33:28** (Mosheh's blessing); **Hos 2:10, 2:24, 7:14, 9:1-2** (the prophetic deployment); **Hag 1:11, 2:19**; **Zech 9:17**; **Neh 5:11, 10:40, 13:5, 13:12**; **2 Chr 31:5, 32:28**. The pair is a fixed-formula of Israelite agricultural-blessing-vocabulary; **dagan v-tirosh** alone (without oil) recurs at Gen 27:28, 37; the triple **dagan-tirosh-yitzhar** (grain-wine-oil) is the more-canonical Deuteronomic-and-prophetic form.

**(iv) The *tirosh*-vs-*yayin* distinction.** *Tirosh* (new wine, must) is distinguished from *yayin* (aged wine) in BH usage: *tirosh* is the freshly-pressed and early-fermentation-stage product, *yayin* is the matured-and-aged product. The agricultural-blessing-vocabulary uses *tirosh* (which marks the freshness-and-newness-of-the-harvest); the ritual-celebratory-vocabulary uses *yayin* (which marks the matured-and-festive-product). The Gen 27:28 deployment is the agricultural-blessing-vocabulary; the chapter's later *yayin* at v 25 (*va-yavei lo yayin va-yesht* — *and he brought him wine and he drank*) is the ritual-celebratory-deployment within the blessing-meal-context. The lexical-distinction is editorially-deliberate.

**The partitive-*min*.** The preposition *mi-* in *mi-tal ha-shamayim u-mi-shemanei ha-aretz* is the **partitive-*min***: *of the dew, of the fatness*. The construction names a portion-from-the-source (the blessing-recipient receives some-but-not-all of the dew, some-but-not-all of the fatness). The partitive reading is fixed at v 28 by the immediately-following clause *v-rov dagan v-tirosh* (*and abundance of grain and new wine*), which is grammatically unambiguous: the blessing-recipient receives *rov* (abundance, much) — clearly a portion (the abundance is the recipient's share, not the whole of grain-and-wine in existence). The partitive reading at v 28 is editorially-uncontested. The contrast with the same construction at vv 39-40 (where the partitive-vs-separative reading is famously disputed; see companion entry on the *mi-shemanei-aretz* partitive-vs-separative ambiguity) is editorially-load-bearing: v 28 is unambiguously partitive, vv 39-40 is famously ambiguous.

**The articulated *ha-Elohim*.** The verse opens with *v-yiten l'kha ha-Elohim* — *and may the Elohim give you*. The articulated form *ha-Elohim* (with definite article) is editorially-distinctive in the chapter: v 20 has unarticulated *YHWH Eloheikha* (*YHWH your Elohim*), v 27 has *YHWH* (the tetragrammaton alone), and v 28 has *ha-Elohim* (the articulated form). The chapter's only direct divine-name-utterance in the blessing-formula uses the articulated *ha-Elohim*. The reading: the articulated form generalizes the deity-reference — *the Elohim* (the divine-being, with definite-article) rather than *YHWH* (the specific-name) or *YHWH your Elohim* (the relational-personal-formula). The choice is consistent with the chapter's wider editorial-handling of divine-names: the patriarchal-blessing invokes the divine-being-in-general rather than the covenant-personal-name. The WoH translation preserves *the Elohim* per the central-glossary `elohim-as-translation` policy.

**The patriarchal-fertility-blessing-formula type-scene.** The Gen 27:28 blessing-formula is the **template-form** of the patriarchal-fertility-blessing across the HB. The form is replicated and elaborated across:

- **Gen 49:25-26** (the Joseph-blessing in Ya'aqov's deathbed-blessings): *birkot shamayim me-al birkot tehom roveset tachat birkot shadayim va-rachem* — *blessings of the skies above, blessings of the deep that crouches beneath, blessings of the breasts and of the womb*.
- **Deut 33:13-17** (Mosheh's Joseph-blessing): the most-elaborate parallel — *mevoreket YHWH artzo mi-meged shamayim mi-tal u-mi-tehom roveset tachat u-mi-meged tevu'ot shamash u-mi-meged geresh y'rachim* — *blessed of YHWH be his land, of the choicest of the skies, of the dew, and of the deep that crouches beneath, and of the choicest yield of the sun, and of the choicest yield of the months*.
- **Deut 33:28** (the closing-blessing of Mosheh): *eretz dagan v-tirosh af-shamav ya'arfu tal* — *a land of grain and new wine; yes, his heavens drop down dew*. The closest verbal-parallel to Gen 27:28.

The pattern is the **ancient-poetic patriarchal-blessing-tradition**: dew-from-above + fatness-from-below + grain-and-wine. The tradition is well-developed across the Pentateuch's blessing-pericopes. **F. M. Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 5 (*The Song of the Sea and Canaanite Myth*) develops the ancient-poetic-tradition argument: the dew-and-fatness blessing-formula derives from the wider Canaanite agricultural-deity-cult and is preserved in Israelite poetic-tradition as part of the patriarchal-period religious-vocabulary. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 191 develops the cross-corpus blessing-formula argument.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline rendering with *shamayim* and *eretz* per the central-glossary entries (*the dew of the skies* and *the fatness of the land*) and *Elohim* in transliteration. The cross-corpus patriarchal-fertility-blessing-formula is documented in commentary. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the blessing-formula is mainstream modern-critical-attested and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 190-191; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 440-441; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 208-209; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 220-222; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 141; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 209-212; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 5 (the ancient-poetic patriarchal-blessing-tradition); Miller *The Religion of Ancient Israel* 2000; Smith *The Early History of God* 1990/2002; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); HALOT s.v. *tal*, *mashman*, *dagan*, *tirosh*; BDB s.v. *tal* p. 378, *mashman* p. 1032, *dagan* p. 186, *tirosh* p. 440. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula's elements are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus parallels (Deut 33:13, 33:28; Hos 14:6; Mic 5:6) are HB-internal-explicit; (c) the partitive-*min* reading is grammatically fixed by the parallel-construction; (d) the *tirosh*-vs-*yayin* distinction is documentary-lexicographic fact (HALOT, BDB); (e) the articulated *ha-Elohim* is grammatically explicit; (f) the ancient-poetic patriarchal-blessing-tradition is documented in Cross 1973 and the standard commentaries.

terms.source: יַֽעַבְד֣וּךָ עַמִּ֗ים וְיִֽשְׁתַּחֲו֤וּ לְךָ֙ לְאֻמִּ֔ים הֱוֵ֤ה גְבִיר֙ לְאַחֶ֔יךָ
terms.translit: ya'avdukha amim v-yishtachavu l'kha le'umim heveh g'vir l-acheikha
terms.strongs: H5647 (avad — serve) + H5971 (am — people) + H7812 (shachah hishtaphel — bow down) + H3816 (le'om — nation) + H1933 (havah — be, imperative) + H1376 (g'vir — lord, master) + H251 (ach — brother)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3mp with 2ms suffix) + noun (mp) + waw-impf-qal-jussive + preposition-with-suffix + noun (mp) + imperative + noun + preposition + noun
terms.literal: let peoples serve you, and let nations bow down to you; be a lord to your brothers
terms.standardEnglish: Let peoples serve thee, and nations bow down to thee: Be lord over thy brethren (ASV); Let people serve thee, and nations bow down to thee: be lord over thy brethren (KJV); Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you. Be lord over your brothers (NRSV); Let peoples serve you, And nations bow to you; Be master over your brothers (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you; be lord over your brothers (ASV-baseline-adjacent; qere *yishtachavu* read; the ketiv/qere flagged in commentary; the peoples-shall-serve formula's cross-corpus reach surfaced)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:29a contains the patriarchal-blessing's political-dominion-clause: *ya'avdukha amim v-yishtachavu l'kha le'umim heveh g'vir l-acheikha* — *let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you; be lord over your brothers*. The formula is the **peoples-shall-serve** blessing-clause, distinguishable from the agricultural-fertility-clause of v 28. The political-dominion-clause projects the blessing-recipient's descendants into a position of supremacy over surrounding peoples — a clause whose subsequent HB-prophetic and Christian-messianic receptions are editorially load-bearing.**

**The ketiv/qere on *yishtachavu*.** The MT preserves a textual variant at the verse's hishtaphel-form: **ketiv** *vyshtchv* (וישתחו — defective spelling, missing the second vav); **qere** *vyshtchvv* (וְיִֽשְׁתַּחֲוּ֤וּ — full spelling with the standard hishtaphel-conjugation). The hishtaphel is a rare conjugation in BH (the *š-ḥ-w-y* root is one of the few hishtaphel-attested verbs; cf. *yishtachavu* across the HB worship-vocabulary). The qere supplies the standard-orthographic form; the ketiv preserves an older defective-spelling. **Translators conventionally follow the qere** (the synagogue-reading); the WoH translation does so. The variant is **orthographic, not semantic**: both forms yield *they shall bow down*. The variant is flagged here for completeness but does not affect the translation choice.

**The *amim* / *le'umim* peoples-and-nations parallelism.** The two terms function as a **fixed-pair** in HB blessing-and-oracle contexts. The parallelism: *am* (singular: people, especially Israelite-internal-tribal-or-confederal-grouping; plural *amim* — *peoples*, the surrounding gentile-peoples) // *le'om* (the broader-ethnopolitical-grouping; plural *le'umim* — *nations*, often used in archaic-or-poetic registers in synonymous-parallelism with *amim*). The fixed-pair appears at:

- **Gen 25:23** — the prenatal-oracle to Rivkah: *shnei goyim b-vitnekh u-shnei le'umim mi-mei'ayikh yip'aredu u-le'om mi-le'om ye'ematz v-rav ya'avod tza'ir* — *two nations are in your womb, and two peoples shall be divided from your bowels; and one people shall be stronger than the other; and the elder shall serve the younger*. The Gen 25:23 prenatal-oracle is the chapter-27's **structural-parallel**: the political-dominion of one brother-line over the other is announced at the womb (Gen 25:23) and **enacted** at the blessing-utterance (Gen 27:29). The *amim* / *le'umim* pair at Gen 27:29 echoes the *goyim* / *le'umim* pair at Gen 25:23 with the *am* / *goy* variation. The chapter is in **direct dialogue** with the Gen 25 oracle.
- **Num 24:7** — Bil'am's third-oracle: *yizal-mayim mi-dalyav v-zar'o b-mayim rabim v-yarom me-Agag malko v-tinasei malkhuto* — *water shall flow from his buckets, and his seed shall be in many waters; and his king shall be higher than Agag, and his kingdom shall be exalted*. The Bil'am-oracles' cluster of *amim* / *le'umim* / *goyim* references.
- **Isa 17:12-13** — *hoy hamon amim rabim ka-hamot yamim yehemayun u-she'on le'umim ki-she'on mayim kabirim yisha'un* — *Ah, the uproar of many peoples, who roar like the roaring of the seas; and the rushing of nations, that rush like the rushing of mighty waters*. The prophetic-oracle-vocabulary's *amim* / *le'umim* fixed-pair.
- **Pss 47:4** — *yadber amim tachteinu u-le'umim tachat ragleinu* — *he shall subdue peoples under us, and nations under our feet*. The royal-psalm's *amim* / *le'umim* fixed-pair, with the political-dominion clause.
- **Pss 67:5** — *yism'chu vi-yran-nu le'umim ki tishpot amim mishor* — *let the nations rejoice and sing for joy; for you shall judge the peoples with equity*.
- **Isa 49:1** — *shim'u i'yim elai v-haqshivu le'umim me-rachoq* — *listen, O coastlands, to me; and pay attention, you peoples from far*.

The **fixed-pair** is editorially-established across the HB poetic-and-prophetic corpus. The Gen 27:29 deployment is the patriarchal-blessing's deployment of the fixed-pair.

**The *heveh g'vir l-acheikha* — *be lord over your brothers* — clause.** The clause is the **imperative-form** of the political-dominion-blessing: *heveh* (be, imperative of *havah* — a rare alternate of *hayah*; cf. the same imperative-form at Isa 16:4 *heveh seter lamo* — *be a shelter to them*) + *g'vir* (lord, master; cf. *gevirah* — the feminine-form, queen-mother; **g'vir** in BH names the household-head or the political-lord). The clause names the blessing-recipient as the **lord over his brothers** — a clause whose meaning is editorially-load-bearing for the Gen 27 chapter and the wider patriarchal-narrative: the blessing-recipient (Ya'aqov, having displaced Esav) is to be lord over his brothers (Esav, and by extension the Edomite-lineage). The clause **sets up the v 40 inversion**: Esav's counter-blessing prophesies that the day will come when Esav will *throw off the yoke* of Ya'aqov's lordship (see companion entry on the *cherev tichye* sword-life Edom-tradition). The chapter's two halves are in **direct dialectical tension**: v 29 establishes Ya'aqov's lordship; v 40 prophesies its eventual partial-undoing.

**The cross-corpus political-dominion-formula.** The peoples-shall-serve / nations-shall-bow-down political-dominion-formula recurs across the HB:

- **Gen 9:25-27** — the Ham / Shem / Yefet blessing-curse cluster after the flood: *arur Kena'an eved avadim yihyeh l-echav* — *cursed be Kena'an, a servant of servants shall he be unto his brothers*; *barukh YHWH Elohei Shem vi-hi Kena'an eved lamo* — *blessed be YHWH the Elohim of Shem; and let Kena'an be his servant*; *yaft Elohim l-Yefet v-yishkon b-ohalei Shem vi-hi Kena'an eved lamo* — *may Elohim enlarge Yefet, and let him dwell in the tents of Shem; and let Kena'an be his servant*. The peoples-shall-serve formula's foundational deployment, applied to the Hamite (Kena'anite) peoples vis-a-vis the Shemite (Israelite) line. The Gen 27 deployment is editorially-paralleled to Gen 9:25-27: both project political-dominion-relations among brother-lines into the future.
- **Num 24:9** — Bil'am's blessing-of-Israel: *m-varekheikha varukh v-orereikha arur* — *blessed be every one that blesseth thee, and cursed be every one that curseth thee*. The Num 24:9 closing-formula is **direct-verbal-echo** of Gen 27:29b (see following entry on the cursed-blessed echo). The Bil'am-oracle reads back through the Gen 27 blessing.
- **Pss 72:11** — *v-yishtachavu lo kol-melakhim kol-goyim ya'avduhu* — *and all kings shall bow down to him; all nations shall serve him*. The Solomon-royal-psalm's deployment, applying the Gen 27 formula to the Davidic-Solomonic king. The verse is the source-text for the subsequent Davidic-messianic reception.
- **Isa 49:23** — *v-hayu m-lakhim om-na'iqh v-saroteihem meiniqotaikh apayim eretz yishtachavu lakh v-afar raglayikh y-lacheku* — *and kings shall be your nursing fathers, and their queens your nursing mothers; they shall bow down to you with their faces to the earth, and lick up the dust of your feet*. The post-exilic prophetic-reception of the bowing-down formula, applied to the restored-Zion / restored-Yisrael.

The cross-corpus reach of the formula is editorially-load-bearing: the Gen 27 deployment is foundational and is read-through in subsequent HB-and-NT receptions.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline-adjacent rendering. The ketiv/qere variant, the *amim* / *le'umim* fixed-pair, the *heveh g'vir* imperative-form, and the cross-corpus political-dominion-formula are documented in commentary. The Gen 25:23 prenatal-oracle parallel is flagged. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the political-dominion-formula is mainstream modern-critical-attested and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 191-192; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 441-443; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 209-211; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 222-225; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 141; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 210-212; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973; HALOT s.v. *avad*, *am*, *le'om*, *havah*, *g'vir*, *shachah*; BDB s.v. *avad* p. 712, *am* p. 766, *le'om* p. 522, *g'vir* p. 150. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 66:1-4; Rashi at Gen 27:29. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula's components are grammatically explicit; (b) the ketiv/qere variant is documentary-textual; (c) the *amim* / *le'umim* fixed-pair is verifiable by lexical search; (d) the Gen 25:23 prenatal-oracle parallel is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the cross-corpus political-dominion-formula is HB-internal-explicit at Gen 9:25-27, Num 24:9, Ps 72:11, Isa 49:23; (f) the Davidic-messianic and post-exilic-prophetic receptions are HB-internal-explicit.

terms.partOfSpeech: qal-participle-mp + qal-passive-participle + waw + piel-participle-mp + qal-passive-participle, all with 2ms-suffixes
terms.literal: those-cursing-you (be) cursed; and those-blessing-you (be) blessed
terms.standardEnglish: Cursed be every one that curseth thee, And blessed be every one that blesseth thee (ASV/KJV); Cursed be those who curse you, and blessed be those who bless you (NRSV); Cursed be they who curse you, blessed they who bless you (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Those who curse you, cursed; and those who bless you, blessed (ASV-baseline-adjacent; the chiastic participle-pair preserved; the Avrahamic-chain-transmission and the Gen 12:3 / Num 24:9 cross-references surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:29b contains the patriarchal-blessing's curse-blessing-protection clause: *orereikha arur u-m'varakheikha barukh* — *those who curse you, cursed; and those who bless you, blessed*. The clause is an **explicit reverse-echo** of Gen 12:3 (Avraham's call: *va-avarkhah m-vareikheikha u-m-qaleelkha a'or* — *and I will bless those who bless you, and him who curses you I will curse*), now transferred to Ya'aqov via the Yitzchaq-patriarchal-blessing rather than received directly via YHWH-theophany. The clause is editorially-load-bearing for the Avrahamic-blessing-transmission chain: Avraham → Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov.**

**The Gen 12:3 / Gen 27:29 textual-relation.** The two verses share the curse-blessing-protection-clause structure but differ in three grammatically-significant ways:

**(i) The verb-root choice for *curse*.** Gen 12:3 uses two different curse-verbs: *m-qaleelkha* (piel-participle of *qalal* — *curse, treat lightly, despise*) and *a'or* (qal-imperfect of *arar* — *curse*). The Gen 27:29 clause uses only *arar*: *orereikha arur*. The Gen 12:3 *qalal* / *arar* asymmetry (those who *qalal* will be *arar*-ed) is editorially-marked — *qalal* is the lighter / contempt-and-mockery curse-verb, *arar* is the binding / efficacious / juridical curse-verb. The Gen 27:29 collapses to *arar* alone, simplifying the lexical-asymmetry. The lexical-shift is editorially-noted by Sarna and Westermann.

**(ii) The verbal-form: 1cs-finite (Gen 12:3) vs. participle-pair (Gen 27:29).** Gen 12:3 has *YHWH* speaking in the 1cs: *I will bless / I will curse*. Gen 27:29 has the participles-and-passive-participles: *those-blessing-you (be) blessed; those-cursing-you (be) cursed*. The Gen 27:29 form **lacks the 1cs subject** — there is no explicit speaker invoked to bless or curse. The form is **jussive-impersonal**: the participle-pair invokes the curse-blessing-mechanism without specifying who effects it. The form is consistent with Yitzchaq's status as the **transmitter** rather than the **source** of the blessing: he passes on what Avraham received from YHWH, without re-invoking YHWH's 1cs-agency.

**(iii) The clause-order.** Gen 12:3 has the *blessing-clause first* (*va-avarkhah m-vareikheikha*) and the *curse-clause second* (*u-m-qaleelkha a'or*). Gen 27:29 has the *curse-clause first* (*orereikha arur*) and the *blessing-clause second* (*u-m'varakheikha barukh*). The clause-order inversion is editorially-deliberate. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 191 notes the inversion; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 442 reads the inversion as a stylistic-variation appropriate to the patriarchal-blessing-genre (vs. the divine-call-theophany of Gen 12).

**The Avrahamic-blessing-chain-transmission.** The curse-blessing-protection-clause is the **third-link** in the Avrahamic-blessing-chain-transmission across the patriarchal-narrative:

- **Gen 12:3** — Avraham receives the curse-blessing-protection-clause directly from YHWH at the Lekh-Lekha call.
- **Gen 27:29** — Ya'aqov (mistaken-for-Esav) receives the clause from Yitzchaq via the patriarchal-blessing.
- **Num 24:9** — Bil'am pronounces the clause over Israel-as-a-whole (after the prenatal-oracle's *am*-and-*le'umim* peoples-language): *m-varekheikha varukh v-orereikha arur* — *blessed be every one that blesseth thee, and cursed be every one that curseth thee*. The verbal-form is **near-identical** to Gen 27:29: the same participle-pair with the same passive-participle-completions, in the **same** order (blessing-first, curse-second — Bil'am's order matches Gen 12:3's, not Gen 27:29's). The Bil'am-deployment is the **non-Israelite-prophet's** invocation of the formula, read by Bil'am through divine-revelation: the formula is now **transferred** from the patriarchal-line to the whole-people-of-Israel.

The chain-transmission is editorially-traceable: the curse-blessing-protection-clause originates in YHWH's call to Avraham (Gen 12:3), is transmitted through Yitzchaq's blessing to Ya'aqov (Gen 27:29), and is finally enlarged to apply to the whole-people-of-Israel through Bil'am's oracle (Num 24:9). The clause's cross-corpus reach establishes the Avrahamic-Yisra'elite-blessing as a **transmissible** patriarchal-inheritance.

**Relation to the existing `nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael` entry.** The existing entry covers Gen 12:3's **universal-blessing clause** (*v-nivrakhu v'kha kol mishp'chot ha-adamah* — *and in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed* / *shall bless themselves*) — the niphal-hithpael-disputed clause. The Gen 27:29 verse uses the **curse-blessing-protection clause** of Gen 12:3 (the *m-vareikheikha varekh, m-qaleelkha a'or* clause) — **not** the universal-blessing-niphal-hithpael clause. The two clauses of Gen 12:3 are distinguishable: the curse-blessing-protection-clause is the **immediate-defensive** clause (those who curse Avraham will be cursed; those who bless him will be blessed); the universal-blessing-niphal-hithpael-clause is the **expansive-promissory** clause (in/through Avraham all the families of the earth will be / will bless themselves). The Gen 27:29 verse picks up **only** the curse-blessing-protection-clause and transfers it to Ya'aqov; the universal-blessing-clause is not redeployed at Gen 27:29. The two entries are complementary: `nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael` covers the universal-blessing-clause; this entry covers the curse-blessing-protection-clause.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline-adjacent rendering. The Gen 12:3 / Gen 27:29 / Num 24:9 chain-transmission is documented in commentary. The Pauline reception (Gal 3:8 cites Gen 12:3's universal-blessing-clause but not the curse-blessing-protection-clause) is flagged-not-preempted. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the curse-blessing-protection-clause is mainstream modern-critical-attested and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 191-192; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 441-443; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 209-211; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 222-225; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 141; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 210-212; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); Brichto *The Problem of 'Curse' in the Hebrew Bible* SBLDS 13 (1963 — the standard monograph on the *arar / qalal / qabav / na'aq* curse-vocabulary); HALOT s.v. *arar*, *barakh*; BDB s.v. *arar* p. 76 and *barakh* p. 138. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the participle-pair construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the Gen 12:3 reverse-echo is HB-internal-explicit; (c) the Num 24:9 Bil'am-deployment is HB-internal-explicit and verbal-near-identical; (d) the chain-transmission Avraham → Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the lexical-distinction between *arar* and *qalal* is documentary-lexicographic fact; (f) the curse-blessing-protection-clause / universal-blessing-clause distinction is grammatically explicit.

terms.source: וַיֶּחֱרַ֨ד יִצְחָ֣ק חֲרָדָה֮ גְּדֹלָ֣ה עַד־מְאֹד֒ ... גַּם־בָּר֖וּךְ יִהְיֶֽה
terms.translit: va-yecherad Yitzchaq charadah gedolah ad-me'od ... gam-barukh yihye
terms.strongs: H2729 (charad — tremble, be terrified) + H2731 (charadah — trembling, anxiety) + H1419 (gadol) + H3966 (me'od) + H1571 (gam) + H1288 (barukh) + H1961 (hayah)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3ms, vav-consec) + proper noun + noun (cognate-accusative) + adjective (fs) + adverb + intensifier ... particle + qal-passive-participle + verb (qal impf 3ms)
terms.literal: and Yitzchaq trembled a great trembling exceedingly ... also/indeed, blessed he shall be
terms.standardEnglish: And Isaac trembled very exceedingly ... yea, and he shall be blessed (ASV/KJV); Then Isaac trembled violently ... and indeed, he shall be blessed (NRSV); Isaac was seized with very violent trembling ... Now he must remain blessed (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: And Yitzchaq trembled a great trembling exceedingly ... Indeed, he shall be blessed (cognate-accusative preserved; the *gam-barukh yihye* blessing-irrevocability-declaration surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:33 contains two editorially-load-bearing clauses, both with cross-corpus reach: (i) the cognate-accusative trembling-construction *va-yecherad Yitzchaq charadah gedolah ad-me'od* — *and Yitzchaq trembled a great trembling exceedingly*, the strongest narrative-emotion-marking in the patriarchal-cycle; (ii) the blessing-irrevocability-declaration *gam-barukh yihye* — *also/indeed, he shall be blessed*, the source-text for the rabbinic and medieval-Christian doctrine of patriarchal-blessing-irrevocability and for the Heb 12:17 reception.**

**The cognate-accusative trembling-construction.** The construction *va-yecherad ... charadah gedolah ad-me'od* is the **cognate-accusative** Hebrew syntactic pattern: a verb is followed by its cognate-noun as object, with intensifying adjectives. The pattern intensifies the action through verb-and-noun-of-the-same-root repetition. The Gen 27:33 deployment combines:
- *va-yecherad* — qal impf 3ms vav-consec of *charad* (tremble, be terrified)
- *charadah* — cognate-noun (the trembling itself)
- *gedolah* — modifying-adjective (great)
- *ad-me'od* — adverbial-intensifier (exceedingly, to-the-utmost)

The stacked-intensification (cognate-accusative + great + exceedingly) is editorially-marked: the narrator deploys the strongest-available HB intensification-pattern to mark the moment. The pattern recurs in the HB at moments of acute-emotional-or-perceptual-rupture:

- **Gen 27:33** (here) — Yitzchaq trembling at the realization of the deception.
- **Exod 19:16** — *va-yecherad kol ha-am asher ba-machaneh* — *and all the people who were in the camp trembled* — at the Sinai-theophany. The cognate-accusative is absent (no *charadah* with the verb), but the *charad* verb appears alone in the same intense-fear register.
- **1 Sam 14:15** — *va-tehi charadah ba-machaneh ba-sadeh u-v-khol ha-am ha-matzav v-ha-mashchit chardu gam-hemmah va-tirgaz ha-aretz va-tehi l-charadat Elohim* — *and there was a trembling in the camp, in the field, and among all the people; the garrison and the spoilers, they also trembled, and the earth quaked; so it became a great trembling*. The Saul-Jonathan-Philistine-rout narrative uses the *charadah*-noun three times in a single verse — the densest HB *charadah*-cluster, marking the Philistine-army's panic-rout. The closing *charadat Elohim* (*a trembling of Elohim*, i.e., a divinely-induced trembling) marks the trembling as supernatural-in-source.
- **Isa 21:4** — *ta'ah l'vavi pallatzut bi'atatni et neshef chishqi sam li la-charadah* — *my heart panted, fearfulness affrighted me; the twilight that I longed for has been turned into trembling unto me*. The prophetic-vision's emotional-rupture.
- **Ezek 26:18** — *atah yacherdu ha-i'yim yom mappaltekh* — *now shall the islands tremble in the day of your fall* — Tyre-oracle.
- **Dan 10:7** — *aval charadah gedolah naflah aleihem* — *but a great trembling fell upon them* — at the angelic-vision. The *charadah gedolah* echoes Gen 27:33's *charadah gedolah* directly.
- **Isa 32:11** — *chirdu sha'ananot* — *tremble, you who are at ease*.

The cross-corpus pattern: the *charad* / *charadah* lexical-cluster names **intense-fear-or-agitation at a perceived rupture** — typically a theophany (Exod 19; Dan 10), a battle-panic (1 Sam 14), a prophetic-vision (Isa 21; Ezek 26), or an emotional-realization (Gen 27:33). The Gen 27:33 deployment is the **patriarchal-narrative concentration-locus** of the cluster.

**The *gam-barukh yihye* blessing-irrevocability-declaration.** The closing clause of v 33 — *gam-barukh yihye* — *also/indeed, he shall be blessed* — is the **declaration that the blessing, once-uttered, cannot be revoked**. The clause is the source-text for the rabbinic-and-medieval doctrine of blessing-irrevocability. The reading: the blessing has been **performatively-spoken** (see the companion `nefesh-blessing-transfer` entry on the declarative-blessing-speech-act framework); once spoken, the speech-act has effected what it said; no retraction is possible. The clause *gam-barukh yihye* is **Yitzchaq's acknowledgement** of the binding-effect: the blessing has been given, it remains given, and the recipient — whoever he is — will be blessed.

**The Heb 12:17 NT reception.** The blessing-irrevocability-declaration is the source-text for **Hebrews 12:17**: *iste gar hoti kai metepeita thelōn klēronomēsai tēn eulogian apedokimasthē, metanoias gar topon ouch heuren, kaiper meta dakryōn ekzētēsas autēn* — *for you know that even when he afterward desired to inherit the blessing, he was rejected; for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears*. The author of Hebrews reads the Gen 27:33 *gam-barukh yihye* clause as the foundation for the patriarchal-blessing's irrevocability: Esav sought to inherit the blessing but could not find *topos metanoias* (a place of repentance / a place-of-change-of-mind / a way to undo the prior speech-act). The Hebrews-reading takes the Gen 27 narrative as a typological-warning about the irrevocability of theological-decisions. The reception is editorially-distant from the Gen 27 narrative-context but is the foundational NT-reception of the chapter.

**The rabbinic reception.** The rabbinic-and-medieval doctrine of *birkhat ha-av eyn la chazarah* (*the paternal-blessing has no return* / *cannot be retracted*) develops from the Gen 27:33 clause across **Genesis Rabbah 67:1-4**, **b. Bava Batra 16b**, **Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 32**, and the medieval-Jewish commentators (Rashi, Ramban, Sforno at Gen 27:33). The doctrine extends to the broader category of **public-utterance-as-binding-speech-act** in Jewish-legal-tradition (esp. in the laws of *neder* — vow — and *shevu'ah* — oath; see m. Ned. and m. Shevu.). The patriarchal-blessing is the foundational-instance.

**The chapter's narrative-mechanics at v 33.** The clause is editorially-significant within the chapter's narrative-mechanics. Yitzchaq has just discovered the deception (vv 30-32 — Esav arrives with his venison; Yitzchaq asks *mi-atah* — *who are you?* — and Esav identifies himself); Yitzchaq trembles (the great trembling exceedingly); he asks the rhetorical-question *mi-efo hu* — *who then is he?* (the question's *efo* — *then, now* — marks the realization-moment); he acknowledges that he has already eaten and blessed (*va-okhal mi-kol b-terem tavo va-avarakhehu* — *and I ate of all before you came, and I blessed him*); and he closes with the irrevocability-declaration (*gam-barukh yihye*). The verse stages the moment of discovery, the emotional-rupture, and the legal-acceptance in a single editorially-tight sequence. The *gam-barukh yihye* is the chapter's structural-keystone: it locks-in the blessing-transfer despite the deception.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the cognate-accusative-and-intensifier construction *trembled a great trembling exceedingly* and the closing *Indeed, he shall be blessed*. The cross-corpus *charadah*-cluster, the blessing-irrevocability-declaration, the Heb 12:17 NT reception, and the rabbinic doctrine are documented in commentary. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the chapter's narrative-mechanics are mainstream modern-critical-attested.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 192; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 443-444; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 211-212; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 226-227; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 141-142; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 210-213; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* JSOTSup 95 (1987); Brichto *The Problem of 'Curse' in the Hebrew Bible* SBLDS 13 (1963); Lane *Hebrews 9-13* WBC 1991 (on the Heb 12:17 reception); HALOT s.v. *charad*, *charadah*; BDB s.v. *charad* p. 353 and *charadah* p. 353. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 67:1-4; b. Bava Batra 16b; Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 32; Rashi, Ramban, Sforno at Gen 27:33. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the cognate-accusative construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus *charadah*-cluster is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Dan 10:7 *charadah gedolah* near-identical parallel is HB-internal-explicit; (d) the *gam-barukh yihye* blessing-irrevocability-declaration is grammatically explicit; (e) the Heb 12:17 NT reception is canon-internal-explicit; (f) the rabbinic doctrine of blessing-irrevocability is documented in mainstream Jewish-tradition sources.

terms.strongs: H6817 (tza'aq — cry out) + H6818 (tza'aqah — outcry, cry) + H1419 (gadol) + H4751 (mar — bitter) + H3966 (me'od)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal impf 3ms, vav-consec) + cognate-noun + adjective (fs) + waw + adjective (fs) + adverb
terms.literal: and he cried out a cry, great and bitter, exceedingly
terms.standardEnglish: he cried with an exceeding great and bitter cry (ASV/KJV); he cried out with an exceedingly great and bitter cry (NRSV); he burst into wild and bitter sobbing (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: he cried out a great and bitter cry exceedingly (cognate-accusative preserved; the doubled-adjective-pair preserved; the Esth 4:1 verbal-borrowing surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:34 contains the chapter's second cognate-accusative-and-intensifier construction (after v 33's *charadah* construction): *va-yitz'aq tza'aqah gedolah u-marah ad-me'od* — *and he cried out a great and bitter cry exceedingly*. The verse stages Esav's discovery-and-grief at the blessing-loss in the strongest-available HB emotional-rupture-vocabulary. The verse is also the source-text for a widely-noted cross-corpus verbal-borrowing at Esther 4:1, marking the bitter-cry-formula as a fixed-phrase of irreversible-loss across the HB narrative-corpus.**

**The cognate-accusative construction.** The construction *va-yitz'aq tza'aqah* is the **second cognate-accusative** of the chapter (paralleling v 33's *va-yecherad charadah*). The doubled-adjective-pair *gedolah u-marah* (great-and-bitter) stacks two qualifiers; the intensifier *ad-me'od* (exceedingly) further amplifies. The construction is structurally-parallel to v 33's: a verb-and-cognate-noun pair, intensified by adjective(s) and *ad-me'od*. The chapter's two cognate-accusatives at vv 33 (Yitzchaq's trembling) and 34 (Esav's crying) are editorially-paired: the **father trembles**, the **son cries** — both in the strongest available HB emotion-vocabulary. The pairing is a literary-structural device: the discovery-moment is matched by an equally-marked grief-moment.

**The *tza'aq* / *za'aq* / *tza'aqah* lexical-cluster.** The verb *tza'aq* (צָעַק — *cry out*) and its variant *za'aq* (זָעַק — same meaning, different sibilant) are a near-doublet in BH: both verbs appear with the same semantic range (*cry out, lament, call for help*), with *za'aq* slightly more frequent in prophetic and historical-narrative contexts. The cognate-nouns are *tza'aqah* (צְעָקָה) and *za'aqah* (זְעָקָה). The lexical-cluster (verbs and nouns) names the **outcry-of-distress / outcry-of-the-oppressed**, with cross-corpus deployments at:

- **Gen 4:10** — *qol d'mei achikha tzo'aqim elai min ha-adamah* — *the voice of your brother's blood cries out to me from the ground* (Kayin and Hevel; the foundational HB outcry-from-the-oppressed).
- **Gen 18:20-21** — *za'aqat S'dom va-Amorah ki-rabbah v-chatatam ki khav'dah me'od* — *the outcry of S'dom and Amorah is great, and their sin is exceedingly grievous* (the S'dom-Amorah judgment-narrative's outcry-from-the-oppressed).
- **Exod 2:23-25** — *va-tit'al shav'atam el ha-Elohim min ha-avodah va-yishma Elohim et-na'aqatam* — *and their cry came up to Elohim by reason of the bondage, and Elohim heard their groaning* (the Egyptian-bondage outcry; the foundational Mosaic-call-narrative outcry).
- **Exod 3:7-9** — YHWH's response: *ra'oh ra'iti et-oni ami asher b-Mitzrayim v-et-tza'aqatam shamati* — *I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Mitzrayim, and have heard their cry* (the *tza'aqah* of the oppressed; the existing `zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed` central-glossary entry covers the lexical-cluster).
- **Esth 4:1** — *u-Mordekhai yada et-kol asher na'asah va-yiqra Mordekhai et-b'gadav va-yilbash saq va-efer va-yetze b-tokh ha-ir va-yiz'aq z'aqah gedolah u-marah* — *and Mordekhai knew all that was done; and Mordekhai rent his clothes, and put on sackcloth with ashes, and went out into the midst of the city, and cried with a loud and bitter cry*. The Esther 4:1 *za'aqah gedolah u-marah* is **verbal-near-identical** to Gen 27:34's *tza'aqah gedolah u-marah* — the sibilant-variation (*z* / *tz*) being the only lexical-difference. The Esther 4:1 deployment is widely-noted in the literary-critical literature as a **deliberate verbal-borrowing** from Gen 27:34: Mordekhai's grief at the destruction-decree echoes Esav's grief at the blessing-loss. **Berlin** *Esther* JPS Bible Commentary (2001) p. 44; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 192; **Levenson** *Esther* OTL (1997) p. 78 — all three commentators note the verbal-borrowing as editorially-deliberate. The borrowing-reading positions Esther as a literary-typological figure of the **threatened-Israelite-people** in a context where Esav's grief at the blessing-loss is being read-back-through.

**The Esther-as-Esav-echo reading (the rabbinic tradition).** The rabbinic-midrashic tradition develops a more elaborate reading of the Esth-Gen 27 verbal-borrowing: **Esther Rabbah** and **Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer** read the Esther 4:1 *za'aqah* as the **inversion** of the Gen 27:34 *tza'aqah* — Mordekhai's cry for the Jewish-people is the **reversal** of Esav's cry for himself. The reading reads the Esther-narrative typologically as the Israelite-people's vindication against the Esav-Edomite-line (Haman is descended from Agag the Amalekite — Amaleq is a grandson of Esav per Gen 36:12 — so the Esther-narrative is structurally the Ya'aqov-Esav rivalry's eschatological-resolution). The reading is downstream-rabbinic and is flagged here for completeness without being smuggled into the translation.

**The *gedolah u-marah* doubled-adjective-pair.** The pair *gedolah u-marah* (*great and bitter*) is editorially-marked. The cross-corpus distribution of the pair:

- **Gen 27:34** (here) — Esav's bitter-cry.
- **Esth 4:1** — Mordekhai's bitter-cry (verbal-near-identical).
- **Ezek 27:30-31** — Tyre-lament: *va-yashmi'u alayikh b-qolam u-yiz'aqu marah* — *and they shall cause their voice to be heard for you, and shall cry bitterly*. The Tyre-lament uses *marah* alone, without the *gedolah*-pairing.
- **Isa 33:7** — *hen er'elam tza'aqu chutzah malachei shalom mar yivkayun* — *behold, their valiant ones cry outside; the ambassadors of peace weep bitterly*. The *mar* adverbial-form.
- **Zeph 1:14** — *qol yom YHWH mar tzore'ach sham gibor* — *the voice of the day of YHWH is bitter; the mighty man cries there bitterly*. The day-of-YHWH-oracle.

The *gedolah u-marah* paired-construction is distinctive to Gen 27:34 and Esth 4:1; the wider *mar* / *marah* (bitterness) cry-vocabulary is more broadly attested. The fixed-phrase pairing is the source-text for the irreversible-loss marker.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the cognate-accusative-and-intensifier construction *cried out a great and bitter cry exceedingly*. The Esther 4:1 verbal-borrowing is documented in commentary with a cross-reference to the existing `zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed` central-glossary entry. The rabbinic Esther-as-Esav-echo reading is flagged-not-preempted. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the chapter's emotional-rupture-vocabulary is mainstream modern-critical-attested.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 192; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 443-444; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 212; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 227; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 142; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 210-213; Berlin *Esther* JPS Bible Commentary 2001 p. 44; Levenson *Esther* OTL 1997 p. 78; Fox *Character and Ideology in the Book of Esther* 1991; HALOT s.v. *tza'aq*, *za'aq*, *tza'aqah*, *za'aqah*, *mar*, *marah*; BDB s.v. *tza'aq* p. 858 and *za'aq* p. 277. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Esther Rabbah 7:10; Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 49; Rashi at Gen 27:34 and Esth 4:1. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the cognate-accusative construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the doubled-adjective-pair *gedolah u-marah* is grammatically explicit; (c) the Esther 4:1 verbal-near-identical parallel is HB-internal-explicit and is widely-documented in mainstream modern-critical commentary; (d) the cross-corpus *tza'aq* / *za'aq* lexical-cluster is verifiable by lexical search; (e) the lexical-relation to the existing `zaaqah-outcry-of-the-oppressed` entry is established by the shared root.

terms.source: הִנֵּ֞ה מִשְׁמַנֵּ֤י הָאָ֙רֶץ֙ יִהְיֶ֣ה מֽוֹשָׁבֶ֔ךָ וּמִטַּ֥ל הַשָּׁמַ֖יִם מֵעָֽל
terms.translit: hineh mi-shemanei ha-aretz yihye moshavekha u-mi-tal ha-shamayim me-al
terms.strongs: H4480 (min — from, of, away from) + H4924 (mashman — fatness) + H776 (eretz) + H4186 (moshav — dwelling) + H2919 (tal — dew) + H8064 (shamayim) + H5921 (me-al — from-above)

terms.literal: behold, of/away-from the fatness of the eretz shall be your dwelling, and of/away-from the dew of the shamayim from-above
terms.standardEnglish: Behold, of the fatness of the earth shall be thy dwelling, and of the dew of heaven from above (ASV/KJV — partitive); Behold, away from the fatness of the earth shall your home be, and away from the dew of heaven on high (NRSV — separative); See, your abode shall enjoy the fat of the earth and the dew of heaven above (NJPS — partitive smoothing); Behold, away from the rich soil shall your home be, away from the dew of heaven above (NJPS-apparatus / Westermann / Speiser — separative)
terms.wohChoice: Behold, away from the fatness of the land shall be your dwelling, and away from the dew of the skies from above (separative reading; modern-critical default per Westermann, Speiser, Sarna-apparatus, NRSV; the chapter-internal contrast with v 28 and the Edomite-Seir-desert geographical-fit surfaced in commentary; partitive reading per ASV/KJV/rabbinic-harmonizing flagged in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:39 contains one of the HB's most-famous and most-disputed prepositional ambiguities: the *mi-* prefix at *mi-shemanei ha-aretz* and *mi-tal ha-shamayim* is open to two diametrically opposite readings — (a) **partitive** (*of the fatness of the land ... and of the dew of the skies* — reading Esav's blessing as a parallel-fertile counterpart to Ya'aqov's), or (b) **separative** (*away from the fatness of the land ... and away from the dew of the skies* — reading Esav's blessing as the negation of Ya'aqov's: Esav is displaced from the fertile-land region into desert-marginality). The two readings yield opposite blessings.**

**The grammatical-ambiguity.** The Hebrew preposition *min* (מִן־) functions in BH as both:

**(i) Partitive-*min***: *of, some of, a portion of*. The preposition introduces a substance from which a portion is taken. The construction is well-attested across the HB (e.g., Gen 27:28's *mi-tal ha-shamayim u-mi-shemanei ha-aretz v-rov dagan v-tirosh* — *of the dew of the skies, and of the fatness of the land, and abundance of grain and new wine* — where the partitive reading is fixed by the parallel-clause *v-rov dagan v-tirosh*, unambiguously partitive).

**(ii) Separative-*min***: *from, away from, out of, far from*. The preposition introduces the location-or-source from which something departs. The construction is equally well-attested (e.g., Gen 27:1's *me-r'ot* — *from seeing*, the privative-min — see companion `kahah-eynayim` entry).

The Hebrew preposition is **grammatically identical** in both readings; the distinction is **contextually-determined**. At Gen 27:39 the context is famously disputed.

**The chapter-internal contrast: v 28 vs. v 39.** The chapter contains the **same construction** (*mi-tal ha-shamayim u-mi-shemanei ha-aretz*) at both:

- **v 28** (Ya'aqov's blessing): *v-yiten l'kha ha-Elohim mi-tal ha-shamayim u-mi-shemanei ha-aretz v-rov dagan v-tirosh* — the partitive reading is **fixed** by the parallel *v-rov dagan v-tirosh* clause (the recipient receives *rov* — abundance, much — clearly a portion).
- **v 39** (Esav's counter-blessing): *hineh mi-shemanei ha-aretz yihye moshavekha u-mi-tal ha-shamayim me-al* — the partitive vs. separative reading is **open**: there is no parallel-clause fixing one reading over the other.

The **chapter-internal parallel** is editorially-load-bearing. The construction's repetition with the **potentially opposite force** at vv 28 and 39 is plausibly a **deliberate pun** — the patriarch uses the same words for both blessings, but with the meaning shifted from positive (v 28: receive a portion of these gifts) to negative (v 39: be displaced from these gifts). The pun is the chapter's deepest rhetorical move: the same-words-different-meaning structure encodes the patriarch's awareness that he is giving Esav a lesser-blessing despite using verbally-similar formula.

**The two readings in detail.**

**(a) The partitive reading: *of the fatness of the land*.** On this reading, Esav's blessing parallels Ya'aqov's — both receive a portion of the dew and fatness. The reading is preserved at:

- **ASV / KJV**: *of the fatness of the earth ... and of the dew of heaven from above* — the dominant English-tradition reading.
- **Rabbinic-harmonizing tradition**: Rashi at Gen 27:39 reads the partitive, taking the verse as a parallel-blessing to v 28 (Esav also receives fertility-blessings). Genesis Rabbah 67:6 develops the partitive-rabbinic-harmonizing reading.
- **NJPS-main-text**: *See, your abode shall enjoy the fat of the earth and the dew of heaven above* — the partitive reading is preserved in the main text (with the separative noted in the apparatus).

The difficulty: the partitive reading creates the **contradiction** that Esav's counter-blessing duplicates rather than counter-balances Ya'aqov's primary-blessing. The chapter's narrative-mechanics suggest Esav is being given a **lesser** blessing (Esav comes too late, Yitzchaq cannot revoke the primary-blessing, Esav weeps and demands at least some blessing — vv 36-38). The partitive reading does not produce a lesser-blessing; it produces a parallel-blessing.

**(b) The separative reading: *away from the fatness of the land*.** On this reading, Esav is **displaced** from the fertile-land and the dew-region into desert-marginality. The reading is preserved at:

- **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 444-445: explicit separative reading; develops the Edomite-Seir-desert geographical-fit.
- **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 212-213: separative reading; cites the chapter-internal contrast and the Edomite-tradition.
- **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 193: notes both readings; the main-text-translation preserves partitive but the commentary develops separative.
- **NRSV**: *Behold, away from the fatness of the earth shall your home be, and away from the dew of heaven on high* — the dominant modern-critical English rendering.
- **NJPS-apparatus**: notes the separative as an alternative.
- **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 227-228: separative reading; aligns with Westermann and Speiser.
- **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 212-213: separative reading.
- **Alter** *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 142: *behold, away from the fat places of the earth shall your dwelling be* — separative reading.

The modern-critical-scholarly consensus is **predominantly separative**: the chapter's narrative-mechanics, the geographical-fit (Esav = Edom, settled in *eretz Seir* — the southern-desert-and-mountain-region southeast of the Dead Sea, which is not characterized by fertile-soil or abundant-dew), and the chapter-internal contrast with v 28 all favor the separative reading.

**The Edomite-Seir-desert geographical-fit.** Esav's descendant-line is the Edomite peoples (Gen 36:8 *va-yeshev Esav b-har Se'ir Esav hu Edom* — *and Esav dwelt in Mount Se'ir; Esav is Edom*). The Edomite-territory is the *eretz Seir* — the southern-desert-and-mountain region southeast of the Dead Sea, between the *Arabah* and the Arabian-desert. The territory is **geographically marginal** in terms of agricultural-fertility: low rainfall, limited dew, mountain-and-desert-terrain rather than the fertile-coastal-plain or the Galilean-and-Judean-highlands that the Israelite-territories occupy. The separative reading (*away from the fatness*) **geographically fits** the Edomite-tradition; the partitive reading (*of the fatness*) **does not**. The geographical-fit is the modern-critical reading's strongest argument.

**The blessing's overall character.** The separative reading also fits the chapter's **structural mechanics**: Yitzchaq cannot give Esav the primary-blessing (already given to Ya'aqov, irrevocable per v 33's *gam-barukh yihye*); the secondary-blessing he gives must be **structurally different** — a lesser-blessing, a counter-blessing, a survival-blessing (the *cherev tichye* sword-life of v 40, the *tarid u-faraqta* eventual-throwing-off of v 40). The separative reading aligns the v 39 dew-and-fatness-clause with the v 40 sword-life clause: both clauses describe a **harsher, desert-warrior** existence, not a parallel-pastoralist-blessing. The partitive reading creates a structural-incoherence between v 39 (which would be a parallel-blessing) and v 40 (which is clearly a lesser-blessing).

**The WoH translation choice.** The WoH translation takes the **separative reading** as the main-text rendering: *behold, away from the fatness of the land shall be your dwelling, and away from the dew of the skies from above*. The decision is the modern-critical-scholarly default (Westermann, Speiser, Sarna-apparatus, Hamilton, Wenham, Alter, NRSV) and is grounded in three converging arguments: (i) the chapter-internal contrast with v 28; (ii) the Edomite-Seir-desert geographical-fit; (iii) the blessing's overall lesser-character that fits the v 40 sword-life sequel. The partitive reading (ASV/KJV/rabbinic-harmonizing) is preserved in apparatus and is the reading-of-record in the English-tradition. The WoH choice diverges from the ASV-baseline on this verse — the only chapter-27 verse where the WoH translation makes a significant divergence from the ASV.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation surfaces both readings in commentary, defends the separative reading on modern-critical-and-geographical-fit grounds, and preserves the partitive reading as the ASV/KJV/rabbinic-tradition alternative. The chapter's reception-history (particularly the medieval-Jewish rabbinic-harmonizing-tradition's partitive reading) is flagged-not-preempted. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the partitive-vs-separative dispute is mainstream modern-critical-scholarship and is editorially neutral on the Wheel of Heaven framing.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 192-193; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 443-445; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 212-213; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 227-228; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 142; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 212-213; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989); Knauf 'Edom' in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* II (1992) pp. 287-295; HALOT s.v. *min* (partitive vs. separative); BDB s.v. *min* p. 577; Waltke and O'Connor *An Introduction to Biblical Hebrew Syntax* 1990 sec. 11.2.11 (partitive-*min*) and sec. 11.2.5 (separative-*min*). Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 67:6; Rashi at Gen 27:39 (partitive-reading); Ramban at Gen 27:39 (partitive, with apparatus). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the partitive-vs-separative ambiguity is grammatically explicit (the same *min* functions in both registers); (b) the chapter-internal contrast with v 28 is HB-internal-explicit and verifiable by lexical-comparison; (c) the Edomite-Seir-desert geographical-fit is documentary (Bartlett 1989; Knauf 1992); (d) the modern-critical-consensus separative reading is documented (Westermann, Speiser, Sarna-apparatus, Hamilton, Wenham, Alter, NRSV); (e) the rabbinic-harmonizing partitive reading is documented (Rashi, Ramban, Gen Rab 67:6); (f) the chapter-internal narrative-mechanics (lesser-blessing structurally-required) is exegetical-narrative-critical analysis with broad scholarly support.

terms.literal: and upon your sword shall you live, and your brother shall you serve; and it shall be when you wander/break-loose, that you shall break his yoke from off your neck
terms.standardEnglish: And by thy sword shalt thou live, and thou shalt serve thy brother; And it shall come to pass, when thou shalt break loose, That thou shalt shake his yoke from off thy neck (ASV); ... when thou shalt have the dominion, that thou shalt break his yoke (KJV — *tarid* as *rule*); ... but when you grow restive, you shall break his yoke from your neck (NJPS); ... but when you break loose, you shall break his yoke from your neck (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: And by your sword shall you live, and your brother shall you serve; and it shall come to pass, when you break loose, that you shall break his yoke from off your neck (ASV-baseline; *tarid* as 'break loose' per the qal-form reading; the Edomite-tradition cross-corpus reach surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:40 contains the two key clauses of Esav's counter-blessing: (i) *v-al-charbekha tichye v-et-achikha ta'avod* — *by your sword shall you live, and your brother shall you serve* — the sword-life-and-service clause; (ii) *v-haya ka-asher tarid u-faraqta ulo me-al tzavarekha* — *and it shall come to pass, when you break loose, that you shall break his yoke from off your neck* — the eventual-throwing-off prophecy. The clauses together establish the chapter's Edomite-tradition setup: Esav-as-Edom will be a warrior-people, will serve Ya'aqov-as-Israel for a time, and will eventually throw off Israelite-vassalage.**

**The *v-al-charbekha tichye* sword-life clause.** The construction *al-charbekha tichye* (*upon your sword shall you live*) names a **sword-dependent existence** — life sustained by the warrior-and-raiding economy rather than by agricultural-pastoral-fertility. The phrase is editorially-marked: the preposition *al* (upon, by means of) with *cherev* (sword) names the sword as the **foundation** of life, not merely the instrument-of-occasional-use. The clause is the **structural-opposite** of Ya'aqov's *gevir l-achekha* (lord over your brothers) at v 29: Ya'aqov's blessing names political-dominion; Esav's counter-blessing names warrior-survival.

**The *v-et-achikha ta'avod* serve-your-brother clause.** The clause is the **inversion-correlate** of v 29's *heveh g'vir l-acheikha* (*be lord over your brothers*). The chapter's two halves form a tight dialectical-pair: v 29 names Ya'aqov as lord; v 40 names Esav as servant. The clause is the source-text for the subsequent HB-and-NT Israel-vs-Edom typological-pair (cf. Mal 1:2-5; Rom 9:13).

**The *tarid* lexical-problem.** The verb *tarid* (תָּרִיד) is the chapter's lexical-crux at v 40. The form is the qal-imperfect of an underlying root, but the root-identification is disputed. Three principal readings:

**(a) *Rud* — wander, be restless** (the dominant modern reading). The root *r-w-d* (wander, rove, be restless; cf. Hos 12:1 *Efraim ... saved im-El v-im-q'doshim ne'eman* and the related *rud* verb at Hos 12:1's parallel; Ps 55:3 *arid b-sichi* — *I am restless in my complaint*; Jer 2:31 *radnu eikh lo navo od elekha* — *we are restless / wander; we will not come to you again*). The qal-form *tarid* would be 2ms-impf of *rud*: *when you wander / become restless / break loose*. The reading is dominant in modern-critical commentary (Westermann, Sarna, Speiser, Hamilton, Wenham, Alter) and is preserved in ASV and NRSV (*break loose*). The reading reads Esav's eventual-throwing-off as a **restlessness-rebellion**: when Esav (and his descendant-line, the Edomites) grow restive under Israelite-vassalage, they will break free.

**(b) *Radah* — rule, have dominion** (the KJV-Targum reading). The root *r-d-h* (rule, have dominion; cf. Gen 1:26 *v-yirdu* — *and let them have dominion*; Num 24:19; Ps 49:15; Isa 14:6; the standard rulership-vocabulary). The reading *tarid* would be a defective-spelling of *tirdeh* (you shall rule). The reading is preserved in the KJV (*when thou shalt have the dominion*) and in Targum Onkelos (*ke-di tedari me-al tzavarekha nira* — *when you shall make him pass from off your neck*). The reading reads Esav's throwing-off as a **rulership-assumption**: when Esav (or his descendants) come to rule, the yoke will be broken. The reading is preserved by some medieval-Jewish exegetes (Rashbam at Gen 27:40 develops a *radah*-leaning interpretation).

**(c) *Yarad* — go down, descend** (a minority position). Some philological reconstructions read *tarid* as a hiphil-form of *yarad* (cause-to-descend) with a meaning *when you bring down* (the yoke). The reading is grammatically-difficult (the hiphil of *yarad* would more standardly produce *torid*, not *tarid*) and is rarely-adopted.

The **dominant modern reading is (a)** — *rud* — wander, be restless, break loose. The WoH translation follows (a) with the ASV-baseline rendering *break loose*. Reading (b) is preserved in apparatus.

**The *paraqta ulo me-al tzavarekha* throwing-off-the-yoke clause.** The construction *u-faraqta ulo me-al tzavarekha* uses the verb *paraq* (break-off, tear-apart; piel-form here) with *ol* (yoke) as object — the standard HB **throw-off-the-yoke** idiom for political-rebellion. The idiom recurs at:

- **Jer 2:20** — *ki me-olam shavart ulekh nitaqti mosrotaikh* — *for from of old you have broken your yoke and burst your bonds*. The Jeremianic-deployment.
- **Jer 5:5** — *ki hemmah yadu derekh YHWH mishpat Eloheihem akh hemmah yachdav shavru ol nitqu mosrot* — *for these have known the way of YHWH, the judgment of their Elohim; but these have all together broken the yoke, burst the bonds*. The leaders' rebellion.
- **Jer 28:2** — Hananiah-false-prophet: *shavarti et-ol melekh Bavel* — *I have broken the yoke of the king of Babel*. The political-rebellion-vocabulary.
- **Jer 30:8** — *eshbor ulo me-al tzavarekha* — *I will break his yoke from off your neck*. The Jeremianic restoration-oracle uses the **identical** construction to Gen 27:40 (*paraq* / *shavar* + *ol* + *me-al tzavar*). The construction is the standard HB throw-off-the-yoke idiom.
- **Nah 1:13** — the Ninevite-judgment: *v-atah eshbor motehu me-alaikh u-mosrotaikh anatteq* — *and now I will break his yoke from off you, and burst your bonds*.

The Gen 27:40 deployment is the **patriarchal-narrative source-text** of the throw-off-the-yoke idiom; the subsequent Jeremianic-prophetic-deployments draw on the patriarchal-precedent.

**The Edomite-tradition cross-corpus reach.** The clause's reception in subsequent HB-and-post-HB-tradition traces the **Edomite-throwing-off** as a fulfilled-prophecy:

- **2 Sam 8:14** — David subdues Edom: *va-yashem b-Edom n'tzivim b-khol Edom sam n'tzivim va-yhi khol-Edom avadim l-David* — *and he set garrisons in Edom; throughout all Edom he set garrisons, and all the Edomites became servants to David*. The Davidic-period subjugation: Edom is reduced to vassalage (cf. v 40's *v-et-achikha ta'avod*).
- **1 Kgs 11:14-22** — the Edomite-revolt under Solomon: *va-yaqem YHWH satan li-Shlomo et Hadad ha-Edomi mi-zera ha-melekh hu b-Edom* — *and YHWH stirred up an adversary to Shlomo, Hadad the Edomite; he was of the king's seed in Edom*. The first-stage of the Edomite-throwing-off.
- **2 Kgs 8:20-22** — the Edomite-revolt under Yehoram of Yehudah (mid-9th century BCE): *b-yamav pasha Edom mi-tachat yad-Yehudah va-yamlikhu aleihem melekh* — *in his days Edom revolted from under the hand of Yehudah, and made a king over themselves*. The full Edomite-throwing-off; the verse is widely-read as the **fulfillment** of Gen 27:40's prophecy.
- **2 Kgs 14:7** — Amazyah of Yehudah's reconquest of Edom (briefly recovered).
- **2 Kgs 16:6** — under Achaz, Edom is permanently lost: *ba-et ha-hi heshiv Retzin melekh-Aram et-Eilat la-Aram va-y-nashel et-ha-Yehudim me-Eilat va-Adomim* — *at that time Retzin king of Aram restored Eilat to Aram, and drove the Jews from Eilat, and the Edomites came to Eilat*.
- **Mal 1:2-5** — the post-exilic prophetic-oracle on Edom: *Ya'aqov ahavti v-et-Esav saneti va-asim et-harav sh'mamah v-et-nachalato l-tannot midbar* — *I loved Ya'aqov, but Esav I hated, and made his mountains a desolation, and his heritage a desolation for jackals*. The post-exilic Edom-destruction tradition.
- **Obadiah** — the entire book is a prophetic-oracle against Edom for its participation in Jerusalem's destruction (c. 586 BCE). The most-developed HB Edom-judgment-text.
- **Lam 4:21-22** — Edom-judgment in the Lamentations.
- **Ezek 25:12-14, 35:1-15** — Ezekielian Edom-oracles.
- **1 Macc 5:65** — Judah Maccabee's Edom-campaign in the 2nd century BCE.
- **Josephus *Antiquities* 13.257-258** — John Hyrcanus's forced-conversion of the Idumeans (the Hasmonean-period Edomites) in c. 125 BCE.

The cross-corpus reach establishes the **Edomite-tradition** as a foundational element of Israelite-history-and-prophecy. Gen 27:40 is the **patriarchal-narrative source-text** of the tradition.

**The Edomite-historical-archaeological record.** The Edomite-peoples are well-attested in the ANE archaeological-and-textual record. The Edomite kingdom is a real Iron Age polity south-east of Judah. **Bartlett** *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) is the standard monograph; **Knauf** 'Edom' in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* II (1992) pp. 287-295 develops the historical-context; **Beit-Arieh** *Tel Aroer* 2007 reports the Iron Age archaeology. The Edomite kingdom's existence is documentary: the Neo-Assyrian records (Salmaneser III, Tiglath-Pileser III, Esarhaddon, Ashurbanipal) list Edomite tribute; the Iron Age archaeology at Buseirah, Tel el-Kheleifeh, Umm el-Biyara, and Tawilan confirms the Edomite-cultural-presence.

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline rendering with *tarid* as *break loose*. The KJV/Targum *radah*-reading is preserved in apparatus. The cross-corpus Edomite-tradition (2 Sam 8, 1 Kgs 11, 2 Kgs 8, 2 Kgs 16, Mal 1, Obadiah, etc.) and the Bartlett/Knauf historical-archaeological evidence are documented in commentary. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the Edomite-tradition is mainstream modern-critical-attested.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 192-193; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 444-445; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 212-213; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 227-229; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 142; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 212-213; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989); Knauf 'Edom' in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* II (1992); Beit-Arieh *Tel Aroer* 2007; HALOT s.v. *cherev*, *rud*, *radah*, *paraq*, *ol*, *tzavar*; BDB s.v. *cherev* p. 352, *rud* p. 923, *radah* p. 921, *paraq* p. 830; Waltke and O'Connor *An Introduction to Biblical Hebrew Syntax* 1990. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 67:7; Rashi, Ramban, Rashbam at Gen 27:40; the rabbinic Esav-as-Rome typology in Lev Rab 13:5, Pirkei d'Rabbi Eliezer 38, Maharal *Netzach Yisrael*. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *al-charbekha tichye* construction is grammatically explicit; (b) the *paraqta ulo me-al tzavarekha* throw-off-the-yoke idiom is HB-internal-attested across Jer 2:20, 5:5, 28:2, 30:8, Nah 1:13; (c) the *tarid* lexical-problem and its three readings are documented in the standard commentaries and lexicons; (d) the cross-corpus Edomite-tradition is HB-internal-explicit across 2 Sam 8, 1 Kgs 11, 2 Kgs 8, 2 Kgs 16, Mal 1, Obadiah, etc.; (e) the Edomite-historical-archaeological record is documentary (Bartlett 1989, Knauf 1992, Beit-Arieh 2007); (f) the rabbinic Esav-as-Rome typology is documented in mainstream Jewish-tradition sources.

terms.literal: I am disgusted-of (my) life because-of the daughters of Chet; if Ya'aqov takes a woman from the daughters of Chet
terms.standardEnglish: I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth; if Jacob take a wife of the daughters of Heth (ASV/KJV); I am weary of my life because of the Hittite women (NRSV); I am disgusted with my life because of the Hittite women (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Het; if Ya'aqov takes a wife of the daughters of Het (ASV-baseline; *bnot Chet* per the existing central-glossary entries; the chapter-46-verse narrator-irony pretext-deployment surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 27:46 contains Rivkah's rhetorical-cover to Yitzchaq: she frames the proposed-sending of Ya'aqov to Lavan in Aram-Naharaim as motivated by her **weariness with the Hittite-wives complaint** of Gen 26:34-35, when the **real motivation** — Esav's death-threat at vv 41-45 — is known only to her and to Ya'aqov. The verse is editorially-load-bearing for the chapter's narrator-irony: the pretext is plausible (Yitzchaq has shared the Hittite-wife complaint per Gen 26:35) but is **not** the true motivation. The chapter ends on a dialogic-deception that mirrors the chapter's opening dialogic-deception (Ya'aqov's deception of Yitzchaq via the matamim-meal at vv 18-29 → Rivkah's deception of Yitzchaq via the Hittite-wives complaint at v 46).**

**The information-asymmetry pattern.** The chapter's narrator-craft is built on **multi-layered information-asymmetry**:

- **Yitzchaq knows** he wants to bless Esav before dying (v 4) but does not know that Rivkah and Ya'aqov are about to deceive him.
- **Rivkah knows** Yitzchaq's plan to bless Esav (v 5 — she eavesdrops) and immediately knows the deception-stratagem (vv 6-13).
- **Ya'aqov knows** the stratagem and executes it (vv 14-29).
- **Esav knows** nothing until v 30 (the arrival-too-late).
- **The reader knows** everything throughout — narrator-omniscience.

After the deception is discovered (vv 30-40), the information-asymmetry reorganizes:

- **Yitzchaq knows** he has been deceived and that Ya'aqov holds the blessing (vv 33-37).
- **Esav knows** the deception and the blessing-loss; he privately plans Ya'aqov's murder (v 41 — *va-yomer Esav b-libo* — *and Esav said in his heart*); Yitzchaq does not know this.
- **Rivkah knows** the murder-plan (the narrator says at v 42 *va-yugad l-Rivkah et-divrei Esav* — *and the words of Esav were told to Rivkah* — though Esav had spoken *b-libo* in his heart, the narrator allows Rivkah's information to circulate); she tells Ya'aqov (vv 42-45); Yitzchaq still does not know.
- **Ya'aqov knows** the murder-plan.
- **Yitzchaq does not know** the murder-plan.

The v 46 deployment is editorially-load-bearing: Rivkah cannot tell Yitzchaq the truth (the death-threat, the murder-plan) because (i) it would force Yitzchaq to confront Esav as an attempted-fratricide; (ii) it would expose Rivkah's own role in the deception that triggered the threat; (iii) it would set the family in open-conflict. Rivkah needs a **plausible alternative reason** for sending Ya'aqov away. The Hittite-wife complaint at Gen 26:34-35 supplies the cover: Yitzchaq already shares the concern (*morat ruach* — bitterness of spirit, per the existing entry); Rivkah can extend the concern into a marriage-related rationale for Ya'aqov's departure. The pretext is **plausible** because it is **partly-true** (the Hittite-wife complaint is genuine; what is **false** is that this is Rivkah's primary motivation).

**The narrator-craft: deception-bracketing the chapter.** The chapter is **structurally bracketed by deception**:

- **Opening deception** (vv 1-29) — Ya'aqov deceives Yitzchaq via the matamim-meal disguise.
- **Closing deception** (v 46) — Rivkah deceives Yitzchaq via the Hittite-wives-pretext.

The two deceptions are editorially-paired. The chapter's narrative-structure encloses the central dramatic-action (the blessing-transfer) within an opening-and-closing-deception frame. The frame's structural-effect is to **mark the entire chapter** as a sustained-deception-narrative — the patriarchal-blessing-transfer is **only possible** through a chain of dialogic-deceptions. The narrator's editorial-judgement on the chapter's events is not explicit (no narrator-verdict is given; the events are simply reported); but the structural-frame **performs** the judgement by making the deception the chapter's organizing-principle.

**Alter and Sternberg on the narrator-irony.** The verse is widely-discussed in literary-critical commentary on the HB narrator's craft. **Alter** *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 6 (on the Genesis narrator's information-asymmetry) reads v 46 as paradigm-instance of the narrator's **multi-layered dialogic-pragmatics**: the narrator allows the reader to track what each character knows and to perceive the gap between Rivkah's stated-motivation and her actual-motivation. The reader is **superior** to Yitzchaq in this scene — we know what Yitzchaq doesn't — and the narrator builds dramatic-irony at Yitzchaq's expense. **Sternberg** *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative* 1985 ch. 6 develops a parallel reading: the verse is a paradigm-instance of HB **strategic-discourse** in which dialogue-content and dialogue-motivation are deliberately-mismatched, with the narrator using the mismatch to characterize speakers and to advance the plot indirectly. **Fokkelman** *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 chs. 4-5 reads the chapter's narrator-craft as a sustained-meditation on speech-as-deception.

**The *qatzti b-chayyai* construction.** The construction *qatzti b-chayyai* (literally: *I have been disgusted in my life*) uses the qal-perfect 1cs of *qutz* (קוץ — feel disgust, loathe) with the locative *b-chayyai* (in my life). The construction is grammatically uncommon; the only close HB-parallel is **Num 21:5** — *qatzah nafshenu b-lechem ha-q'loqel* — *our nefesh has been disgusted with the wretched bread* — the Wilderness-complaint about the manna. The grammatical-affinity is editorially-suggestive: Rivkah's complaint at v 46 echoes the Israelite-wilderness-complaint-vocabulary — a connection that may signal the narrator's quiet-irony, casting Rivkah's complaint as overdramatic or as a calculated-rhetorical-flourish (paralleling the Israelite-wilderness-complainers' rhetorical-flourishes).

**The Gen 26:34-35 / Gen 27:46 narrative-link.** The verse is **direct dialogic-quotation** of the Gen 26:35 narrator's notice (covered by the existing `morat-ruach-bitterness-of-spirit-esau-hittite-wives` entry). The narrator's *morat ruach* (the wives' effect on Yitzchaq and Rivkah) is reframed by Rivkah at v 46 as her own *qatzti b-chayyai* (I am disgusted with my life). The continuity is editorial-structural: the chapter-26 closing verses are not vestigial; they are the deliberate set-up for chapter 27's mother-and-son stratagem and for chapter 27's mother-husband pretext-dialogue at v 46.

**The chapter-28 bridge.** The v 46 pretext sets up the immediately-following Gen 28:1-5: Yitzchaq, accepting the pretext, **calls Ya'aqov, blesses him again** (this time deliberately and in full knowledge — the **non-deceived** blessing at Gen 28:3-4 invokes the Avrahamic-Sarah-Yitzchaq-line covenant promises), and **commands him** to go to Lavan in Aram-Naharaim for a wife. The chapter-28 opening is the **structural-completion** of the chapter-27 closing: the pretext is accepted, the journey-to-Lavan is initiated, the marriage-rationale supplies the explicit-narrative-purpose for the Lavan-cycle (Gen 28-31). The narrator's craft is editorially-tight: the chapter-27 closing verse seamlessly threads into the chapter-28 opening, and the chapter-divide is editorially-arbitrary (some modern critical readings treat Gen 27:46 + 28:1-9 as a single editorial-unit, perhaps from the P-source supplementing the J-or-E-source narrative of Gen 27).

**The lens-quiet rendering.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline rendering. The narrator-irony pretext-deployment and the v 46 / chapter-28 bridge are documented in commentary. The literary-critical readings (Alter, Sternberg, Fokkelman) are surfaced. The WoH lens does not surface at this verse; the narrator-craft is mainstream modern-critical-attested.

**The *inferred* claim_type.** The claim_type for this entry is **inferred** rather than direct, because:

- The Hittite-wives-as-pretext reading is a **narrative-critical inference** rather than a grammatical-explicit fact. The HB text does not say *Rivkah used the Hittite-wives complaint as a cover for the real reason*; the reader is left to **infer** the pretext-deployment from the chapter's information-asymmetry-structure.
- The inference is well-supported (Alter, Sternberg, Fokkelman, Sarna all develop it) and is the modern-critical-consensus reading.
- But the inference is not direct: a reader unfamiliar with the chapter's narrator-craft and information-asymmetry-mechanics could plausibly read v 46 as Rivkah's **sincere** complaint, with the Hittite-wives concern being her **actual** primary-motivation. The narrative-critical reading rejects this surface-reading but acknowledges it as grammatically-possible.

The claim_type **inferred** records the narrative-critical character of the reading and flags it for the Reviewer's attention.
