**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 195; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 446-447; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 213-214; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 229-231; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 143 and *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 6 (on the narrator's information-asymmetry); Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 211-213; Sternberg *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative* 1985 ch. 6 (on strategic-discourse); Fokkelman *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 chs. 4-5; Friedman *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 (on the P-source supplementation reading); HALOT s.v. *qutz*, *chai*; BDB s.v. *qutz* p. 880. Rabbinic-tradition sources: Genesis Rabbah 67:13; Rashi at Gen 27:46. // The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the pretext-deployment is a narrative-critical inference from the chapter's information-asymmetry-structure, not a grammatically-explicit text-feature; (b) the inference is well-supported by mainstream modern-critical commentary (Alter, Sternberg, Fokkelman, Sarna); (c) the surface-reading (Rivkah's sincere Hittite-wives complaint) is grammatically-possible though narratively-naive; (d) the chapter-28 bridge confirms the pretext's narrative-function (Yitzchaq accepts the cover and sends Ya'aqov to Lavan for the stated-marriage-reason); (e) the Gen 26:34-35 / Gen 27:46 dialogic-quotation back-link is HB-internal-explicit.

terms.literal: assembly of peoples
terms.standardEnglish: company of peoples (ASV/KJV); assembly of peoples (NRSV); community of peoples (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: assembly of peoples
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:3 is the first patriarchal occurrence of the *q'hal amim* (assembly of peoples) construct in the multiplication-blessing formula.** Yitzchaq's commissioning of Ya'aqov *v-hayita li-q'hal amim* — *that you may become an assembly of peoples* — introduces a distinctive lexeme into the patriarchal blessing-tradition. The construct *q'hal X* (assembly of X) recurs precisely twice more in the patriarchal corpus: **Gen 35:11** (the deity to Ya'aqov on his return to Bethel: *goy u-q'hal goyim yihyeh mimekha* — *a nation and an assembly of nations shall come from you*); **Gen 48:4** (Ya'aqov to Yosef, recalling the El-Shaddai blessing at Luz: *u-netatikha li-q'hal amim* — *and I will make you an assembly of peoples*). The three verses form a closed cross-corpus cluster — Gen 28:3 (Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov), Gen 35:11 (deity → Ya'aqov), Gen 48:4 (Ya'aqov → Yosef) — bound together by the El-Shaddai divine-name (see [el-shaddai](#el-shaddai)) and by the *q'hal* lexeme.

**The *q'hal* lexeme.** *Q'hal* (קְהָל) is the standard HB noun for **assembly, convocation, congregation**, with a strong cultic register. The lexeme is concentrated in legal-cultic contexts: **Num 16:3** (Korah and company: *kol ha-edah kullam q'doshim u-v-tokham YHWH u-madua titnasse'u al q'hal YHWH* — *all the congregation are holy, every one of them, and YHWH is among them; why do you lift yourselves up above the assembly of YHWH?*); **Num 20:4** (the people's complaint: *v-lamah haveitem et q'hal YHWH el-ha-midbar ha-zeh*); **Deut 23:2-9** (the exclusion-laws: *lo yavo... bi-q'hal YHWH* — *shall not enter the assembly of YHWH*; the legal-cultic register of *q'hal* as the worshipping-community of Israel); **1 Chr 28:8** (David to the assembly: *l-einei kol Yisrael q'hal YHWH*); **Neh 13:1** (the post-exilic exclusion: *lo yavo... bi-q'hal ha-Elohim*). The construct also recurs in political-administrative contexts (**Exod 12:6** the Passover-night assembly; **Lev 16:17** the Day of Atonement) and in eschatological-restoration contexts (**Jer 31:8** *q'hal gadol yashuvu hennah*).

**The patriarchal cluster's distinctive register.** Importing the cultic-assembly lexeme *q'hal* into the patriarchal multiplication-blessing is editorially distinctive: the patriarch's progeny will not merely be numerous but will constitute a **worship-community / cultic-congregation**. The choice contrasts with the alternative multiplication-vocabularies (*goy gadol* — *great nation* — Gen 12:2; the *afar-ha-aretz* / *kokhavim* / *chol* triad; *zera rav* — *much seed*; *me'od me'od* — *exceedingly*). The *q'hal*-lexeme adds the cultic-community dimension to the demographic-multiplication promise, marking the blessing as covenantal-religious rather than merely genealogical. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 196 reads the lexeme as importing the *qehillah*-as-cultic-assembly register and as anticipating the later Israelite worship-community. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 448-449 reads the lexeme as a P-or-late-P editorial signature linking the patriarchal blessing-tradition to the later Israelite cultic-legal language.

**The Gen 35:11 *q'hal goyim* variant.** Gen 35:11 has *q'hal goyim* (assembly of nations), where Gen 28:3 and 48:4 have *q'hal amim* (assembly of peoples). The variation is editorially significant. The HB consistently uses *am* for **the people Israel** (the Sinai-people, the covenant-people) and *goy* for **the foreign-nations** (or for Israel only when read from an outsider's perspective, e.g., *goy qadosh* at Exod 19:6). The *q'hal amim* variant of Gen 28:3 and 48:4 frames the multiplication-promise in terms of multiple *peoples* — plural rather than singular *am* — anticipating the twelve-tribe development. The *q'hal goyim* variant of Gen 35:11 frames the promise in terms of *nations* — suggesting either (a) a wider ethnic-political reach beyond the twelve tribes, possibly including the Edomite and Ishmaelite lineages, or (b) a P-source editorial convention distinct from the J/E variant. The variation is preserved in the WoH translation (*peoples* at 28:3, *nations* at 35:11).

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *assembly of peoples* — adopting the NRSV-adjacent rendering — preserving the cultic-assembly register against the looser ASV/KJV *company of peoples* (which loses the cultic register entirely) and the NJPS *community of peoples* (which preserves community-of but flattens the worshipping-congregation register). The choice is editorially neutral on the lens; the *q'hal*-as-cultic-assembly reading is mainstream modern-critical-consensus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 196-197; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 448-450; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 215-216; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 233-235; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 215-216; HALOT s.v. *qahal*; BDB s.v. *qahal* p. 874; Milgrom *Leviticus 1-16* AB 1991 (on the *q'hal*-as-cultic-assembly legal register). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construct *q'hal amim* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus cluster (Gen 28:3 / 35:11 / 48:4) is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *q'hal* cultic-register and the *q'hal*-vs-*goy* / *am* lexical distribution are documentary; (d) the modern critical reading of the lexeme is mainstream consensus.

terms.literal: blessing of Avraham
terms.standardEnglish: blessing of Abraham (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: blessing of Avraham
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:4 is the first verse in the patriarchal corpus to name the Avrahamic-promise-cluster as a discrete, reified, bequeathable object: *birkat Avraham* — the blessing of Avraham — that Yitzchaq transmits to Ya'aqov.** Prior to this verse, the *berakhah*-of-Avraham has been narrated as a sequence of YHWH-to-Avraham speech-acts (Gen 12:1-3 — the *land + great-nation + universal-blessing* triad; Gen 15 — the *brit-bein-ha-betarim* covenant; Gen 17 — the *brit-olam* / circumcision covenant; Gen 22:17-18 — the *aqedah* covenant-confirmation). Gen 28:4 lexicalizes the cluster into a single fixed-phrase that names the blessing as an inheritable property — a *berakhah* the patriarch can transmit. The lexicalization is editorially load-bearing for the subsequent corpus: from Gen 28 forward, the *birkat Avraham* functions as the patriarchal-line's signature inheritance.

**The lexicalization.** The construct *birkat Avraham* uses *birkah* (the construct-form of *berakhah*) with the proper-name *Avraham*. The form is grammatically straightforward but semantically significant: it treats the blessing as a **transferable possession** rather than as a series of speech-acts. The verb *natan* (give) at v 4 (*v-yiten l-kha et-birkat Avraham*) confirms the **gift-transmission** semantic: Yitzchaq does not *bless* Ya'aqov with the Avrahamic-promises; he *gives* him *the Avrahamic blessing* as a discrete bequeathable entity. The construction parallels the *berakhah*-as-portable-object pattern of Gen 27 (the chapter's central irony: the blessing is in some sense an object that can be conferred only once — see the existing `ha-qol-qol-yaakov-deception-formula` entry's commentary on the speech-act framework).

**The cluster's content.** The verse names two components of the *birkat Avraham*: (a) *l-kha u-l-zarekha itakh* — *to you and to your seed with you* — the seed-promise component; (b) *l-rishtekha et-eretz megurekha asher natan Elohim l-Avraham* — *that you may possess the land of your sojournings, which Elohim gave to Avraham* — the land-promise component. The two-component reduction maps onto the Avrahamic promise-cluster covered by the existing `zera-eretz-promise` entry. The *birkat Avraham* at Gen 28:4 is thus the Avrahamic-promise-cluster's first **lexicalized name**.

**The cross-corpus *birkat Avraham* deployment.** The fixed-phrase *birkat Avraham* occurs only here in the canonical HB — a single named-occurrence. But the **concept** recurs as the patriarchal-line's signature inheritance: the *berakhah* is renewed to Ya'aqov at the Bethel ladder-vision (Gen 28:13-14 — *anokhi YHWH Elohei Avraham avikha v-Elohei Yitzchaq* — see the [anokhi-elohei-X-avikha-ancestral-god-formula](#anokhi-elohei-X-avikha-ancestral-god-formula) entry); reconfirmed at Bethel-return (Gen 35:11-12); transmitted by Ya'aqov to Yosef (Gen 48:3-4) and to Yosef's sons (Gen 48:15-16, 20); and recapitulated across the Deuteronomic-paranetic-cluster (Deut 1:11; 6:23; etc.) as the *brit Avraham* / *shvuah l-Avraham* (the Avraham-covenant / the oath to Avraham) — the canonical Deuteronomic name for what Gen 28:4 first lexicalized as *birkat Avraham*.

**Pauline reception (flagged-not-preempted).** **Gal 3:14** — *hē eulogia tou Abraam* — *the blessing of Abraham* — explicitly cites the Genesis lexicalization in arguing for the gospel-to-the-gentiles extension. Paul reads the *birkat Avraham* as the textual ground for the universal-blessing-promise (Gen 12:3 / 18:18 / 22:18 / 26:4 / 28:14 *v-nivrakhu vekha kol mishpechot ha-adamah* — see [nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael](#nivrakhu-bekha-niphal-hithpael)) and for the inheritance of the blessing by *those of faith* (*hoi ek pisteōs*) rather than by ethnic-Jewish descent alone. The Pauline reception is post-biblical and downstream; the Genesis lexicalization is direct and is not preempted by Paul's reading.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *the blessing of Avraham* — ASV-baseline with the WoH transliteration of the proper-name. The choice is editorially neutral; the *birkat Avraham*-as-reified-inheritance reading is mainstream modern-critical-consensus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 197; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 449-450; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 215-216; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 234-236; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 147-148; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 215-216; Mitchell *The Meaning of BRK To Bless in the Old Testament* 1987 (on the *berakhah*-as-transferable-object semantic); de Pury *Promise to the Patriarchs* in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* V 1992. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construct *birkat Avraham* is grammatically explicit; (b) the lexicalization-as-reification is a documentary feature of the verse; (c) the cross-corpus *brit Avraham* / *shvuah l-Avraham* developments are HB-internal-explicit; (d) the Pauline reception at Gal 3:14 is documentary and is flagged-not-preempted.

terms.literal: and he encountered / lighted upon / met at the place
terms.standardEnglish: and he lighted upon the place (ASV/KJV); he came to a certain place (NRSV); he came upon a certain place (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and he lighted upon the place
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:11 deploys *va-yifga ba-maqom* — *and he lighted upon / encountered the place* — in a triple-articulated definite-article framework (the verse contains *ba-maqom*, *ha-maqom*, *ba-maqom ha-hu* — three definite-articled *maqom* references in a single verse).** The construction is editorially load-bearing on two grounds: (i) the verb *paga* is semantically ambiguous between accidental-encounter and intentional-meeting; (ii) the definite-articled *ha-maqom* is recognized in modern critical scholarship as a **technical cultic-site designator** across the patriarchal narratives.

**The verb *paga* (פָּגַע).** The lexical range spans (a) **neutral encounter** — *meet, come upon, light upon* (Gen 32:2 *va-yifge'u vo mal'akhei Elohim* — *messengers of Elohim met him*; Exod 5:20 *va-yifge'u et-Moshe v-et-Aharon* — *they met Moses and Aaron*); (b) **intensive encounter** — *fall upon, attack* (Josh 2:16 *pen yifg'u vakhem ha-rod'fim* — *lest the pursuers fall upon you*; Judg 8:21 *qum atah u-fga banu* — *rise up and fall upon us*; 1 Sam 22:17, 22:18 — Saul's command to *paga* the priests of Nob); (c) **intercessory encounter** — *intercede, plead* (Isa 53:6 *YHWH hifgi'a bo et avon kullanu*; 53:12 *l-poshim yafgi'a* — *he interceded for the transgressors*; Job 21:15 *u-mah yo'il ki nifga bo* — *what profit have we if we pray to him?*). The chapter's verb is genuinely ambiguous between (a) and the rabbinic-tradition reading at (c).

**Three readings of *va-yifga ba-maqom* circulate.**

*(i) Accidental encounter.* Ya'aqov *happened upon* the place by chance, traveling at sunset, in the absence of premeditated cultic intention. The reading is the modern critical default — Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 453-454 — and is supported by the verse's narrative-circumstance (*ki va ha-shemesh* — *for the sun had set*; the lodging at the place is opportunistic). ASV's *lighted upon* preserves this register.

*(ii) Deliberate cultic arrival.* The rabbinic-tradition reading: the *maqom* leapt out to meet Ya'aqov — Genesis Rabbah 68:9; Rashi at Gen 28:11 — drawing on the *paga*-as-intercede sense and reading the verb as *the place encountered him*, an active divine-providential arrangement. The reading is downstream of the lexical range; not the modern critical default.

*(iii) The *ha-maqom* as already-recognized cultic site.* The reading does not contest the verb but draws attention to the **definite article**. Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198 and Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218 read the definite-articled *ha-maqom* as a **technical cultic-site designator** that recurs across the patriarchal narratives at Gen 12:6 (Shechem); 22:3-4, 22:14 (Mount Moriah); 28:11, 16-17, 19 (Bethel); 35:7, 14-15 (Bethel return). The article signals that the site was already a *maqom* — an existing sacred-site — before Ya'aqov arrived; what was new was Ya'aqov's discovery (v 16: *anokhi lo yadati* — *I did not know*) of what the site already was. The reading sits comfortably alongside (i): the encounter is accidental from Ya'aqov's side, but the place is not arbitrary.

**The triple *maqom* of v 11.** The verse uses *maqom* three times in 15 Hebrew words: *va-yifga ba-maqom va-yalen sham ki va ha-shemesh va-yiqqach me-avnei ha-maqom va-yasem mer'ashotav va-yishkav ba-maqom ha-hu*. The triple-articulation is editorially deliberate. The *maqom*-lexeme will recur again at v 16 (*akhen yesh YHWH ba-maqom ha-zeh*); v 17 (*mah norah ha-maqom ha-zeh*); v 19 (*va-yiqra et-shem ha-maqom*). The chapter contains seven occurrences of *maqom*, all definite-articled — the densest *maqom*-concentration in the patriarchal corpus.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *lighted upon the place* — ASV-baseline preserving the accidental-encounter register, with the definite-article preserved (against the NRSV/NJPS *came upon a certain place*, which reads the definite article as indefinite). The dispute is documented in commentary.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 453-454; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 221; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 240-241; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 148; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218; HALOT s.v. *paga*, *maqom*; BDB s.v. *paga* p. 803. Rabbinic-tradition: Genesis Rabbah 68:9; Rashi at Gen 28:11; b. Chullin 91b. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the verb *paga* and its lexical range are documentary; (b) the definite-articled *ha-maqom* is grammatically explicit; (c) the cultic-site-designator pattern across the patriarchal corpus is documentary and is documented in the cited scholarly literature; (d) the rabbinic-tradition reading is documented and not preempted; (e) the WoH translation choice (the modern critical default) is one of the two mainstream positions.

terms.literal: stairway / ladder / ramp
terms.standardEnglish: ladder (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS; LXX *klimax*; Vulgate *scala*)
terms.wohChoice: stairway (modern critical preference per Sarna, Speiser, Hamilton, Houtman 1977; the *simmiltu ša šamê* / ziggurat-architecture intertext load-bearing for the chapter)
terms.rationale:
***Sulam* (סֻלָּם) is the chapter's keystone noun — the object Ya'aqov sees in the dream at v 12 — and is a hapax legomenon: the word occurs nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible.** The translation choice is genuinely contested in modern critical scholarship and has substantial editorial-weight for the chapter's architectural-cosmological framing.

**Three lexical possibilities stand.**

*(a) Ladder.* The conventional rendering across the LXX (*klimax*), Vulgate (*scala*), ASV, KJV, NRSV, NJPS. The reading assumes a cognate to the Akkadian *simmiltu* via either a metathesis (*s-l-m* ↔ *s-m-l*) or an unattested intermediate form. The *ladder* is the rendering everyone recognizes; it has dominated English biblical translation from the LXX forward.

*(b) Stairway / staircase / ramp.* The modern critical preference, developed extensively in **Cornelis Houtman**'s *VT* article 'What did Jacob see in his dream at Bethel? Some remarks on Genesis xxviii 10-22' (*Vetus Testamentum* 27, 1977, pp. 337-351) and adopted by **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 240-241; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 454-455. The argument runs as follows. *Sulam* derives from the root *s-l-l* (סָלַל) — *cast up, build a way, heap up* — the root behind *m'silah* (highway, raised road; cf. Num 20:19; Isa 11:16; 19:23) and *solelah* (siege-mound; 2 Sam 20:15). The root's semantic core is **raised earthen/stone construction**, not rungs-on-uprights. The Akkadian cognate *simmiltu* — well-attested in Mesopotamian literary and architectural texts — denotes the **ziggurat staircase**: the great processional ramp ascending the stepped-temple-tower (the *e-temen-an-ki* at Babylon, the *e-kur* at Nippur, etc.). The Nergal-and-Ereshkigal myth (Standard Babylonian version) contains the fixed phrase *simmiltu ša šamê* — *the stairway of the heavens* — referring to a divine-mediation-architecture connecting the upper-world and the underworld (see ANET pp. 103-104; Heidel *The Babylonian Genesis* 1951). On the *stairway* reading, the *sulam* is not a ladder but a **ziggurat-style ramped staircase** — a stepped architectural form set on the earth with its top reaching the heavens.

*(c) Mound / pile / heap.* The minority reading developed by **C. Cohen** *JANES* 9 (1978) and a few subsequent treatments. On this reading the *sulam* is a raised earthen mound — not a staircase but a primitive ziggurat-platform. The reading is editorially marginal and is not adopted in the major commentaries.

**The chapter's wider Akkadian-architectural vocabulary.** The stairway-reading is reinforced by two further lexemes in the immediate context.

**(i) *Rosho magia ha-shamayma*** — *its top reaches the heavens* (v 12). The phrase is a near-direct verbal echo of the Babel-builders' *u-rosho ba-shamayim* (Gen 11:4) — *and its top in the heavens*. The chapter is **deliberately re-deploying** the Babel-vocabulary: where the Babel-builders attempted to build a *migdal* (tower) whose top reaches the heavens and were dispersed for the attempt, Ya'aqov **dreams** of a *sulam* whose top reaches the heavens and is **blessed** at the dream-site. The intertext is editorially load-bearing: the chapter reframes the Babel-failure into the patriarchal-blessing.

**(ii) *Sha'ar ha-shamayim*** — *gate of the heavens* (v 17). The compound is functionally identical to the Akkadian *bab-ili* (*gate of god*), from which the city-name *Babylon* derives — the same name the HB at Gen 11 polemically reads as *Babel* (from *b-l-l*, *confuse*). See the [beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula](#beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula) entry. The chapter's *sha'ar ha-shamayim* recapitulates the Akkadian *bab-ili* without the Gen 11 polemical-reading: the patriarch *discovers* the gate-of-heaven that the Babel-builders failed to build.

**The combined intertext.** Three elements together — the *sulam* as *simmiltu*-ziggurat-staircase, the *rosho magia ha-shamayma* as Babel-echo, the *sha'ar ha-shamayim* as *bab-ili* — make the Akkadian-ziggurat-architecture the chapter's most editorially-distinctive intertextual layer. The traditional *ladder* rendering loses all three connections; the *stairway* rendering preserves them.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *stairway* — following the modern critical consensus (Sarna, Speiser, Hamilton, Houtman, Westermann). The departure from ASV's *ladder* is editorially-significant and is flagged in the editor's escalation report. The traditional *ladder* is preserved in apparatus.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Houtman 'What did Jacob see in his dream at Bethel? Some remarks on Genesis xxviii 10-22' *VT* 27 (1977) pp. 337-351 (the foundational modern critical treatment); Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 240-241; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 454-455; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 221-222; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 148-149 (preserves *ladder* but documents the staircase argument); Heidel *The Babylonian Genesis* 1951 (on the *simmiltu ša šamê* cluster); ANET pp. 103-104 (Nergal-Ereshkigal); Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1 (on the El-Bethel cult-site); Cohen *JANES* 9 (1978) (the minority mound-reading); HALOT s.v. *sulam*; BDB s.v. *sulam* p. 700. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the hapax-status of *sulam* is verifiable by lexical search; (b) the etymological derivation from *s-l-l* is documentary; (c) the Akkadian *simmiltu* and *simmiltu ša šamê* cognates are documentary; (d) the modern critical preference for the *stairway* reading is documented in the named scholars; (e) the Gen 11 Babel-echo and the *bab-ili* / *sha'ar ha-shamayim* parallel are HB-internal-explicit and ANE-documentary. Project-specific synthesis (e.g., the WoH lens on the *sulam* as physical-architectural-platform) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.strongs: H4397 (mal'akh) + H430 (Elohim) + H5927 (alah — go up) + H3381 (yarad — go down)
terms.literal: messengers of Elohim ascending and descending on it
terms.standardEnglish: the angels of God ascending and descending on it (ASV/KJV); the angels of God were ascending and descending on it (NRSV); angels of God going up and down on it (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: the messengers of Elohim ascending and descending on it
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:12 deploys *mal'akhei Elohim olim v-yordim bo* — *messengers of Elohim ascending and descending on it* — the patriarchal corpus's only plural-messenger ascending-descending image and the chapter's most editorially-distinctive theophany-element.** The construction differs from the singular *mal'akh Elohim* (Gen 21:17 — the Hagar-encounter) and *mal'akh YHWH* (Gen 16; Gen 22) of prior patriarchal narratives. The plural-messenger image is unique to this verse in Genesis.

**The plural construct.** *Mal'akhei Elohim* (the construct-plural of *mal'akh* governing *Elohim*) is grammatically straightforward. The translation preserves *messengers of Elohim* — consistent with the WoH practice on *mal'akh* (see the existing [malakh-elohim-messenger](#malakh-elohim-messenger) entry) and on *Elohim* (the entry's transliteration policy). The standard rendering *angels of God* (ASV/KJV/NRSV) imports later angelology and collapses *Elohim* into the generic *God*; the WoH choice preserves both the messenger-etymology of *mal'akh* and the *Elohim*-transliteration.

**The ascending-before-descending word-order.** The participles *olim v-yordim* (ascending and descending) are editorially significant in their **word-order**: **ascending before descending**, against the natural expectation that messengers *from the heavens* would descend first to the earth and only afterward ascend. The order has been the subject of substantial reception-history attention.

- **Rabbinic reception.** Genesis Rabbah 68:12 and the Targumic traditions develop the word-order extensively. One rabbinic reading: the messengers were the *malakhei eretz Yisrael* (messengers-of-the-land-of-Israel) and the *malakhei chutz la-aretz* (messengers-of-outside-the-land); the ascending ones were the Israel-messengers leaving as Ya'aqov departs the land, and the descending ones were the diaspora-messengers arriving as Ya'aqov enters the foreign territory. Another reading (b. Chullin 91b): the messengers had been with Ya'aqov from his birth and were ascending to return-and-report; descending messengers were the next-shift arriving.

- **NT reception (flagged-not-preempted).** **John 1:51** — Jesus to Nathanael: *amēn amēn legō hymin, opsesthe ton ouranon aneōigota kai tous angelous tou theou anabainontas kai katabainontas epi ton huion tou anthrōpou* — *truly, truly, I say to you, you shall see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending upon the Son of Man*. The verse explicitly echoes Gen 28:12 with the *anabainontas kai katabainontas* (ascending and descending) preserved in order. The NT reception relocates the *sulam* / cosmic-mediation-locus from the Bethel stone-pillar to the Son-of-Man Christology. The reading is post-biblical Christian exegesis; the Genesis verse it draws on is HB-direct.

- **Modern critical reception.** Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 455-456 reads the plural-messenger image as the **E-document's broader immanence-mediation pattern**: where the J-document tends to narrate direct divine-appearances (Gen 16; Gen 18), the E-document tends to mediate divine-immanence through *mal'akh*-figures, dreams, and visions (Gen 20:3 — dream-mediation; Gen 21:17 — *mal'akh Elohim* called-from-heaven; Gen 28:12 — the *sulam*-ladder-or-stairway with the *mal'akhei Elohim*; Gen 31:11 — the *mal'akh ha-Elohim* in Ya'aqov's dream; Gen 46:2 — *Elohim said to Israel in visions of the night*). Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198 reads the ascending-descending pattern as a cosmographic image — the *mal'akhim* circulating between the earthly *sha'ar ha-shamayim* and the upper-heavens. Hamori 2008 *When Gods Were Men* treats the *mal'akhei Elohim* as a distinct class-register from the *mal'akh YHWH*, with the plural-form signaling a divine-court-functionary set rather than the single-envoy.

**The ANE cosmographic background.** The *sulam* + *mal'akhei Elohim olim v-yordim* image has well-attested ANE parallels. The Mesopotamian *simmiltu ša šamê* (stairway of the heavens) and the Egyptian *aakhet* (horizon-gateway) both function as cosmic-mediation structures with attendant divine-functionary movement. The patriarchal-narrative-image stands within the broader ANE cosmographic tradition (see Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1; Smith *The Early History of God* 1990/2002).

**The claim_type discussion.** This entry is `claim_type: direct` because:

- the plural construct *mal'akhei Elohim* is grammatically explicit;
- the ascending-descending word-order is grammatically explicit;
- the patriarchal-corpus uniqueness of the plural-messenger-image is verifiable by lexical search;
- the rabbinic and NT receptions are documentary and flagged-not-preempted;
- the E-document immanence-mediation pattern is mainstream modern critical reading.

A case could be made for `claim_type: inferred` if the entry made a synthetic claim about what the messengers *are* (e.g., a project-specific ontological reading). The entry makes no such claim. The lens-distinctive reading — the messengers as physical attendants of a physical cosmic-mediation-architecture — is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted here.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *the messengers of Elohim ascending and descending on it* — preserving the lexeme family, the *Elohim* transliteration, and the participle word-order.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 455-456; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 198; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 222-223; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 241-242; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 149; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218; Hamori *When Gods Were Men: The Embodied God in Biblical and Near Eastern Literature* 2008; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1; Smith *The Early History of God* 1990/2002; Brown *The Gospel According to John I-XII* AB 1966 (on John 1:51). Rabbinic-tradition: Genesis Rabbah 68:12; b. Chullin 91b; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the plural construct and the participle word-order are grammatically explicit; (b) the patriarchal-uniqueness of the plural-messenger image is verifiable; (c) the rabbinic and NT receptions are documentary; (d) the E-document immanence-mediation pattern is mainstream consensus.

terms.strongs: H2009 (hineh) + H3068 (YHWH) + H5324 (natzav — niphal participle, be stationed) + H5921 (al — upon, beside) + 3ms suffix
terms.literal: and behold, YHWH (was) stationed upon-it / beside-him
terms.standardEnglish: and behold, YHWH stood above it (ASV/KJV; the *sulam*-top reading); behold, YHWH stood beside him (NRSV/NJPS; the Ya'aqov-side reading)
terms.wohChoice: and behold, YHWH stood above it (ASV-baseline; the *sulam*-top reading preserves the v 17 *sha'ar ha-shamayim* architecture; the dispute documented in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:13's *v-hineh YHWH nitzav alav* contains a genuinely ambiguous preposition-with-suffix construction.** The preposition *al* with 3ms suffix (*alav*) ranges across the HB from *upon, above* (with non-personal referent) to *beside, near* (with personal referent). The referent of *alav* in the verse is **grammatically undetermined**: it could be the *sulam* of v 12 (yielding *YHWH stood above the stairway*) or Ya'aqov himself (yielding *YHWH stood beside Ya'aqov*).

**Three readings circulate.**

*(a) Above it — referent = the *sulam*.* The deity stands at the top of the stairway, consistent with the v 17 *sha'ar ha-shamayim* (gate of the heavens) architecture and with the *sulam*-as-cosmic-mediation-structure reading (see [sulam-stairway-or-ladder](#sulam-stairway-or-ladder)). The reading is supported by: **LXX** *epestēriktō ep' autēs* (was supported upon it — clearly the stairway-referent); **Targum Onkelos** *uvmaymra d-YHWH m'attad iluhi* (YHWH stationed upon it — the stairway-referent); **Rashi** *YHWH nitzav alav... al ha-sulam* (YHWH stationed upon it... upon the *sulam*); ASV; KJV; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 199 (notes the ambiguity but prefers *above it* for the architecture). The reading preserves the verse's cosmographic-architecture: YHWH at the gate-of-heavens at the top of the stairway.

*(b) Beside him — referent = Ya'aqov.* The deity stands beside the sleeping patriarch — the unambiguous *imakh* (with you) of v 15 supports this reading by locating the divine-presence directly adjacent to Ya'aqov rather than at remote-height. The *al* with personal-referent reading is well-attested elsewhere (Gen 18:2 — *va-yare v-hineh shloshah anashim nitzavim alav* — *and he looked, and behold, three men stationed beside him* — Avraham at Mamre, with the *nitzavim alav* clearly *beside Avraham*, not *above the trees*; Exod 18:13-14 — Moses sat to judge and the people stood *alav* — *beside him*). The reading is adopted by: **NRSV** *and the Lord stood beside him*; **NJPS** *the Lord was standing beside him*; **Alter** *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 149 (extensive note arguing for the beside-Ya'aqov reading on the basis of v 15's *imakh* and the parallel of Gen 18:2 *nitzavim alav*); **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 456 (prefers the personal-referent reading); **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218.

*(c) Above him — referent = Ya'aqov, spatial-vertical.* The deity towers above the sleeping patriarch. The reading is grammatically possible but is editorially the weakest of the three; it preserves the *above* sense but loses both the architectural-cosmographic reading and the intimacy of the *beside* reading.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *YHWH stood above it* — adopting the ASV / LXX / Targum Onkelos / Rashi reading. The choice preserves the v 17 *sha'ar ha-shamayim* architectural-cosmographic framing of the chapter and the *sulam*-as-cosmic-mediation-structure reading. The competing *beside him* reading is documented in apparatus.

**The dispute is genuinely irresolvable on grammatical grounds.** Both readings are within the standard semantic range of *al* + 3ms suffix; the Hebrew does not disambiguate. The translation choice is editorial — selecting the reading that best preserves the chapter's wider architectural-cosmographic coherence — rather than philological-decisive.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 199; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 456; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 223; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 241-242; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 149 (extensive note); Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 218; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 p. 376; HALOT s.v. *al*, *natzav*; BDB s.v. *al* p. 752. Rabbinic-tradition: Rashi at Gen 28:13; Genesis Rabbah 69:3; Ibn Ezra ad loc. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the preposition *al* and its semantic range are documentary; (b) the three readings and their philological grounds are documented in the cited scholarly literature; (c) the LXX, Targum, and Rashi readings of the verse are textually-explicit; (d) the modern critical preference is divided between (a) and (b); (e) the WoH translation choice (the *above it* default) is one of the two mainstream positions and is editorially-motivated by the chapter's architectural-cosmographic coherence.

terms.source: אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדָעְתִּי
terms.translit: akhen yesh YHWH ba-maqom ha-zeh v-anokhi lo yadati
terms.strongs: H403 (akhen) + H3426 (yesh) + H3068 (YHWH) + H4725 (maqom) + H595 (anokhi) + H3045 (yada)
terms.partOfSpeech: adverb-particle + existence-particle + divine-name + preposition-with-definite-article + noun + demonstrative + waw + 1cs-pronoun + negative + qal-perfect 1cs
terms.literal: surely there-is YHWH in-the-place this; and-I — not I-knew
terms.standardEnglish: Surely the LORD is in this place; and I knew it not (ASV/KJV); Surely the LORD is in this place — and I did not know it! (NRSV); Surely the LORD is present in this place, and I did not know it! (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Surely YHWH is in this place, and I did not know it
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:16 contains the HB's foundational *I-did-not-know-this-place-was-sacred* discovery-formula.** Ya'aqov awakens from the dream and declares *akhen yesh YHWH ba-maqom ha-zeh v-anokhi lo yadati* — *surely YHWH is in this place, and I did not know it*. The construction is editorially distinctive on several grounds.

**The components.**

*(i) *Akhen*** (אָכֵן) — *surely, truly, indeed*. The adverbial-particle marks a **realization-of-truth-against-prior-supposition**. Cross-corpus, the particle frequently opens discoveries that overturn prior expectations: **Isa 49:4** (the Servant's *akhen mishpati et-YHWH*); **Jer 3:20** (*akhen bag'dah ishah me-rea'h*); **Jer 8:8** (*akhen hineh la-sheqer asah*); **Ps 73:1** (the *akhen tov l-Yisrael Elohim* of Asaph's psalm of theodicy-resolution). The particle's register is the **moment-of-recognition**.

*(ii) *Yesh*** (יֵשׁ) — the existence-particle: *there is*. The construction *yesh + divine-name + locative-phrase* is editorially distinctive: *yesh* normally takes an indefinite-noun subject (*yesh ish* — *there is a man*); its use with the proper-divine-name YHWH is unusual and emphatic. The construction declares the divine-presence as an **objective-locative-fact** discovered by the speaker, rather than as an evident or anticipated presence.

*(iii) *Ba-maqom ha-zeh* + *v-anokhi lo yadati*** — *in this place, and I did not know*. The locative-phrase chains to the chapter's seven-fold *maqom*-deployment (see [paga-ba-maqom-cultic-encounter](#paga-ba-maqom-cultic-encounter)); the *anokhi* — emphatic-1cs-pronoun rather than the shorter *ani* — marks the speaker's role in the realization. The *lo yadati* completes the discovery-formula: the place was always sacred; what was discovered is the speaker's prior ignorance.

**Cross-corpus structural cognates (none exact).**

- **Exod 3:5** (Moses at the burning bush): *al-tiqrav halom shal-n'alekha me-al raglekha ki ha-maqom asher atah omed alav admat-qodesh hu* — *do not come near; take off your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy ground*. The verse stages the **place-is-holy discovery** without the *yesh*-construction: the deity declares the holiness; Moses discovers his proximity to it. The structural cognate is partial.
- **Josh 5:15** (the captain of YHWH's host to Joshua): par. Exod 3:5 — the *shal na'alkha me-al raglekha ki ha-maqom asher atah omed alav qodesh hu* — directly echoes Exod 3:5. The cognate is closer.
- **Judg 13:22** (Manoah after the *mal'akh YHWH* departs): *mot namut ki Elohim ra'inu* — *we shall surely die, for we have seen Elohim*. The discovery-of-divine-presence is registered as fear-of-consequence; the *akhen yesh* construction is not used.
- **1 Kgs 19:11-13** (Eliyahu at Horev): the *qol demamah daqah* discovery-moment; the cognate is more atmospheric than constructional.

The Gen 28:16 *akhen yesh* construction has no exact HB parallel. The verse stands as a unique discovery-formula.

**The pairing with v 17.** The v 16 discovery-formula pairs with the v 17 *mah-norah ha-maqom ha-zeh* awe-formula and the v 17 *ein zeh ki im-beit Elohim v-zeh sha'ar ha-shamayim* cultic-identification (see [beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula](#beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula)). The two verses together constitute the chapter's **discovery-and-identification** unit: v 16 names *what* Ya'aqov did not know (YHWH is in this place); v 17 names *what* the place is (the house of Elohim, the gate of the heavens). The pairing is editorially load-bearing for the chapter's cultic-foundation-narrative function — the place's sanctity is discovered, named, and consecrated within three verses (vv 16-19).

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *Surely YHWH is in this place, and I did not know it* — ASV-baseline with the WoH transliteration of YHWH preserved. The *akhen* is rendered *surely*; the *yesh* is rendered *is* (preserving the existential register but allowing the English copula to do the lexical work); the *anokhi lo yadati* preserves the emphatic-1cs-pronoun. The English mirrors the Hebrew's discovery-register.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 199; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 456-457; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 223-224; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 243-244; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 149; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 218-219; HALOT s.v. *akhen*, *yesh*; BDB s.v. *akhen* p. 38, *yesh* p. 441. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construction's components are grammatically explicit; (b) the *akhen* + *yesh* + locative pattern is verifiable by lexical search and is shown to have no exact HB parallel; (c) the structural cognates at Exod 3:5 / Josh 5:15 / Judg 13:22 are documentary; (d) the v 16 / v 17 pairing as the chapter's discovery-and-identification unit is mainstream modern-critical reading.

terms.source: אֵין זֶה כִּי אִם־בֵּית אֱלֹהִים וְזֶה שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם
terms.translit: ein zeh ki im-beit Elohim v-zeh sha'ar ha-shamayim
terms.partOfSpeech: compound-cultic-formula: two construct-chains in chiastic pair
terms.literal: this is nothing but the house of Elohim, and this is the gate of the heavens
terms.standardEnglish: this is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven (ASV/KJV); this is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven (NRSV); this is none other than the abode of God, and that is the gateway to heaven (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: this is none other than the house of Elohim, and this is the gate of the heavens
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:17 contains the chapter's keystone cultic-formula: two compound-construct-chains paired in a chiastic-declarative structure.** Ya'aqov, awakening from the dream, declares: *mah-norah ha-maqom ha-zeh — ein zeh ki im-beit Elohim v-zeh sha'ar ha-shamayim* — *how awesome is this place! This is nothing other than the house of Elohim, and this is the gate of the heavens*. The two compound-construct-chains — *beit Elohim* (house of Elohim) and *sha'ar ha-shamayim* (gate of the heavens) — together name the *maqom* as a sacred-cultic-architectural site. Both lexemes are independently editorially load-bearing for the chapter and have substantial cross-corpus reach.

**(i) *Beit Elohim*** — *house of Elohim*. The construct *bayit* (house, dwelling) + *Elohim* (deity) names the **deity's dwelling-house** — the foundational HB temple-cultic-site formula. The lexeme functions in free variation with *beit YHWH* across the HB; the choice between *beit Elohim* and *beit YHWH* often reflects source-critical or stylistic considerations (the E-document and the wider patriarchal-narratives prefer *Elohim*; the Deuteronomic-and-Chronistic literature alternates). Cross-corpus:

- *Patriarchal use.* The Gen 28:17 deployment is the first; Gen 28:22 (Ya'aqov's vow: *v-ha-even ha-zot... yihyeh beit Elohim* — *and this stone... shall be the house of Elohim*); Gen 35:7 (the Bethel return: *va-yiqra la-maqom El Beit-El* — *and he called the place El of Beit-El*).
- *Pre-Solomonic use.* Judg 17-18 (the Mikhayhu / Dan-tribe shrine-narrative — the *beit Elohim* / *beit pesilim* shrine outside the Jerusalem cultus); 1 Sam 1:7, 1:24, 3:15 (the *beit YHWH* at Shiloh — the variation between *beit YHWH* and *beit Elohim*).
- *Solomonic and post-Solomonic.* The *beit YHWH* / *beit Elohim* nomenclature for the Jerusalem temple dominates Kings, Chronicles, Ezra-Nehemiah. The interchange is well-attested.

**(ii) *Sha'ar ha-shamayim*** — *gate of the heavens*. The chapter's most editorially-distinctive lexeme. The construct *sha'ar* (gate, entrance) + *ha-shamayim* (the heavens, dual-form definite) is virtually unique to this verse in the HB; the close cognates (e.g., the *sha'arei tzedek* at Ps 118:19; the *sha'arei mavet* at Ps 9:14) use *sha'ar* in metaphorical-not-cosmographic registers.

**The *bab-ili* / Babylon connection.** The compound *sha'ar ha-shamayim* is **functionally identical** to the Akkadian *bab-ili* (gate of god). The Akkadian compound — composed of *babu* (gate) + *ilu* (god) — gives the city-name **Babylon** (*Bāb-ili*, *Bāb-ilim*, *Bāb-ili-ki* across the cuneiform texts), and is the cosmographic name of the place where the ziggurat *e-temen-an-ki* (House of the Foundation of Heaven and Earth) was held to provide a divine-mediation-locus connecting earth and the upper-heavens. The Gen 11 Babel-narrative polemically reads the city-name *Bāb-ili* as Hebrew *Bavel* (from *b-l-l*, *confuse*) — the **scattering** at the failed *migdal* (tower) whose top would reach the heavens (Gen 11:4 *u-rosho ba-shamayim*). The Gen 28:17 *sha'ar ha-shamayim* is the chapter's **verbal recapitulation of and counterpoint to** the Babel-builders' attempted gate-to-heaven: where the Babel-builders tried to build the gate-to-heaven and were scattered, Ya'aqov **discovers** the gate-to-heaven and is blessed. The intertext is editorially load-bearing; the *sulam* of v 12 (with *rosho magia ha-shamayma* — *its top reached the heavens*, a near-direct Gen 11:4 verbal echo) is the chapter's structural anti-Babel image.

**The Bethel-cult foundation-narrative reading.** Modern critical scholarship (Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 457-459; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 199-200; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1; Albright *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968) reads the chapter as the **Bethel-cult's foundation-narrative**: the chapter aetiologically grounds the cultic-status of Bethel (which became the dynastic-cultic center of the northern kingdom under Yarov'am, 1 Kgs 12:29-33, and the focus of the Amos prophetic-critique cluster, Amos 7:13) by attributing its sanctity to Ya'aqov's patriarchal discovery. The chapter's compound formula *beit Elohim* + *sha'ar ha-shamayim* + the matzevah at v 18 + the place-name *Beit-El* at v 19 together stage the full Bethel-cult-foundation in a single tightly-structured pericope.

**Downstream receptions (flagged-not-preempted).**

- **NT reception.** John 1:51 (Jesus as the Son-of-Man-as-gateway in the *anabainontas kai katabainontas* — *ascending and descending* — Christology) explicitly Christianizes the chapter's gateway-image. The *sha'ar ha-shamayim* in the NT reading is no longer the Bethel-stone but the person of Jesus.
- **Rabbinic reception.** Genesis Rabbah 69:7 reads the *maqom* as identified with the future Temple Mount: the Bethel-cult is the proto-Temple. The reading is part of the rabbinic effort to consolidate cultic-legitimacy on Jerusalem against the northern-kingdom Bethel-cult and against Samaritan alternatives.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *house of Elohim* (preserving the *Elohim* transliteration per the project policy) and *gate of the heavens* (preserving the literal-plural *ha-shamayim* against the smoothed singular *heaven* of most English Bibles). The choice maintains lexical visibility for both compound-construct-chains.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 199-200; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 457-459; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 223-224; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 244-246; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 149; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 219; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1; Albright *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968; Heidel *The Babylonian Genesis* 1951 (on *bab-ili*); ANET pp. 60-72 (on Babel-Babylon ziggurat-architecture); Smith *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism* 2001; Brown *The Gospel According to John I-XII* AB 1966 (on John 1:51). Rabbinic-tradition: Genesis Rabbah 69:7; Rashi at Gen 28:17; b. Pesachim 88a. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the two compound-construct-chains are grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus *beit Elohim* / *beit YHWH* distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *sha'ar ha-shamayim* / *bab-ili* parallel is ANE-documentary; (d) the Gen 11 Babel-echo is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the Bethel-cult foundation-narrative reading is mainstream modern-critical-consensus; (f) the NT and rabbinic receptions are documentary and flagged-not-preempted. Project-specific synthesis (e.g., the WoH lens on the *sha'ar ha-shamayim* as physical-architectural-gateway) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: standing-stone / sacred-pillar / cultic-stele
terms.standardEnglish: pillar (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: pillar (ASV-baseline; the patriarchal-El-religion / Deuteronomic-prohibition cross-corpus tension surfaced in apparatus)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:18 is the first occurrence of *matzevah* (מַצֵּבָה — standing-stone, sacred-pillar) in Genesis and the foundational patriarchal-matzevah deployment. Ya'aqov takes the stone he had placed under his head, *sets it up as a matzevah*, and pours oil on its top.** The act has a complex cross-corpus reception that makes the lexeme one of the HB's most editorially-load-bearing cultic-history doublets.

**The *matzevah* lexeme.** The noun derives from the root *n-tz-v* (נצב) — *stand, be stationed, be set up*. The morphology is the niphal-participial feminine: *that which is set up*. The lexeme names a **cultic standing-stone** — a stele set vertically as a sacred-site marker or memorial. The cultic-stone is a well-attested West-Semitic and broader-ANE religious feature: the Ugaritic *skn* (cultic-stele), the Phoenician *baetyl* (sacred-stone), the Nabataean *masjid* / *dhū-šarā* stones, and the wider Levantine standing-stone tradition. Archaeological evidence: the Hazor temple-precinct standing-stones (Yadin excavations); the Gezer high-place stones (Macalister); the Arad sanctuary masseboth (Aharoni).

**Affirmatively present in the patriarchal cult.**

- **Gen 28:18, 22** (here — Ya'aqov sets up the Bethel matzevah; v 22 names it the *beit Elohim*).
- **Gen 31:13** (the deity, via the *mal'akh*, recalls the Bethel matzevah to Ya'aqov: *anokhi ha-El Beit-El asher mashachta sham matzevah* — *I am the El of Beit-El where you anointed a matzevah*). The back-reference is editorially significant: the deity self-identifies through the Bethel cultic-site, with the matzevah as the cultic-anchor.
- **Gen 31:45, 51-52** (the Lavan-Ya'aqov treaty at Gal-Ed: Ya'aqov sets up a stone as a matzevah; the brothers gather stones into a *gal* — the matzevah and the gal together function as the treaty-witness).
- **Gen 35:14, 20** (the Bethel return-narrative: Ya'aqov sets up a matzevah at Bethel and pours oil and drink-offering on it, v 14; Rachel's burial matzevah at Bethlehem, v 20).
- **Exod 24:4** (Mosheh at Sinai sets up twelve matzevot for the twelve tribes — the matzevah-tradition's last unambiguously-affirmative HB use).
- **2 Sam 18:18** (Avshalom's monument *yad Avshalom* — the personal-memorial-pillar use, distinguished from the cultic-religious use but lexically continuous).

**Explicitly outlawed in later Yahwism.**

- **Lev 26:1** (the holiness-code: *u-matzevah lo taqimu lakhem* — *and you shall not set up a matzevah for yourselves*).
- **Deut 7:5** (the Canaanite-cult destruction-command: *u-matzevotam t'shaberu* — *and you shall break their matzevot*).
- **Deut 12:3** (par.: the Canaanite-cult-sites command); **Deut 16:22** (the explicit prohibition: *v-lo taqim l'kha matzevah asher sane YHWH Elohekha* — *and you shall not raise up a matzevah, which YHWH your Elohim hates*).
- **2 Kgs 17:10** (the indictment of Israel's syncretism: *va-yatzivu la-hem matzevot* — *and they set up matzevot for themselves*); **2 Kgs 18:4** (Hezekiah's reform: *v-shibar et ha-matzevot*); **2 Kgs 23:14** (Josiah's reform: *va-yi-shaber et ha-matzevot* — the explicit matzevah-smashing of the Josianic Deuteronomic-reform).

**Lamented in the prophets.**

- **Hos 3:4** (the Israel-without-cultic-stone lament: *ein matzevah*); **Hos 10:1-2** (the matzevah-as-syncretic-cult: *l-tzelamim hetiv matzevot* — *they made matzevot for the images*); **Mic 5:12 [MT 5:13]** (the eschatological matzevah-destruction: *v-hikhrati pseilekha u-matzevotekha* — *I will cut off your idols and your matzevot*).

**The patriarchal-affirmation / Deuteronomic-prohibition tension.** The lexeme stands as one of the HB's most editorially-load-bearing **cultic-history doublets**: **the patriarchs build, anoint, and consecrate the very cultic-objects that the later legislation absolutely prohibits.** The tension has been the subject of extensive modern critical scholarship.

- **Alt** *Der Gott der Väter* 1929 reads the patriarchal-matzevah as a relic of the pre-Mosaic patriarchal-clan-El-religion, subsequently overlaid by the YHWH-tradition.
- **Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1 develops the El-cult-identification reading: the patriarchal *El*-titles (El-Shaddai, El-Elyon, El-Olam, El-Roi, El-Beit-El) and the patriarchal cultic-objects (matzevah, asherah, mizbeach) together constitute a coherent pre-Mosaic El-cult that Deuteronomic-reform subsequently suppressed and remembered only as the *fathers'* religion.
- **Smith** *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism* 2001 and *The Early History of God* 2002 develop the convergence-and-differentiation model: the early-Israelite religion shared cultic-vocabulary with the wider West-Semitic milieu, and the Deuteronomic-reform marked the boundary between Israelite-Yahwism and surrounding-cult.
- **Mettinger** *No Graven Image? Israelite Aniconism in its Ancient Near Eastern Context* (ConBOT 42, 1995) reads the matzevah-tradition as an *aniconic* (non-image) sacred-stone tradition that coexisted with the wider iconic ANE cult for an extended period before being suppressed by the Deuteronomic reform.

The patriarchal-narratives' continued positive depiction of the matzevah (Gen 28:18, 22; Gen 31:13, 31:45-52; Gen 35:14, 20) is therefore editorially-evidence of the pre-Deuteronomic period in which the cultic-stone was an affirmative-religious-object. The narratives are not later-projected-back: their attestation of the matzevah is **inconsistent with** Deuteronomic-period legislation, and the survival of the affirmation suggests the narratives preserve genuinely-older traditions.

**The Ya'aqov-anointing**. Ya'aqov pours oil on the matzevah-top (*va-yitzoq shemen al-roshah*). The verb *yatzaq* + *shemen* (pour oil) recurs in the later HB for the priestly-anointing-legislation (Exod 29:7 — Aaron's priestly anointing; Lev 8:12 — Mosheh's anointing of Aaron). The Ya'aqov-matzevah-anointing is the first HB anointing-of-a-cultic-object and supplies the lexical-precedent for the later legislative-development.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *pillar* (ASV-baseline) — adopting the standard English rendering that preserves the architectural-form. The cultic-stone-specific register is preserved in apparatus rather than in the English text. The choice deliberately avoids the lens-load of *sacred-stone-marker* in the English rendering; the lens-distinctive reading (the matzevah as a physical stone-marker of a physical divine-encounter-site) is reserved for the wiki.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Alt *Der Gott der Väter* 1929 (Stuttgart: Kohlhammer; ET in *Essays on Old Testament History and Religion* 1966 pp. 1-77); Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 ch. 1; Smith *The Origins of Biblical Monotheism* 2001 and *The Early History of God* 1990/2002; Mettinger *No Graven Image? Israelite Aniconism in its Ancient Near Eastern Context* (ConBOT 42) 1995; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 200; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 459-460; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 224-225; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 246-247; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 149-150; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 219; Albright *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968; Avner *The Masseboth Sites in the Negev and Sinai and Their Significance* (PhD 1990; Hebrew); HALOT s.v. *matzevah*, *n-tz-v*; BDB s.v. *matzevah* p. 663. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *matzevah* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution across the patriarchal-affirmation / Deuteronomic-prohibition / prophetic-lament cluster is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the Alt 1929 / Cross 1973 / Smith 2001 / Mettinger 1995 readings are documentary; (d) the cultic-stone-tradition's ANE context is archaeologically documented; (e) the patriarchal-affirmation / Deuteronomic-prohibition tension is HB-internal-explicit. Project-specific synthesis (the matzevah-as-physical-marker-of-physical-divine-encounter-site lens-reading) is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: house of El / almond(-tree)
terms.standardEnglish: Bethel ... Luz (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: Beit-El ... Luz (transliterated per WoH chapter convention)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:19 stages the foundational HB place-renaming-by-patriarch: *va-yiqra et-shem ha-maqom ha-hu Beit-El v-ulam Luz shem ha-ir la-rishonah* — *and he called the name of that place Beit-El; but the name of the city was Luz at the first*.** The verse explicitly identifies the new name **Beit-El** (*house of El* / *house of Elohim*) as a renaming of the previously-named **Luz**. The verse establishes the doublet that will recur across the HB.

**Beit-El (Bethel).** The construct *bayit* (house) + *El* (deity) names the place as the **deity's dwelling-house** — explicitly lexicalizing the v 17 *beit Elohim* declaration into the place-name. The name has substantial cross-corpus reach: Beit-El is the dynastic-cultic center of the **northern kingdom** under Yarov'am (1 Kgs 12:29-33 — the golden calves at Beit-El and Dan; the alternative-cult to the Jerusalem-temple); the focus of the **Amos** prophetic-critique cluster (Amos 7:13 — Amaziah's *u-Veit-El lo tosif od l-hinave ki miqdash melekh hu u-veit mamlakhah hu* — *do not prophesy again at Beit-El, for it is the king's sanctuary and a house of the kingdom*); the target of the **Josianic-reform** destruction (2 Kgs 23:15-20 — the altar at Beit-El is broken down and burned).

**Luz.** The earlier name *Luz* (לוּז) derives, in post-biblical Hebrew, from the noun *luz* meaning *almond, almond-tree*. The etymology suggests a **tree-named cultic-site** — the Canaanite-period name reflecting an almond-tree at the location. The pattern is consistent with the broader West-Semitic sacred-tree-and-cultic-site tradition (the *elon Moreh* at Gen 12:6; the *eshel* of Avraham at Be'er Sheva, Gen 21:33; the *eitz Mamre* at Gen 18:1; etc.).

**The doublet's cross-corpus recurrence.**

- **Gen 35:6-7** (Ya'aqov's return-narrative): *va-yavo Ya'aqov Luzah asher b-eretz Kena'an hi Beit-El* — *and Ya'aqov came to Luz which is in the land of Kena'an, that is Beit-El*. The verse re-states the doublet and reaffirms the patriarchal renaming, including the renaming of the place as **El Beit-El** (*El of the house of El*) at v 7.
- **Josh 16:2** and **Josh 18:13** (the tribal-allotment boundary-markers): the Beit-El / Luz identification is used as a geographical reference-point in the Ephraim-Benjamin boundary-narrative.
- **Judg 1:23-26** (the Beit-El capture-narrative): *v-shem ha-ir l-fanim Luz* — *and the name of the city was formerly Luz*; the verse preserves the doublet and adds a continuation: the man of Luz who reveals the city's defenses to the Israelite scouts is allowed to go to *eretz ha-Chittim* (the land of the Hittites), where he founds a new city and names it Luz; the etymology is thus carried into a secondary geographical context.
- **Judg 4:5** — the *tomer Devorah* (palm of Deborah) is *bein ha-Ramah u-vein Beit-El* — *between Ramah and Beit-El* — using Beit-El as a fixed geographical-cultic-anchor.
- **1 Kgs 12:29-33** — Yarov'am's Beit-El cult-center foundation.
- **2 Kgs 17:28** — a Beit-El priest is brought back from exile to teach the *ger* (immigrant) population *mishpat Elohei ha-aretz* (the manner of the Elohim of the land).
- **2 Kgs 23:15-20** — Josiah's reform destroys the Beit-El altar.
- **Amos 3:14; 4:4; 5:5-6; 7:10-13** — the Amos critique cluster.
- **Hos 4:15; 10:5, 15; 12:5** — the Hosea critique cluster (with the polemical re-naming of Beit-El as **Beit-Aven** — *house of iniquity* — at Hos 4:15; 5:8; 10:5; the post-exilic Beit-El-Beit-Aven polemic).

**The patriarchal-place-renaming pattern.** The chapter's *va-yiqra et-shem ha-maqom* construction is a developed HB convention. Patriarchal-place-renamings include: **Gen 22:14** (*YHWH Yir'eh* — *YHWH will see / provide*; the *aqedah*-site); **Gen 28:19** (Beit-El, here); **Gen 32:31** (Peni'el — face-of-El; the wrestling-site); **Gen 33:20** (*El Elohei Yisrael*); **Gen 35:7** (El Beit-El); **Gen 35:8** (*Allon-Bakhut* — oak-of-weeping); **Gen 35:15** (Beit-El reaffirmed); **Gen 35:18** (Ben-Oni / Binyamin). The pattern marks the patriarchal-period as the era when the geography of the land was being **named for divine-encounter-events**.

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders *Beit-El* and *Luz* — transliterated per the WoH chapter-convention. The transliteration preserves the **Hebraic-etymological visibility** (the *Beit-El* is readable as *house-of-El*; *Luz* as a foreign place-name) against the smoothed conventional *Bethel* (which loses the morpheme-boundary).

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 200; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 460-461; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 225-226; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 247-249; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 150; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 219-220; Albright *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968 (on the Beit-El cult); Köhler 'Bethel' in *Anchor Bible Dictionary* I 1992; Smith *The Early History of God* 1990/2002 (on the northern-cult-center); Schmid *Erzväter und Exodus* 1999 (on the patriarchal-place-name pattern); HALOT s.v. *Beit-El*, *Luz*; BDB s.v. *Beit-El*. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the doublet is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus distribution of both names is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the patriarchal-place-renaming pattern is HB-internal-documentary; (d) the Beit-El cultic-history (Yarov'am / Amos / Hosea / Josianic-reform) is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the patriarchal-renaming-convention is mainstream modern-critical-consensus.

terms.source: וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר ... עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ
terms.translit: va-yidar Ya'aqov neder ... aser a'asrenu lakh
terms.strongs: H5087 (nadar — vow) + H5088 (neder — vow, noun) + H6237 (asar — give a tenth)
terms.literal: and Ya'aqov vowed a vow ... I will surely tithe to you
terms.standardEnglish: and Jacob vowed a vow ... I will surely give the tenth unto thee (ASV/KJV); Jacob then made a vow ... I will set aside a tithe for You (NJPS); Jacob made a vow ... I will surely give one-tenth to you (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: and Ya'aqov vowed a vow ... I will surely give a tenth to you (ASV-baseline preserving both cognate-accusatives)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 28:20-22 contains the HB's first explicit *neder* (vow) — Ya'aqov's tripartite-conditional vow at Bethel — and the second patriarchal *ma'aser* (tithe). The pericope is editorially-foundational on both counts.**

**The first explicit *neder* in the HB.** The verb-noun pair *va-yidar... neder* (vowed a vow) — a **cognate-accusative construction** in which the verb (*nadar*, to vow) takes its own cognate noun (*neder*, vow) as the direct object — is the standard HB vow-formula. Gen 28:20 is its **first occurrence** in the canonical sequence. The construction recurs across the HB:

- **Num 21:2** (Israel's *cherem* vow before Arad: *va-yidar Yisrael neder l-YHWH* — *and Israel vowed a vow to YHWH*).
- **Num 30** (the Mosaic vow-legislation: the *neder* is regulated as a sworn-religious-obligation; vows by men, by unmarried women, by married women, by widows, and by divorcees are treated separately).
- **Judg 11:30-39** (Yiftach's failed vow: *va-yidar Yiftach neder l-YHWH* — *and Yiftach vowed a vow to YHWH* — leading to the daughter-sacrifice tragedy; the cross-corpus parallel to Gen 28's vow as a **conditional-obligation-to-the-deity** with concrete consequences).
- **1 Sam 1:11** (Channah's vow at Shiloh: *va-tidor neder va-tomar YHWH Tzva'ot* — *and she vowed a vow and said, YHWH Tzva'ot*; with the conditional structure: *if you give your maidservant a man-child, then I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life*).
- **2 Sam 15:7-8** (Avshalom's deception-vow at Hebron).
- **Eccl 5:3-5** (the vow-discipline wisdom: *tov asher lo tidor mi-she-tidor v-lo t'shalem* — *better not to vow than to vow and not pay*).
- **Jonah 1:16** (the sailors' vows in the storm).

**The vow's tripartite-conditional structure.** Ya'aqov's vow has the formal structure of an ANE conditional-religious-obligation. The **protasis** (vv 20-21a — the condition) names three divine actions:

*(i)* **presence** — *im-yihyeh Elohim imadi* (*if Elohim will be with me*; the *imadi* form is the divine-presence-formula's lexeme — see [elohim-imkha-divine-presence-formula](#elohim-imkha-divine-presence-formula));
*(ii)* **guarding** — *u-shmarani ba-derekh ha-zeh asher anokhi holekh* (*and will guard me on this way that I am going*);
*(iii)* **provision-and-safe-return** — *v-natan li lechem le'ekhol u-veged li-lvosh v-shavti b-shalom el-beit avi* (*and will give me bread to eat and clothing to put on, and I return in peace to my father's house*).

The **apodosis** (vv 21b-22 — the promise) names three reciprocal obligations:

*(i)* **deity-relationship** — *v-hayah YHWH li le-Elohim* (*then YHWH shall be my Elohim*; the *am-li / li-le-Elohim* covenantal-formula pre-figuring the Sinai-cluster);
*(ii)* **cultic-foundation** — *v-ha-even ha-zot asher samti matzevah yihyeh beit Elohim* (*and this stone... shall be the house of Elohim* — see [matzevah-sacred-pillar-patriarchal-religion](#matzevah-sacred-pillar-patriarchal-religion) and [beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula](#beit-elohim-shaar-ha-shamayim-cultic-formula));
*(iii)* **tithe-promise** — *v-khol asher titen-li aser a'asrenu lakh* (*and of all that you give me I will surely give a tenth to you*).

The vow's protasis-apodosis structure is the **conditional-religious-obligation** type: the human party promises specific actions contingent on the deity's specific actions. The structure has parallels in ANE vow-literature (the *zikrum*-vow of Old-Babylonian Mari letters; the Ugaritic KTU 1.14 Krt-epic vow-to-Asherah; the post-Christian-era Carthaginian *molk*-vows).

**The *aser a'asrenu* cognate-accusative.** The closing tithe-promise uses a **second cognate-accusative**: *aser a'asrenu* — the piel-infinitive-absolute (*aser* — tithing) + piel-imperfect-1cs (*a'asrenu* — *I will tithe it*) — yielding the most-emphatic Hebrew tithe-formula. The construction parallels the opening *va-yidar... neder* cognate-accusative; the pericope is **bracketed by two cognate-accusatives**, marking the vow-and-tithe as an editorially-deliberate paired-construction.

**The second patriarchal tithe.** The Ya'aqov-tithe is the HB's **second** explicit patriarchal-tithe, after Avraham's tithe to Malki-Tzedek at **Gen 14:20** (covered by the existing [maaser-first-pre-sinai](#maaser-first-pre-sinai) entry). The two pre-Sinai patriarchal-tithe-instances together establish the **pre-Sinai tithe institution** (cf. Heb 7:1-10's reception of the pre-Aaronic locus). The cross-corpus tithe-development is extensively documented in [maaser-first-pre-sinai](#maaser-first-pre-sinai) and is not duplicated here.

**The vow-and-tithe as paired-foundational-institutions.** The pericope's editorial-distinctiveness is the **direct binding** of the *matzevah* (sanctuary-stone, v 22 first clause) and the *ma'aser* (tithe, v 22 second clause) into a single inaugural cultic-institution: the stone is the proto-temple, the tithe is the proto-temple-offering, two parts of one editorial-unit. The structure pre-figures the developed HB temple-cult in which the temple (the *beit Elohim*) and the tithe (*ma'aser*) function as paired institutions (Mal 3:8-10 explicitly binds *ma'aser* to *beit Elohim*: *bring all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be food in my house*).

**The translation choice.** The WoH renders both cognate-accusatives literally: *vowed a vow* (preserving the *va-yidar... neder*) and *I will surely give a tenth* (preserving the *aser a'asrenu* with the *surely*-emphatic for the infinitive-absolute). The ASV-baseline is followed.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Cartledge *Vows in the Hebrew Bible and the Ancient Near East* (JSOTSup 147) 1992 — the foundational modern monograph on the HB vow-institution and its ANE context; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 200-201; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 461-463; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 225-226 (extensive treatment of the vow's tripartite structure); Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 249-251; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 150-151; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 219-220; Berlinerblau *The Vow and the Popular Religious Groups of Ancient Israel: A Philological and Sociological Inquiry* (JSOTSup 210) 1996; Milgrom *Leviticus 23-27* AB 2001 (on the Lev 27 vow-and-tithe legislation); HALOT s.v. *nadar*, *neder*, *asar*, *ma'aser*; BDB s.v. *nadar* p. 623, *neder* p. 623, *asar* p. 797. Rabbinic-tradition: Genesis Rabbah 70:1-6; Rashi at Gen 28:20-22; b. Chullin 91b. // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the *va-yidar... neder* cognate-accusative is grammatically explicit; (b) the *aser a'asrenu* cognate-accusative is grammatically explicit; (c) the tripartite-conditional vow-structure is grammatically explicit; (d) the cross-corpus vow-formula distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (e) the second-patriarchal-tithe status is verifiable; (f) the ANE conditional-religious-obligation type is documented in Cartledge 1992 and Berlinerblau 1996; (g) the vow-and-tithe as paired-foundational-institutions reading is mainstream modern-critical-consensus.

terms.source: type-scene (literary-structural unit, not a single lexeme)
terms.translit: (see-also: be'er ba-sadeh, va-yagel et ha-even, va-tarotz va-tagged)
terms.literal: betrothal-at-the-well narrative pattern
terms.standardEnglish: (no standard rendering; commentary-only convention)
terms.wohChoice: (no translation-level intervention; commentary surfaces the type-scene structure verse-by-verse)
terms.rationale:
**The well-betrothal type-scene is one of the Hebrew Bible's clearest demonstrations of a fixed narrative convention with deliberate per-instance variation, identified and theorized by Robert Alter in *The Art of Biblical Narrative* (1981, ch. 3, *Biblical Type-Scenes and the Uses of Convention*). The three canonical instances are Gen 24 (Eliezer fetches Rivkah for Yitzchaq), Gen 29:1-14 (Ya'aqov meets Rachel — the most-elaborated), and Exod 2:15-22 (Moshe meets Tzipporah — the most-compressed).**

**The type-scene's fixed components** (Alter 1981, pp. 51-62): (a) the future bridegroom (or an agent acting for him) **journeys to a foreign land**; (b) he arrives at a **well** (*be'er*); (c) **one or more young women** arrive at the well to draw water for their father's flocks; (d) **water is drawn** — either the man does the drawing or the women do, and the man helps; (e) the young woman **runs home** to announce the stranger's arrival; (f) the **family welcomes** the stranger into the household; (g) a **meal is offered**; (h) **betrothal arrangements** follow. Each instance modifies the template in ways that characterize the protagonist and prefigure the marriage.

**The Gen 29 distinctives.** (i) The **great stone** on the well's mouth — *ha-even gedolah al-pi ha-be'er* — has no parallel in Gen 24 or Exod 2; it adds a physical-strength test that Ya'aqov passes single-handedly (v 10). (ii) **Three flocks** are already lying at the well, with Rachel's the implied fourth — a compositional triangulation absent from the other two scenes. (iii) Ya'aqov **rolls the stone and waters the flock before he identifies himself** to Rachel (vv 10-12), inverting the Gen 24 sequence where Eliezer asks for water first and gifts come after recognition; the love-at-first-sight reading depends on this inversion. (iv) Ya'aqov **kisses Rachel before introduction** (v 11) and weeps — the only HB type-scene where the kiss precedes the family-introduction. (v) Lavan **runs out** to meet Ya'aqov (v 13) — directly recalling Lavan's earlier run-out to Eliezer at Gen 24:29-31 — making Lavan the only HB character with two well-running-out narrations (Sarna *JPS* p. 203 notes this; Alter 1981 p. 56 makes it central to his reading).

**The Exod 2:15-22 compression.** Moshe rescues seven daughters of Re'uel from hostile shepherds, draws water *for* them (the gender-reversal of Gen 24's *I-will-also-draw-for-your-camels* pattern), and the daughters run home to announce. The compression makes Moshe the most-active of the three protagonists; the type-scene functions to characterize him as a deliverer-of-the-oppressed *before* his Sinai-commissioning.

**Per-protagonist characterization.** Alter (1981 pp. 58-60) reads the three instances as a deliberate-set: Eliezer is a passive agent and the type-scene's first iteration is the most-conventional; Ya'aqov is the most-active protagonist (rolls the stone himself) and the type-scene is the most-elaborated; Moshe inverts the convention (he draws water *for* the women, not the women for him) and the type-scene is the most-compressed. The variation tracks character. Sternberg *The Poetics of Biblical Narrative* (1985, pp. 134-138) extends the reading to a fourth proto-type-scene at 1 Sam 9 (Saul meets the maidens at the well outside Tziph), arguing for a Saul-typology that the canonical-text deliberately under-develops.

**Scholarly literature.** Alter *The Art of Biblical Narrative* 1981 ch. 3 (foundational); Sternberg *Poetics of Biblical Narrative* 1985 pp. 134-138, 365-440; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 162-170 (Gen 24), 202-203 (Gen 29); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 384-392 (Gen 24), 465-468 (Gen 29); Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 138-152, 232-234; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 138-167, 252-260; Fokkelman *Narrative Art in Genesis* 1975 pp. 123-130 (on the Gen 29 narrative-structure); Williams 'The Beautiful and the Barren' *JSOT* 1980 (on the Gen 29 elaboration). Subsequent treatments: Fewell and Gunn *Gender, Power, and Promise* 1993; Frymer-Kensky *Reading the Women of the Bible* 2002. // The translation-level intervention is **none** — the type-scene is a structural unit visible only at the chapter level, surfaced through commentary at v 1 and the per-verse commentary on vv 2, 10, 11, 13. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the three-instance set is uncontested in modern critical scholarship; (b) the fixed components are grammatically and narratively explicit at each instance; (c) the per-instance variations are individually-attestable; (d) Alter 1981 is the foundational secondary-source and the reading has been canonical since.

terms.literal: my bone and my flesh
terms.standardEnglish: my bone and my flesh (ASV/KJV); my own flesh and blood (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: my bone and my flesh (ASV-baseline preserving the *etzem-va-basar* idiom)
terms.rationale:
**The *etzem-va-basar* (bone-and-flesh) idiom is the Hebrew Bible's standard fictive-kinship-confirmation formula, declaring blood-tie or chosen-kinship between two parties.** The formula's first occurrence is at **Gen 2:23** — Adam's recognition of Chavvah: *zot ha-pa'am etzem me-atzamai u-vasar mi-bsari* (*this one at last, bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh*). The idiom is from there forward the HB's lexicalization of blood-relation, marriage-tie, and tribal-loyalty.

**Cross-corpus distribution.**

- **Gen 2:23** (Adam to Chavvah) — the inaugural-instance; the *etzem me-atzamai u-vasar mi-bsari* doubled-construct is the most-emphatic form.
- **Gen 29:14** (Lavan to Ya'aqov) — *akh atzmi u-vsari atah* (*surely you are my bone and my flesh*) — the formula in its simple form, here at the moment of Ya'aqov's reception into the Lavan household.
- **Judg 9:2** (Avimelekh to the men of Shechem) — *ki atzmkhem u-vsarkhem ani* (*for I am your bone and your flesh*) — invoked to claim Shechemite loyalty against the seventy sons of Yeruba'al.
- **2 Sam 5:1** (the tribes to David at Hebron) — *hinneh atzmkha u-vsarkha anachnu* (*behold, we are your bone and your flesh*) — the formula used by the northern tribes to confirm David's kingship over them.
- **2 Sam 19:13-14** (David to the elders of Yehudah, and to Amasa) — *achai atem atzmi u-vsari atem* (*you are my brothers, you are my bone and my flesh*); and to Amasa specifically *halo atzmi u-vsari atah* (*are you not my bone and my flesh?*) — the formula used to repair the Avshalom-rebellion fractures.
- **1 Chr 11:1** (the Chronicler's parallel to 2 Sam 5:1).

**Editorial irony at Gen 29:14.** Lavan's deployment of the kinship-formula at the moment of Ya'aqov's reception is editorially-marked: within fourteen verses (v 25) Lavan will be revealed as an unreliable kinsman who substituted Le'ah for Rachel under cover of the wedding-feast. The formula's first deployment in the Lavan-Ya'aqov cycle is thus a **set-up for its later violation**; the *akh* (surely) intensifier in the Hebrew is in retrospect ironic. The chapter's twin Lavan-statements at v 14 (*atzmi u-vsari*) and v 26 (*lo ye'aseh khen bi-mqomenu*) form a deception-frame around the wedding-narrative: the first establishes the kinship-claim that the second is violating. Alter (1981 p. 56; 2004 p. 152) and Sarna (*JPS* p. 203) both flag this structure.

**Scholarly literature.** BDB s.v. *etzem* p. 782, *basar* p. 142; HALOT s.v. *etzem*, *basar*; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 23 (Gen 2:23), 203 (Gen 29:14); Westermann *Genesis 1-11* 1984 pp. 230-232 (the Gen 2:23 inaugural); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 467-468; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 232; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 257; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 22, 152; Reiss 'The Birth of Eve' *Jewish Bible Quarterly* 2008 (on the Gen 2:23 syntax). // The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula's lexical components are grammatically explicit at each instance; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the fictive-kinship-confirmation function is uncontested in modern lexicography; (d) the editorial-irony reading at Gen 29:14 is mainstream modern-critical (Alter, Sarna).

terms.strongs: H5869 (ayin — eye) + H7390 (rakh — tender, soft, delicate; weak)
terms.literal: and the eyes of Le'ah (were) tender/soft/weak
terms.standardEnglish: tender-eyed (ASV/KJV — archaic, ambiguous); weak (NRSV/NJPS, LXX *astheneis*); lovely (Speiser AB; Onkelos *ya'eyan*)
terms.wohChoice: tender (ASV/Alter — preserves the Hebrew's semantic range without resolving the dispute)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 29:17's *einei Le'ah rakkot* is one of the Hebrew Bible's most editorially-consequential lexical disputes. The Hebrew adjective *rakh* (feminine plural *rakkot*) has a semantic range spanning *tender / soft / delicate* on one end and *weak / dim / feeble* on the other; the choice between readings determines whether the verse describes Le'ah's beauty-defect or her beauty-feature, and consequently the moral evaluation of Ya'aqov's preference for Rachel.**

**Four readings of *rakkot* circulate in the scholarly literature.**
