The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *pilegesh* is grammatically explicit at Gen 35:22 and the cross-corpus verses; (b) the *pilegesh* / *shifchah* / *amah* / *ishah* / *zonah* status-distinction is documentary and supported by the standard lexica; (c) the ANE-comparative secondary-wife status is documented in Westbrook, Mendelsohn, Brenner; (d) the succession-claim-by-bed reading is mainstream modern critical scholarship and is supported by HB-internal parallels (2 Sam 16, 1 Kgs 2, 2 Sam 3, Deut 22:30); (e) the Gen 49:3-4 retrospective is HB-internal-explicit.

terms.partOfSpeech: preposition + infinitive construct of *yatzah* + noun with 3fs pronominal suffix
terms.literal: as her nephesh was going out / in the going-out of her nephesh
terms.standardEnglish: as her soul was departing (ASV); as her soul was passing (NJPS); as her life was departing (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: as her nephesh was departing (preserving *nephesh* in transliteration; avoiding the ASV/Platonic *soul* register)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 35:18's *va-yhi b-tzet nafshah ki metah* — *and it came to pass, as her nephesh was going out, for she was dying* — is the explicit Hebrew Bible formula for the *nephesh-departure-at-death*.** The construction names *nephesh* (נֶפֶשׁ — *life-breath, animating vitality, throat*) as the departing-life-component at the moment of death. The formula is editorially distinctive: most patriarchal-death-formulae use *va-yigva va-yamot va-ye'asef el-ammav* (*expired and died and was gathered to his peoples*) without specifying the *nephesh* as the departing-component; Gen 35:18 makes the *nephesh-departure* explicit.

**The cross-corpus distribution.**

*Patriarchal:*
- **Gen 25:8** (Avraham): *va-yigva va-yamot Avraham b-seyvah tovah zaqen v-save'a va-ye'asef el-ammav* — without the explicit *tzet nafsho*; the *gavah / mut / ye'asef* triple-formula.
- **Gen 35:18** (Rachel, here): explicit *tzet nafshah*.
- **Gen 49:33** (Ya'aqov): *va-ye'asef raglav el-ha-mittah va-yigva va-ye'asef el-ammav* — without the explicit *tzet nafsho*.

*Wisdom-and-prophetic:*
- **1 Kgs 17:21-22** (Eliyahu's revival of the Tzarfat-widow's son): *tashov-na nefesh ha-yeled ha-zeh al-qirbo... va-tashov nefesh ha-yeled al-qirbo va-yechi* — *let the boy's nephesh return to his interior... and the boy's nephesh returned to his interior, and he lived*. The explicit-formula of **return-of-nephesh** parallel to the Gen 35:18 **departure-of-nephesh**.
- **Ps 146:4** (*tetze rucho yashuv l-admato* — *his ruach goes out, he returns to his dust*; the parallel **tetze-ruach** formula using *ruach* rather than *nephesh*).
- **Jonah 4:3** (Jonah's death-wish: *kach-na et-nafshi mimmenni* — *take my nephesh from me*).
- **Job 11:20** (*mafach-nefesh* — *the breathing-out of nephesh*; the related-but-distinct death-formula).

**The Gen 2:7 inverse-formula.** Gen 2:7 deploys the inverse construction: *va-yipach b-apav nishmat chayyim va-yhi ha-adam l-nefesh chayyah* — *and he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and the adam became a living nephesh*. The conferral-of-nephesh at the breath-inbreathing (Gen 2:7) and the departure-of-nephesh at the breath-exhalation (Gen 35:18) form a paired editorial framework: the nephesh is conferred at the inbreathed beginning and departs at the exhaled end. The Gen 2:7 conferral-formula is covered in the central [nefesh-chayah](#nefesh-chayah) entry; the present entry covers the departure-formula and its cross-corpus distribution.

**The Hebrew anthropological-vocabulary cluster.** The HB's foundational anthropological vocabulary includes *nephesh* (life-breath, throat, animating vitality), *ruach* (spirit, breath, wind), *neshamah* (breath, life-breath), *basar* (flesh, body), and *lev* (heart, mind, will). The cluster is not the Platonic body-and-soul dualism that the LXX-and-Vulgate translation-tradition imposed: the *nephesh* is not a separable immortal soul but a life-component of the embodied person; at death the *nephesh* departs (Gen 35:18) or is taken (Jonah 4:3) or returns (1 Kgs 17:22). The semantic core is *breath-life-animating-presence*, not *separable-immortal-soul*. Modern HB scholarship is unanimous on this: **Wolff** *Anthropology of the Old Testament* 1974 (the foundational modern monograph); **Brown-Driver-Briggs** s.v. *nephesh*; **HALOT** s.v. *nephesh*; **Westermann** *Theologische Beobachtungen zum Alten Testament* 1974.

**The Platonic-dualist mistranslation problem.** The ASV/KJV/Geneva *as her soul was departing* uses the English word *soul* — which by the 16th-19th centuries had acquired Platonic-Christian connotations of a separable-immortal-component of the person. The translation imports a body-soul dualism that the Hebrew lexeme does not support. The NRSV/NJPS modernize to *life* (NRSV: *as her life was departing*; NJPS: *as her soul was passing*), which avoids the worst of the Platonic-import but loses the lexical-specificity. The WoH choice preserves *nephesh* in transliteration, matching the project's practice with other technical anthropological vocabulary and the central [nefesh-chayah](#nefesh-chayah) policy.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 244; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 556-557; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 327; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 379-380; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 273; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 198; Wolff *Anthropology of the Old Testament* 1974; Westermann *Theologische Beobachtungen* 1974; HALOT s.v. *nephesh*; BDB pp. 659-661; TDOT IX:497-519 (s.v. *nephesh*); on the Hebrew-vs-Platonic anthropology: Boman *Hebrew Thought Compared with Greek* 1960; Pedersen *Israel: Its Life and Culture* 1926; Dahood *Anchor Bible Psalms* (introductory anthropological notes on *nephesh*).

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construction *b-tzet nafshah* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus departure-of-nephesh formula at 1 Kgs 17:21-22 (with the inverse return-formula) is HB-internal-explicit; (c) the Gen 2:7 inverse-formula is documentary; (d) the modern critical anthropological consensus (Wolff, HALOT, BDB) is documented and is mainstream; (e) the Platonic-mistranslation critique is the unanimous modern-critical position.

terms.partOfSpeech: compound phrase: noun (sg.) + waw + noun (sg. construct) + noun (m.pl.)
terms.literal: a nation and an assembly of nations
terms.standardEnglish: a nation and a company of nations (ASV); a nation and an assembly of nations (NRSV/NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: a nation and a company of nations (ASV-baseline)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 35:11's *goy u-qhal goyim yihyeh mimmekha* — *a nation and a company of nations shall be from you* — deploys the patriarchal-promise formula's federative-dimension.** The construction pairs the singular *goy* (nation) with the plural *qhal goyim* (an assembly of nations), naming the patriarch's seed as both a single *goy* and a collective *qhal goyim*. The formula's recurrence across the patriarchal-cycle is editorially-load-bearing and is a central P-source signature.

**The cross-corpus distribution.**

- **Gen 17:5-6** (Avraham's renaming and covenant-promise): *ki av-hamon goyim n'tatikha... v-hifreiti otkha bi-m'od m'od u-n'tatikha l-goyim u-melakhim mimkha yetze'u* — *for I have made you father of a multitude of nations... and I will make you exceedingly fruitful, and I will make you into nations, and kings shall come out of you*. The Avraham-renaming-formula's *av-hamon goyim* and the v 6 *u-n'tatikha l-goyim u-melakhim mimkha yetze'u* establish the **father-of-multitude-nations** and **kings-from-your-seed** promise components.
- **Gen 17:16** (Sarah's renaming): *u-berakhti otah... v-haytah l-goyim malkhei amim mi-mennah yihyu* — *and I will bless her... and she shall be for nations; kings of peoples shall come from her*; the matriarchal-parallel formula.
- **Gen 17:20** (Yishma'el's parallel-promise): *u-l-Yishma'el shma'tikha... v-hifreiti oto v-hirbeti oto bi-m'od m'od shneim-asar n'siim yolid u-n'tativ l-goy gadol* — *and as for Yishma'el, I have heard you... I will make him fruitful and multiply him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget, and I will make him a great nation*; the Avraham-promise's parallel to Yishma'el.
- **Gen 28:3** (Yitzchaq's blessing of Ya'aqov): *v-El Shaddai yevarekh otkha v-yafrekha v-yarbekha v-hayita li-qhal amim* — *and El Shaddai bless you, and make you fruitful and multiply you, that you may be a *qhal amim* (assembly of peoples)*. The *qhal amim* / *qhal goyim* lexical alternation is editorially-significant: *qhal goyim* names the federative-nations dimension; *qhal amim* (assembly of peoples) is the parallel construction with *am* (people, kin-group) substituting for *goy*.
- **Gen 35:11** (here): *goy u-qhal goyim yihyeh mimmekha u-melakhim me-chalatzekha yetze'u*.
- **Gen 48:4** (Ya'aqov's recapitulation to Yosef on his sons' adoption): *hinni mafrekha v-hirbitikha u-n'tatikha li-qhal amim* — *behold, I will make you fruitful and multiply you, and I will make you a *qhal amim**.

**The formula's editorial-structural function.** The *goy u-qhal goyim* (and its variants *l-goyim*, *qhal amim*, *av-hamon goyim*) names the patriarchal-promise's **federative-future**: the patriarch's seed will not be a single ethnic-group but a federation-of-nations. The formula has been read by modern critical scholarship as:

- *(i)* **Ethnographic-historical reference**: the formula reflects the second-temple-period Israelite consciousness of being a *qhal amim* — a federation of tribal-groups and proselytized communities. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 256-258; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 122-124.
- *(ii)* **Eschatological-universalistic interpretation**: the formula anticipates the prophetic-eschatological *nations-streaming-to-Zion* (Isa 2:2-4 / Mic 4:1-3) and the deuteronomistic *kings of the earth shall serve YHWH* (Ps 72; Isa 60). **Cross** *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973 (on the P-document's universalizing-tendency); **Kaminsky** *Yet I Loved Jacob* 2007.
- *(iii)* **P-source signature**: the *peru u-rvu* + *goy u-qhal goyim* + *El Shaddai* triple is the P-document's programmatic patriarchal-covenant-formula. The triple recurs only at Gen 17 (the Avraham-covenant), Gen 28:3 (Yitzchaq blesses Ya'aqov), and Gen 35:11 (the Bethel-return), with the Gen 48 recapitulation. The cluster's P-source identification is the standard modern-critical position. **Friedman** *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003; **Carr** *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996.

**The companion *melakhim me-chalatzekha yetze'u* — *kings shall come from your loins* — formula.** The Gen 35:11 verse's full construction pairs *goy u-qhal goyim* with *u-melakhim me-chalatzekha yetze'u* (*and kings shall come from your loins*). The *melakhim-from-loins* formula parallels Gen 17:6 (*u-melakhim mimkha yetze'u*, Avraham), Gen 17:16 (*malkhei amim mi-mennah yihyu*, Sarah). The *kings*-component of the patriarchal-promise is editorially-significant because it anticipates the Israelite-monarchy-period (Saul, David, Solomon, and the Davidic dynastic-promise of 2 Sam 7 and Ps 89). The formula's textual-history is treated in **von Rad** *Theology of the Old Testament* 1957 vol. 1 (on the patriarchal-promise's relationship to the Davidic-covenant).

**Translation choice.** The WoH renders *a nation and a company of nations* (ASV-baseline). The NRSV/NJPS *an assembly of nations* preserves the *qhal*-as-formal-assembly register; the ASV *company of nations* renders *qhal* with the broader *company*. Both are defensible; the WoH choice follows the ASV-baseline. The phrase *u-melakhim me-chalatzekha yetze'u* is rendered *kings shall come out of your loins* (the literal *chalatzekha* — *your loins* — is preserved over the NJPS-style smoothed *your line*).

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 122-124, 242; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 256-258, 553-554; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 326-327; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 377-378; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 124-125, 271-272; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 197-198; Cross *Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic* 1973; von Rad *Theology of the Old Testament* 1957; Friedman *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003; Carr *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996; Kaminsky *Yet I Loved Jacob* 2007; HALOT s.v. *goy*, *qahal*; BDB pp. 156, 874; TDOT II:426-433 (s.v. *goy*), XII:546-561 (s.v. *qahal*).

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the construction *goy u-qhal goyim* is grammatically explicit at Gen 35:11; (b) the cross-corpus formula's recurrence at Gen 17:5-6, 17:16, 17:20, 28:3, 48:4 is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the P-source identification (Friedman, Carr, Westermann) is mainstream modern-critical; (d) the federative-future reading is documented in the standard commentaries.

terms.partOfSpeech: narrative-formula (vav-consecutive 3mp qal + accusative pronoun + named brothers + apposition)
terms.literal: and Esav and Ya'aqov his sons buried him
terms.standardEnglish: and Esau and Jacob his sons buried him (ASV)
terms.wohChoice: and Esav and Ya'aqov his sons buried him (ASV-baseline with WoH transliteration)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 35:29's *va-yiqberu oto Esav v-Ya'aqov banav* — *and Esav and Ya'aqov his sons buried him* — is the second instance of the *reconciled-brothers-bury-father* motif in Genesis, after Gen 25:9.** The motif marks the close of each patriarchal-generation-transition: the previously-estranged brothers of one generation reconcile sufficiently to bury their father together.

**The motif's two patriarchal instances.**

*(i) Gen 25:9* — Yitzchaq and Yishma'el bury Avraham: *va-yiqberu oto Yitzchaq v-Yishma'el banav el-me'arat ha-Makhpelah el-sdeh Efron ben-Tzochar ha-Chitti asher al-pnei Mamre* — *Yitzchaq and Yishma'el his sons buried him in the cave of the Makhpelah, in the field of Efron son of Tzochar the Chitti, which is before Mamre*. The earlier estrangement: Yishma'el was expelled by Sarah at Avraham's reluctant agreement (Gen 21:9-21); his line was sent away into the wilderness; the Avraham-Yishma'el relationship was not narratively-reconciled in the intervening chapters. Yet at Avraham's burial, Yitzchaq and Yishma'el appear together. The narrative-reconciliation is implicit in the joint-burial.

*(ii) Gen 35:29* — Esav and Ya'aqov bury Yitzchaq: *va-yiqberu oto Esav v-Ya'aqov banav*. The reconciliation has been more developed in the intervening narrative: the Gen 33 Esav-Ya'aqov reunion-and-embrace explicitly stages the brothers' reconciliation; the Gen 35:29 burial completes the reconciliation by joint-action at the father's burial.

**The structural-function of the motif.** The pattern is editorially-deliberate: the patriarchal-generation arc closes with the brothers-reconciled-at-the-funeral. The reconciliation is not narrated as a separate event but is **performed** by the joint-burial. The motif's structural-significance:

*(a)* **It validates the patriarchal succession.** The chosen-line continues (Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov; Ya'aqov → Yosef-and-the-twelve-tribes), but the non-chosen-line (Yishma'el, Esav) is not severed from the family. The burial-together establishes that the chosen-line operates within a broader-family framework.
*(b)* **It pre-figures the Yosef-cycle reconciliation.** The motif's recurrence in the patriarchal-cycle prepares the Yosef-cycle's culminating reconciliation: Yosef and his brothers reconcile (Gen 45-50), and Yosef's brothers (and Yosef himself) eventually bury him (Gen 50:25-26, by request; Josh 24:32 narrates the burial-fulfillment generations later).
*(c)* **It registers the joint-acknowledgment of patrimonial obligation.** In ANE family-law, joint-burial of the father is a marker of joint-acknowledgment of the patrimonial-line and shared-honor. The motif's HB-deployment uses the joint-burial as a narrative-marker of post-rivalry-reconciliation.

**The makhpelah cross-corpus connection.** The Gen 35:29 burial-location is implicit (the verse names only the burial-by-Esav-and-Ya'aqov without specifying location), but the cross-corpus tradition places Yitzchaq's burial in the Makhpelah-cave at Hevron — Gen 49:31 explicitly names Yitzchaq and Rivkah as buried there. The cave-of-Makhpelah burial-chain is treated in the central [me-arat-ha-makhpelah-cave-etymology-hebron-tomb](#me-arat-ha-makhpelah-cave-etymology-hebron-tomb) entry.

**The post-patriarchal pattern.** The reconciled-brothers-bury-father motif does not recur explicitly after the patriarchal-cycle — the Yosef-and-brothers post-reconciliation does not yield a joint-Ya'aqov-burial (the funeral-cortège to Hevron at Gen 50:1-14 is led by Yosef and his brothers but the explicit reconciled-brothers-as-burial-agents formula is not deployed). The motif is therefore patriarchal-cycle-specific: it brackets the three patriarchal-generations (Avraham → Yitzchaq, Yitzchaq → Ya'aqov) but does not extend beyond.

**Modern critical scholarship.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 246; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 561-562; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 328; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 381-382; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 274-275; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 199-200. The motif's editorial-deliberateness is the unanimous modern-critical reading. **Janzen** *Abraham and All the Families of the Earth* 1993 develops the motif's theological-significance for the patriarchal-promise's universalizing-dimension.

**The Gen 35:29 chronological-displacement note.** The narrative-order places Yitzchaq's death at Gen 35:29, before the Yosef-cycle begins at Gen 37. By Genesis-chronology Yitzchaq dies twelve-plus years *after* the Yosef-cycle is well-underway (Yosef is sold at age 17 per Gen 37:2; the famine-and-reunion run twenty-plus years past that; Yitzchaq's death at age 180 per Gen 35:28 falls within the Yosef-Egypt period). The narrative-order versus story-time-order discrepancy is editorially-deliberate: the patriarchal-generation arc must close before the Yosef-narrative begins, even though the chronology requires the temporal-overlap. The discrepancy is documented in **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 246 explicitly; **Speiser**, **Westermann**, **Hamilton**, **Skinner** concur.

**Translation choice.** The WoH renders *and Esav and Ya'aqov his sons buried him* (ASV-baseline with WoH transliteration of the proper-names). The formula is short and conventional; no translation-choice is contested. The transliteration *Esav* (rather than ASV *Esau*) and *Ya'aqov* (rather than ASV *Jacob*) is the WoH chapter-convention.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 246; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 561-562; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 328; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 381-382; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 274-275; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 199-200; Janzen *Abraham and All the Families of the Earth* 1993; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 427-428.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *va-yiqberu oto Esav v-Ya'aqov banav* is grammatically explicit at Gen 35:29; (b) the parallel formula at Gen 25:9 (Yitzchaq + Yishma'el bury Avraham) is HB-internal-explicit; (c) the structural-function reading is mainstream modern-critical scholarship; (d) the chronological-displacement is documented in the standard commentaries.

terms.strongs: H5927 (alah — go up, ascend) + H5921 (al — upon) + H430 (Elohim)
terms.partOfSpeech: verb (qal vav-consec 3ms) + preposition + 3ms suffix + divine name
terms.literal: and Elohim went up from upon him
terms.standardEnglish: and God went up from him (ASV); and God ascended from him (KJV-modernized); and God went up from beside him (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: And Elohim went up from him (preserving *Elohim* in transliteration)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 35:13's *va-ya'al me'alav Elohim ba-maqom asher dibber itto* — *and Elohim went up from him in the place where he had spoken with him* — deploys the verb *alah* (*go up, ascend*) of divine departure following an encounter.** The construction is a patriarchal-narrative formula for the **physical-vertical-departure** of the deity after a face-to-face encounter.

**The patriarchal cross-corpus.** The formula *va-ya'al Elohim* (with or without *me'alav*) recurs at:

- **Gen 17:22** (the El Shaddai covenant with Avraham): *va-ya'al Elohim me'al Avraham* — *and Elohim went up from upon Avraham*. The verbal-and-lexical identity to Gen 35:13 is exact: the same verb, the same preposition, the same construction. The two verses are the only patriarchal-narrative occurrences of the formula and are P-source pericope conclusions.
- **Gen 35:13** (here): the Bethel-return covenant conclusion.

The construction-pair forms a P-source bracketing of the Avraham-and-Ya'aqov El Shaddai covenants: the formula opens (with *ani El Shaddai* at Gen 17:1 and 35:11) and closes (with *va-ya'al Elohim me'alav* at Gen 17:22 and 35:13). The two pericopes are formally-paired by the bracketing-formula.

**The broader divine-departure cross-corpus.**

- **Judg 13:20** (the Manoah-and-his-wife encounter with the *mal'akh YHWH*): *va-yhi ba-alot ha-lahav me'al ha-mizbeach ha-shamayma va-ya'al mal'akh YHWH b-lahav ha-mizbeach* — *and as the flame went up from the altar toward heaven, the mal'akh of YHWH went up in the flame of the altar*. The construction uses *alah* of the *mal'akh*'s ascent within the altar-flame; the *ha-shamayma* (heavenward) destination is explicit. The Judg 13 ascent-formula is the most-physically-specific HB ascent-of-divine-figure.
- **Gen 5:24** (Chanokh): *va-yithallekh Chanokh et-ha-Elohim v-eynennu ki-laqach oto Elohim* — *and Chanokh walked with Elohim, and he was not, for Elohim took him*. The construction uses *laqach* (took) rather than *alah*; the *laqach*-of-divine-removal recurs at 2 Kgs 2:1-12 (Eliyahu's removal by whirlwind — *u-Vahasarah... b-sa'arah ha-shamayim* — *Eliyahu went up by whirlwind to the heavens*).
- **Exod 19:20-25** (Mosheh at Sinai): *va-yered YHWH al-har Sinai... va-yikra YHWH l-Mosheh el-rosh ha-har va-ya'al Mosheh* — *YHWH came down on Mount Sinai... and YHWH called Mosheh to the top of the mountain, and Mosheh went up*; the descent-ascent pairing of the deity and the prophet at the Sinai-theophany.
- **2 Kgs 2:11** (Eliyahu): *va-ya'al Eliyahu ba-s'arah ha-shamayim* — *Eliyahu went up by whirlwind to the heavens*.

**The motif's editorial-significance.** The *va-ya'al Elohim me'alav* formula is part of the patriarchal-narrative's editorially-load-bearing **physical-divine-presence-and-departure** vocabulary. The deity does not vanish or simply cease-to-appear; the deity **physically departs vertically** from the encounter-site. The pattern is consistent with the wider HB pattern of physical-deity-vocabulary: the deity appears (*va-yera*, *niglu*), speaks (*dibber*), blesses (*va-yvarekh*), and ascends (*va-ya'al*).

**Modern critical scholarship on the formula.** **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 256-258, 555 (the formula's P-source identification and editorial-frame function); **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 124, 242; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 24-25, 326; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 23-24, 378; **Friedman** *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 (P pericope identification); **Carr** *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996. The modern-critical reading treats the formula as part of the P-document's distinctive divine-encounter-and-departure register.

**Lens-discipline note.** Any project-specific synthesis of the physical-vertical-departure vocabulary as evidence for a particular cosmological-or-craft-departure reading belongs in the wiki and long-form material, not in this glossary entry. The entry documents the mainstream-philological observation: the patriarchal-narrative uses physical-vertical-motion verbs of divine encounter-and-departure. The interpretive-leverage that this observation may carry for the Wheel of Heaven framework is not in this entry.

**Translation choice.** The WoH renders *And Elohim went up from him*. The ASV *and God went up from him* preserves the literal-construction; the WoH choice substitutes *Elohim* in transliteration per the central policy. The NJPS *went up from beside him* uses *beside* for the preposition *me-al-av*; the literal *from upon him* is preserved in the WoH's *from him*.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 124, 242; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 256-258, 555; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 24-25, 326; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 23-24, 378; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 124-125, 272; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 86-87, 197; Friedman *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003; Carr *Reading the Fractures of Genesis* 1996; HALOT s.v. *alah*; BDB pp. 748-755; TDOT XI:76-95.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *va-ya'al... Elohim me'alav* is grammatically explicit; (b) the cross-corpus parallel at Gen 17:22 (the P-bracketing pair) is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the wider HB physical-departure-of-divine-figure formula (Judg 13:20, 2 Kgs 2:11) is HB-internal-explicit; (d) the P-source identification is mainstream modern-critical scholarship.

terms.literal: rings (of ear or nose; the qualifier specifies)
terms.standardEnglish: rings (ASV/KJV); earrings (NJPS/NRSV when in-ear); nose-rings (NJPS/NRSV when in-nose)
terms.wohChoice: rings (ASV-baseline, with the contextual qualifier *in their ears* clarifying)
terms.rationale:
**The lexeme *nezem* (singular) / *nezamim* (plural) names a metal ring worn either as an ear-ring or as a nose-ring; the wearing-position is specified by the contextual qualifier.** At Gen 35:4 the qualifier is explicit (*b-azneihem* — *in their ears*), locating these as ear-rings. The lexeme's cross-corpus distribution reveals an editorially-significant pattern: ear-rings recur as cultic-amuletic objects associated with the construction of unauthorized cult-objects.

**The cross-corpus distribution.**

*Patriarchal-period uses* (nose-rings, ornamental):
- **Gen 24:22** (Eli'ezer gives Rivkah a *nezem zahav beqa mishqalo* — *a gold nezem of one beqa weight*); **Gen 24:30, 47** (*va-yasem ha-nezem al-apah* — *and he put the nezem on her nose* — explicit nose-ring placement). The Eli'ezer-Rivkah cycle's *nezem* is a nose-ring as a betrothal-gift; the ornamental-and-marital register.
- **Gen 35:4** (here): the qualifier *b-azneihem* explicitly locates these as ear-rings, distinct from Rivkah's nose-ring.

*Ear-rings as cultic-amulet material:*
- **Exod 32:2-3** (the golden-calf construction): *paraqu nizmei ha-zahav asher b-aznei n'sheikhem b-neikhem u-vnoteikhem v-haviu elay* — *break off the gold nezamim that are in the ears of your wives, your sons, and your daughters, and bring them to me*. Aharon then *va-yatzar oto ba-cheret va-ya'asehu egel masekhah* — *fashioned it with a graving-tool and made it a molten calf*. The ear-rings are the construction-material of the calf; the cultic-amulet-association is unambiguous.
- **Judg 8:24-26** (Gideon's spoil): *tnu-li ish nezem sh'lalo ki nizmei zahav la-hem ki Yishm'elim heim* — *each give me the nezem of his spoil; for they had gold nezamim, for they were Yishm'elites*. Gideon then constructs the *ephod* from the gold nezamim: *va-ya'as oto Gid'on l-ephod* — *and Gideon made it into an ephod* — which the Israelites then *zanu... acharav* — *played the harlot after it* (cultic-apostasy language). The ear-rings → ephod → cultic-apostasy chain parallels Exod 32's ear-rings → calf → cultic-apostasy chain.
- **Hos 2:15 [MT 2:13]** (the polemical-divorce metaphor): *u-pakadti aleha et-y'mei ha-be'alim asher taqtir lahem va-ta'ad nizmah v-chelyatah* — *I will punish her for the days of the Be'alim, to whom she burnt incense, and decked herself with her nezem and her jewelry, and went after her lovers*. The *nezem* is here associated with the Ba'al-cult ornamentation; the cultic-association is explicit.

*Ornamental and figurative uses:*
- **Prov 11:22** (*nezem zahav b-af chazir* — *a gold nezem in a pig's snout*; a proverbial-comparison).
- **Prov 25:12** (*nezem zahav v-chali-ketem mokhiach chakham al-ozen shoma'at* — *a gold nezem and an ornament of fine gold is a wise reprover to a listening ear*).
- **Isa 3:21** (the catalog of women's ornaments includes nose-rings); **Ezek 16:12** (the bride-imagery of Yerushalayim: *va-eten nezem al-apekh v-agilim al-aznayikh v-ateret tif'eret b-roshekh* — *I put a nezem on your nose, and earrings on your ears, and a crown of beauty on your head* — distinguishing *nezem* (nose) from *agilim* (earrings) explicitly; one of the rare HB texts that lexically-distinguishes the two ring-types).
- **Job 42:11** (Job's friends each bring him a *nezem zahav* — gold ring — at the restoration).

**The editorial-pattern.** The Gen 35:4 burial-of-ear-rings-with-foreign-elohim sits within a broader HB pattern: ear-rings function as cultic-amulet-material that can be converted into cult-objects. The pattern is editorially-deliberate: at the moment of cultic-renunciation (Gen 35:2-4, Ya'aqov's covenant-renewal at Bethel), the ear-rings are buried alongside the foreign elohim — the cult-amulet-association makes them objects of comparable significance for renunciation. The Exod 32 and Judg 8 parallels confirm that ear-rings were materially-and-cultically linked to cult-object construction in the broader Israelite milieu.

**The Egyptian-and-ANE-comparative material.** Ear-rings as amulets are widely-attested in the Egyptian-and-ANE material culture: the Egyptian *udjat*-ring and the Mesopotamian protective-amulet ear-rings are documented at sites from Old-Babylonian Mari through Late-Bronze-Age Ugarit and Iron-Age Cypro-Phoenician contexts. **Albright** *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968; **King and Stager** *Life in Biblical Israel* 2001; **Day** *Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan* 2000 (on the broader Levantine cultic-jewelry tradition). The Gen 35:4 / Exod 32 / Judg 8 cluster reflects this broader ANE pattern.

**Modern critical scholarship on Gen 35:4 specifically.** **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 241; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 553-554; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 322-323; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 375-376; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 271; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 197. The cultic-amulet reading is the mainstream modern-critical interpretation; the ear-rings are not merely jewelry being-incidentally-renounced but are themselves cultic-amulets being-buried-with-the-cult-objects-they-are-associated-with.

**Translation choice.** The WoH preserves *rings* (ASV-baseline) with the contextual qualifier *in their ears* clarifying the wearing-position. The NJPS/NRSV *earrings* makes the wearing-position explicit in the noun-translation; the ASV-baseline relies on the explicit qualifier. The WoH choice maintains the literal-translation-with-qualifier rather than the smoothed compound-noun.

**Scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 241; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 553-554; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 322-323; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 375-376; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 271; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 197; King and Stager *Life in Biblical Israel* 2001 (on Israelite jewelry-and-amulet material culture); Albright *Yahweh and the Gods of Canaan* 1968; Day *Yahweh and the Gods and Goddesses of Canaan* 2000; HALOT s.v. *nezem*; BDB p. 633; TDOT IX:316-321.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme *nezamim* is grammatically explicit and the wearing-qualifier *b-azneihem* is grammatically explicit at Gen 35:4; (b) the cross-corpus ear-ring-as-cultic-amulet pattern at Exod 32:2-3, Judg 8:24-26, Hos 2:15 is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the modern-critical cultic-amulet reading is documented in the standard commentaries; (d) the ANE-comparative material is documented in King and Stager 2001, Albright 1968.

terms.literal: paragraph(-break) in the middle of a verse
terms.standardEnglish: paragraph break in the middle of a verse (technical-Masoretic; no smoothed-translation register)
terms.wohChoice: *piska b-emtza pasuq* (technical-Masoretic term preserved; descriptive gloss in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**The *piska b-emtza pasuq* — *paragraph(-break) in the middle of a verse* — is a Masoretic-textual phenomenon in which the MT marks a paragraph-break (*p'tucha* or *s'tumah*, designated (פ) or (ס)) in the middle of a verse rather than at the verse-end.** The phenomenon occurs approximately twenty-eight times in the Masoretic text (the count varies by manuscript-tradition and edition); Gen 35:22 is one of the most-discussed instances and is the first canonical-occurrence.

**Gen 35:22 specifically.** The verse reads *va-yhi bi-shkhon Yisra'el ba-aretz ha-hi va-yelekh Re'uven va-yishkav et-Bilhah pilegesh aviv va-yishma Yisra'el* (פ) *va-yihyu b-nei Ya'aqov shneim asar* — *And it came to pass, while Yisra'el dwelt in that land, that Re'uven went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and Yisra'el heard. And the sons of Ya'aqov were twelve.* The (פ) marks a *parashah p'tuchah* — open-paragraph-break — between *va-yishma Yisra'el* (*Yisra'el heard*) and *va-yihyu b-nei Ya'aqov shneim asar* (*and the sons of Ya'aqov were twelve*). The break is editorially-significant: it interrupts a single verse mid-sentence, marking the gravity of the deed and (per the rabbinic tradition) Ya'aqov's silence.

**The cross-corpus distribution of the phenomenon.** The principal *piska'ot b-emtza pasuq* in the MT include:

- **Gen 4:8** (Cain's invitation to Hevel: *va-yomer Qayin el-Hevel achiv* (פ) *va-yhi bi-hyotam ba-sadeh* — *and Cain said to Hevel his brother* (break) *and it came to pass when they were in the field*). The break marks the missing-direct-speech: what did Cain say? The break invites the gap.
- **Gen 35:22** (here, Re'uven's deed).
- **Num 25:19** (the close of the Bil'am cycle and the transition to the post-plague census; the paragraph-break-mid-verse).
- **Deut 2:8** (the transition from the Edomite-passage to the Moabite-encounter).
- **2 Sam 12:13** (Natan's confrontation of David: *chatati l-YHWH* — *I have sinned against YHWH*; the break before Natan's reply).
- **2 Sam 19:1** (David's lament for Avshalom).
- **1 Kgs 5:14** (the transition between Solomon-narratives).
- Approximately twenty additional instances across the Prophets and Writings.

The most-comprehensive modern catalogue is **Yeivin** *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* (SBL Masoretic Studies 5, 1980) §157-158, listing the documented instances and the variant-readings across the principal MT manuscripts (Aleppo Codex, Leningrad Codex, Cairo Prophets Codex).

**The rabbinic reception of Gen 35:22.** The rabbinic tradition treats the (פ) at Gen 35:22 as marking the deed's gravity and Ya'aqov's silence. The principal rabbinic-discussions:

- **b. Shabbat 55b** — Rav Sh'mu'el bar Nachmani in the name of Rabbi Yonatan: *kol ha-omer Re'uven chata eno ela to'eh* — *whoever says Reuben sinned is mistaken*. The Talmud's exegetical-defense of Re'uven (reading the deed as a less-than-literal succession-rebuke) is partly motivated by the (פ)-paragraph-break: the break is taken to signal a discontinuity that softens the literal-reading. The Talmud reads the deed as Re'uven's protest against Ya'aqov's bed being moved to Bilhah's tent rather than to Le'ah's tent after Rachel's death; the protest involves Re'uven physically-displacing the bed but not literally-lying-with-Bilhah. The reading is preserved through Rashi (at Gen 35:22) into the wider Jewish exegetical tradition.
- **b. Sotah 13a** — the broader discussion of patriarchal-failings and the editorial-marking of grave-deeds; the Re'uven deed is treated within this cluster.
- **b. Megillah 18a** — the discussion of paragraph-break significance in scriptural-reading; the Gen 35:22 instance is referenced.

**Modern critical scholarship on the phenomenon.**

- **Yeivin** *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* 1980 — the foundational modern monograph; treats the *piska b-emtza pasuq* as a distinct Masoretic technique, possibly preserving older traditions of verse-division that pre-date the standard MT verse-divisions.
- **Tov** *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 1992 / 2012 (3rd ed.) — treats the phenomenon within the wider context of Masoretic paragraph-marking and the relationship between verse-divisions, paragraph-divisions, and ancient interpretive-traditions.
- **Würthwein** *The Text of the Old Testament* 1995 (ET) — covers the Masoretic apparatus including the *piska b-emtza pasuq* as part of the Tiberian-Masorah's textual-philological-marking system.
- **Korpel and Oesch** eds. *Delimitation Criticism: A New Tool in Biblical Scholarship* 2000; *Studies in Scriptural Unit Division* 2002 — the modern-monograph development of delimitation-criticism as a field, with extensive treatment of *piska b-emtza pasuq* and related phenomena.

**The phenomenon's editorial-function.** Modern critical scholarship reads the *piska b-emtza pasuq* as marking one or more of:
*(i)* a discontinuity in the source-material (the paragraph-break preserves an older textual-seam that has been smoothed over but not erased in the MT verse-division);
*(ii)* a deliberate-editorial pause marking a thematic-break or emotional-gravity (Gen 35:22 reading);
*(iii)* a gap in the source-text where material may have been deliberately omitted (Gen 4:8 reading);
*(iv)* an interpretive-tradition's marker (the rabbinic reception interprets the break as carrying interpretive-weight).

**Translation choice.** The WoH preserves the technical-Masoretic term *piska b-emtza pasuq* in transliteration in commentary; the chapter-text preserves the (פ) marker after *va-yishma Yisra'el* per the MT. The phenomenon is not directly translated in the surface-text but is preserved in the source-text field of the chapter JSON and is documented in the per-verse commentary and this glossary entry.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Yeivin *Introduction to the Tiberian Masorah* SBL Masoretic Studies 5 (1980); Tov *Textual Criticism of the Hebrew Bible* 1992/2012; Würthwein *The Text of the Old Testament* 1995 (ET); Korpel and Oesch eds. *Delimitation Criticism: A New Tool in Biblical Scholarship* 2000; Korpel and Oesch eds. *Studies in Scriptural Unit Division* 2002; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 243-244 (on Gen 35:22 specifically); Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 558-559; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 327-328; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 380-381; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 199; Rashi at Gen 35:22; b. Shabbat 55b; b. Sotah 13a; b. Megillah 18a; Genesis Rabbah 82:11.

The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the Masoretic-textual phenomenon is documentary and verifiable by examination of the MT manuscripts; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is catalogued by Yeivin 1980; (c) the rabbinic-reception is documented in the named sources; (d) the modern-critical readings (Yeivin, Tov, Würthwein, Korpel and Oesch) are mainstream textual-criticism scholarship; (e) the Gen 35:22 specific application is HB-internal-explicit (the (פ) is preserved in the MT manuscripts).

terms.strongs: H441 (aluf — chief, leader; from H502 alaph, to learn / be familiar)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine singular (chief / clan-head); also collective use (clan)
terms.literal: chief, chieftain, clan-head; (collective) clan
terms.standardEnglish: chief (ASV); duke (KJV); clan / chief (NRSV); clan (NJPS); clan-chief (Alter)
terms.wohChoice: chief (ASV-baseline; the technical clan-chief register preserved; the tribal-confederation pre-statehood significance surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**The lexeme *aluf* (אַלּוּף) names a tribal-clan chief in the Edomite/Horite material distinctively. Gen 36 deploys the term approximately forty times (vv 15-19, 21, 29-30, 40-43) as the technical title of an Edomite or Horite clan-head, with restricted distribution elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible.** The lexeme's editorial-load is twofold: (a) it marks a *pre-monarchic* tribal-confederation register, structurally contrasted with the v 31 *melakhim* (kings) who succeed the *alufim* in the chapter's diachronic compression; (b) it identifies the Edomite and Horite peoples as ordered by clan-leadership rather than by hereditary-monarchy, consistent with the historical-archaeological picture of pre-7th-century Iron Age Edomite social organization.

**The technical clan-chief sense and its cross-corpus distribution.** Outside Gen 36 / 1 Chr 1 (the parallel king-list), the lexeme in the clan-chief sense appears at:

- **Exod 15:15** (*az nivhalu alufei Edom eilei Mo'av yochazemo ra'ad* — *then were the chiefs of Edom dismayed; the mighty men of Mo'av, trembling took hold of them*) — the Song of the Sea couples *alufei Edom* with *eilei Mo'av*, treating the Edomite clan-chiefs as the regional-political-counterparts of the Mo'avite mighty-men. The Song of the Sea is widely treated as one of the earliest Hebrew compositions (cf. Cross & Freedman *Studies in Ancient Yahwistic Poetry* 1975) and preserves the pre-monarchic tribal-confederation register.
- **Jer 13:21** (*ki at limmadt otam alayikh alufim l-rosh* — *for you have taught them, the *alufim* over you as head*) — the prophetic-judgment context preserves the technical sense (chiefs as overlords).
- **Zech 9:7** (*v-haya k-aluf bi-Yhudah* — *and he shall be as a *aluf* in Yehudah*) — the *aluf* used metaphorically for tribal-leadership in Yehudah.
- **Zech 12:5-6** (*v-amru alufei Yhudah b-libbam* — *and the *alufim* of Yehudah shall say in their heart*) — the *alufim*-of-Yehudah usage is post-exilic and reads as a deliberate-archaizing use of the pre-monarchic register.
- **1 Chr 1:51-54** — the parallel Edomite-clan-chief list reproducing Gen 36:40-43.

**The friend-companion sense distribution.** A homophonous (and possibly cognate, possibly distinct) lexeme *aluf* in the sense *familiar friend, intimate companion* appears at: **Ps 55:14 [MT 55:14]** (*v-atah enosh k-erki alufi u-meyudai* — *but it was you, a man my equal, my *aluf* and my acquaintance*); **Prov 2:17** (*ozevet aluf n'ureha* — *forsaking the *aluf* of her youth*); **Prov 16:28** (*v-nirgan mafrid aluf* — *and a whisperer separates *aluf*-friends*); **Prov 17:9** (*u-shoneh b-davar mafrid aluf* — *he that repeats a matter separates *aluf*-friends*); **Mic 7:5** (*me-shokhevet cheiqekha sh'mor pitchei pikha* — and the surrounding *al-tivt'chu b-rea al-tivat'chu b-aluf*); **Jer 3:4** (*ha-lo me-attah qarat-li avi aluf n'urai attah* — *will you not from now on cry to me, My father, you are the *aluf* of my youth*); **Jer 11:19** (*v-ani k-keves aluf yuval li-tboach* — *and I was like a gentle [aluf] lamb led to the slaughter*). The friend-companion sense is treated by **BDB** p. 48 (s.v. *aluf* II) and **HALOT** s.v. *aluf* as a separate-lexeme from the chief-sense (BDB I); modern lexica are divided on whether the two senses derive from a single root (*alaph* — to learn / be familiar) or from distinct roots.

**The etymology.** The root *ʾ-l-p* (alaph) carries the sense *to learn, to be familiar, to be tame*; the noun *eleph* (אֶלֶף) also means *thousand* and *cattle-herd / cattle*. The clan-chief sense is sometimes traced to the *eleph* = *cattle / herd* / *military-unit-of-thousand* semantic — the *aluf* as the head-of-the-thousand or head-of-the-herd. **BDB** p. 48 reads the chief-sense as derivative of the cattle-herd / military-unit sense; **HALOT** s.v. *aluf* prefers a separate-root analysis. **TDOT** I:411-413 (s.v. *eleph* / *aluf*) provides the most thorough treatment.

**The *aluf*-as-eponym pattern.** Gen 36:40-43 lists eleven *alufim* (Timna, Alvah, Yetet, Oholivamah, Elah, Pinon, Qenaz, Teman, Mivtzar, Magdi'el, Iram) *l-mishp'chotam li-mqomotam bi-shmotam* — *according to their families, after their places, by their names*. The list reads as a **toponymic-eponym** catalogue: each *aluf* names both a clan (descended-from-this-eponym) and a geographical territory (named-after-this-eponym). The pattern is structurally parallel to the Horite *alufim* list at vv 20-30 (where each Horite *aluf* is similarly the eponym of a clan-and-territory). **Bartlett** *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 86-102 reads the Gen 36 *alufim* lists as preserving a pre-7th-century Iron Age tribal-confederation organization, with the *alufim* as the territorial-political-units that pre-date the centralized Edomite monarchy attested in the Neo-Assyrian record (9th-7th centuries BCE).

**The chapter's diachronic compression: *alufim* → *melakhim*.** The chapter's editorial-structure stages the *alufim* (vv 15-19, 21, 29-30) → *melakhim* (vv 31-39) → *alufim* (vv 40-43) sequence. The pattern is variously read as: (a) two distinct *alufim* lists framing the eight-kings-list (Westermann *Genesis 12-36* pp. 565-567); (b) a tribal-confederation phase (the v 15-19 / v 20-30 *alufim*) succeeded by a monarchic phase (vv 31-39 *melakhim*) succeeded by a post-monarchic tribal-confederation phase (vv 40-43); (c) the two *alufim* lists drawn from distinct sources (the v 40-43 list possibly from a different geographical-source than the v 15-19 list; cf. Speiser *AB Genesis* pp. 283-284 noting the differing-names between the v 15-19 *alufim* — Teman, Omar, Tzefo, etc. — and the v 40-43 *alufim* — Timna, Alvah, Yetet, etc.). The diachronic-compression reading sits comfortably with the v 31 documentary anchor (covered by `edom-kings-before-israel-monarchy-documentary-anchor`): the chapter preserves an Edomite-internal political-history (clans → kings → clans) embedded within the broader Genesis-as-prologue-to-Israel framework.

**Translation choice.** The WoH preserves ASV-baseline *chief* throughout Gen 36 (vv 15-19, 21, 29-30, 40-43). The KJV *duke* is anachronistic (the English *duke* carries medieval-European-feudal connotations absent in the Hebrew). The NJPS / NRSV *clan* renders the collective sense rather than the individual-chief sense and loses the singular-chief register that the Hebrew *aluf* preserves at each occurrence. The ASV *chief* sits at the intersection of the singular-leader register and the standard-English-translation-tradition; it is the WoH-baseline.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 250-253; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 564-570; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 336-340; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 396-405; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 282-284; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 203-205; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 86-102; Cross & Freedman *Studies in Ancient Yahwistic Poetry* 1975 (on the Song of the Sea register); HALOT s.v. *aluf*; BDB p. 48; TDOT I:411-413. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the lexeme is grammatically explicit at each occurrence; (b) the cross-corpus distribution is verifiable by lexical search; (c) the *alufim*-vs-*melakhim* diachronic distinction is HB-internal-explicit (the v 31 formula structurally separates the two); (d) the tribal-confederation reading is mainstream modern scholarship (Bartlett 1989); (e) the dual-sense (chief vs friend) is documented in BDB, HALOT, TDOT.

terms.source: וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ לֶאֱלִיפַז בֶּן־עֵשָׂו וַתֵּלֶד לֶאֱלִיפַז אֶת־עֲמָלֵק
terms.translit: ve-Timna haytah pilegesh le-Elifaz ben-Esav va-teled le-Elifaz et-Amaleq
terms.partOfSpeech: narrative-genealogical notice; the lexical-core is the proper-noun *Amaleq* situated within an Esavite-secondary-line genealogy
terms.literal: and Timna was concubine to Elifaz the son of Esav, and she bore to Elifaz Amaleq
terms.standardEnglish: and Timna was concubine to Eliphaz Esau's son; and she bare to Eliphaz Amalek (ASV); ... bare to Eliphaz Amalek (KJV); ... Timna was a concubine of Esau's son Eliphaz; she bore Amalek to Eliphaz (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: and Timna was concubine to Elifaz the son of Esav; and she bore to Elifaz Amaleq (ASV-baseline preserved; the archetypal-enemy-of-Israel cross-corpus significance surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 36:12 is structurally the seed-verse of the Amaleq-as-archetypal-enemy-of-Israel tradition. The verse names Amaleq as the son of Elifaz (Esav's firstborn) by Timna his concubine, placing Amaleq within the Esavite secondary-line — making Amaleq the grandson of Esav and a great-great-grandson of Avraham through the collateral-line. The genealogical-placement is editorially-load-bearing: the archetypal enemy of Israel is, by descent, Israel's near-kin.**

**The cross-corpus Amaleq-tradition.** The Amaleq-cycle runs across the entire Pentateuchal-and-Deuteronomistic corpus with editorial-singularity:

- **Exod 17:8-16** — *va-yavo Amaleq va-yillachem im-Yisra'el bi-Rfidim* — *and Amaleq came and fought with Yisra'el at Rfidim*. The first military encounter between Israel and Amaleq, immediately after the Exodus. Mosheh extends his arms (held up by Aharon and Chur) while Yehoshu'a fights; the victory is editorially-coded as YHWH-aided. The chapter closes with the formula *milchamah la-YHWH ba-Amaleq mi-dor dor* — *YHWH has war with Amaleq from generation to generation* — and the divine-oath: *macho emcheh et-zekher Amaleq mi-tachat ha-shamayim* — *I will utterly blot out the memory of Amaleq from under heaven*. The verse pair is the foundational editorial statement of Amaleq as the perpetual-enemy.
- **Num 24:20** (Bil'am's oracle) — *reshit goyim Amaleq v-acharito adei oved* — *Amaleq was the first of the goyim, but his latter end shall be unto destruction*. The Bil'am-oracle inserts Amaleq into the prophetic-eschatological frame.
- **Deut 25:17-19** — *zakhor et asher asah lekha Amaleq ba-derekh b-tzetkhem mi-Mitzrayim... timcheh et-zekher Amaleq mi-tachat ha-shamayim lo tishkach* — *remember what Amaleq did to you on the way as you came out of Egypt... you shall blot out the memory of Amaleq from under heaven; you shall not forget*. The Deuteronomic *zakhor et asher asah lekha Amaleq* command becomes the editorial-keystone for the post-Pentateuchal Amaleq-tradition.
- **1 Sam 15** — Shemu'el's command to Sha'ul to enact the *cherem* on Amaleq and on Agag king of the Amaleqites; Sha'ul's partial-execution (he spares Agag and the best livestock) becomes the prophetic-rejection-of-Sha'ul. The chapter ends with Shemu'el's hewing-of-Agag (*va-y'shasef Sh'mu'el et-Agag lifnei YHWH ba-Gilgal* — *and Shemu'el hewed Agag in pieces before YHWH at Gilgal*).
- **1 Sam 30** — David at Tziqlag: an Amaleqite raid burns Tziqlag; David pursues and defeats the raiding-party, rescuing the captives. The episode is structurally-significant in the David-vs-Sha'ul-vs-Amaleq triangle.
- **2 Sam 1:1-16** — the Amaleqite (*ger Amaleqi anokhi* — *I am an Amaleqite sojourner*) who reports Sha'ul's death and is executed by David.
- **Est 3:1** — Haman the son of Hammedata *ha-Agagi* — *the Agagite* — the descendant-of-Agag designation places Haman as the Amaleqite-archetype-revenant within the post-exilic Esther-narrative. Mordokhai's refusal-to-bow is editorially-coded as the Israelite-Amaleqite enmity's last canonical episode.
- **1 Chr 1:36** — the parallel-genealogy lists Amaleq differently: *Elifaz ... and Teman, and Omar, Tzefi, and Ga'tam, Qenaz, and Timna, and Amaleq*, treating Amaleq and Timna as both sons of Elifaz (omitting the Gen 36:12 concubine-detail). The Chronicler's variant has been read as: (a) a textual-error / parablepsis; (b) a deliberate-omission of the concubine-detail (the Chronicler harmonizing-upward); (c) a separate genealogical tradition that the Gen 36 redactor has elaborated. Modern critical scholarship (Klein *1 Chronicles* Hermeneia 2006 p. 75; Knoppers *1 Chronicles 1-9* AB 2003 pp. 281-282) prefers reading (c) — the Gen 36:12 concubine-detail is editorially-deliberate, downgrading the Amaleq-line within the Esavite hierarchy.

**The Timna-figure cross-reference.** Gen 36:22 names *achot Lotan Timna* — *Lotan's sister Timna* — among the Horite sons of Lotan. The same Timna (per the dominant modern critical reading; cf. Sarna *JPS Genesis* p. 250; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* p. 562; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* p. 338) is both the Horite *achot Lotan* of v 22 and the concubine-of-Elifaz of v 12. The double-identification places Amaleq's mother as a Horite — meaning Amaleq is matrilineally Horite and patrilineally Esavite, structurally embedded at the seam between the displacing-Esavite and the displaced-Horite populations. The rabbinic tradition (Genesis Rabbah 82:14; Sanhedrin 99b — Timna seeks to convert to Avraham's-line and is rejected; she becomes Elifaz's concubine; the bad-treatment of a would-be-proselyte produces Amaleq the archetypal-enemy) reads the double-identification as editorially-significant in a different register.

**The 1 Chr 1:36 textual variant: Amaleq as Elifaz's direct son.** As noted above, 1 Chr 1:36 treats Amaleq as a direct-son of Elifaz, omitting the Timna-concubine detail. The Gen 36:12 expansion is read by modern critical scholarship (Westermann, Speiser, Hamilton, Sarna) as preserving an editorially-deliberate concubine-status downgrade of the Amaleq-line. The downgrade is structurally-significant: Amaleq is not merely a son of Esav's firstborn; he is the son of Esav's firstborn's concubine — a doubly-subordinated genealogical position. The editorial-effect: the archetypal-enemy of Israel is, even within his own Esavite lineage, a marginal figure.

**The Saul-Agag-Esther vector.** The Amaleq-tradition's most-elaborated thread runs through the Sha'ul / Agag / Esther sequence: Sha'ul spares Agag (1 Sam 15) → Agagite Haman threatens the post-exilic Yehudim (Est 3:1) → Mordokhai (a descendant of Sha'ul's tribe Binyamin per Est 2:5) executes the Esther-narrative reversal. The Sha'ul-Mordokhai-Esther / Agag-Haman / Yisra'el-Amaleq triple-parallel is editorially-deliberate. **Berlin** *Esther* JPS 2001 and **Levenson** *Esther* OTL 1997 treat the Sha'ul-Mordokhai parallel as the structural-backbone of the Esther narrative. The Gen 36:12 verse is the genealogical-source of this entire Amaleq-thread.

**The historical-archaeological Amaleq.** The Amaleqites are not attested in the extra-biblical ANE record under that name; the standard scholarly identification places them as a pastoral-nomadic confederation of the Negev / northern-Sinai / southern-Trans-Jordan region (cf. Knauf *Midian* 1988; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* 1989 pp. 80-81; Levin *The Search for Ancient Israel* 2003). The Genesis-and-Exodus-Amaleq is a southern-tribal-confederation neighbor-and-rival of Israel, structurally-similar to the Midianites and the Yishma'elites; the elaborate-enemy-archetype is editorially-developed across the canonical-redaction process.

**Lens-discipline note.** The Wheel of Heaven project may have a distinctive reading of the Amaleq-archetype (the why-of-the-perpetual-enmity, the cosmic-or-civilizational layer of the Amaleq-Israel conflict, the proto-something reading). Any such reading is **wiki-reserved**: it does not belong in the translation apparatus. This entry documents the structural-genealogical significance of Gen 36:12 (Amaleq as Esav's grandson, Timna's son, doubly-subordinated within the Esavite lineage) and the cross-corpus Amaleq-tradition (Exod 17, Deut 25, Num 24, 1 Sam 15, 1 Sam 30, 2 Sam 1, Est 3, 1 Chr 1) without WoH-distinctive synthesis. The lens-discipline pattern (ratified at chapter 35) reserves the WoH-distinctive synthesis for the wiki.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 247-251; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 561-562; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 337-339; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 393-396; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 281-282; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 203-204; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 80-81; Klein *1 Chronicles* Hermeneia 2006 p. 75; Knoppers *1 Chronicles 1-9* AB 2003 pp. 281-282; Berlin *Esther* JPS 2001; Levenson *Esther* OTL 1997; Knauf *Midian* 1988; Levin *The Search for Ancient Israel* 2003; Genesis Rabbah 82:14; b. Sanhedrin 99b; Rashi at Gen 36:12. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the genealogical-placement of Amaleq within the Esavite-secondary-line is grammatically explicit at Gen 36:12; (b) the cross-corpus Amaleq-archetype tradition is HB-internal-explicit at Exod 17, Deut 25, 1 Sam 15, Est 3; (c) the Timna-double-identification (v 12 concubine / v 22 Horite-sister) is HB-internal-textual; (d) the 1 Chr 1:36 textual variant is documentary; (e) the historical-archaeological Amaleqite-identification is mainstream modern scholarship (Bartlett, Knauf, Levin). Any WoH-distinctive reading of the Amaleq-archetype is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.literal: the Horite / the Horites / the sons of Se'ir the Horite
terms.standardEnglish: Horite / Horites (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS — transliterated, no in-text gloss); occasionally Hurrian (modern scholarly)
terms.wohChoice: *Chori* / *Chorim* (transliterated, matching WoH proper-noun convention; the Hurrian-vs-troglodyte philological question surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**The HB *Chorim* (the Horites, the pre-Edomite inhabitants of Se'ir per Gen 14:6, Deut 2:12, 22, Gen 36:20-30, 1 Chr 1:38-42) are a philologically-contested ethnonym whose identification is one of the most-discussed open questions in Pentateuchal ethnography. The lexical-historical question — are the HB *Chorim* identical to the Hurrians, or are they an independent Canaanite-region group folk-etymologized from *chor* (cave) as cave-dwellers / troglodytes? — is genuinely unresolved in modern scholarship.**

**Reading (a): The Hurrian-identification.** The first major modern reading identifies the HB *Chorim* with the **Hurrians** — the Mesopotamian-Mitanni-origin people attested in cuneiform sources from the 18th century BCE forward, with a Late Bronze Age kingdom of Mitanni (c. 1500-1300 BCE) centered in northern Mesopotamia and a substantial Hurrian population scattered across Anatolia, Syria, and the Levant (Nuzi, Alalakh, Ugarit). **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 282-283 is the foundational modern-critical statement of this reading; **Speiser** *Hurrians and Subarians* JAOS Supplement 13 (1948) provides the lexical-historical argument that Hebrew *Chori* = Akkadian/Hurrian *Hurri* (the same root, with the Hebrew-script lacking the *h*/*ch* distinction that the Hurrian preserves). **Albright** *BASOR* 163 (1961); **Wilhelm** *The Hurrians* 1989 (ET); **De Vaux** *The Early History of Israel* 1978 (ET) all endorse some version of the Hurrian-identification. On this reading, the HB *Chorim* are remnants of the Mitanni-era Hurrian diffusion into the southern Levant, surviving as a pre-Edomite population in Se'ir.

**Reading (b): The Canaanite-region distinct-group / cave-dweller folk-etymology.** The second major modern reading treats the HB *Chorim* as a distinct group native to the Se'ir-region, with the ethnonym derived from Hebrew *chor* (חוֹר — *hole, cave*) in a folk-etymological *cave-dweller / troglodyte* sense. The reading is supported by: (i) the **Deut 2:12, 22** geographical-pairing (*u-v-Se'ir yashvu ha-Chorim l-fanim u-vnei Esav yirashum* — *and in Se'ir the Chorim dwelt formerly, and the children of Esav displaced them*; the verses' editorial-framing places the Chorim as autochthonous-pre-Edomite inhabitants of a mountainous-cave-rich region); (ii) the absence of clear Hurrian-cultural-markers in the Gen 36:20-30 Horite-clan list (the Horite names — Lotan, Shoval, Tziv'on, Anah, Dishon, Etzer, Dishan — do not preserve identifiable Hurrian etymologies; cf. Westermann *Genesis 12-36* p. 564 — *die Hurriter-Hypothese ist sehr fraglich*); (iii) the Greek translation tradition (LXX *Χορραιοι* / Vulgate *Horraeus*) treats the ethnonym as a transliterated proper noun without Hurrian-identification. **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 p. 564; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 395-396; **Bartlett** *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 78-81 endorse this reading. Bartlett 1989 pp. 78-81 is the most-thorough modern treatment, concluding that the Hurrian-identification is *not supported by the onomastic evidence of the Gen 36 list*.

**Reading (c): A composite — folk-etymological label overlaid on a possibly-Hurrian historical-substratum.** A third reading treats the HB *Chorim* as a folk-etymological label (*cave-dweller / troglodyte*) applied to a population whose historical-substratum may or may not have been Hurrian. On this reading the question is partly-unresolvable: the surface-lexical evidence supports the cave-dweller folk-etymology (the names in Gen 36:20-30 are not identifiably Hurrian), but the broader Bronze Age Hurrian-diffusion may have left traces in the Se'ir region that the HB's editors lost track of. **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 105-106 (at Gen 14:6); **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 336 lean toward this position.

**The chapter-14 cross-reference.** Gen 14:6 (the war of the four kings) names *ha-Chorim b-hararam Se'ir* — *the Chorim in their Mount Se'ir* — as one of the regional-populations defeated by Kedorla'omer. The chapter-14 narrative places the Chorim in Se'ir prior to the patriarchal-period, consistent with Deut 2:12, 22's editorial-framing of the Chorim as the displaced-autochthonous inhabitants whom the Edomites later succeeded. The Gen 36 chapter then provides the genealogical-detail of the Horite clans (vv 20-30) and their integration with the Esavite line (Oholivamah daughter of Anah son of Tziv'on at v 2 / v 14 / v 25). The three-text cluster (Gen 14:6, Gen 36:20-30, Deut 2:12, 22) is the HB's complete documentation of the Horite-tradition.

**The 2 Chr 25:14 reference.** A late post-exilic reference: *va-yhi acharei vo Amatzyahu me-hakkot et-Adomim va-yave et-elohei bnei Se'ir va-ya'amidem lo le-elohim... va-yishtachu lifneihem v-l-hem y-qatter* — *and Amatzyahu, after he came from killing the Edomites, brought the gods of the children of Se'ir, and set them up for his gods... and bowed down before them, and burned incense to them*. The verse references the gods-of-the-children-of-Se'ir as the local-pantheon of the Edomite-Horite population in the 8th century BCE. The reference is consistent with both readings (a) and (b): a distinct local-pantheon may reflect either a Hurrian-substratum-cult or a Canaanite-regional-cult.

**The *Chivvi*-where-text-expects-*Chori* problem at Gen 36:2.** The MT at Gen 36:2 names Oholivamah's grandfather Tziv'on as *ha-Chivvi* (the Hivite), whereas at Gen 36:20 the same Tziv'on appears among the sons of Se'ir *ha-Chori* (the Horite). The Samaritan Pentateuch and the LXX at Gen 36:2 read *ha-Chori* (correcting the MT to match v 20). Modern critical scholarship (BHS apparatus; Westermann p. 561; Speiser pp. 280-281; Hamilton pp. 393-394; Skinner *ICC* p. 432) generally treats the MT *ha-Chivvi* as a scribal-error or tradition-variant for *ha-Chori*. The Hivite-vs-Horite confusion is independent of the broader Hivite-vs-Hittite confusion (Gen 34:2 Chivvi Shechem; covered by `bnei-het-hittites-canaanite-anatolian-question` and the chapter-34 overlay material) and is a chapter-36-specific text-critical question.

**The translation-normalization decision.** The chapter-14 stable text uses *the Horites* (ASV-baseline transliteration with full English-Christian-translation-tradition spelling). The chapter-36 draft uses *Chori* / *Chorim* (WoH-transliterated proper-noun convention, matching the broader chapter-36 transliteration of Edomite / Horite / Esavite proper nouns). The WoH-transliterated convention is preferred for cross-corpus consistency with other proper-noun transliterations (*Esav*, *Edom*, *Se'ir*, *Avraham*, *Yitzchaq*, *Ya'aqov*, etc.). The chapter-14 *Horites* text is flagged for retroactive update under a corpus-normalization pass; the chapter-36 *Chori* / *Chorim* is the standing baseline.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 105-106, 250-253; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 564-566; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 336-339; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 395-398; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 pp. 282-283; Speiser *Hurrians and Subarians* JAOS Supplement 13 (1948); Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 203-205; Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 78-81; Albright *BASOR* 163 (1961); Wilhelm *The Hurrians* 1989 (ET); De Vaux *The Early History of Israel* 1978 (ET); Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 430-434; HALOT s.v. *Chori* and *chor*; BDB p. 360 (s.v. *Chori*) and p. 359 (s.v. *chor*); TDOT IV:309-311. The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the *Chorim* lexeme is HB-internal-explicit; (b) the Hurrian-vs-cave-dweller identification-question is genuinely contested in modern philological scholarship (Speiser vs Westermann/Bartlett); (c) the surface-lexical evidence (the Gen 36:20-30 onomasticon) does not unambiguously support either reading; (d) the *Chivvi*-where-text-expects-*Chori* problem at Gen 36:2 is documentary; (e) the chapter-14 cross-reference is HB-internal-explicit. The entry presents both readings without WoH-distinctive synthesis.

terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine plural (with definite article); hapax legomenon
terms.literal: [uncertain]; possibly *hot springs* / possibly *mules* / possibly *demons* / possibly *warm waters* / possibly emended *eimim* (terror-figures)
terms.standardEnglish: hot springs (ASV/NJPS/NRSV/NIV); mules (KJV); the warm waters / Yemim (less common)
terms.wohChoice: hot springs (ASV-baseline modern-majority; the full five-reading apparatus surfaced in commentary; lens-discipline applied — no WoH-distinctive synthesis)
terms.rationale:
**The lexeme *yemim* (הַיֵּמִם) at Gen 36:24 — *hu Anah asher matza et-ha-yemim ba-midbar bi-r'oto et-ha-chamorim le-Tziv'on aviv* (*this is Anah who found the YEMIM in the wilderness while he was pasturing the donkeys for Tziv'on his father*) — is a Hebrew Bible hapax legomenon whose identification is one of the most-contested philological cruxes in the Genesis text. The crux is genuinely philologically-unresolved: at least five distinct readings are attested across the ancient versions, the rabbinic tradition, the medieval commentators, and the modern critical literature. The lens-discipline pattern (ratified at chapter 35) applies: the entry documents all five readings without WoH-distinctive synthesis.**

**Reading (a): hot springs / thermal waters.** The dominant modern-majority reading. The lexeme is identified with a cognate Arabic root *yamīm* / *yamūm* (warm; hot) and is read as *hot springs* / *thermal waters*. Supported by: **LXX** *τὸν Ιαμιν* (Iamin — a transliteration but with some manuscripts reading *τὸν Ιαμειν* glossed *hot waters*); **Vulgate** *aquas calidas* (hot waters); **Jerome** ad loc.; **Symmachus** (cited by Jerome) *thermae*; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 251-252; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 565-566; **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 283; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 398; **Wenham** *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 339; **Alter** *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 204; **ASV**, **NJPS**, **NRSV**, **NIV**, **ESV** all *hot springs* / *hot waters*. The reading is geographically-supported: the Se'ir region preserves natural hot-springs (Hammat Gader in the wider region; the Edomite-territory thermal-spring tradition). The reading places Anah as the *discoverer* of a geographically-significant feature of the Edomite landscape — a placement consistent with the chapter's broader genealogical-aetiological mode.

**Reading (b): mules.** The dominant pre-modern Western reading and the rabbinic-classical reading. **Talmud b. Pesachim 54a** identifies *yemim* with mules and reads Anah as the discoverer (i.e., the *inventor*) of cross-breeding horses and donkeys to produce mules; the rabbinic-exegetical reading treats Anah's act as a transgression of natural-kinds boundaries (cf. the Deut 22:9-11 *kil'ayim* prohibitions on mixing animal-kinds). **Rashi** at Gen 36:24 reproduces the Talmudic reading: *Anah hivria chamor al ha-sus va-tilad ha-niqra pered* — *Anah crossed a donkey on a horse, and the mule was born*. **Targum Onqelos** *gibarya* (the giants / the strong-ones — different from Pseudo-Jonathan); **Targum Pseudo-Jonathan** reads similarly but glosses with mule-tradition; **KJV** *mules* (from the Vulgate's pre-Jerome tradition and the rabbinic-medieval transmission). The mule-reading is supported by: (i) the immediate-context *bi-r'oto et-ha-chamorim* (*while he was pasturing the donkeys*) — the donkeys are right there; (ii) the rabbinic ear-for-paronomasia: *yemim* and the Talmudic *yom yamim* (*great horses*) may be associated; (iii) the Deut 22 mixed-kinds prohibition retrospectively-providing a transgression-charge. The reading was the Western majority for over a millennium before being displaced by the modern philological-comparative reading in the 19th-20th centuries.

**Reading (c): demons / spirits.** A rabbinic-minority reading. **Targum Pseudo-Jonathan** glosses *yemim* with terms associated with demons / hybrid-spirit-beings (a tradition consistent with reading Anah as discovering something supernatural in the wilderness). **Genesis Rabbah 82:14** preserves multiple readings including the demon-reading. The reading is consistent with the broader rabbinic-mystical pattern of reading wilderness-encounters as having a supernatural-encounter dimension (cf. Lilith, Asazel, *se'irim*). The reading is not adopted by modern translations.

**Reading (d): warm waters / Onqelos *eimanaya*.** **Targum Onqelos** reads *yat eimanaya* — which has been variously translated as *the giants*, *the warm waters*, *the strong-ones*, and *the Emim-people* (i.e., as if the text read *eimim*, the Rephaim-cluster giants of Deut 2:10-11 and Deut 2:20). The Onqelos reading is genuinely ambiguous: the Aramaic *eimanaya* sits at the intersection of *strong-warm* and *strong-people*, and the rabbinic-interpretive-tradition has read Onqelos both ways across history.

**Reading (e): emendation to *eimim* (terror-figures / Rephaim-cluster giants).** Some modern critical scholars consider emending the MT *yemim* (יֵמִם) to *eimim* (אֵימִם) — the *terror-figures* / Rephaim-cluster autochthonous-giants attested at Deut 2:10-11 (*ha-Eimim l-fanim yashvu vah... v-rephaim yechashvu af-hem ka-Anaqim* — *the Eimim formerly dwelt there... and they are also accounted Rephaim, as the Anaqim*) and Deut 2:20-21. The emendation reads *Anah found the Eimim-giants in the wilderness* — placing Anah at an encounter with the autochthonous-giant-population structurally-parallel to the Deuteronomic giants-clusters. **Skinner** *ICC Genesis* 1910 p. 433 considers the emendation but does not adopt it; the emendation is rejected by Speiser, Westermann, Sarna, Hamilton, Alter, Wenham. The reading would require a yod-aleph paleographic confusion (yod-mem-mem vs aleph-yod-mem-mem) which is not paleographically clean.

**The translation choice.** The WoH preserves the ASV / NJPS / NRSV / modern-majority *hot springs* in the main translation surface, per the ratified principle of *accuracy above lens*. The KJV *mules* reading carries the rabbinic-classical tradition but is not philologically-supported by the dominant modern reading. The crux is genuinely-unresolved; the translation does not pretend otherwise. The commentary surfaces the five-reading apparatus.

**Lens-discipline note.** The Wheel of Heaven project may have a distinctive reading of *yemim* (e.g., something the lens treats as more interesting than hot-springs / mules / demons). Any such reading is **wiki-reserved**: it does not belong in the translation apparatus. This entry documents the five-reading apparatus without WoH-distinctive synthesis. The chapter-keystone application of the lens-discipline pattern (ratified at chapter 35) applies here especially clearly: the *yemim* crux is exactly the kind of hapax that invites lens-leakage, and the discipline of preserving the mainstream readings without WoH-distinctive synthesis is the editorial constraint.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 251-252; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 565-566; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 339; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 398; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 283; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 204; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 p. 433; Targum Onqelos at Gen 36:24; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan at Gen 36:24; b. Pesachim 54a; Genesis Rabbah 82:14; Rashi at Gen 36:24; Vulgate at Gen 36:24; LXX at Gen 36:24 (and variant manuscript readings); HALOT s.v. *yemim*; BDB p. 411 (s.v. *yemim*); TDOT VI:69 (s.v. *yemim* — brief note on the hapax-status and the principal readings). The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the lexeme is a hapax with no clear cognate that resolves the identification; (b) the five readings are documentary and well-attested across the textual tradition; (c) the modern-majority reading (*hot springs*) is not universally-adopted (KJV / rabbinic-tradition disagree); (d) the philological-comparative argument (Arabic *yamīm* = hot) supports reading (a) but is not decisive; (e) the lens-discipline application is the editorial constraint. Any WoH-distinctive reading of the lexeme is reserved for the wiki and is not preempted by this entry.

terms.source: וְאֵלֶּה הַמְּלָכִים אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ־מֶלֶךְ לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל
terms.translit: ve-eleh ha-melakhim asher malkhu b-eretz Edom lifnei melokh-melekh livnei Yisra'el
terms.strongs: H4427 (malakh — to be/become king); H4428 (melekh — king)
terms.partOfSpeech: narrative-formulaic prefatory clause with infinitive-construct *melokh* + noun *melekh* + preposition + construct *livnei Yisra'el*
terms.literal: and these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom, before any king reigned for / over the children of Yisra'el
terms.standardEnglish: and these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom, before there reigned any king over the children of Israel (ASV); ... before any Israelite king reigned (NJPS); ... before any Israelite king reigned over the Israelites (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: and these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom, before any king reigned over the children of Yisra'el (ASV-baseline; the documentary-critical significance surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 36:31 is one of the most-decisive documentary-critical anchors in the Pentateuch. The prefatory formula *lifnei melokh melekh livnei Yisra'el* — *before any king reigned over the children of Yisra'el* — presupposes the Israelite monarchy as already established in the implied-reader's present. The verse's editorial-perspective is therefore post-monarchic at the earliest: the text is written, or at minimum edited, from a vantage-point after Saul or David (c. 1000 BCE) or, more commonly per modern critical scholarship, from the divided-monarchy or post-exilic vantage. The documentary-critical observation is mainstream modern scholarship; it is presented here as such, not as a WoH-distinctive claim.**

**The documentary-critical case.** The classic statement of the documentary-critical reading of Gen 36:31 traces to **Wellhausen** *Prolegomena zur Geschichte Israels* (1878; ET *Prolegomena to the History of Israel* 1885) pp. 8-9, who treats the verse as decisive evidence that the Pentateuchal redactor wrote (or at minimum edited) from a post-monarchic perspective. Wellhausen's argument: the formula *lifnei melokh melekh livnei Yisra'el* is intelligible only if the reader is already-acquainted with an Israelite monarchy; a Mosaic-period writer (c. 1400-1200 BCE per the traditional chronology, or c. 1250-1150 BCE per the modern-mainstream Late Bronze / early Iron Age chronology) could not have written this formula because the Israelite monarchy did not yet exist. The argument is reproduced and developed in **Eissfeldt** *The Old Testament: An Introduction* 1965 (ET) p. 198; **Friedman** *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 pp. 33-34 and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 pp. 96-97 (assigning Gen 36:31-43 to a post-monarchic editorial layer); **Speiser** *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 284; **Westermann** *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 568-569; **Hamilton** *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 401-402; **Skinner** *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 434-435; **Sarna** *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 252-253. The verse anchors a wider documentary-critical position: that the final-form Pentateuch is a post-monarchic (likely exilic or post-exilic) editorial product, even if it incorporates earlier source-material.

**The medieval anticipation: Ibn Ezra.** The documentary-critical observation was anticipated, in a more-guarded form, by the medieval Jewish commentator **Ibn Ezra** (1089-1164). Ibn Ezra at Gen 36:31 famously writes: *v-im tavin sod ha-shneim asar... ve-eleh ha-melakhim asher malkhu b-eretz Edom v-yodaim tidom* — *and if you understand the secret of the twelve [verses or kings]... and these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom — and if you know, be silent*. The cryptic *yodaim tidom* (*the knowing-ones will keep silent*) is generally interpreted (by later medieval and modern interpreters: Spinoza in the *Tractatus Theologico-Politicus* 1670 ch. 8; the modern critical tradition; cf. Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible* 1987 pp. 18-22) as Ibn Ezra's coded-acknowledgment that the verse implies post-Mosaic authorship — a position too dangerous for the medieval Jewish exegete to state openly. The *sod ha-shneim asar* (*the secret of the twelve*) refers to twelve verses across the Pentateuch (Gen 12:6 *v-ha-Kena'ani az ba-aretz*; Gen 22:14 *b-har YHWH yera'eh*; Deut 1:1 the introductory geographic formulae; Deut 3:11 the Og iron-bedstead notice; Deut 34 Mosheh's death-narrative; etc.) that Ibn Ezra identified as bearing post-Mosaic editorial traces. Gen 36:31 is one of the twelve.

**The harmonizing-traditional reading.** The traditional rabbinic-and-conservative reading of Gen 36:31 treats the formula as either: (a) **predictive-prophecy** — Mosheh prophetically-anticipates the Israelite monarchy and writes the formula proleptically; (b) **referring to the patriarchal-promise** — the *melekh livnei Yisra'el* refers to the Gen 17:6 / 17:16 / 35:11 patriarchal-promise that kings will descend from Avraham/Sarah and Ya'aqov (so Rashi at Gen 36:31, reading *lifnei melokh melekh* as *before the [promised] king reigned*); (c) **late editorial-addition by an authoritative-prophet** (e.g., Shemu'el or Ezra) under inspiration, preserving Mosaic authorship of the core-text. **Cassuto** *The Documentary Hypothesis and the Composition of the Pentateuch* 1941 (ET 1961) is the major modern apologetic statement, treating Gen 36:31 (with the other Ibn-Ezra-twelve verses) as not-decisive evidence for late authorship: the formula could be retrospective-narrative-comment without requiring late composition of the verse-as-written. Cassuto's position is that the documentary-critical case overreads the verse.

**The eight-kings-list structure.** Gen 36:31-39 lists eight kings: (i) Bela ben-Be'or of Dinhavah; (ii) Yovav ben-Zerach of Botzrah; (iii) Chusham of the land of the Temanites; (iv) Hadad ben-Bedad (who struck Midyan in the field of Mo'av) of Avit; (v) Samlah of Masreqah; (vi) Sha'ul of Rechovot by the river; (vii) Ba'al-Chanan ben-Akhbor; (viii) Hadar (MT) / Hadad (1 Chr 1:50 / SamP) of Pa'u.
