The list is **non-dynastic**: each king is associated with a different city (the cities are not a single dynastic-capital); the succession-formula *va-yamot X va-yimlokh tachtav Y* (*and X died, and Y reigned in his stead*) signals each king is succeeded but **not necessarily by a son** — none of the kings is named as the son of the previous. The non-dynastic-succession pattern is consistent with a tribal-confederation pre-statehood organization (in which leadership rotates through clan-heads or is elected from the tribal-confederation) rather than with a hereditary-monarchy. **Bartlett** *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 86-102 is the major modern monograph treatment, reading the eight-kings-list as preserving genuine historical-memory of an Edomite pre-monarchic confederation, possibly drawn from a separate Edomite-source-tradition. The 1 Chr 1:43-51 parallel reproduces the list with a single variant (Hadar at Gen 36:39 → Hadad at 1 Chr 1:50). The list's pre-monarchic phase ends with the v 40-43 *alufim*-list, which on Bartlett's reading represents a later phase (or a separate genealogical-tradition).

**The chronological-question.** If the eight kings represent a real Edomite confederation, the chronological question is when they ruled. Mainstream reconstructions place them in the Late Bronze Age (c. 1300-1200 BCE) or in the early Iron Age (c. 1200-1000 BCE), prior to the Israelite monarchic-period. The Neo-Assyrian record attests Edomite kings from the 9th century BCE forward (Qaushmalaku at the time of Tiglath-Pileser III, c. 730s BCE); the Gen 36 eight-kings-list would represent a pre-Neo-Assyrian-attestation phase. The Iron Age archaeological record at Buseirah (Bozrah, mentioned at Gen 36:33), Tel el-Kheleifeh, Umm el-Biyara, and Tawilan supports an Edomite presence in the relevant region from the early Iron Age forward. **Bartlett** 1989 and **Levy** *Some Recent Discoveries in Faynan and the Wadi al-Hasa* 2004 provide the historical-archaeological reconstruction.

**The lens-discipline application.** The documentary-critical reading of Gen 36:31 is **mainstream modern scholarship** — Wellhausen, Eissfeldt, Speiser, Westermann, Friedman, Sarna, Hamilton, Skinner all endorse it. The harmonizing-traditional reading is **the traditional Jewish-Christian conservative position** — Rashi, Cassuto. **Both are presented as named positions in modern scholarship**, not as WoH-distinctive claims. The lens-discipline pattern applies: the documentary-critical observation is not a Wheel-of-Heaven novelty but is the mainstream modern critical reading, presented as such. No WoH-distinctive synthesis is introduced.

**Modern scholarly literature.** Wellhausen *Prolegomena zur Geschichte Israels* 1878 / *Prolegomena to the History of Israel* 1885 (ET) pp. 8-9; Eissfeldt *The Old Testament: An Introduction* 1965 (ET) p. 198; Friedman *Who Wrote the Bible?* 1987 pp. 18-22, 33-34, and *The Bible with Sources Revealed* 2003 pp. 96-97; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 284; Westermann *Genesis 12-36* 1985 pp. 568-569; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 401-402; Skinner *ICC Genesis* 1910 pp. 434-435; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 252-253; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 339-340; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 pp. 204-205; Cassuto *The Documentary Hypothesis and the Composition of the Pentateuch* 1941 (ET 1961); Bartlett *Edom and the Edomites* JSOTSup 77 (1989) pp. 86-102; Levy *Some Recent Discoveries in Faynan and the Wadi al-Hasa* 2004; Ibn Ezra at Gen 36:31; Rashi at Gen 36:31; Spinoza *Tractatus Theologico-Politicus* 1670 ch. 8. The claim_type is *direct* because (a) the formula *lifnei melokh melekh livnei Yisra'el* is grammatically explicit; (b) the documentary-critical reading is the mainstream modern position (verifiable across the standard commentaries); (c) the harmonizing-traditional reading is the traditional Jewish-Christian conservative position (verifiable across the rabbinic and apologetic literature); (d) the eight-kings-list non-dynastic-succession structure is HB-internal-explicit; (e) the historical-archaeological Edomite-confederation reconstruction is mainstream modern scholarship (Bartlett 1989).

terms.translit: She'ol (here in the locative/directional form שְׁאֹלָה, She'olah, 'down to She'ol')
terms.partOfSpeech: noun, common (gender debated; usually treated feminine); here with directional he (locative)
terms.literal: She'ol — the realm of the dead, the underworld; (older renderings) the grave, the pit
terms.standardEnglish: the grave (KJV); the grave / Sheol (ASV — 'grave' in text, marginal note); Sheol (NJPS, NRSV, Alter — transliterated); the netherworld / underworld (von Rad, Speiser glosses)
terms.wohChoice: She'ol (transliterated, per the corpus loaded-term / proper-name transliteration practice; the underworld/grave/realm-of-the-dead semantic range surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 37:35 records Ya'aqov's refusal of comfort: *va-yom'er ki ered el-beni avel She'olah* — *and he said, For I will go down to She'ol to my son mourning*. This is the first weighty occurrence of שְׁאוֹל in the Yosef cycle and the chapter's principal theological-lexical crux; the rendering-decision must be made at glossary level, not by the Translator, because the term governs every subsequent occurrence across the corpus.**

**The lexeme.** שְׁאוֹל (*she'ol*) denotes the abode of the dead in ancient-Hebrew cosmology — a shadowy subterranean realm to which all the dead descend, conceived as a place 'down' (hence the recurring idiom *yarad She'olah*, 'to go down to She'ol', as here). The etymology is genuinely uncertain: proposals include (a) a root שׁאל ('to ask / enquire', perhaps 'the place of enquiry / of the oracle of the dead'); (b) a root meaning 'to be hollow / a hollow place' (cf. Akkadian *šu'āru*?); (c) a loan or a non-Semitic substrate term. HALOT s.v. *she'ol* and BDB p. 982 both register the etymological uncertainty. The word occurs ~66x in the HB, concentrated in Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Isaiah, and the narrative books.

**The rendering-traditions.** Three rendering-traditions compete:

(a) **'the grave' / 'the pit'** — the KJV/ASV tradition frequently renders *she'ol* by 'the grave' (so KJV here: *I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning*), treating the term as a near-synonym for *qever* (the literal tomb). This reading flattens the cosmological dimension: *she'ol* is not the individual tomb but the collective realm of the dead. The 'grave' rendering is defensible where the context is a personal death and obscures the underworld-cosmology, but it loses the term's distinctive register.

(b) **transliteration 'Sheol'** — the modern critical tradition (NJPS, NRSV, Alter, NIV-in-poetry) transliterates, on the principle that *she'ol* names a culture-specific entity for which no English word is an exact equivalent (it is neither the Christian 'hell' — there is no punitive dimension in the older strata — nor merely 'the grave'). Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 261; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 354; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 42; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 431; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 291 all transliterate or gloss as the underworld rather than 'the grave'.

(c) **'the netherworld / underworld'** — a glossing-translation that renders the cosmological sense explicitly. Favored in commentary-glosses (von Rad, Speiser) but rarely adopted as the running translation because it over-specifies.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation transliterates *She'ol* (with the apostrophe marking the aleph, consistent with the corpus's treatment of loaded culture-specific terms and proper-name-like entities — cf. the transliteration practice for *Mitsrayim*, *Kena'an*, and the divine-name and place-name conventions throughout). The transliteration (i) preserves the term's distinctive cosmological register that 'the grave' loses; (ii) does not import the later Christian 'hell' overtones that 'the underworld' might suggest to some readers; (iii) matches the dominant modern critical tradition (NJPS / NRSV / Alter). The semantic range — realm of the dead / the grave / the underworld — is documented here and surfaced in the per-verse commentary so the reader can audit the choice. **No cosmological synthesis beyond the mainstream ANE realm-of-the-dead picture is imported into the translation surface or this rationale; any project-specific reading of the descent-to-She'ol motif is wiki-reserved.**

**Cross-corpus reach.** *She'ol* recurs at Gen 42:38 and 44:29 + 31 (within the Yosef cycle, Ya'aqov repeating the descent-in-mourning motif), Num 16:30 + 33 (Korach's company swallowed alive *She'olah*), Deut 32:22, 1 Sam 2:6, 2 Sam 22:6, and pervasively in Job (7:9; 11:8; 14:13; 17:13-16; 21:13; 24:19; 26:6), Psalms (6:6; 9:18; 16:10; 18:6; 30:4; 49:15-16; 55:16; 86:13; 88:4; 89:49; 116:3; 139:8; 141:7), Proverbs (1:12; 5:5; 7:27; 9:18; 15:11 + 24; 23:14; 27:20; 30:16), Isaiah (5:14; 14:9 + 11 + 15; 28:15 + 18; 38:10 + 18; 57:9), and the prophets (Ezek 31-32; Hos 13:14; Am 9:2; Jon 2:3; Hab 2:5). The cross-corpus distribution decisively justifies a **central** glossary entry; this entry governs the lemma project-wide, with the *appliesTo* anchored at the first weighty Genesis occurrence and extensible as later books are translated.

The claim_type is *inferred* (not *direct*) because (a) the transliteration choice over 'the grave' is a defensible reading well-supported by the modern critical tradition but is not the only-defensible rendering (the KJV/ASV 'grave' tradition is venerable); (b) the choice-between-renderings is interpretive rather than grammatically-forced; (c) the underworld-cosmology is a scholarly reconstruction (mainstream, but a reconstruction). The transliteration is the philological-conservative choice that preserves the term's irreducibility.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *she'ol*; BDB p. 982; TDOT XIV:239-248 (*she'ol* by Heinz-Josef Fabry); NIDOTTE 4:6-7; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 261; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 354; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 42; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 431; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 291; for the underworld-cosmology: N. J. Tromp *Primitive Conceptions of Death and the Nether World in the Old Testament* 1969; Ph. S. Johnston *Shades of Sheol: Death and Afterlife in the Old Testament* 2002.

terms.strongs: H3801 (ketonet — tunic, coat) + H6446 (pas, pl. passim — meaning contested: flat of hand/foot; or piece/strip)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun phrase: feminine noun in construct + masculine plural noun
terms.literal: tunic of passim — a tunic of (flats-of-the-hands-and-feet? / pieces? / fine-stuff?)
terms.standardEnglish: a coat of many colours (KJV/ASV; LXX chitōna poikilon; Vulgate tunicam polymitam); a long robe with sleeves (NRSV); an ornamented tunic (NJPS); a long ornamented tunic (Speiser); an elaborate / striped tunic (Alter 'an ornamented tunic')
terms.wohChoice: a coat of many colours (ASV-baseline preserved; the long-sleeved / ornamented-tunic alternatives documented in commentary and recurring at vv 23, 32)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 37:3 records that Yisra'el *asah lo ketonet passim* — *made him a* [ketonet passim] — and the phrase recurs at v 23 (the stripping) and v 32 (the bloodied garment brought to the father). The meaning of פַּסִּים (*passim*) is the single largest philological crux of the chapter and a project-defining lexical decision. The garment is load-bearing: its making marks the favoritism that drives the plot, and its bloodying is the deception that ends the chapter. The phrase occurs only here and in one other place in the entire HB — 2 Sam 13:18-19, of Tamar's garment — which makes that parallel decisive for the philology.**

**The crux.** The noun פַּס (*pas*), plural פַּסִּים (*passim*), is rare and its meaning genuinely uncertain. Three families of reading are attested:

**(a) 'of many colours' — the LXX/Vulgate/KJV/ASV tradition.** The Septuagint renders χιτὼν ποικίλος (*chitōn poikilos*, 'a many-coloured / variegated tunic'); the Vulgate *tunica polymita* ('a tunic of many threads / colours'); the KJV and ASV follow with 'a coat of many colours'. This reading takes *passim* as denoting variegation or many-colouredness. It is the **dominant English-translation tradition** and the basis of the garment's place in Western culture (the 'coat of many colours'). It has no clear philological derivation from *pas* and appears to be an interpretive expansion in the ancient versions, but it is venerable and culturally entrenched.

**(b) 'reaching to the palms and soles' / 'long-sleeved' — the rabbinic/Ibn-Ezra tradition, anchored in the 2 Sam 13 parallel.** *Pas* in later Hebrew and Aramaic means the flat of the hand (palm) or foot (sole) — cf. *pas yad* ('palm of the hand', Dan 5:5 *pas yeda*, the hand that wrote on the wall). On this reading *ketonet passim* is a tunic *reaching to the palms and soles* — i.e., a long-sleeved, ankle-length garment, distinct from the short working-tunic. This is the reading of b. Shabbat 10b and Ibn Ezra ad loc., and it is **the reading the 2 Sam 13:18-19 parallel supports**: there the *ketonet passim* is explicitly *the garment that the king's virgin daughters wore* — a distinctive royal / high-status robe (NRSV 'a long robe with sleeves'). Since 2 Sam 13:18 is the **only other occurrence** of the phrase in the HB, and there it plainly denotes a high-status full-length garment rather than a colourful one, the philological weight favors this reading. The point of the garment in both texts is **status**, not colour: it marks the wearer as set apart, exempt from menial labour (a long-sleeved ankle-length robe is impractical for shepherding or field-work).

**(c) 'an ornamented / fine tunic' — the NJPS/Speiser modern tradition.** A third reading derives *pas* from a textile term (a 'piece / strip / band' of fine fabric, or an applied ornamental border), giving 'an ornamented tunic' (NJPS), 'a long ornamented tunic' (Speiser, AB), or 'a richly ornamented robe' (NIV). This reading blends the status-marking force of (b) with a sense of decorative fineness; it is the consensus of modern critical translation. Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 290 connects *passim* to the Akkadian *kitû pišannu*, a ceremonial garment-term attested in Mesopotamian inventory-texts, supporting 'a ceremonial / ornamented robe'.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'a coat of many colours'** at vv 3, 23, 32, per the accuracy-above-lens convention (the translation surface stays ASV-baseline 1901). The choice is conservative and culturally legible, and the ancient-versional support (LXX, Vulgate) is genuine. **However, the commentary explicitly documents that the philologically-stronger readings are (b) the long-sleeved / palm-and-sole-reaching robe (the 2 Sam 13:18 parallel) and (c) the ornamented status-tunic (NJPS / Speiser), and that the modern critical consensus has moved away from 'many colours' toward a status-marking rather than colour-marking garment.** The retention of the ASV-baseline rendering is a translation-surface conservatism decision, not a philological endorsement of 'many colours' over the better-attested status-reading; the commentary carries the correction.

**Central vs overlay.** This entry is housed in the **central** glossary, not the overlay, despite the phrase's near-singularity within Genesis, because the decisive philological evidence is the **cross-corpus 2 Sam 13:18-19 parallel** — the entry must govern both occurrences (Genesis and 2 Samuel) when the Samuel material is translated, and the lexeme *pas* itself reaches further (Dan 5:5 *pas yeda*). The cross-corpus reach into 2 Samuel and Daniel justifies central housing; the *appliesTo* is anchored at the three Genesis occurrences and is extensible to 2 Sam 13:18-19.

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) all three readings are grammatically and lexically supportable; (b) the choice-between-readings is interpretive; (c) the ASV-baseline 'many colours' is the retained surface but is the *weakest* of the three philologically, with the commentary flagging the status-reading as stronger — the inferred label signals that the surface rendering is a conservative-traditional choice over a defensible-and-arguably-better alternative, exactly the case the *inferred* category exists for.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *pas*; BDB p. 821; TDOT XII:514-515; NIDOTTE 3:680; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 290 (the Akkadian *kitû pišannu* connection); Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 255; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 36; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 407; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 351; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 p. 350; Alter *The Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 207 (note on the 2 Sam 13 parallel); for the 2 Sam 13:18 garment: P. K. McCarter *II Samuel* AB 1984 p. 325.

terms.literal: saris — a court official / royal functionary; (also, in some contexts) a eunuch, a castrated man
terms.standardEnglish: an officer (ASV/KJV); a courtier (NJPS); an officer / official (NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: an officer (ASV-baseline; the court-official sense; the literal eunuch-sense and its tension with Potifar's marriage documented in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 37:36 describes Potifar as *seris Par'oh sar ha-tabbachim* — *a* [saris] *of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard*. The term סָרִיס (*saris*) is ambiguous between two senses, and the ambiguity is a recognized crux because of its tension with the narrative.**

**The lexeme.** סָרִיס is generally taken as a loanword from Akkadian *ša rēši* ('the one of the [king's] head', i.e., a high palace official, a courtier in attendance on the king). From this functional-administrative sense the word came to denote, in many contexts, a **castrated court official** — a eunuch — because eunuchs were characteristically employed in royal households (in particular guarding the harem). The two senses — (a) the general court-official / royal functionary and (b) the literal castrated eunuch — coexist in Biblical Hebrew, and which is operative must be judged from context. HALOT s.v. *saris* and BDB p. 710 register both senses; the Akkadian etymology (*ša rēši*) is settled (CAD Š/2 s.v. *ša rēši*).

**The crux at Gen 37:36 / 39:1.** Potifar is called a *saris* of Pharaoh, yet Gen 39 presents him as a married man (*eshet adonav*, 'his master's wife', the woman who attempts to seduce Yosef). A literal eunuch with a wife is not impossible (the marriage could be titular, or the wife's role in ch 39 could be read against a eunuch-husband's situation — some midrashic readings exploit exactly this), but the tension is real and is why most modern translations render *saris* here by the **functional sense** ('officer', 'courtier', 'official') rather than 'eunuch'. The functional reading dissolves the tension: Potifar is a senior court official, not necessarily castrated. The eunuch-reading is etymologically and lexically available and is preferred by some (it sharpens the irony of ch 39); but the marriage makes the functional reading the natural one.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation renders the ASV-baseline **'an officer of Pharaoh's'** (the court-official / functional sense), which (i) matches the dominant translation tradition (ASV / KJV 'officer'; NJPS 'courtier'; NRSV 'officer'); (ii) dissolves the eunuch-vs-marriage tension at the surface; (iii) is the natural reading given Gen 39's married Potifar. The literal eunuch-sense and its narrative tension are documented in commentary so the reader can audit the choice.

**Central housing.** The lexeme *saris* recurs across the corpus in both senses — within the Yosef cycle at Gen 40:2 + 7 (Pharaoh's *sarisim*, the chief cupbearer and chief baker) and pervasively in the later HB (1 Sam 8:15; 2 Kgs 8:6; 9:32; 18:17; 20:18; 23:11; 24:12 + 15; 25:19; Isa 39:7; 56:3-4 — the famous eunuch-inclusion text; Jer 29:2; 34:19; 38:7 — Eved-Melekh the Kushite *saris*; 41:16; 52:25; Est 1:10 + 12 + 15; 2:3 + 14-15 + 21; 4:4-5; 6:2 + 14; 7:9; Dan 1:3 + 7-11 + 18). The cross-corpus distribution — and the fact that the two senses must be adjudicated context-by-context across all of them — decisively justifies a **central** entry governing the lemma project-wide. The *appliesTo* is anchored at Gen 37:36 and extensible to Gen 40 and the later occurrences.

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) both senses are lexically established; (b) the choice between them at this verse is interpretive (driven by the Gen 39 marriage context rather than by the word itself); (c) the ASV-baseline 'officer' is the defensible-and-dominant reading but the eunuch-sense is genuinely available.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *saris*; BDB p. 710; CAD Š/2 s.v. *ša rēši*; TDOT X:344-350; NIDOTTE 3:288-289; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 261; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 42; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 432; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 291; for the *ša rēši* administrative title: A. L. Oppenheim 'A Note on ša rēši' JANES 5 (1973) 325-334; for the eunuch-question: H. Tadmor 'Was the Biblical *sārîs* a Eunuch?' in *Solving Riddles and Untying Knots* (1995) 317-325.

terms.strongs: H8269 (sar — chief, captain) + H2876 (tabbach — slaughterer, cook, guardsman)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun phrase: masculine noun in construct + definite masculine plural noun
terms.literal: chief of the tabbachim — chief of the slaughterers / cooks / butchers (idiomatically: captain of the [body]guard / chief executioner / chief steward)
terms.standardEnglish: the captain of the guard (ASV/KJV); his chief steward (NJPS); the captain of the guard (NRSV); the high chamberlain / chief steward (various)
terms.wohChoice: the captain of the guard (ASV-baseline; the literal 'chief of the slaughterers' sense and the chief-steward/chief-executioner alternatives documented in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 37:36 titles Potifar *sar ha-tabbachim* — literally *chief of the* [tabbachim]. The phrase recurs through the Yosef cycle (Gen 39:1; 40:3-4; 41:10 + 12) as Potifar's standing title and is the title held by Nevuzaradan, the Babylonian official, across 2 Kings and Jeremiah. The translation of *tabbachim* is a recognized crux.**

**The lexeme.** טַבָּח (*tabbach*), plural טַבָּחִים (*tabbachim*), is derived from the root טבח (*tavach*, 'to slaughter / butcher'). The primary literal sense is **'slaughterer / butcher / cook'** — one who slaughters animals (for food or sacrifice). A *sar ha-tabbachim* is therefore literally a 'chief of the slaughterers / chief cook / chief butcher'. But the phrase is conventionally an **office-title**, and three rendering-traditions compete for what office it names:

(a) **'captain of the guard'** — the KJV/ASV/NRSV tradition. The reasoning: a body of royal 'slaughterers' is taken metaphorically/idiomatically as the king's executioners or armed bodyguard (those who 'slaughter' at the king's command), hence the *sar ha-tabbachim* is the captain of the (executioner-)guard. This is supported by the parallel office of Nevuzaradan *rav-tabbachim* in 2 Kgs 25:8 + 11 + 20 and Jer 39:9-13; 40:1-5; 41:10; 43:6; 52:12-30, who functions as a senior military / security official overseeing executions and deportations. The 'captain of the guard' reading is the dominant English tradition.

(b) **'chief steward' / 'chief cook'** — the NJPS tradition ('his chief steward'). This keeps closer to the literal 'cook / butcher' sense: the *sar ha-tabbachim* is the head of the royal kitchen / household provisioning. The Yosef-cycle context (Potifar runs a household into which Yosef is sold as a domestic; the chief cupbearer and chief baker of Gen 40 are food-office holders) gives this reading contextual support.

(c) **'chief executioner'** — a reading splitting the difference: the 'slaughterers' are the king's executioners specifically (cf. the Aramaic *tabbachayya* in Dan 2:14, the king's executioners whom Daniel meets). This sharpens the literal 'slaughter' sense into a judicial-execution office.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'the captain of the guard'**, which (i) matches the dominant English tradition; (ii) is supported by the Nevuzaradan parallel (a senior security/military official); (iii) reads cleanly. The literal 'chief of the slaughterers' sense and the chief-steward (NJPS) and chief-executioner (Dan 2:14 *tabbachayya*) alternatives are documented in commentary.

**Central housing.** The title recurs as a fixed phrase across the Yosef cycle (Gen 39:1; 40:3-4; 41:10 + 12) and cross-corpus in the parallel form *rav-tabbachim* (the Babylonian-administration title) at 2 Kgs 25:8-20 and throughout Jeremiah 39-52, plus the Aramaic *tabbachayya* at Dan 2:14. The cross-corpus reach justifies a **central** entry governing the lemma and the office-title project-wide; the *appliesTo* is anchored at Gen 37:36 and extensible to the Gen 39-41 occurrences and the 2 Kings / Jeremiah parallels.

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the literal sense ('slaughterers') is direct but the office it names is interpretive; (b) the three office-readings are all defensible; (c) the ASV-baseline 'captain of the guard' is the dominant-and-defensible choice but not the only one (NJPS 'chief steward' keeps closer to the literal).

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *tabbach*; BDB p. 371; TDOT V:392-393; NIDOTTE 2:302; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 261; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 42; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 432; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 291 (favoring 'chief steward'); von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 354; for the *rav-tabbachim* Babylonian-administration parallel: CAD Ṭ s.v. *ṭābihu*; for Nevuzaradan: J. Bright *Jeremiah* AB 1965.

terms.translit: bor (plural borot; with directional he ha-borah, 'into the pit')
terms.literal: bor — a pit, a (usually rock-cut) water-cistern; (by extension) a dungeon-pit
terms.standardEnglish: pit (ASV/KJV); pit / cistern (NRSV); pit (NJPS); pit (Alter)
terms.wohChoice: pit (ASV-baseline; the specific dry-water-cistern sense — which v 24 makes explicit — surfaced in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 37 turns on a *bor*: the brothers cast Yosef into 'one of the* [borot]*' (v 20), Re'uven specifies 'this* [bor] *that is in the wilderness' (v 22), they cast him into 'the* [bor]*' which 'was empty, no water in it' (v 24, *ha-borah... ve-ha-bor req ein bo mayim*), the Midyanim draw him 'up out of the* [bor]*' (v 28), and Re'uven returns to find Yosef 'not in the* [bor]*' (v 29). The lexeme בּוֹר recurs five times and is load-bearing for the scene.**

**The lexeme.** בּוֹר (*bor*) denotes specifically a **rock-cut water-cistern** — a bottle-shaped shaft hewn into bedrock and plastered to hold rainwater run-off, a characteristic feature of highland-Levantine settlements where springs were scarce. The narrative trades on exactly this: v 24's explicit gloss '*the bor was empty, there was no water in it*' tells the reader that this particular *bor* was a **dry** cistern — which is why Yosef could be cast in and survive, and why the brothers could sit down to eat beside it. The dry-cistern image is precise: a deep, smooth-walled, bottle-necked shaft from which a man cannot climb out unaided (hence Yosef must be 'drawn up', *vaya'alu*, v 28). The generic ASV 'pit' loses this cistern-specificity — a 'pit' suggests a dug hole, not the engineered rock-cut water-shaft the Hebrew names.

**The rendering-traditions.** The KJV/ASV/NJPS tradition renders 'pit' throughout (a generic but defensible rendering); NRSV and many modern commentaries prefer 'cistern' to capture the dry-water-cistern sense that v 24 makes explicit. Sarna, Westermann, Speiser, and von Rad all gloss the *bor* as a cistern in their notes. The lexeme also carries a derived sense of 'dungeon / pit-prison' (cf. *beit ha-bor*, the 'house of the pit / dungeon', Exod 12:29; Jer 37:16) and a poetic sense of the grave / She'ol-adjacent pit (Ps 28:1; 30:4; 88:5-7; Isa 14:15 + 19; 38:18; Ezek 26:20; 31:14-16; 32:18-30) — the 'pit' as the descent of the dead, which resonates with the chapter's She'ol-motif at v 35.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'pit'** at vv 20, 22, 24, 28, 29, per the accuracy-above-lens / ASV-baseline convention. The dry-water-cistern sense — that the *bor* is a rock-cut cistern, and that v 24's gloss flags it as dry — is documented in commentary so the cistern-specificity the Hebrew trades on is not lost to the reader. (Retaining 'pit' also preserves the resonance with the poetic 'pit' of descent that the chapter's She'ol-motif activates at v 35.)

**Central housing.** The lexeme recurs within the Yosef cycle (Gen 40:15 + 41:14, where Yosef calls the Egyptian prison *ha-bor*, 'the pit / dungeon') and pervasively across the corpus in both the literal cistern-sense (Exod 21:33-34; Deut 6:11; 1 Sam 19:22; 2 Sam 3:26; 2 Sam 23:15-16 + 20; 2 Kgs 18:31; Neh 9:25; Jer 2:13 — 'broken cisterns'; Jer 38:6 — Jeremiah cast into a muddy *bor*; Prov 5:15) and the poetic pit-of-the-dead sense (the Psalms / Isaiah / Ezekiel cluster above). The cross-corpus reach decisively justifies a **central** entry; the *appliesTo* is anchored at the five Gen 37 occurrences and is extensible to Gen 40-41 and the wider corpus.

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) 'pit' and 'cistern' are both lexically defensible; (b) the choice is interpretive (the cistern-specificity is contextually established by v 24 rather than forced by the word in isolation); (c) the ASV-baseline 'pit' is retained as the surface reading while the commentary carries the cistern-correction.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *bor*; BDB p. 92; TDOT II:463-465; NIDOTTE 1:751; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 258; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 41; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 421; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 291; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 352; for ancient-Levantine cistern-technology: L. E. Stager 'The Archaeology of the Family in Ancient Israel' BASOR 260 (1985) 1-35.

terms.translit: beit ha-sohar (the place); sar beit ha-sohar (its keeper)
terms.strongs: H1004 (bayit, 'house') + H5470 (sohar, 'prison, round-house' — a noun attested only in this phrase, Gen 39-40)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun phrase: noun in construct + definite noun (sohar); + sar ('chief, keeper') in the officer-title
terms.literal: house of the sohar — the prison / the round-house / the house of confinement; sar beit ha-sohar — the chief / keeper of the prison
terms.standardEnglish: the prison (ASV/KJV/NRSV); the prison (NJPS); the prison-house / the round-house (some commentators); keeper of the prison (ASV/KJV) / chief jailer (NJPS/NRSV)
terms.wohChoice: the prison (ASV-baseline; the sohar 'round-house / enclosure' etymology and the loanword question documented in commentary); the keeper of the prison (sar beit ha-sohar)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 39:20 places Yosef in the *beit ha-sohar* — *the house of the* sohar — 'the place where the king's prisoners were bound' — and the phrase becomes the chapter's standing word for his confinement: *beit ha-sohar* (vv 20 twice, 21, 22, 23) and its overseer *sar beit ha-sohar* ('keeper of the prison', vv 21, 22, 23). The term is distinct from the *bor* ('pit / cistern') of Gen 37 into which the brothers had cast him; this is a different word for a different place of confinement, and the choice of vocabulary is part of the Yosef-cycle's prison-lexicon.**

**The lexeme *sohar*.** סֹהַר (*sohar*) is a rare noun attested **only** in the *beit ha-sohar* phrase, confined to the Yosef narrative (Gen 39:20-23; 40:3, 5). Its etymology is genuinely uncertain and is debated along three lines: (a) **the 'round / enclosure' reading** — *sohar* is linked to a root *s-h-r* connoting 'round, circular, enclosed' (cf. the noun *sahar*, 'roundness', in Song 7:3; the *socherah* of Ps 91:4, debated), giving 'the round-house' — a circular fortress-tower or walled enclosure used as a place of detention. (b) **the Egyptian-loanword reading** — given the narrative's Egyptian setting, several scholars take *sohar* as an Egyptian loanword for a specific institution (a fortress, a labor-detention compound, or the prison attached to a royal estate); the loan-source is not securely identified, which keeps the reading hypothetical. (c) **the by-form-of-*sahar* reading** — *sohar* as a phonological variant of a confinement-noun. The three readings converge on a sense of an *enclosed, secured detention-structure* — a state-prison rather than the improvised dry cistern of Gen 37 — but none is demonstrable, and the noun's hapax-restriction to the Yosef cycle is itself part of the datum (the narrator uses a marked, perhaps deliberately Egyptianizing, term for the Egyptian prison).

**The rendering-traditions.** The English tradition is near-unanimous on the functional 'prison' (ASV / KJV / NRSV / NJPS / Alter), since whatever the etymology, the *place where the king's prisoners were bound* is plainly a prison. The 'round-house' rendering surfaces in commentary and in some philological discussions but does not displace 'prison' on the surface of any major version, because the etymology is too uncertain to carry a translation. The officer-title *sar beit ha-sohar* is rendered 'keeper of the prison' (ASV/KJV) or 'chief jailer' (NJPS/NRSV); the same *sar*-of-an-office construction governs the *sar ha-mashqim* / *sar ha-ofim* (chief cupbearer / chief baker) of Gen 40.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'the prison'** (vv 20-23) and **'the keeper of the prison'** (the *sar beit ha-sohar*, vv 21-23), per the accuracy-above-lens / ASV-baseline convention — the functional 'prison' is uncontested and the *sohar* etymology too uncertain to license a marked surface rendering. The 'round-house / enclosure' etymology, the Egyptian-loanword question, and the *sohar*-as-hapax (restricted to the Yosef cycle) datum are documented in commentary so the reader can audit the distinction from the Gen 37 *bor*.

**Central housing.** The phrase recurs immediately and load-bearingly in **Gen 40** — *beit ha-sohar* at 40:3 and 40:5 (the place where the chief cupbearer and chief baker are confined alongside Yosef) — and is the spatial setting of the Gen 40-41 dream-interpretation sequence that pivots the whole Yosef cycle. The cross-cycle recurrence (Gen 39-40, with the institution structuring the Gen 40-41 narrative) justifies a **central** entry governing the phrase and the *sar beit ha-sohar* office-title project-wide; the *appliesTo* is anchored at the Gen 39 occurrences and is extensible to Gen 40:3 + 5 when that chapter is drafted. (Note the related but distinct confinement-vocabulary: the *bor* of Gen 37 — see *bor-pit-cistern-dry-water-shaft* — and the *beit ha-bor* 'house of the pit / dungeon' that Yosef himself uses of the prison at Gen 40:15 + 41:14; the prison-lexicon spans three terms and is cross-referenced across those entries.)

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the functional 'prison' is direct and uncontested, but (b) the *sohar* etymology — the recorded substance of the entry — is genuinely unsettled across the round-house / loanword / by-form readings; (c) the ASV-baseline 'prison' is retained as the surface while the commentary carries the etymological and lexicon-distinction material. The entry does not adjudicate the etymology; it documents the debate.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *sohar* and *sahar*; BDB p. 690; NIDOTTE 3:235; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 274; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 70; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 469; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 304; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 366; for the Egyptian-prison-institution background: J. Vergote *Joseph en Égypte* (1959) 25-28; D. B. Redford *A Study of the Biblical Story of Joseph* (VTSup 20, 1970) 23-24.

terms.translit: chesed (here in the idiom va-yet elav chased, 'and inclined chesed toward him')
terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine singular (here the object of natah, 'to incline/extend')
terms.literal: chesed — loyal-love, steadfast-love, covenant-loyalty, kindness extended (esp. by a stronger party to a weaker, beyond what is owed)
terms.standardEnglish: mercy / kindness (ASV/KJV); steadfast love (NRSV); favor / kindness (NJPS); steadfast kindness (Alter)
terms.wohChoice: kindness (ASV-baseline 'showed kindness unto him'; the covenant-loyalty / steadfast-love range and the chesed-v-emet pair-relation documented in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Gen 39:21 records the prison-turn of the Yosef narrative in covenant-loyalty language: *va-yehi YHWH et-Yosef va-yet elav chased* — *and YHWH was with Yosef and inclined* chesed *toward him* — the same divine-presence refrain that frames the chapter (vv 2, 3, 5, 23) now glossed as YHWH's *chesed* extended to Yosef at his lowest point. This is *chesed* standing ALONE — not the word-pair *chesed v-emet* — and is governed by a dedicated entry distinct from the pair-entry.**

**Relation to the pair-entry (companion, not duplicate).** The central entry *chesed-v-emet-steadfast-love-and-truth* governs the doubled virtue-formula *chesed v-emet* ('chesed and truth') across the corpus (Gen 24:27, 24:49; Gen 32:11; Exod 34:6; the Psalter; the wisdom corpus; etc.) and preserves *chesed* untranslated *within that fixed formula*. The present entry governs the far more frequent SOLITARY *chesed* — the noun on its own, not in the *v-emet* pair — beginning at its first solitary occurrence in the WoH corpus at Gen 39:21. The two entries are companions: the pair-entry documents the hendiadys and keeps *chesed* untranslated inside the formula; this entry documents solitary *chesed* and follows the ASV-baseline 'kindness' on the surface. They are deliberately kept separate (one lexical decision each) and cross-reference one another.

**The lexeme.** *Chesed* is the central Hebrew Bible relational-virtue noun, with no single English equivalent. Its semantic core (after Glueck and Sakenfeld) covers: (a) loyal-action toward a partner in an established relationship; (b) gracious-action exceeding what is strictly owed; (c) the disposition grounding (a) and (b). **Glueck** (*Das Wort Hesed*, 1927; ET *Hesed in the Bible*, 1967) established the covenant-loyalty reading; **Sakenfeld** (*The Meaning of Hesed in the Hebrew Bible*, HSM 17, 1978) refined it: *chesed* is characteristically the act of a more-powerful party on behalf of a less-powerful party in an established relationship, where the stronger party could have declined and the weaker has no formal-legal claim. That refinement fits Gen 39:21 precisely: YHWH 'inclines *chesed*' to the imprisoned, powerless Yosef — an unforced act of covenant-loyalty by the stronger party. The classical English renderings cover the range: KJV/ASV 'mercy / kindness' (older, narrower); NRSV 'steadfast love' (modern critical default after Glueck); NJPS 'favor / kindness'; scholarly 'loyal-love', 'covenant-loyalty', 'covenant-faithfulness'.

**The idiom *natah... chesed*.** The verb here is *natah* ('to stretch out, incline, extend') with *chesed* as object — *to extend / incline chesed toward someone* — a relatively rare construction (cf. Ezra 7:28; 9:9, *hittah elai chesed*, of God inclining chesed toward the exiles before the Persian kings; and the more common *asah chesed im/et*, 'to do chesed with'). The 'inclining / extending' image fits the narrative's reversal: at the moment Yosef is delivered into the prison, YHWH *inclines* loyal-love toward him, and the chapter closes (vv 21-23) with that *chesed* materializing as favor in the eyes of the *sar beit ha-sohar*.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'showed kindness unto him'** (v 21), per the accuracy-above-lens / ASV-baseline convention — 'kindness' is a defensible rendering of solitary *chesed* and the dominant older-English choice. The fuller covenant-loyalty / steadfast-love range (Glueck / Sakenfeld), the *natah... chesed* 'inclining' idiom, and the relation to the *chesed v-emet* pair-entry are documented in commentary. (Solitary *chesed* is NOT preserved untranslated here, unlike *chesed* inside the fixed *v-emet* formula: the pair-entry's untranslated-preservation is specific to the formula, where the hendiadys resists a single English word; the solitary noun takes the ASV-baseline 'kindness' with the range carried in apparatus.)

**Central housing.** Solitary *chesed* is one of the most cross-corpus-reachable lemmas in the entire Hebrew Bible — ~245 occurrences across the Torah, the historical books, the Psalter (where it is a structural keyword — Ps 136's refrain *ki le-olam chasdo*, 'for his chesed endures forever'; Ps 103; Ps 130:7), the wisdom corpus, and the prophets (Hos 6:6, *chesed chafatzti ve-lo zavach*; Mic 6:8, *ahavat chesed*; Jer 9:23). A single governing lexical decision for solitary *chesed* must reach across all of these → decisively **central**. The *appliesTo* is inaugurated at Gen 39:21 (the first solitary occurrence in the WoH corpus) and is extensible across the corpus as solitary *chesed* recurs (within the Yosef cycle at Gen 40:14 — Yosef's request to the cupbearer, *ve-asita-na imadi chesed*; and Gen 47:29).

The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) 'kindness', 'mercy', and 'steadfast love' are all lexically defensible renderings of solitary *chesed*; (b) the choice among them is interpretive (the covenant-loyalty depth is established by the scholarly tradition, not forced by the bare noun); (c) the ASV-baseline 'kindness' is retained on the surface while the commentary carries the covenant-loyalty range. The entry documents the semantic range without importing project-specific synthesis (which is wiki-reserved).

**Scholarly literature.** Glueck *Das Wort Hesed* 1927 (ET *Hesed in the Bible* 1967); Sakenfeld *The Meaning of Hesed in the Hebrew Bible* HSM 17 (1978); Clark *The Word Hesed in the Hebrew Bible* JSOTSup 157 (1993); Britt *Choosing the Right Word: Hesed and 'Faithful Love' in Translation* (2003); HALOT s.v. *chesed*; BDB p. 338; TDOT V:44-64; NIDOTTE 2:211-218; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 274; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 70; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 p. 470; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 224.

terms.translit: ve-yabbem (Piel imperative of y-b-m); the institution: yibbum
terms.strongs: H2992 (yabam — Piel: to perform the levir's duty, marry a brother's widow); cf. H2993 yabam 'husband's brother', H2994 yevamah 'sister-in-law / widow subject to levirate'
terms.partOfSpeech: verb, Piel (denominative from yavam 'husband's brother'); imperative masculine singular
terms.literal: act as a husband's-brother toward her; perform the levir's duty
terms.standardEnglish: perform the duty of a husband's brother (ASV/KJV); do your duty as a brother-in-law / perform the levirate duty (NJPS, NRSV); do the brother-in-law's part (Alter)
terms.wohChoice: perform the duty of a husband's brother unto her (ASV-baseline; the levirate institution and its cross-corpus reach documented in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**Yehudah's instruction to Onan, *bo el-eshet achikha ve-yabbem otah ve-haqem zera le-achikha* (Gen 38:8), deploys the denominative Piel *ve-yabbem* — the technical verb of levirate marriage. The verb is built on the noun *yavam* (H2993, 'husband's brother', the surviving brother) and *yevamah* (H2994, the widow on whom the duty falls). It denotes a specific institution: when a man dies without a son, his brother takes the widow, and the firstborn of that union is reckoned legally to the dead man so that his name and line are not blotted out.**

**The lexeme and the institution.** The root y-b-m occurs in the HB only as this technical cluster: the verb *yabbem* (Gen 38:8; Deut 25:5, 7), the noun *yavam* (Deut 25:5, 7), and *yevamah* (Deut 25:7; Ruth 1:15, of Orpah as Naomi's other daughter-in-law). The institution is codified in **Deut 25:5-10** — the brother who refuses incurs the *chalitzah* shaming-ceremony (the removed sandal, *beit chalutz ha-na'al*, 'the house of him whose sandal was removed', Deut 25:10) — and it is the legal substrate of **Ruth 4**, where the redemption-marriage (*go'el* + the sandal-transfer, Ruth 4:7-8) recapitulates and extends the levirate logic to produce Oved, grandfather of David (Ruth 4:17-22). Gen 38 is thus the narrative *precedent* the Deuteronomic law and the Ruth narrative both presuppose; the three texts form the HB's levirate dossier and must be governed by a single lexical decision — hence **central** housing.

**The ANE comparanda.** Levirate-type institutions are attested in the Middle Assyrian Laws (MAL A §§30-33, 43) and the Hittite Laws (§193); the practice answers a shared ANE concern with the continuity of the patriline and the protection of the widow within the husband's household. The Gen 38 form is the earliest narrative attestation in the biblical corpus. Standard treatments: Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 266-267; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 pp. 50-52; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 435-437; for the institution: R. Westbrook *Property and the Family in Biblical Law* 1991; D. A. Leggett *The Levirate and Goel Institutions in the Old Testament* 1974; E. W. Davies 'Inheritance Rights and the Hebrew Levirate Marriage' VT 31 (1981) 138-144, 257-268.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation retains the ASV-baseline periphrasis **'perform the duty of a husband's brother unto her'** — the standard English rendering, which (unlike a transliteration or the bare 'marry her') correctly conveys that this is a *duty* attaching to the brother-in-law qua brother-in-law, not an ordinary marriage. The technical institution (levir / levirate), the Deut 25 codification, the Ruth 4 redemption-marriage extension, and the ANE comparanda are documented in commentary and here, not on the surface. The claim_type is *inferred* because the surface preserves the ASV periphrasis while the recorded substance is the technical levirate institution and its cross-corpus dossier — a lexical-institutional reading, not a grammatically self-evident gloss.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *ybm*; BDB p. 386; TDOT V:367-373; NIDOTTE 2:387-389; plus the institutional studies above.

terms.strongs: H6948 (qedeshah, feminine of H6945 qadesh; from the root q-d-sh 'to be set apart, consecrated, holy', H6942)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun, feminine singular (a feminine agent-noun from q-d-sh 'consecrate / set apart')
terms.literal: a set-apart / consecrated woman
terms.standardEnglish: harlot (ASV/KJV — collapsing it into zonah); cult prostitute / temple prostitute / sacred prostitute (older critical translations and many commentaries); prostitute (NJPS, with a note); consecrated/sacred woman (etymological renderings)
terms.wohChoice: qedeshah (transliterated; the cultic-vs-common-prostitute distinction and the contested status of 'sacred prostitution' documented in commentary)
terms.rationale:
**In Gen 38:21-22 the vocabulary shifts. The narrator twice called the disguised Tamar a *zonah* (a common prostitute, v 15), but when Hirah the Adullamite asks the locals for her, he uses a different word: *ayyeh ha-qedeshah* — 'where is the *qedeshah* that was at Einayim by the way?' (v 21) — and the men answer *lo haytah va-zeh qedeshah* ('there has been no *qedeshah* here', vv 21, 22). The *qedeshah* is a feminine agent-noun from the root q-d-sh, 'to be set apart / consecrated / holy' — literally a 'consecrated / set-apart woman'. The shift from *zonah* (narrator's word) to *qedeshah* (Hirah's word) is one of the most-discussed lexical features of the chapter, because it raises the contested question of cultic or 'sacred' prostitution in the ancient Near East.**

**The two scholarly positions — documented, not adjudicated.**

*(1) The traditional cultic reading.* On the older and still-widely-held view, the masculine *qadesh* (H6945) and feminine *qedeshah* (H6948) denote *cult functionaries* attached to a sanctuary, whose role included ritual sexual activity ('sacred', 'temple', or 'cultic' prostitution). The evidence assembled for this reading includes: the pairing of *qadesh*/*qedeshah* with *zonah* and with the cultic prohibitions in **Deut 23:18-19 [MT 17-18]** (no *qedeshah* among the daughters of Israel, no *qadesh* among the sons; the *etnan zonah* and *mechir kelev* — 'the wage of a prostitute' and 'the price of a dog' — barred from the YHWH-house); **Hos 4:14** (where men sacrifice *im ha-qedeshot*, 'with the qedeshot', in parallel with consorting with *zonot*); the *qedeshim* purged in the Deuteronomistic reform-notices (1 Kgs 14:24; 15:12; 22:47; 2 Kgs 23:7); and the perceived analogy with Mesopotamian temple-women and with the Herodotus 1.199 'Babylonian' custom and the Lucian *De Dea Syria* account. This is the reading behind the renderings 'cult prostitute / temple prostitute / sacred prostitute'. Representative: W. F. Albright; E. A. Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 300; G. von Rad; and the older ANE handbooks.

*(2) The revisionist denial.* A substantial body of recent scholarship argues that institutional 'sacred prostitution' is a **modern scholarly construct with no secure ancient basis** — that the ancient sources (Herodotus, Strabo, Lucian) are late, polemical, or misread, and that the Akkadian and biblical terms denote *consecrated cult-personnel* whose sexual role was inferred rather than attested. On this view *qedeshah* means simply a woman dedicated to a deity / a cult-servant, and the Genesis usage may mean no more than that Hirah euphemistically upgrades the *zonah* to a higher-status word (a 'consecrated woman' being a more polite designation than 'whore'). The decisive monographs are **S. L. Budin, *The Myth of Sacred Prostitution in Antiquity* (2008)** and J. G. Westenholz, 'Tamar, Qedesa, Qadistu, and Sacred Prostitution in Mesopotamia' HTR 82 (1989) 245-265; see also M. I. Gruber, 'Hebrew qedesah and Her Canaanite and Akkadian Cognates' UF 18 (1986) 133-148; P. A. Bird, *Missing Persons and Mistaken Identities* 1997, esp. the *zonah*/*qedeshah* essays; and the discussion in Frymer-Kensky *In the Wake of the Goddesses* 1992. On this reading the older 'sacred prostitute' translations import an institution that did not exist.

**The lexical core both positions share.** q-d-sh = 'set apart, consecrated, holy'; the *qedeshah* is, etymologically, a 'consecrated woman'. What the consecration entailed — temple cult-service with a sexual dimension (position 1), or cult-service whose sexual dimension is a modern projection (position 2) — is precisely what is contested.

**The translation choice — transliterate.** The WoH translation renders **'qedeshah' (transliterated)** at vv 21 and 22, exactly so as not to prejudge the cultic question on the surface. To render 'cult prostitute' would commit the text to position (1); to render 'harlot/prostitute' (ASV's collapse into *zonah*) would erase the deliberate *zonah*→*qedeshah* lexical shift that is the chapter's whole point at this seam; to render 'consecrated woman' would over-resolve toward position (2). Transliteration preserves the shift, flags the crux, and leaves the cultic question open — consistent with the She'ol / chashmal transliteration-of-the-irreducible-term precedent. Both named mainstream positions are documented here and in commentary; the project does not adjudicate.

**Central housing.** *Qedeshah* / *qadesh* recur cross-corpus — **Deut 23:18, Hos 4:14, 1 Kgs 14:24; 15:12; 22:47, 2 Kgs 23:7** — and the lexical decision must govern all of them uniformly. Decisive cross-corpus reach → **central**.

The claim_type is *inferred*: the surface (transliteration) preserves the MT lexeme, and the recorded substance is the documented standoff between two named mainstream positions plus the shared lexical core — an interpretive-philological reading, not a grammatically self-evident fact, and emphatically not a project-specific synthesis.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *qadesh* / *qedeshah*; BDB p. 873; TDOT XII:521-545 (q-d-sh); NIDOTTE 3:539-540; Budin 2008; Westenholz 1989; Gruber 1986; Bird 1997; Frymer-Kensky 1992; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 pp. 268-269; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 446-447; Wenham *WBC Genesis 16-50* 1994 pp. 368-369.

terms.strongs: H6556 (peretz, 'breach, bursting forth'); from H6555 (paratz, 'to break through, burst out, breach')
terms.partOfSpeech: proper noun (name) with explicit cognate aetiology on the verb/noun paratz / peretz
terms.literal: How have you broken-through / made-a-breach upon yourself? a breach! — hence 'Peretz' (Breach / Breaking-forth)
terms.standardEnglish: a breach ... Perez/Pharez (ASV/KJV); What a breach you have made for yourself! ... Perez (NJPS, NRSV); Breach (Alter, glossing the name)
terms.wohChoice: Wherefore hast thou made a breach for thyself? ... Peretz (ASV-baseline cognate clause; the name transliterated, so the paratzta/paretz/Peretz wordplay survives)
terms.rationale:
**At the twins' birth (Gen 38:29) the midwife's exclamation supplies an explicit name-aetiology: *mah-paratzta alekha paretz* — 'how have you broken through / what a breach you have made upon yourself!' — *va-yiqra shemo Paretz* ('and his name was called Peretz'). The name *Peretz* (H6556) is a cognate of the verb *paratz* (H6555, 'to break through, burst out, breach a wall') and the noun *peretz* ('a breach, a gap, a bursting-forth'). The midwife's cry and the name share the root p-r-tz, so the aetiology is built into the verse.**

**The lexeme.** *paratz* is the standard verb for breaching a wall or bursting out (2 Sam 5:20 *Ba'al-Peratzim*, 'the lord of breakings-forth', where YHWH 'breaks through' the enemy like a *peretz mayim*, 'a breach of waters'; Job 1:10; the breach of a city-wall). The noun *peretz* is a 'breach' in a wall (1 Kgs 11:27; Amos 4:3; Isa 58:12 *goder peretz*, 'repairer of the breach'). The naming-cry plays on the firstborn-displacement: Zerach's hand emerged first (v 28) but Peretz 'burst through' ahead of him to be born first — a breach of birth-order, of a piece with the Genesis younger-displaces-elder pattern (Yitzchaq/Yishmael, Ya'aqov/Esav, Ephraim/Menasheh).

**The Davidic-messianic line — the cross-corpus reach.** Peretz is no minor figure: he heads the **Davidic line**. **Ruth 4:18-22** gives the genealogy Peretz → Hetzron → ... → Boaz → Oved → Yishai → David, and Ruth 4:12 invokes him by name ('let your house be like the house of *Peretz*, whom Tamar bore to Yehudah'). **1 Chr 2:4-5** repeats the Peretz line; **1 Chr 4:1; Neh 11:4-6** track the Perezite clan; and the New Testament genealogy **Matt 1:3** carries Peretz (Phares) into the line of the Messiah. A single lexical/onomastic decision must therefore govern the Genesis aetiology *and* the Ruth, Chronicles, and (if the corpus reaches it) Matthew occurrences — decisive cross-corpus reach → **central**.

**The translation choice.** The WoH translation keeps the ASV-baseline cognate clause **'Wherefore hast thou made a breach for thyself?'** and transliterates the name **'Peretz'**, so the *paratzta / paretz / Peretz* wordplay survives through the transliteration without needing to gloss the name on the surface (the way 'Breach' would). This is the established WoH name-aetiology pattern: transliterate the name, render the cognate clause at ASV-baseline, carry the etymology in the apparatus. The claim_type is *inferred* because the surface is the ASV cognate clause + transliterated name while the recorded substance is the p-r-tz aetiology and the Davidic-line cross-corpus dossier.

**Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *prtz* / *peretz*; BDB pp. 829-830; TDOT XII:106-110; NIDOTTE 3:691-693; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 271; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 453-454; on Peretz and the Davidic line: Sarna ad loc.; the Ruth 4 genealogy commentaries (Hubbard *NICOT Ruth* 1988; Bush *WBC Ruth-Esther* 1996).

terms.translit: sar ha-mashqim (chief of the cupbearers/butlers); sar ha-ofim (chief of the bakers); base nouns mashqeh ('cupbearer', vv 1, 5, 13, 21) and ofeh ('baker', vv 1, 5)
terms.strongs: H8269 (sar — chief) + H4945 (mashqeh — cupbearer, drink, irrigation) + H644 (ofeh — baker, participle of afah, 'to bake')
terms.partOfSpeech: noun phrases: sar in construct + definite plural noun; base nouns mashqeh and ofeh
terms.literal: sar ha-mashqim — chief of those-who-give-drink (chief cupbearer / chief butler); sar ha-ofim — chief of the bakers; mashqeh — one who gives drink, a cupbearer
terms.standardEnglish: the chief butler / chief baker (ASV/KJV); the chief cupbearer / chief baker (NRSV/NJPS/Alter)
terms.wohChoice: the chief butler; the chief baker (ASV-baseline; the more literal 'cupbearer' for mashqeh — 'one who gives drink' — and the sar ha-X court-title pattern documented in commentary)
terms.rationale: **Genesis 40 turns on two Egyptian court-officials and their office-titles: the *sar ha-mashqim* (the *chief of the cupbearers / butlers*, vv 2, 9, 20, 21, 23) and the *sar ha-ofim* (the *chief of the bakers*, vv 2, 16, 20, 22). Their base offices — *mashqeh* (cupbearer/butler, vv 1, 5, 13, 21) and *ofeh* (baker, vv 1, 5) — open the chapter. The *sar ha-X* construction is the same office-title pattern the central glossary already governs for *sar ha-tabbachim* (captain of the guard) and *sar beit ha-sohar* (keeper of the prison).** // **The lexeme *mashqeh*.** מַשְׁקֶה (*mashqeh*) is a participial noun from the root שקה (sh-q-h, hiphil 'to give drink, to irrigate'), so its literal sense is **'one who gives drink' — a cupbearer**. The same noun denotes 'drink' (the thing given, Lev 11:34) and 'irrigated land / well-watered region' (Gen 13:10, *kullah mashqeh*, 'well watered everywhere'). As a court-office, *mashqeh* designates the official who served the king's wine and tasted it for poison — a position of high trust and intimate royal access (the *mashqeh* stands close to the king, hence the cupbearer's value to Yosef as a channel to Pharaoh). The older English tradition (KJV/ASV) renders 'butler' (from Old French *bouteillier*, 'bottle-keeper'); the modern tradition (NRSV/NJPS/Alter) prefers the more literal and more accurate **'cupbearer'**, since the office is wine-service-and-tasting, not the modern English 'butler' (a head household-servant). The ASV-baseline convention retains 'butler', with the more-literal 'cupbearer' noted. // **The lexeme *ofeh*.** אֹפֶה (*ofeh*) is the participle of אפה (a-f-h, 'to bake'), so 'baker' is exact and uncontested; v 17 has the cognate *ma'aseh ofeh* ('the work of a baker', baked goods). // **The court-title pattern.** The *sar ha-X* construction (literally 'chief of the X') names the head of a department of royal household officials — *sar ha-mashqim* heads the cup-bearing corps, *sar ha-ofim* heads the baking corps. Egyptian court administration is known to have had exactly such graded household-provisioning offices (the wdpw cupbearers and the royal bakery establishment), and the Genesis narrative's pairing of a chief-cupbearer and chief-baker as fellow-prisoners reflects an authentic Egyptian-court-milieu detail; the historicity-vs-novella question is discussed in the commentaries (Vergote, Redford). The two officials are also called *sarisim* (Pharaoh's 'officers', vv 2, 7) — governed by the central `saris-court-official-eunuch-ambiguity` entry — and confined in the same `beit-ha-sohar` (prison) under the *sar ha-tabbachim* (captain of the guard); the chapter's court-title vocabulary is dense and these office-titles interlock. // **The translation choice.** The WoH translation preserves the ASV-baseline **'chief butler' / 'chief baker'** (and 'butler' / 'baker' for the base offices), per the accuracy-above-lens / ASV-baseline convention. The more-literal-and-modern 'cupbearer' for *mashqeh* (the 'one who gives drink' etymology, the wine-service-and-tasting office, and the modern-version preference) is documented in commentary so the register-shift from 'butler' is auditable. // **Central housing.** *Mashqeh* in the cupbearer-office sense recurs cross-corpus — **Neh 1:11** (Nehemiah: *va-ani hayiti mashqeh la-melekh*, 'and I was cupbearer to the king' [of Persia], a load-bearing self-identification) and **1 Kgs 10:5 = 2 Chr 9:4** (the *mashqim* among the Solomonic-court splendors that astonish the Queen of Sheba) — and the *sar ha-X* office-title construction recurs throughout the HB court-administration vocabulary. A single governing decision must reach those occurrences → **CENTRAL**, parallel to the already-central `sar-ha-tabbachim-captain-of-the-guard`. The *appliesTo* is anchored at the Gen 40 occurrences and is extensible to Gen 41:9-10 (the chief cupbearer's recall) and the Nehemiah / Kings cupbearer-occurrences when those texts are drafted. // The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) 'baker' is direct and 'cupbearer/butler' is lexically secure, but (b) the butler-vs-cupbearer register-choice is interpretive and the *sar ha-X* office-identification carries an interpretive Egyptian-court-administration observation; (c) the ASV-baseline 'chief butler' / 'chief baker' stands on the surface. Named scholarship only; no project synthesis. // **Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *mashqeh* and *afah*; BDB pp. 1052, 66; TDOT XV:464-468 (sh-q-h); NIDOTTE 4:240-242; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 276; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 pp. 73-74; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 476-477; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 306; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 370; for the Egyptian-court-office background: J. Vergote *Joseph en Égypte* 1959 pp. 35-41; D. B. Redford *A Study of the Biblical Story of Joseph* VTSup 20, 1970 pp. 21-24.

terms.translit: pitron (plural pitronim, v 8); cognate verb p-t-r — poter (participle, v 8), patar (vv 16, 22)
terms.partOfSpeech: noun, masculine singular (pitron); with cognate verb p-t-r ('to interpret [a dream]')
terms.literal: pitron — the interpretation / solution / decoding of a dream; p-t-r — to interpret, to solve (a dream)
terms.standardEnglish: the interpretation (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS); to interpret
terms.wohChoice: the interpretation; to interpret (ASV-baseline; the 'solution / decoding' nuance of pitron and the root's near-exclusive restriction to the Yosef dream-cycle documented in commentary)
terms.rationale: **Genesis 40 introduces the keyword of the entire Yosef dream-cycle: *pitron* (פִּתְרוֹן), the *interpretation / solution* of a dream, with its cognate verb p-t-r 'to interpret'.** The noun appears at vv 5, 8 (plural *pitronim*), 12, 18, and the verb at v 8 (*poter*, participle: 'one who interprets'), v 16, and v 22 (*patar*). The cluster saturates the chapter and continues through Gen 41 (the Pharaoh-dream sequence). // **The lexeme.** The root פתר (p-t-r) means 'to interpret, to solve, to decode' — specifically the *decoding of a dream's hidden meaning*. The noun *pitron* is the result-noun: the solution arrived at. The semantic core is closer to 'solution / decipherment' than to the looser 'meaning' — a dream is treated as an encoded message whose *pitron* is its decryption, which is why some commentators (Sarna, Alter) flag the 'solving' nuance. The classical English tradition (ASV/KJV/NRSV/NJPS) uniformly renders 'interpretation' / 'to interpret', which is defensible and dominant; the 'solution / decoding' nuance is carried in apparatus rather than on the surface. // **The root's near-exclusive restriction to the Yosef cycle.** This is the philologically striking datum: the root p-t-r in the sense 'interpret a dream' is **almost wholly confined to Genesis 40-41** within the Hebrew Bible. The noun *pitron* occurs ONLY in Gen 40-41 (40:5, 8, 12, 18; 41:11). The verb *patar* in the dream-interpretation sense is likewise confined to Gen 40-41 (40:8, 16, 22; 41:8, 12, 13, 15 [twice]). The synonymous Aramaic root פשר (p-sh-r) carries the same dream-interpretation function in the later corpus (the *pesher* of Daniel 2, 4, 5, 7 and the Qumran *pesharim*), and the relationship between Hebrew p-t-r and Aramaic p-sh-r is a recognized comparative datum (the two are cognate by the regular Hebrew ת / Aramaic שׁ correspondence). The lexeme's restriction marks Gen 40-41 as a self-contained dream-interpretation novella with its own technical vocabulary. // **Distinct from the dream-as-revelation formula.** This entry governs *pitron* — the human (or divine-enabled) *decoding* of a dream — and is deliberately distinct from the central `divine-dream-communication-formula` entry, which governs the *va-yavo Elohim el-X ba-chalom ha-lailah* clause-pattern (the dream as the medium of direct divine address, Gen 20:3, 31:24). The two are different categories: in the divine-dream-communication-formula the deity speaks IN the dream and the message is plain; in the Gen 40-41 *pitron*-pattern the dream is a symbolic puzzle (vines, baskets, cattle, ears of grain) whose meaning is hidden and must be *decoded*. The Gen 40 dreams are NOT theophanic dream-revelations and the divine-dream-communication-formula is correctly NOT applied to them; v 8's *ha-lo le-Elohim pitronim* ('Do not interpretations belong to Elohim?') claims that the *decoding-capacity* — not the dream itself — is Elohim's gift, which is the cycle's distinctive theology of dream-interpretation. // **Central housing.** The lemma's load-bearing recurrence across Gen 40 AND Gen 41 (the two chapters of the dream-cycle, where *pitron* / p-t-r is THE technical keyword), together with the Aramaic *pesher* cognate-cluster in Daniel and the wider dream-interpretation tradition, makes this a clear cross-chapter cluster that a single governing decision must reach → **CENTRAL**. The *appliesTo* is inaugurated at the Gen 40 occurrences and is extensible to Gen 41:8, 11-13, 15 when that chapter is drafted. // The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) 'interpretation' is the direct and dominant rendering, but (b) the 'solution / decoding' nuance and the root's technical-keyword status are interpretive observations carried in apparatus; (c) the ASV-baseline 'interpretation' stands on the surface. The entry records named scholarship only; no project-specific synthesis. // **Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *pitron* and *patar*; BDB p. 837; TDOT XII:167-170; NIDOTTE 3:715-716; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 277; Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 pp. 75-77; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 478-480; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 307; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 371; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 227; for the dream-interpretation tradition and the p-t-r / p-sh-r relation: A. L. Oppenheim *The Interpretation of Dreams in the Ancient Near East* 1956; J.-M. Husser *Dreams and Dream Narratives in the Biblical World* 1999.

terms.translit: nasa rosh (here yissa ... et-rosh, imperfect); v 13 yissa et-roshekha; v 19 yissa et-roshekha me-alekha; v 20 va-yissa et-rosh
terms.literal: to lift up the head — (idiomatically) to raise to honor / restore to favor / single out / take a census / pardon-and-release; here pivoted punningly toward decapitation
terms.standardEnglish: to lift up the head (ASV/KJV); lift up your head (NRSV); lift up / single out the head (NJPS)
terms.wohChoice: lift up the/thy head (ASV-baseline, rendered uniformly across vv 13, 19, 20 so the deliberate Hebrew pun survives in English; the idiom's range and the Gen 40 reversal documented in commentary)
terms.rationale: **Genesis 40 is built on a deliberate pun on the idiom *nasa rosh* — 'to lift up the head'.** Yosef uses *yissa Par'oh et-roshekha* of the cupbearer at v 13 in its positive idiomatic sense (*lift up thy head* = restore to office, raise to favor) and of the baker at v 19 in a grimly literal-and-punning sense (*yissa et-roshekha me-alekha* — *lift up thy head FROM OFF THEE* = decapitate, the *me-alekha* 'from off thee' weaponizing the idiom); then at v 20 the narrator uses the very same idiom of BOTH men together at Pharaoh's birthday feast (*va-yissa et-rosh sar ha-mashqim ve-et-rosh sar ha-ofim* — 'he lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker'), where the single idiom resolves oppositely for each: restoration for one, execution for the other. The wordplay is the chapter's structural keystone. // **The idiom.** *Nasa rosh* ('lift the head') is a Hebrew Bible idiom with a documented range of senses, all turning on the literal image of raising a bowed or lowered head: (a) **to restore to honor / lift from disgrace** — to raise the head of one who has hung it in shame (the v 13 sense; cf. 2 Kgs 25:27 = Jer 52:31, *nasa Evil-Merodakh et-rosh Yehoyakhin* — the Babylonian king 'lifted up the head of Jehoiachin' = released him from prison and restored his honor, a close parallel to the cupbearer's restoration); (b) **to take a census / number** — *se'u et-rosh* ('lift the head of [the congregation]') = count them, the technical census-idiom of Exod 30:12, Num 1:2, 1:49, 4:2, 4:22, 26:2 (counting by 'lifting each head'); (c) **to single out / show favor** — to lift one person's head above others, to grant precedence. The idiom is thus thoroughly cross-corpus and well-attested. // **The Gen 40 reversal.** The literary art of Gen 40 is to take the favorable idiom (sense a, restoration — the cupbearer at v 13, exactly the 2 Kgs 25:27 Jehoiachin-release sense) and reverse it for the baker at v 19 by adding *me-alekha* ('from off thee'): the head is still 'lifted', but lifted OFF. The grim pun is intensified because v 19 then continues *ve-talah otkha al-etz* ('and shall hang thee on a tree') and *ve-akhal ha-of et-besarkha* ('and the birds shall eat thy flesh') — the lifted-off head and the hanged body. At v 20 the narrator's single idiom (*va-yissa et-rosh* of both men 'in the midst of his servants') is deliberately neutral-ambiguous, resolving in opposite directions at vv 21-22. **Preserving the pun requires rendering all three with 'lift up the/thy head'** — the ASV-baseline does exactly this, and any differentiated rendering ('restore to office' v 13 / 'behead' v 19) would destroy the wordplay the Hebrew builds. The uniform rendering is therefore both the accuracy-above-lens ASV-baseline choice AND the choice that best preserves the source's literary device. // **Central housing.** *Nasa rosh* is a cross-corpus idiom (the census-formula of Exodus and Numbers; the Jehoiachin-release of 2 Kgs 25:27 / Jer 52:31; the favor-and-precedence sense elsewhere). A single governing decision for the idiom must reach those occurrences → **CENTRAL**. The *appliesTo* is inaugurated at the three Gen 40 occurrences (the locus of the deliberate pun) and is extensible to the census-formula and the 2 Kgs 25:27 parallel when those texts are drafted. // The claim_type is *inferred* because (a) the literal 'lift up the head' is direct, but (b) the idiom's sense-range and the deliberate Gen 40 reversal are interpretive literary observations carried in apparatus; (c) the ASV-baseline uniform rendering stands on the surface. Named scholarship only; no project synthesis. // **Scholarly literature.** HALOT s.v. *nasa* (the *nasa rosh* idiom-entry); BDB p. 670 (nasa, sense 1.b with rosh); TDOT X:24-40; NIDOTTE 3:160-163; Sarna *JPS Genesis* 1989 p. 278 (the v 13 / v 19 pun); Westermann *Genesis 37-50* 1986 p. 77; Hamilton *NICOT Genesis 18-50* 1995 pp. 480-481; Speiser *AB Genesis* 1964 p. 307; von Rad *Genesis* OTL 1972 p. 371; Alter *Five Books of Moses* 2004 p. 228 (on preserving the wordplay); for the 2 Kgs 25:27 release-idiom parallel: J. Gray *I & II Kings* OTL 1970.
